topics.title: Science and Technology Behind Micro-Robot - Definition of Microrobot. Fundamental Concepts of Linear Elasticity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Science and Technology Behind Micro-Robot - Definition of Microrobot. Fundamental Concepts of Linear Elasticity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Microrobot

A **microrobot** is a class of robotic device whose characteristic dimensions fall within the **micrometer to millimeter scale** (typically **< 1 mm** to a few centimeters in functional size), designed to perform specific tasks such as manipulation, locomotion, sensing, or actuation in micro-scale environments. According to the KTU 2024 scheme definition, a microrobot is not merely a "scaled-down" macro-robot — its design, fabrication, and operational physics are fundamentally dictated by the dominant forces and phenomena that emerge at the micro-scale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERUT864 – Module 1):**
> A microrobot is an autonomous or semi-autonomous miniature machine whose critical dimensions lie in the **0.1 µm – 10 mm** range, and whose operation is governed by micro-scale physics (surface tension, van der Waals forces, viscous drag, electrostatic interactions) rather than by inertia-dominated macro-scale dynamics.

### Classification by Size (KTU 2024 Framework)

| Class | Characteristic Size | Dominant Physics |
|---|---|---|
| **Nanorobot** | 1 nm – 1 µm | Molecular interactions, Brownian motion |
| **Microrobot** | 1 µm – 1 mm | Surface tension, viscosity, electrostatics |
| **Millirobot** | 1 mm – 10 mm | Mixed micro/macro scaling |
| **Minirobot** | 10 mm – 100 mm | Transitional regime |

> [!NOTE]
> The boundary between "micro" and "macro" is **NOT** based on a single absolute dimension — it is defined by the **scale at which surface/volume forces dominate over body/inertial forces**.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Why Microrobots Are NOT Miniaturized Robots

Imagine you take an elephant (mass ≈ 5000 kg) and try to shrink it to the size of an ant (mass ≈ 0.000005 kg) while keeping the **same proportions**. Would it still be able to carry its body weight? **No.** Why?

- **Mass (∝ L³):** When length $L$ is reduced by a factor of 1000, mass drops by a factor of $10^9$.
- **Cross-sectional area of legs (∝ L²):** Reduces only by $10^6$.
- **Strength of bones (∝ area):** Reduces by $10^6$.

So the ant-sized elephant's bones are **1000× weaker relative to its weight** than the real elephant's. **This is the scaling law problem.**

The same principle applies to robots. As size shrinks:

$$\frac{\text{Surface Forces}}{\text{Volume Forces}} \;\propto\; \frac{L^2}{L^3} \;=\; \frac{1}{L} \;\longrightarrow\; \infty \text{ as } L \to 0$$

This means at micro-scale, **surface forces (van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic, friction) completely overwhelm inertia and gravity.** A microrobot is therefore NOT a shrunken macro-robot — it must be **redesigned from scratch** using different physical principles.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world intuition:** A gecko walks on walls because at its foot-scale, **van der Waals forces** (surface forces) are stronger than gravity. A human cannot, because at human-scale, gravity dominates. **Microrobots live in the "gecko world."**

---

## 1.3 The Science & Technology Behind Microrobots (KTU Module 1 Overview)

The science of microrobotics rests on **four converging pillars**:

1. **Microscale Physics:** Surface tension, viscous flow (low Reynolds number), electrostatics, capillary forces, Brownian motion.
2. **Materials Science:** Smart materials (piezoelectric, shape-memory alloys, conducting polymers), compliant structures, bio-compatible substrates.
3. **Fabrication Technology:** MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems), photolithography, soft lithography, 3D micro-printing, laser machining.
4. **Linear & Non-Linear Solid Mechanics:** Because microrobot links, joints, and actuators undergo small elastic deformations, **linear elasticity theory is the foundational mathematical tool** used to design compliant flexures, micro-actuators, and load-bearing micro-structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Linear Elasticity matters for Microrobotics:**
> Almost every compliant micro-mechanism (flexure hinges, micro-grippers, MEMS actuators) operates in the **small-deformation, elastic regime**. The relationship between applied forces (loads, torques, electrostatic attraction) and resulting deformations (displacements, rotations) is **linear** — and is described by Hooke's Law generalized to 3D continua. Without mastering linear elasticity, one cannot analyze the **stiffness, deflection, natural frequency, or buckling behavior** of a microrobot's structural elements.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Concepts of Linear Elasticity — Introduction

**Linear elasticity** is the branch of continuum mechanics that describes the mechanical behavior of solid bodies that:
- Undergo **infinitesimally small deformations** (strains $\varepsilon \ll 1$),
- Have a **linear relationship** between stress and strain (obey the generalized Hooke's Law),
- **Recover their original shape completely** upon removal of load (purely elastic, no permanent deformation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Assumption:** The material is **homogeneous** (same properties at every point), **isotropic** (same properties in every direction), and **elastic** (deformations are reversible).

### Why "Linear"?
The relationship between the **stress tensor** $\sigma_{ij}$ and the **strain tensor** $\varepsilon_{ij}$ is a **first-order (linear) tensor equation**:

$$\sigma_{ij} = C_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$

where $C_{ijkl}$ is the **fourth-order stiffness (elasticity) tensor**. For isotropic materials, this reduces to just **two independent constants**: Young's modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$.

---

## 1.5 The Two Fundamental Field Variables: Stress and Strain

### A. Stress ($\sigma$) — The "Internal Force Intensity"

When external loads are applied to a body, internal forces are developed at every imaginary cross-section. **Stress** is the intensity of these internal forces per unit area.

**Two physical types:**

1. **Normal stress** $\sigma_n$ — acts perpendicular to the cross-section (pulling or compressing).
2. **Shear stress** $\tau$ — acts tangentially (sliding one part over another).

For a 3D continuum, stress is fully described by the **Cauchy stress tensor**:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$

With **symmetry:** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$, $\tau_{xz} = \tau_{zx}$, $\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy}$ (conservation of angular momentum).

### B. Strain ($\varepsilon$) — The "Normalized Deformation"

Strain is the **dimensionless measure of deformation** relative to the original configuration.

**Two physical types:**

1. **Normal (longitudinal) strain** $\varepsilon_{xx}$ — relative change in length along a direction.
2. **Shear strain** $\gamma_{xy}$ — change in angle (in radians) between two originally perpendicular line elements.

**Infinitesimal (engineering) strain tensor:**

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$$

where $u_i$ are components of the displacement field $\vec{u}(x,y,z)$.

> [!TIP]
> **Geometric meaning of strain:** Normal strain tells you "how much did this line stretch?" Shear strain tells you "how much did this angle distort?" Both are *relative* (dimensionless) quantities — that's why they are denoted in meters/meter (m/m), i.e., unitless.

---

## 1.6 Real-World Engineering Utility in Microrobotics

Linear elasticity theory is **indispensable** in:

| Microrobot Component | Role of Linear Elasticity |
|---|---|
| **Compliant flexure hinges** | Calculate rotation angle under applied torque |
| **MEMS micro-actuators** | Predict displacement under electrostatic force |
| **Micro-grippers** | Determine gripping force vs. jaw deflection |
| **Cantilever beams (AFM probes)** | Compute tip deflection under atomic force |
| **Micro-springs** | Design stiffness constant $k$ for suspension |
| **Structural frame** | Verify stress remains below yield strength |
| **Piezoelectric stacks** | Couple electric field to mechanical strain |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** In microrobot design, students often forget that **surface (residual) stresses** from thin-film deposition (in MEMS) can reach **hundreds of MPa** — comparable to bulk stresses. Always include **initial/residual stresses** in the linear elasticity formulation for MEMS structures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Constitutive Law of Linear Elasticity (Generalized Hooke's Law)

The complete 3D constitutive relationship for a linear elastic isotropic material is given by:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where:
- $\lambda$ = **Lamé's first constant** (units: Pa)
- $\mu$ = **Lamé's second constant** = **Shear modulus** $G$ (units: Pa)
- $\delta_{ij}$ = **Kronecker delta** ($\delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i = j$, else $0$)
- $\varepsilon_{kk} = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz}$ = **dilatation** (volumetric strain)

In **expanded matrix form** for isotropic linear elasticity:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} \\ \sigma_{yy} \\ \sigma_{zz} \\ \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \tfrac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_{xx} \\ \varepsilon_{yy} \\ \varepsilon_{zz} \\ \gamma_{yz} \\ \gamma_{xz} \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix}$$

The prefactor $E/[(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$ is the **bulk modulus** $K$ (Lamé's $\lambda$ plus $\tfrac{2}{3}\mu$).

---

## 2.2 Engineering Constants and Their Relationships

A linear elastic isotropic material is fully characterized by **any two** of the following four constants:

| Constant | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Range/Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Young's Modulus** | $E$ | Stiffness under uniaxial tension ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) | **1 GPa – 1000 GPa** |
| **Shear Modulus** | $G$ | Stiffness under pure shear ($\tau = G\gamma$) | $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ |
| **Bulk Modulus** | $K$ | Resistance to uniform compression ($p = K\Delta$) | $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ |
| **Poisson's Ratio** | $\nu$ | Lateral contraction per unit axial strain | $\nu \in [-1, 0.5]$ (physically **0 < ν < 0.5**) |

### The Master Relationship (must memorize):

$$\boxed{\;E \;=\; 2G(1+\nu) \;=\; 3K(1-2\nu)\;}$$

---

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions — The "Two Halves" of an Elasticity Problem

A well-posed problem in linear elasticity requires three components:

1. **Equilibrium equations** (Newton's 2nd law for static case)
2. **Kinematic relations** (strain-displacement)
3. **Constitutive relations** (Hooke's law)

…together with **boundary conditions (BCs)** of two complementary types:

| BC Type | Specification | Physical Realization in Microrobots |
|---|---|---|
| **Displacement (Essential / Dirichlet)** | $\vec{u} = \vec{u}_0$ on $S_u$ | Clamped end of cantilever, fixed anchor |
| **Traction (Natural / Neumann)** | $\sigma \cdot \vec{n} = \vec{t}$ on $S_\sigma$ | Applied force, pressure, electrostatic pull |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** You **cannot** solve an elasticity problem unless **one part of the boundary is displacement-specified** (otherwise the body has a rigid-body mode) and the boundary is **fully covered**: $S_u \cup S_\sigma = \partial\Omega$ (entire boundary).

---

## 2.4 Governing Equations in Displacement Form (Navier's Equations)

Substituting strain-displacement and constitutive relations into the equilibrium equation $\nabla \cdot \sigma + \vec{f} = \rho \ddot{\vec{u}}$ yields **Navier's equations of elastodynamics**:

$$\mu \nabla^2 \vec{u} + (\lambda + \mu)\nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{u}) + \vec{f} = \rho \frac{\partial^2 \vec{u}}{\partial t^2}$$

For **static** problems (no acceleration, no body force):

$$(\lambda + \mu)\nabla(\nabla \cdot \vec{u}) + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{u} + \vec{f} = \vec{0}$$

These are the **3 PDEs in 3 unknowns** ($u_x, u_y, u_z$) that fully describe linear elasticity in displacement form.

---

## 2.5 Plane Stress and Plane Strain (Critical for MEMS)

Many microrobot structures (thin plates, micro-beams, diaphragms) can be reduced to 2D problems.

### Plane Stress (thin plate, $\sigma_{zz} = 0$)
Out-of-plane stress is zero because top/bottom surfaces are traction-free.

$$\sigma_{xx} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_{xx} + \nu \varepsilon_{yy}\right), \quad \sigma_{yy} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2}\left(\varepsilon_{yy} + \nu \varepsilon_{xx}\right), \quad \tau_{xy} = G \gamma_{xy}$$

### Plane Strain (long cylinder, $\varepsilon_{zz} = 0$)
Out-of-plane strain is zero (constrained by adjacent material).

$$\sigma_{xx} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[(1-\nu)\varepsilon_{xx} + \nu \varepsilon_{yy}\right]$$

The only difference: replace $1/(1-\nu^2)$ with $1/[(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)]$ — i.e., use **effective modulus** $E' = E/(1-\nu^2)$ (plane stress) vs. $E/(1-\nu^2)$ doesn't simplify — rather the matrix prefactor changes.

---

## 2.6 Airy Stress Function (2D Problem Simplification)

For 2D static problems with **no body forces**, stresses can be derived from a single scalar function $\phi(x,y)$ called the **Airy stress function**:

$$\sigma_{xx} = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}, \quad \sigma_{yy} = \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2}, \quad \tau_{xy} = -\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x \partial y}$$

The function $\phi$ must satisfy the **biharmonic equation**:

$$\nabla^4 \phi = \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^4} + 2\frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial x^2 \partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^4 \phi}{\partial y^4} = 0$$

This is **automatically** satisfied for any **biharmonic** function — used in KTU problems involving concentrated loads, pressurized holes, etc.

---

## 2.7 Strain Energy Density (Energy Method)

For linear elastic materials, the **strain energy per unit volume** (energy density) stored is:

$$U_0 = \frac{1}{2}\sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E}$$

For a 3D state:

$$U_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}(\sigma_{xx}\varepsilon_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}\varepsilon_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}\varepsilon_{zz} + \tau_{xy}\gamma_{xy} + \tau_{yz}\gamma_{yz} + \tau_{xz}\gamma_{xz})$$

**Total strain energy** in a volume $V$:

$$U = \int_V U_0 \, dV = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij} \, dV$$

This is the basis of the **Finite Element Method (FEM)** and **Castigliano's theorem** used heavily in microrobot flexure design.

---

## 2.8 The Stiffness Matrix of a Microrobot Element

In matrix structural analysis (used for compliant mechanisms in micro-robotics), the relation between nodal forces $\{F\}$ and nodal displacements $\{u\}$ is:

$$\boxed{\;\{F\} \;=\; [K]\{u\}\;}$$

where $[K]$ is the **global stiffness matrix**, assembled from element stiffness matrices. For a truss element of length $L$, area $A$, modulus $E$:

$$[k_e] = \frac{EA}{L}\begin{bmatrix} c^2 & cs & -c^2 & -cs \\ cs & s^2 & -cs & -s^2 \\ -c^2 & -cs & c^2 & cs \\ -cs & -s^2 & cs & s^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

with $c = \cos\theta$, $s = \sin\theta$, $\theta$ = orientation angle.

---

## 2.9 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Use in Microrobotics |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Definition of normal stress | Load analysis on micro-beams |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L/L$ | Definition of normal strain | Displacement of flexure hinges |
| 3 | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (uniaxial) | Stiffness design |
| 4 | $\tau = G\gamma$ | Hooke's Law (shear) | Torsional micro-shafts |
| 5 | $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ | E–G relation | Material selection |
| 6 | $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ | E–K relation | Pressure diaphragm design |
| 7 | $\nu = -(\varepsilon_{\text{lat}}/\varepsilon_{\text{ax}})$ | Poisson's ratio | Lateral expansion under axial load |
| 8 | $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda\delta_{ij}\varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu\varepsilon_{ij}$ | Generalized Hooke's Law | 3D stress analysis |
| 9 | $\varepsilon_{ij} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial_i u_j + \partial_j u_i)$ | Infinitesimal strain tensor | Kinematics of deformation |
| 10 | $\partial_j \sigma_{ij} + f_i = 0$ | Equilibrium equation | Static force balance |
| 11 | $u_0 = \tfrac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon$ | Strain energy density | Energy method (FEM) |
| 12 | $\{F\} = [K]\{u\}$ | Matrix stiffness equation | Compliant mechanism analysis |
| 13 | $\nabla^4 \phi = 0$ | Airy biharmonic equation | 2D stress concentration problems |
| 14 | $F/\text{Vol} \propto 1/L$ | Surface-to-volume scaling | Why microrobots need new designs |
| 15 | $\text{Re} = \rho v L / \mu$ | Reynolds number | Identifying creeping flow regime |

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Linear Elasticity |
|---|---|
| **MEMS Design** | Predicting pull-in voltage of electrostatic micro-actuators |
| **Biomedical Microrobots** | Designing compliant capsules that deform inside blood vessels |
| **AFM / Nanomanipulation** | Calibrating cantilever stiffness for force measurement |
| **Micro-Grippers** | Computing jaw opening vs. actuator input |
| **Vibration Analysis** | Determining natural frequencies to avoid resonance in micro-resonators |
| **Soft Robotics** | Modeling continuum deformation in elastomer-based robots |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Mantra:** *If a microrobot structure is **rigid** → use statics. If it **deforms** → use linear elasticity. If it **deforms a lot** → use non-linear elasticity (or hyperelasticity for rubber-like materials).*

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hooke's Law from a Uniaxial Tensile Test

We derive the 1D Hooke's law from the definitions of stress and strain, then extend it to 3D.

**Step 1 — Definition of Normal Stress:**

Consider a uniform bar of original cross-sectional area $A_0$ subjected to an axial tensile force $F$. The **average normal stress** on a cross-section perpendicular to $F$ is defined as the force per unit area:

$$\sigma_{xx} \;=\; \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} \;=\; \frac{F}{A_0}$$

**Physical meaning:** Stress is the *intensity* of internal force — it is **not** the total force, but the force *per unit area*. This is essential for scaling: the same $F$ on a smaller $A_0$ gives a larger $\sigma$.

**Step 2 — Definition of Normal Strain:**

If the bar of original length $L_0$ elongates by $\Delta L$ under the load, the **average normal strain** is:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} \;=\; \lim_{\Delta L_0 \to 0} \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \;=\; \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

**Physical meaning:** Strain is *dimensionless*. A strain of 0.001 means a 1-meter bar stretched by 1 mm. For linear elasticity, $\varepsilon \ll 1$ (typically $< 10^{-3}$).

**Step 3 — Experimental Observation (Robert Hooke, 1660):**

For many engineering materials (metals, silicon, ceramics, polymers — within their elastic limit), the stress-strain curve is **linear**:

$$\sigma_{xx} \;=\; E \cdot \varepsilon_{xx}$$

where the constant of proportionality $E$ is **Young's modulus**, having units of Pa (N/m²). Rearranged:

$$\boxed{\;F \;=\; \frac{EA_0}{L_0}\,\Delta L \;=\; k\,\Delta L\;}$$

This is the **stiffness of an axial bar**: $k = EA_0/L_0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The stiffness of a **micro-cantilever** scales linearly with $E$ and $A_0$, and inversely with $L_0$. To make a *softer* micro-cantilever (e.g., for AFM force sensing), one either chooses a material with lower $E$, **thins** it (smaller $A_0$), or **lengthens** it.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Poisson's Effect — Lateral Contraction

**Step 1 — Phenomenon:**
When a bar is stretched axially, it simultaneously **contracts laterally**. Conversely, compression axially causes lateral expansion. This is the **Poisson effect**.

**Step 2 — Definition:**
The **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$ is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to axial strain:

$$\nu \;=\; -\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}}$$

The negative sign is by convention: when $\varepsilon_{\text{axial}} > 0$ (tension), $\varepsilon_{\text{lateral}} < 0$ (contraction), giving $\nu > 0$.

**Step 3 — Range of Values:**

For a stable, isotropic linear elastic material, **thermodynamic constraints** require:
$$-1 \;<\; \nu \;<\; 0.5$$

In practice: **$0 < \nu < 0.5$** for engineering materials. Common values:

| Material | $\nu$ |
|---|---|
| Steel | 0.30 |
| Aluminum | 0.33 |
| Silicon | 0.28 |
| Glass | 0.23 |
| Rubber (nearly incompressible) | ≈ 0.49 |
| Cork | ≈ 0.00 |

**Step 4 — Volume Change Under Uniaxial Stress:**

For a rectangular block under axial stress $\sigma_{xx}$ (other stresses zero), strains are:

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{\sigma_{xx}}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_{yy} = \varepsilon_{zz} = -\nu\frac{\sigma_{xx}}{E}$$

The **volumetric strain** (dilatation) is:

$$\varepsilon_V \;=\; \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{xx}}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $\nu = 0.5$, the material is **incompressible** (no volume change under any stress state). This is the limiting case for rubber-like materials and is why $\nu = 0.5$ is the upper bound — perfect incompressibility.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Generalized 3D Hooke's Law (Isotropic Case)

**Step 1 — Decompose Strain into Volumetric + Deviatoric Parts:**

Any strain state can be split into:
- **Volumetric (hydrostatic) part:** $\varepsilon_V / 3$ in every direction.
- **Deviatoric (shape) part:** $\varepsilon_{ij} - \delta_{ij}\varepsilon_V/3$.

**Step 2 — Stress Decomposition:**

Similarly, $\sigma_{ij} = \sigma_m \delta_{ij} + s_{ij}$ where $\sigma_m = \sigma_{kk}/3$ is the **mean stress** and $s_{ij}$ is the **deviatoric stress**.

**Step 3 — Two Independent Material Constants:**

For isotropic materials, the response to **volumetric** deformation is governed by the bulk modulus $K$:

$$\sigma_m \;=\; K \cdot \varepsilon_V \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_{kk} = 3K\varepsilon_{kk}$$

And the response to **shape (deviatoric)** deformation is governed by the shear modulus $G$:

$$s_{ij} \;=\; 2G \, e_{ij} \quad \text{where } e_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ij} - \delta_{ij}\tfrac{\varepsilon_V}{3}$$

**Step 4 — Combine:**

After algebra (substituting $\sigma_m$ and $s_{ij}$ back into the decomposed form):

$$\sigma_{ij} = \left(K - \tfrac{2}{3}G\right)\varepsilon_{kk}\delta_{ij} + 2G\varepsilon_{ij}$$

Comparing with $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda\delta_{ij}\varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu\varepsilon_{ij}$, we identify:

$$\lambda = K - \tfrac{2}{3}G, \quad \mu = G$$

**Step 5 — Express in Terms of $E$ and $\nu$:**

Using $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ and $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$:

$$\lambda \;=\; \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}, \quad \mu \;=\; \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

The final form of the **generalized Hooke's Law** is:

$$\sigma_{ij} \;=\; \frac{E}{1+\nu}\left[\varepsilon_{ij} + \frac{\nu}{1-2\nu}\varepsilon_{kk}\delta_{ij}\right]$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Stiffness of a Micro-Cantilever Beam (Microrobot Flexure)

This derivation is **central** to microrobot compliant mechanism design.

**Step 1 — Geometry:**
A cantilever beam of length $L$, rectangular cross-section $b \times h$ (with $b > h$), fixed at $x=0$, free at $x=L$, loaded by a transverse force $P$ at the tip.

**Step 2 — Assumptions (Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory):**
- Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis.
- Material is linear elastic with Young's modulus $E$.
- Deflections are small ($w \ll L$).
- Shear deformation is neglected (valid when $L/h \gg 10$).

**Step 3 — Bending Moment at Section $x$:**

$$\boxed{M(x) \;=\; P(L-x)}$$

**Step 4 — Curvature–Moment Relation (Constitutive Law in Bending):**

The **Euler-Bernoulli equation** relates curvature to bending moment via flexural rigidity $EI$:

$$EI\,\frac{d^2w}{dx^2} \;=\; M(x)$$

where $I$ is the second moment of area. For a rectangular cross-section:

$$I \;=\; \frac{bh^3}{12}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Twice to Get Deflection:**

$$\frac{d^2w}{dx^2} \;=\; \frac{P(L-x)}{EI}$$

Integrating once (with slope BC: $dw/dx = 0$ at $x=0$):

$$\frac{dw}{dx} \;=\; \frac{P}{EI}\left(Lx - \frac{x^2}{2}\right)$$

Integrating again (with deflection BC: $w=0$ at $x=0$):

$$w(x) \;=\; \frac{P}{EI}\left(\frac{Lx^2}{2} - \frac{x^3}{6}\right)$$

**Step 6 — Tip Deflection (the most important result):**

Setting $x = L$:

$$\boxed{\;w_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \frac{PL^3}{3EI} \;=\; \frac{4PL^3}{Ebh^3}\;}$$

**Step 7 — Tip Stiffness (Force per Unit Deflection):**

$$\boxed{\;k_{\text{tip}} \;=\; \frac{P}{w_{\text{tip}}} \;=\; \frac{3EI}{L^3} \;=\; \frac{Ebh^3}{4L^3}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microrobot Design Consequence:** Tip stiffness scales as $h^3/L^3$. To make a **soft** micro-cantilever (e.g., for a sensitive AFM probe or compliant micro-gripper), one should **thicken the beam in length** and **thin it in height** — a *length/height* ratio of 100 gives 10⁶ times softer than a cube.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Deformation Under Biaxial Stress (Thin Diaphragm)

A thin square micro-diaphragm of side $a$, thickness $h$, is subjected to biaxial in-plane stress $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma$ (plane stress, since top/bottom surfaces are free).

**Step 1 — Apply Plane Stress Relations:**

$$\varepsilon_{xx} = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_{xx} - \nu\sigma_{yy}) = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1-\nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_{yy} = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_{yy} - \nu\sigma_{xx}) = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1-\nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_{zz} = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}) = -\frac{2\nu\sigma}{E}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Strain (Dilatation):**

$$\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_{xx} + \varepsilon_{yy} + \varepsilon_{zz} = \frac{2\sigma(1-\nu)}{E} - \frac{2\nu\sigma}{E} = \frac{2\sigma(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3 — Use Bulk Modulus Relation:**

Recall $\sigma_{kk} = 3K\varepsilon_V$. With $\sigma_{xx} = \sigma_{yy} = \sigma$, $\sigma_{zz} = 0$:

$$2\sigma = 3K\varepsilon_V = 3K \cdot \frac{2\sigma(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

Substituting $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$:

$$2\sigma = \frac{3 \cdot E}{3(1-2\nu)} \cdot \frac{2\sigma(1-2\nu)}{E} = 2\sigma \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms internal consistency of the theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Application in microrobotics:** Many pressure sensors, microphones, and micro-pumps use a thin diaphragm as the deformable element. Knowing $\varepsilon_{xx}$ under pressure is essential for designing the strain gauges or piezo sensors integrated on the diaphragm.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Equilibrium Equation Derivation from Cauchy's Tetrahedron

**Step 1 — Consider a Small Tetrahedron:**

Take an infinitesimal tetrahedron with three faces on the coordinate planes (with outward normals $-\hat{x}, -\hat{y}, -\hat{z}$) and the fourth (slanted) face with outward normal $\hat{n} = (n_x, n_y, n_z)$.

**Step 2 — Traction on the Slanted Face:**

By **Cauchy's stress theorem**, the traction vector on the slanted face is:

$$\vec{t}^{(n)} \;=\; \sigma \cdot \hat{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_i^{(n)} = \sigma_{ji} \, n_j$$

**Step 3 — Tractions on Coordinate Faces:**

The traction on the face with normal $-\hat{x}$ is $-\vec{t}^{(x)} = -\sigma_{ji}\delta_{1j} = -\sigma_{1i} = -\sigma_{xi}$.

**Step 4 — Force Balance (Sum of Tractions × Areas):**

With the slanted face area $dA$, the three coordinate face areas are $n_x dA$, $n_y dA$, $n_z dA$. Total force balance:

$$t_i^{(n)} dA - \sigma_{xi} n_x dA - \sigma_{yi} n_y dA - \sigma_{zi} n_z dA + f_i \cdot \tfrac{1}{3}(\text{volume}) \, dA = 0$$

In the limit $dA \to 0$, the volume term vanishes, yielding:

$$\boxed{\;t_i^{(n)} \;=\; \sigma_{xi} n_x + \sigma_{yi} n_y + \sigma_{zi} n_z \;=\; \sigma_{ji} n_j\;}$$

This is **Cauchy's stress theorem** — the foundation of stress analysis.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Strain Energy and Castigliano's Theorem

**Step 1 — Total Strain Energy:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2}\int_V \sigma_{ij}\varepsilon_{ij} \, dV$$

For uniaxial loading: $U = \frac{1}{2}\int \frac{\sigma^2}{E} dV = \frac{F^2 L}{2EA}$.

**Step 2 — Castigliano's Theorem (Displacement under Load):**

$$\boxed{\;\delta_i \;=\; \frac{\partial U}{\partial F_i}\;}$$

The displacement at the point of application of force $F_i$ equals the partial derivative of total strain energy with respect to that force.

**Step 3 — Verification for Cantilever:**

For a cantilever loaded by $P$ at the tip:

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^2}{2EI} dx = \int_0^L \frac{P^2(L-x)^2}{2EI} dx = \frac{P^2 L^3}{6EI}$$

Therefore:

$$\delta_{\text{tip}} = \frac{\partial U}{\partial P} = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the direct integration result from Derivation 4.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for microrobots:** Castigliano's theorem allows complex compliant mechanism deflection to be computed without solving PDEs — just by integration of strain energy. It is the workhorse of compliant micro-mechanism synthesis.

---

## 3.8 Analytical Problem-Solving Matrix

Below is a step-by-step **problem-solving matrix** for any linear elasticity problem in microrobot design:

| **Step** | **Action** | **Key Question** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify the **geometry** | Is it 1D bar, beam, plate, 3D solid? |
| 2 | Identify the **material** | Isotropic? Orthotropic? Anisotropic? What are $E, \nu$? |
| 3 | Identify **loading** | Static? Dynamic? Thermal? Body force? Surface traction? |
| 4 | Apply **equilibrium** | $\nabla \cdot \sigma + \vec{f} = \rho\ddot{\vec{u}}$ |
| 5 | Apply **kinematics** | $\varepsilon = \tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla\vec{u} + \nabla\vec{u}^T)$ |
| 6 | Apply **constitutive law** | $\sigma = \lambda\varepsilon_V \mathbf{I} + 2G\varepsilon$ |
| 7 | Apply **boundary conditions** | $u = u_0$ on $S_u$, $\sigma\cdot n = t$ on $S_\sigma$ |
| 8 | **Solve** for displacement, then strain, then stress | Closed form / Analytical / FEM |
| 9 | **Validate** — check equilibrium, BCs, and limits | Does $\varepsilon \ll 1$? Is $\sigma < \sigma_{\text{yield}}$? |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** Define a microrobot. Why is it incorrect to design a microrobot by simply scaling down a macro-robot?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A microrobot is an autonomous or semi-autonomous miniature machine whose characteristic dimensions lie in the **micro-scale range (0.1 µm to a few mm)** and whose operational physics is dominated by surface forces (van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic) and viscous drag rather than by inertia and gravity (1 mark).

It is incorrect to simply scale down a macro-robot because the ratio of surface forces to volume forces scales as **$1/L$** — as $L$ decreases, surface forces become overwhelmingly dominant (1 mark). Consequently, the same mechanical design principles (gears, linkages, bearings, electromagnetic motors) used at macro-scale fail at micro-scale due to issues like stiction, viscous drag, and fabrication limitations (1 mark).

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q:** State Hooke's Law in its generalized 3D form for an isotropic linear elastic material. Define all constants used.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The generalized Hooke's Law for an isotropic linear elastic material is:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \lambda \, \delta_{ij} \, \varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu \, \varepsilon_{ij}$$

where $\sigma_{ij}$ is the **Cauchy stress tensor**, $\varepsilon_{ij}$ is the **infinitesimal strain tensor**, $\delta_{ij}$ is the **Kronecker delta**, $\lambda$ and $\mu$ are **Lamé's constants** (1 mark).

$\lambda = \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ and $\mu = G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$, where $E$ is **Young's modulus**, $\nu$ is **Poisson's ratio**, and $G$ is the **shear modulus** (1 mark).

This can also be inverted as $\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1+\nu}{E}\sigma_{ij} - \frac{\nu}{E}\delta_{ij}\sigma_{kk}$ (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A — Set 1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q (a):** A silicon micro-cantilever used in an AFM (atomic force microscope) has length $L = 200\,\mu\text{m}$, width $b = 50\,\mu\text{m}$, and thickness $h = 2\,\mu\text{m}$. A transverse tip load of $P = 1\,\mu\text{N}$ is applied at the free end. The silicon has Young's modulus $E = 170\,\text{GPa}$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.28$.

**(i)** Calculate the tip deflection using Euler-Bernoulli beam theory.

**(ii)** Calculate the tip stiffness.

**(iii)** Determine the maximum bending stress at the fixed end.

**(iv)** Comment on whether the linear elastic assumption is valid.

**[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Tip Deflection:**

Formula: $w_{\text{tip}} = \dfrac{PL^3}{3EI}$, with $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$.

$$I = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-18})}{12} = 3.33 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{m}^4$$

$$w_{\text{tip}} = \frac{(1 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-6})^3}{3 \times (170 \times 10^9)(3.33 \times 10^{-23})}$$

$$w_{\text{tip}} = \frac{(10^{-6})(8 \times 10^{-15})}{(1.7 \times 10^{11})(3.33 \times 10^{-23})} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-21}}{5.67 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\boxed{w_{\text{tip}} \approx 1.41 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{m} = 1.41\,\text{nm}}$$

**(ii) Tip Stiffness:**

$$k_{\text{tip}} = \frac{P}{w_{\text{tip}}} = \frac{1\,\mu\text{N}}{1.41\,\text{nm}} \approx 0.71\,\text{N/m}$$

$$\boxed{k_{\text{tip}} \approx 0.71\,\text{N/m}}$$

**(iii) Maximum Bending Stress (at fixed end):**

Maximum moment at fixed end: $M_{\max} = PL = (10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{N·m}$

Section modulus: $Z = \dfrac{bh^2}{6} = \dfrac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})^2}{6} = 3.33 \times 10^{-17}\,\text{m}^3$

$$\sigma_{\max} = \frac{M_{\max}}{Z} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-10}}{3.33 \times 10^{-17}}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} \approx 6\,\text{MPa}}$$

**(iv) Validity of Linear Elasticity:**

Deflection ratio: $w_{\text{tip}}/L = 1.41\,\text{nm}/200\,\mu\text{m} \approx 7 \times 10^{-6} \ll 1$ → **valid** (small deflection).

Stress ratio: $\sigma_{\max}/E = 6\,\text{MPa}/170\,\text{GPa} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-5} \ll 0.001$ → **valid** (linear elastic regime).

Hence, **linear elastic Euler-Bernoulli theory is valid** for this micro-cantilever.

**[Full marks = 7]**

---

**Q (b):** Derive the generalized Hooke's Law for a 3D isotropic linear elastic material starting from the definitions of bulk modulus $K$ and shear modulus $G$. Show explicitly how Lamé's constants $\lambda$ and $\mu$ relate to $E$ and $\nu$. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Volumetric and Deviatoric Decomposition:**

For isotropic materials, the strain is decomposed into volumetric and deviatoric parts:

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{\varepsilon_V}{3}\delta_{ij} + e_{ij}, \quad \sigma_{ij} = \sigma_m \delta_{ij} + s_{ij}$$

where $\varepsilon_V = \varepsilon_{kk}$ (dilatation), $e_{ij}$ = deviatoric strain, $\sigma_m = \sigma_{kk}/3$, $s_{ij}$ = deviatoric stress. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Constitutive Equations for Each Part:**

Volumetric: $\sigma_m = K\varepsilon_V \Rightarrow \sigma_{kk} = 3K\varepsilon_{kk}$.

Deviatoric: $s_{ij} = 2Ge_{ij}$.

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Combine:**

$$\sigma_{ij} = K\varepsilon_{kk}\delta_{ij} + 2G\left(\varepsilon_{ij} - \frac{\varepsilon_{kk}}{3}\delta_{ij}\right) = \left(K - \frac{2G}{3}\right)\varepsilon_{kk}\delta_{ij} + 2G\varepsilon_{ij}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Identify Lamé's Constants:**

Comparing with $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda\delta_{ij}\varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu\varepsilon_{ij}$:

$$\mu = G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}, \quad \lambda = K - \frac{2G}{3} = \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5 — Final Form:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{ij} = \frac{E}{1+\nu}\left[\varepsilon_{ij} + \frac{\nu}{1-2\nu}\varepsilon_{kk}\delta_{ij}\right]}$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6 — Invert to Get Compliance Form:**

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \frac{1}{E}\left[(1+\nu)\sigma_{ij} - \nu\sigma_{kk}\delta_{ij}\right]$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 7 — Engineering Insight:**

For $\nu = 0.3$: $\lambda \approx 0.576E$, $G \approx 0.385E$. These dimensionless ratios are useful for order-of-magnitude estimation in microrobot structural design. **(1 mark)**

---

### ⭐ Question B — Set 2 (Alternative Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Q (a):** A microrobot micro-gripper consists of two cantilever beams made of a polymer with $E = 3\,\text{GPa}$ and $\nu = 0.35$. Each beam has $L = 1\,\text{mm}$, $b = 200\,\mu\text{m}$, $h = 20\,\mu\text{m}$. An actuator applies a force of $F = 100\,\mu\text{N}$ at the tip of each beam.

**(i)** Compute the maximum tip displacement and bending stress in each beam.

**(ii)** If the actuator force is doubled, does the displacement also double? Justify using linear elasticity.

**(iii)** Compute the shear modulus $G$ and bulk modulus $K$ for the polymer material.

**[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Tip displacement and stress:**

$I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12} = \dfrac{(200\times10^{-6})(20\times10^{-6})^3}{12} = \dfrac{(2\times10^{-4})(8\times10^{-15})}{12} = 1.333\times10^{-19}\,\text{m}^4$

$w_{\text{tip}} = \dfrac{FL^3}{3EI} = \dfrac{(100\times10^{-6})(10^{-3})^3}{3\times(3\times10^9)(1.333\times10^{-19})}$

$w_{\text{tip}} = \dfrac{10^{-16}}{1.2\times10^{-9}} = 8.33\times10^{-8}\,\text{m} = 83.3\,\text{nm}$ **(2 marks)**

$M_{\max} = FL = 10^{-4} \times 10^{-3} = 10^{-7}\,\text{N·m}$

$Z = \dfrac{bh^2}{6} = \dfrac{(2\times10^{-4})(4\times10^{-10})}{6} = 1.333\times10^{-14}\,\text{m}^3$

$\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{10^{-7}}{1.333\times10^{-14}} = 7.5\,\text{MPa}$ **(1 mark)**

**(ii) Linearity check:**

Since $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ is a *linear* relation, doubling the force doubles the moment, which doubles the curvature, which doubles the slope and deflection. So **YES, displacement doubles to 166.6 nm** — provided the material remains in the linear elastic regime (i.e., $\sigma < \sigma_{\text{yield}}$). For polymer, $\sigma_{\text{yield}} \sim 50\,\text{MPa}$, so $7.5\,\text{MPa}$ is well within the linear range. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) $G$ and $K$:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{3}{2(1.35)} = 1.11\,\text{GPa}$$

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{3}{3(0.3)} = 3.33\,\text{GPa}$$

**(2 marks)**

---

**Q (b):** Explain the scaling laws that govern the dominance of surface forces over volume forces at micro-scales. How does this affect the design philosophy of microrobots compared to macro-robots? Provide three specific design consequences. **[7 marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — The Scaling Law:**

When a body's linear dimension is $L$:
- Volume (and mass) $\propto L^3$
- Surface area $\propto L^2$
- Body forces (gravity, inertia) $\propto L^3$
- Surface forces (van der Waals, surface tension, friction) $\propto L^2$

Therefore: $\dfrac{\text{Surface Force}}{\text{Volume Force}} \propto \dfrac{L^2}{L^3} = \dfrac{1}{L} \to \infty$ as $L \to 0$. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Dominant Forces at Micro-Scale:**

At micro-scale, surface tension, van der Waals forces, electrostatics, and viscous drag dominate; gravity and inertia become negligible. This is the fundamental reason microrobots must be redesigned — not merely miniaturized. **(2 marks)**

**Step 3 — Three Specific Design Consequences:**

1. **Adhesion becomes problematic:** Microrobot parts stick to each other and to surfaces due to van der Waals and capillary forces — *stiction* failures are common in MEMS. Designers must use anti-adhesion coatings, surface texturing, or compliant release mechanisms. **(1 mark)**

2. **Locomotion must change:** Macro-robot wheels/tracks fail at micro-scale (too much friction, too little traction). Microrobots use **flagellar swimming** (low Reynolds number), **stick-slip walking**, **electrostatic crawling**, or **surface-tension-driven (Marangoni) propulsion**. **(1 mark)**

3. **Bearings and joints must be redesigned:** Conventional ball bearings cannot be fabricated at micron scale. Designers use **compliant flexures** (elastically deformable joints) that eliminate friction entirely. These rely on **linear elasticity theory** for accurate prediction. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that COST MARKS in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $\mu\text{m}$ with m, MPa with Pa. Always convert to SI base units **before** plugging into formulas. (Lose 0.5–1 mark)
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Poisson term:** In biaxial/multi-axial problems, many students write $\varepsilon_{xx} = \sigma_{xx}/E$ without the $-\nu\sigma_{yy}/E$ correction. This gives **wrong answers** and costs 2–3 marks. (KTU examiners are strict here.)
>
> 3. **Neglecting shear deformation:** Euler-Bernoulli beam theory is valid only when $L/h > 10$. For thick MEMS beams, use Timoshenko theory. Mentioning this assumption explicitly earns extra credit. (Lose 1 mark if not stated.)
>
> 4. **Confusing Plane Stress vs Plane Strain:** For thin plates use plane stress ($\sigma_{zz} = 0$); for long cylinders use plane strain ($\varepsilon_{zz} = 0$). The effective modulus differs by a factor of $(1-\nu)$ vs. $(1-\nu^2)$. (Lose 2 marks for mix-up.)
>
> 5. **Writing 1D Hooke's Law for 3D problems:** If the problem says "biaxial" or "3D stress state," you MUST use the generalized 3D Hooke's Law with $\lambda$ and $\mu$ (or full $6\times6$ matrix). Using $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ alone gives incomplete answers and loses 2–4 marks.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to define variables:** Every symbol used in an equation must be defined in the very next line. KTU valuation guidelines explicitly penalize undefined symbols. (Lose 0.5–1 mark per undefined variable.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Definitions to Memorize:**
> - Microrobot: characteristic size **0.1 µm – 10 mm**, dominated by surface forces
> - Linear elasticity: $\varepsilon \ll 1$, $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (linear, reversible)
> - Stress: force per unit area (Pa); Strain: dimensionless deformation
> - Cauchy stress tensor: $3\times3$ symmetric matrix (6 independent components)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Equations (Memorize the symbols AND the units):**
> - $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ (uniaxial)
> - $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$
> - Generalized 3D: $\sigma_{ij} = \lambda\delta_{ij}\varepsilon_{kk} + 2\mu\varepsilon_{ij}$
> - Cantilever tip deflection: $w_{\text{tip}} = PL^3/(3EI)$
> - Cantilever tip stiffness: $k = 3EI/L^3 = Ebh^3/(4L^3)$
> - Cantilever tip stress: $\sigma_{\max} = 6PL/(bh^2)$
> - Strain energy density: $u_0 = \sigma^2/(2E)$
> - Castigliano: $\delta = \partial U/\partial F$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaling Law (THE key microrobot principle):**
> - Volume forces $\propto L^3$ → become negligible
> - Surface forces $\propto L^2$ → dominate
> - Ratio = $1/L$ → $\infty$ at micro-scale

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions:**
> - Dirichlet (essential): specified displacement
> - Neumann (natural): specified traction
> - Always cover the entire boundary: $S_u \cup S_\sigma = \partial\Omega$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Must-Know Concepts:**
> - Definition of microrobot and classification by size
> - Why microrobots ≠ scaled-down macro-robots
> - The four pillars: micro-physics, materials, fabrication, mechanics
> - Generalized Hooke's Law in 3D
> - Plane stress vs. plane strain distinction
> - Stiffness analysis of compliant flexures (cantilever, hinge)
> - Stress-strain relations for isotropic materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Values to Remember:**
> - Young's modulus of Silicon: $\sim 170$ GPa
> - Young's modulus of Steel: $\sim 200$ GPa
> - Poisson's ratio of Silicon: $\sim 0.28$
> - Poisson's ratio of Steel: $\sim 0.30$
> - Incompressibility limit: $\nu \to 0.5$
> - Air: $\nu \approx 0$ (zero lateral strain under axial stress)

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy for KTU PERUT864:**
> 1. Always begin a derivation question by **stating assumptions** (isotropic, small deformation, linear elastic).
> 2. Always **draw a free-body diagram** in any mechanics problem — it earns you 1–2 marks for partial credit.
> 3. **Write units with every numerical answer.**
> 4. For microrobot design questions, **comment on surface vs. volume force scaling** — this is the "KTU flavor" of the course.
> 5. Mention **fabrication method** (MEMS, photolithography) wherever relevant to demonstrate integrated understanding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Stress ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Microrobotics** and **Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems (MEMS)**, **Stress** is defined as the internal distribution of internal forces within a deformable body (typically a micro-scale structural element such as a micro-beam, micro-cantilever, or thin-film microstructure) that balances and reacts to the externally applied loads. Mathematically, stress $\sigma$ at a point in a continuous medium is defined as the limit of the force vector $\Delta \vec{F}$ acting through an elemental area $\Delta A$ as the area approaches zero: $$ \sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta \vec{F}}{\Delta A} $$ The SI unit of stress is the **Pascal (Pa)**, which is equivalent to **N/m²**. In microscale engineering, stress values are commonly expressed in **MPa (10⁶ Pa)** or **GPa (10⁹ Pa)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In microrobotics, stress is NOT just a mechanical curiosity — it is the **primary failure criterion** for micro-actuators, micro-grippers, micro-cantilevers, and compliant mechanisms. The 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes **residual stress** (a byproduct of microfabrication) and **surface/interface stress** (which dominates at the microscale). ### 1.2 Classification of Stress Stress is broadly classified into two fundamental categories: **A. Normal Stress ($\sigma_n$)** - Acts **perpendicular** to the surface of the element. - Either **Tensile** (pulling apart, positive convention) or **Compressive** (pushing together, negative convention). **B. Shear Stress ($\tau$)** - Acts **tangentially** (parallel) to the surface. - Tends to deform the element by causing adjacent planes to slide relative to each other. ### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy — The Tense Rubber Band & The Crowded Bus** > > Imagine you are stretching a **rubber band** with both hands. The force you apply is distributed across the cross-sectional threads of the band. That internal "pulling" force per thread-area is exactly **tensile stress**. If you press your palms together with a thin sheet of paper between them, the paper experiences **compressive stress**. > > Now, in a **crowded bus**, when a group of people leans sideways all at once, their jackets slide over each other — that tangential sliding is **shear stress**. > > In **microrobotics**, these stresses are tiny in absolute force but **massive per unit area** because the cross-sections are microscopic. A micro-cantilever thinner than a human hair can experience stresses equivalent to those in a bridge cable! ### 1.4 The Stress Tensor — A 3D Generalization At any point inside a 3D body, stress is not a single number but a **second-order tensor** (a 3×3 matrix) because forces can act in any of the three coordinate directions on any of the three coordinate planes. This is captured in **Cauchy's Stress Tensor**: $$ \sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix} $$ Where $\sigma_{ii}$ are normal stresses and $\tau_{ij}$ are shear stresses. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Decomposition of the Stress State Any arbitrary 3D stress tensor can be mathematically decomposed into two physically meaningful parts: **Step 1 — Hydrostatic (Volumetric) Component** $$ \sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz}}{3} $$ This represents the mean stress responsible for **volume change** (dilatation) of the element. **Step 2 — Deviatoric Component** $$ s_{ij} = \sigma_{ij} - \sigma_m \cdot \delta_{ij} $$ This represents the "shape-distortion" part responsible for **angular deformation**. **Step 3 — Physical Interpretation** - Hydrostatic stress → causes uniform compression/expansion. - Deviatoric stress → causes shear distortion and is the primary driver of **yielding** and **plastic deformation** in ductile MEMS materials like polysilicon and gold. ### 2.2 Principal Stresses and Mohr's Circle At any point in a stressed body, there exist **three mutually orthogonal planes** on which the shear stress is zero. The normal stresses acting on these planes are called **Principal Stresses** ($\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$). For a 2D stress state (which is the most common case analyzed in KTU micro-cantilever problems): $$ \sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{xx} - \sigma_{yy}}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} $$ The **Maximum Shear Stress** in 2D is: $$ \tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{xx} - \sigma_{yy}}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} $$ ### 2.3 Residual Stress — The Microrobotics-Specific Phenomenon > [!IMPORTANT] > **Residual Stress** is the stress that remains in a micro-structure after the original cause of the stress (external load, thermal gradient, fabrication process) has been removed. It is **THE** most critical stress concept in MEMS design. Sources of Residual Stress in Microrobotics: 1. **Thermal Mismatch** — Different thermal expansion coefficients ($\alpha$) of thin film and substrate. 2. **Intrinsic Growth Stress** — Stress built-in during deposition (e.g., LPCVD polysilicon). 3. **Lattice Mismatch** — In epitaxial layers. 4. **Ion Implantation Damage** — Stress from lattice disruption. The **Stoney Formula** (1909) gives the residual stress in a thin film: $$ \sigma_f = \frac{E_s \cdot t_s^2}{6 \cdot (1 - \nu_s) \cdot t_f \cdot R} $$ Where: - $E_s$ = Young's modulus of the substrate - $\nu_s$ = Poisson's ratio of the substrate - $t_s, t_f$ = thicknesses of substrate and film respectively - $R$ = radius of curvature of the substrate after film deposition ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Formula Table — Stress in Microrobotics** | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |--------------|------------------------|-------------------|-----------------| | Cauchy Stress Definition | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $F$ = N, $A$ = m² | Basic stress computation | | Normal Stress | $\sigma_n = \dfrac{F_n}{A}$ | $F_n$ = N (perpendicular) | Axial loading of micro-bars | | Shear Stress | $\tau = \dfrac{F_t}{A}$ | $F_t$ = N (tangential) | Torsional micro-shafts | | Stress Tensor Element | $\sigma_{ij} = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0}\dfrac{\Delta F_j}{\Delta A_i}$ | $i,j = x,y,z$ | 3D stress state | | Hydrostatic Stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{1}{3}(\sigma_{xx}+\sigma_{yy}+\sigma_{zz})$ | Pa | Volume-change analysis | | Principal Stress (2D) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_{xx}+\sigma_{yy}}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_{xx}-\sigma_{yy}}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Pa | Mohr's circle problems | | Max Shear Stress (2D) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert}{2}$ | Pa | Failure analysis | | Thermal Stress | $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Pa | Thermal mismatch | | Stoney (Residual) | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s t_s^2}{6(1-\nu_s) t_f R}$ | Pa | Thin-film stress | | Bending Stress (Beam) | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{M \cdot c}{I}$ | Pa | Micro-cantilever design | | Stress Concentration | $K_t = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{nom}}$ | Dimensionless | Notch/corner design | > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` inside table cells is replaced by `\vert` (e.g., $\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert$) to preserve markdown table syntax integrity. ### 2.5 Why Stress is the "Heart" of Microrobotics - **Actuator Force Generation:** Electrostatic, piezoelectric, and thermal micro-actuators generate force by deliberately inducing controlled stress fields. - **Failure Prediction:** Stress concentrations at sharp corners cause micro-cracks — a primary reliability issue in MEMS. - **Shape Control:** Residual stress gradients are exploited to create 3D curled micro-structures (e.g., for micro-grippers and cell-catching probes). - **Sensor Operation:** Piezoresistive stress sensors measure mechanical quantities by detecting resistance changes proportional to stress. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Force Vector to Cauchy's Stress Tensor **Problem Context:** Establish the rigorous tensor definition of stress at a point by considering an infinitesimal tetrahedron. **Step 1 — Consider an Infinitesimal Tetrahedron** Let a small tetrahedral element be carved from a continuum at point P, with three faces perpendicular to the coordinate axes and the fourth face (the oblique face) having an outward normal unit vector $\vec{n} = (n_x, n_y, n_z)$. **Step 2 — Apply Newton's Equilibrium Law** The sum of forces on the tetrahedron must equal mass × acceleration. As the tetrahedron shrinks to zero, inertia terms become negligible compared to surface forces. Only surface tractions remain: $$ \sum F_i = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Express Traction on Each Face** On the face perpendicular to the x-axis with area $\Delta A_x$, the traction vector is $\vec{T}^{(x)} = (\sigma_{xx}, \tau_{yx}, \tau_{zx})$. Similarly for y and z faces. The oblique face has area $\Delta A_n$ with traction $\vec{T}^{(n)}$. **Step 4 — Geometric Relationship Between Areas** Because the areas are projections: $$ \Delta A_x = n_x \Delta A_n, \quad \Delta A_y = n_y \Delta A_n, \quad \Delta A_z = n_z \Delta A_n $$ **Step 5 — Force Balance in the i-th Direction** $$ T_i^{(n)} \Delta A_n = T_i^{(x)} \Delta A_x + T_i^{(y)} \Delta A_y + T_i^{(z)} \Delta A_z $$ Substituting the area relations: $$ T_i^{(n)} = \sigma_{ix} n_x + \sigma_{iy} n_y + \sigma_{iz} n_z $$ **Step 6 — Cauchy's Stress Theorem (Compact Form)** $$ \boxed{T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j} $$ This is the **Cauchy's Stress Theorem**, which states that the traction vector on any plane equals the stress tensor contracted with the normal vector of that plane. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Principal Stresses from the Characteristic Equation **Step 1 — Define the Eigenvalue Problem** Principal stresses are eigenvalues of the stress tensor where the shear traction vanishes. The condition is that the traction vector $\vec{T}$ must be parallel to the normal $\vec{n}$: $$ T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j = \lambda n_i $$ **Step 2 — Rearrange into the Characteristic Equation** $$ (\sigma_{ij} - \lambda \delta_{ij}) n_j = 0 $$ **Step 3 — For a Non-Trivial Solution, the Determinant Must Vanish** $$ \det(\sigma_{ij} - \lambda \delta_{ij}) = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Expand the Determinant for 3D** $$ \begin{aligned} \begin{vmatrix} \sigma_{xx}-\lambda & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy}-\lambda & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz}-\lambda \end{vmatrix} = 0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Expand to Obtain the Cubic Equation** This yields the **Stress Invariant Equation**: $$ \lambda^3 - I_1 \lambda^2 + I_2 \lambda - I_3 = 0 $$ Where the three **Stress Invariants** are: $$ I_1 = \sigma_{xx} + \sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{zz} $$ $$ I_2 = \sigma_{xx}\sigma_{yy} + \sigma_{yy}\sigma_{zz} + \sigma_{zz}\sigma_{xx} - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2 $$ $$ I_3 = \det(\sigma_{ij}) $$ **Step 6 — The Three Real Roots $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$ are the Principal Stresses** The physical significance: **$I_1$ is invariant under coordinate rotation**, meaning that regardless of how you orient your coordinate axes, the sum of normal stresses at a point remains constant. This is why the hydrostatic stress $\sigma_m = I_1/3$ is a **physical invariant**. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Stress in a Bi-Material Micro-Beam **Step 1 — Define the Physical Setup** A thin polysilicon film (thickness $t_f$, $\alpha_f$, $E_f$) is deposited on a silicon substrate (thickness $t_s$, $\alpha_s$, $E_s$) at temperature $T_{dep}$. The system is cooled to room temperature $T_{room}$. **Step 2 — Define Strain Compatibility** Both materials must contract together (assuming perfect bonding), so the total strain in each must be equal: $$ \varepsilon_{total} = \varepsilon_{mechanical} + \varepsilon_{thermal} $$ $$ \varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_f $$ **Step 3 — Express Mechanical and Thermal Strains** Mechanical strain from stress: $\varepsilon_{mech} = \sigma / E$ Thermal strain: $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ where $\Delta T = T_{room} - T_{dep}$ (negative for cooling). **Step 4 — Write Strain Equations for Both Materials** $$ \frac{\sigma_s}{E_s} + \alpha_s \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} + \alpha_f \Delta T $$ **Step 5 — Apply Force Equilibrium** The internal forces in film and substrate must cancel (no external force): $$ \sigma_s t_s + \sigma_f t_f = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_s = -\sigma_f \frac{t_f}{t_s} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute and Solve for Film Stress** $$ -\frac{\sigma_f t_f}{t_s E_s} + \alpha_s \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_f}{E_f} + \alpha_f \Delta T $$ Rearranging: $$ \sigma_f \left( \frac{1}{E_f} + \frac{t_f}{t_s E_s} \right) = (\alpha_s - \alpha_f) \Delta T $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{(\alpha_s - \alpha_f) \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_f} + \dfrac{t_f}{t_s E_s}}} $$ **Step 7 — Physical Interpretation** - If $\alpha_s > \alpha_f$, the substrate "wants" to shrink more than the film → film is left in **tensile** stress. - If $\alpha_s < \alpha_f$, the substrate "wants" to shrink less → film is left in **compressive** stress. - Compressive stress above a critical buckling threshold causes **micro-beam buckling** — a notorious failure mode in MEMS. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example > [!NOTE] > **Sample Problem (For Practice):** A micro-cantilever made of polysilicon has $\sigma_{xx} = 50$ MPa, $\sigma_{yy} = -20$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa. Find the principal stresses and maximum shear stress. **Solution:** Step 1 — Average Normal Stress: $$ \frac{\sigma_{xx}+\sigma_{yy}}{2} = \frac{50 + (-20)}{2} = 15 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 2 — Radius of Mohr's Circle: $$ R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{50-(-20)}{2}\right)^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{35^2 + 30^2} = \sqrt{1225+900} = \sqrt{2125} \approx 46.10 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 3 — Principal Stresses: $$ \sigma_1 = 15 + 46.10 = 61.10 \text{ MPa} $$ $$ \sigma_2 = 15 - 46.10 = -31.10 \text{ MPa} $$ Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stress: $$ \tau_{max} = 46.10 \text{ MPa} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Visualizing Normal vs Shear Stress (Schematic Block Diagram) Because stress cannot be directly "seen" in a photograph at the microscale, the following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the conceptual architecture of how stress is classified and visualized in microrobotic structures: ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["STRESS AT A POINT
IN MICROROBOTICS"] nodeB["NORMAL STRESS sigma_n
Acts PERPENDICULAR to face"] nodeC["SHEAR STRESS tau
Acts TANGENTIAL to face"] nodeD["TENSILE sigma_gt_0
Pulls micro-element APART"] nodeE["COMPRESSIVE sigma_lt_0
Pushes micro-element TOGETHER"] nodeF["MICRO-CANTILEVER BENDING
top fiber stretches bottom compresses"] nodeG["DIRECT SHEAR
parallel planes slide"] nodeH["TORSIONAL SHEAR
twisting of micro-shaft"] nodeI["ELECTROSTATIC ACTUATOR
parallel plates attract"] nodeJ["PIEZOELECTRIC FILM
voltage induces strain"] nodeK["MICRO-GRIPPER CONTACT
normal force on object"] nodeL["RESIDUAL STRESS
from microfabrication"] nodeM["STRESS CONCENTRATION
at notches and corners"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeC nodeB --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeE nodeD --> nodeF nodeE --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeG nodeC --> nodeH nodeF --> nodeI nodeF --> nodeJ nodeF --> nodeK nodeL --> nodeM nodeI --> nodeM nodeJ --> nodeM nodeK --> nodeM ``` ### 4.2 Cauchy's Tetrahedron — The Geometric Foundation of Stress Tensor ```mermaid flowchart LR stepA["ORIGIN POINT P
at center of continuum"] stepB["THREE COORDINATE FACES
perpendicular to x y z axes"] stepC["OBLIQUE FACE
with outward normal n vector"] stepD["FORCE on OBLIQUE FACE
Traction Tn"] stepE["FORCE on x FACE
Traction Tx"] stepF["FORCE on y FACE
Traction Ty"] stepG["FORCE on z FACE
Traction Tz"] stepH["EQUILIBRIUM CONDITION
Sum of forces equals zero"] stepI["CAUCHY STRESS TENSOR sigma_ij
relates Tn to n"] stepA --> stepB stepA --> stepC stepC --> stepD stepB --> stepE stepB --> stepF stepB --> stepG stepD --> stepH stepE --> stepH stepF --> stepH stepG --> stepH stepH --> stepI ``` ### 4.3 Micro-Fabrication Stress Generation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD p1["DEPOSITION STEP
LPCVD or sputtering"] p2["THERMAL EXPANSION MISMATCH
between film and substrate"] p3["INTRINSIC GROWTH STRESS
from grain boundaries"] p4["COOLING DOWN TO ROOM TEMP
induced thermal strain"] p5["RESIDUAL STRESS BUILDS UP
in thin film"] p6["STONEY FORMULA computes
sigma_f from curvature R"] p7["RELEASE STEP
etching sacrificial layer"] p8["MICRO-STRUCTURE FREES UP
residual stress causes deformation"] p9["DESIGN OUTCOME 1
flat micro-cantilever desired"] p10["DESIGN OUTCOME 2
controlled curl for 3D structure"] p11["DESIGN OUTCOME 3
uncontrolled buckling FAILURE"] p1 --> p2 p1 --> p3 p2 --> p4 p3 --> p5 p4 --> p5 p5 --> p6 p5 --> p7 p7 --> p8 p8 --> p9 p8 --> p10 p8 --> p11 ``` ### 4.4 Stress Distribution Across a Micro-Cantilever Cross-Section ```mermaid flowchart TD q1["FIXED END
at silicon substrate anchor"] q2["CROSS SECTION ANALYSIS
at distance x from anchor"] q3["NEUTRAL AXIS
zero stress layer"] q4["TOP FIBER
sigma equals Mc over I tensile"] q5["MIDDLE FIBERS
stress varies linearly"] q6["BOTTOM FIBER
sigma equals Mc over I compressive"] q7["TIP REGION
zero bending moment"] q8["FREE END
no stress at extreme tip"] q9["MAXIMUM BENDING STRESS
at fixed end at top and bottom"] q1 --> q2 q2 --> q3 q3 --> q4 q3 --> q5 q3 --> q6 q1 --> q9 q2 --> q9 q7 --> q8 ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Dec 2024 Style]** **Q1. [3 Marks]** *Define the terms 'normal stress' and 'shear stress' as applied to micro-scale structures. Give one example of each in a MEMS device.* **Model Answer:** Normal stress is the internal force per unit area acting **perpendicular** to a surface, denoted $\sigma_n = F_n / A$. It can be tensile (positive, pulling) or compressive (negative, pushing). **Example in MEMS:** The longitudinal stress along the length of a micro-cantilever during bending. Shear stress is the internal force per unit area acting **tangentially** (parallel) to a surface, denoted $\tau = F_t / A$. **Example in MEMS:** The torsional shear stress on the cross-section of a micro-mirror's torsion hinge during angular actuation. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [3 Marks]** *What is residual stress in thin films used for MEMS devices? Name any two sources.* **Model Answer:** Residual stress is the stress that remains locked inside a thin film after all external loads and processing steps (e.g., deposition, annealing) are complete. It is a **permanent internal stress**. Two sources: 1. **Thermal mismatch** between the thin film and substrate due to different coefficients of thermal expansion ($\alpha_f \neq \alpha_s$). 2. **Intrinsic growth stress** built up during deposition (e.g., due to grain boundary formation in LPCVD polysilicon). Other acceptable answers: Lattice mismatch, ion implantation damage, phase transformation stress. (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, June 2024 Style]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Option 1* **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the general expression for the 3D stress tensor at a point using Cauchy's tetrahedron approach. Clearly state Cauchy's Stress Theorem. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Setup:** Consider an infinitesimal tetrahedron at point P with three faces perpendicular to coordinate axes and a fourth oblique face with outward unit normal $\vec{n} = (n_x, n_y, n_z)$. **Step 2 — Area Relations:** The areas of the coordinate faces relate to the oblique face area $\Delta A$ by projection: $$ \Delta A_x = n_x \Delta A, \quad \Delta A_y = n_y \Delta A, \quad \Delta A_z = n_z \Delta A $$ **Step 3 — Force Equilibrium:** In the limit of zero volume, surface tractions dominate. Summing forces in the $i$-direction: $$ T_i^{(n)} \Delta A = T_i^{(x)} \Delta A_x + T_i^{(y)} \Delta A_y + T_i^{(z)} \Delta A_z $$ **Step 4 — Substitute Area Relations:** $$ T_i^{(n)} = T_i^{(x)} n_x + T_i^{(y)} n_y + T_i^{(z)} n_z $$ **Step 5 — Define Stress Components:** Writing each traction component explicitly: $$ T_i^{(x)} = \sigma_{ix}, \quad T_i^{(y)} = \sigma_{iy}, \quad T_i^{(z)} = \sigma_{iz} $$ **Step 6 — Cauchy's Stress Theorem:** $$ \boxed{T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j \quad (i,j = 1,2,3)} $$ This means the traction on any plane equals the stress tensor contracted with the normal vector of that plane. **Step 7 — Symmetry of Stress Tensor:** From angular momentum balance (no body couples), $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$, reducing 9 components to 6 independent components. (7 marks) --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon micro-cantilever of length $L = 500 \, \mu m$, width $w = 100 \, \mu m$, and thickness $t = 5 \, \mu m$ experiences a tip load of $F = 50 \, \mu N$. The Young's modulus is $E = 170$ GPa. Calculate: (i) The maximum bending moment. (ii) The maximum bending stress (assume rectangular cross-section). (iii) The tip deflection. **Model Solution:** **(i) Maximum Bending Moment:** The maximum bending moment occurs at the fixed end of a cantilever with tip load: $$ M_{max} = F \cdot L = (50 \times 10^{-6}) \times (500 \times 10^{-6}) = 25 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{N·m} = 25 \, \text{nN·m} $$ **(ii) Maximum Bending Stress:** For a rectangular cross-section, the moment of inertia is: $$ I = \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-6}) \times (5 \times 10^{-6})^3}{12} = \frac{(10^{-4})(1.25 \times 10^{-16})}{12} $$ $$ I = 1.0417 \times 10^{-21} \, \text{m}^4 $$ Distance from neutral axis to extreme fiber: $c = t/2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m. $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I} = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-9})(2.5 \times 10^{-6})}{1.0417 \times 10^{-21}} $$ $$ \sigma_{max} = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-14}}{1.0417 \times 10^{-21}} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{7} \, \text{Pa} = 60 \, \text{MPa} $$ **(iii) Tip Deflection:** For a cantilever with end load: $$ \delta = \frac{F L^3}{3EI} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(500 \times 10^{-6})^3}{3 \times (170 \times 10^9)(1.0417 \times 10^{-21})} $$ $$ \delta = \frac{(50 \times 10^{-6})(1.25 \times 10^{-13})}{(3)(170 \times 10^9)(1.0417 \times 10^{-21})} $$ $$ \delta = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-18}}{5.312 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 1.177 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m} \approx 11.77 \, \text{nm} $$ (7 marks) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Choice Option 2* **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of residual stress in MEMS thin films. Derive the Stoney formula for residual stress in a thin film deposited on a substrate. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition:** Residual stress in MEMS thin films is the internal stress that remains after deposition and cooling, arising from thermal mismatch, lattice mismatch, or intrinsic growth processes. **Step 2 — Physical Setup:** Consider a thin film of thickness $t_f$ deposited on a thick substrate of thickness $t_s$ ($t_s \gg t_f$). The substrate bends to a radius of curvature $R$ due to the film's stress. **Step 3 — Force and Moment Equilibrium:** Force balance: $\int \sigma \, dA = 0$. Moment balance about the neutral axis: $$ M = \int \sigma \cdot z \, dA $$ **Step 4 — Bending Strain in Substrate:** A point at distance $z$ from the neutral surface experiences strain $\varepsilon = z / R$ (pure bending). **Step 5 — Stress in Substrate:** $$ \sigma_s(z) = \frac{E_s}{1 - \nu_s} \cdot \frac{z}{R} $$ **Step 6 — Bending Moment from Substrate:** $$ M = \int_{-t_s/2}^{t_s/2} \frac{E_s}{1-\nu_s} \cdot \frac{z^2}{R} \cdot w \, dz = \frac{E_s w t_s^3}{12(1-\nu_s) R} $$ **Step 7 — Force Balance for Film:** The film's average stress $\sigma_f$ produces a moment through its lever arm $\approx t_s/2$: $$ M = \sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot w \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} $$ **Step 8 — Equate Moments and Solve:** $$ \sigma_f \cdot t_f \cdot w \cdot \frac{t_s}{2} = \frac{E_s w t_s^3}{12(1-\nu_s) R} $$ $$ \boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{E_s t_s^2}{6(1-\nu_s) t_f R}} $$ **Step 9 — Discussion:** The Stoney formula is widely used because it depends only on substrate properties and easily measurable curvature, not on film properties — an elegant engineering simplification. (7 marks) --- **(b) [7 Marks]** A $1 \, \mu m$ thick polysilicon film is deposited on a $500 \, \mu m$ thick silicon wafer. After deposition, the wafer curvature is measured as $R = 10 \, m$ (convex up). For silicon: $E_s = 170$ GPa, $\nu_s = 0.28$. Find the residual stress in the polysilicon film and comment on whether it is tensile or compressive. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify Values:** - $E_s = 170 \times 10^9$ Pa - $\nu_s = 0.28$ - $t_s = 500 \times 10^{-6}$ m - $t_f = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ m - $R = 10$ m **Step 2 — Substitute into Stoney's Formula:** $$ \sigma_f = \frac{E_s t_s^2}{6(1-\nu_s) t_f R} $$ **Step 3 — Compute Numerator:** $$ E_s t_s^2 = (170 \times 10^9)(500 \times 10^{-6})^2 = (170 \times 10^9)(2.5 \times 10^{-7}) = 42.5 $$ $$ \text{Units: Pa·m}^2 = 42.5 $$ Wait — recompute carefully: $$ (500 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}^2 $$ $$ E_s \cdot t_s^2 = (170 \times 10^9)(2.5 \times 10^{-7}) = 4.25 \times 10^4 \, \text{N} $$ **Step 4 — Compute Denominator:** $$ 6(1-\nu_s) t_f R = 6(1-0.28)(1 \times 10^{-6})(10) = 6(0.72)(10^{-5}) = 4.32 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}^2 $$ Wait, units check: $6 \cdot t_f \cdot R$ has units of m². So the denominator has units of m² (dimensionless term $(1-\nu_s)$ is unitless): $$ 6 \times (0.72) \times (10^{-6}) \times (10) = 4.32 \times 10^{-5} $$ **Step 5 — Final Computation:** $$ \sigma_f = \frac{4.25 \times 10^4}{4.32 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.9838 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa} \approx 984 \, \text{MPa} $$ **Step 6 — Sign Determination:** The wafer is convex up → film is on the concave (lower) side → film is trying to **contract** more than the substrate → film is in **TENSION**. Therefore: $\sigma_f \approx +984 \, \text{MPa}$ (Tensile). **Step 7 — Engineering Comment:** A tensile stress of ~1 GPa in polysilicon is **dangerously close to its fracture strength (~1.2–2 GPa)**. This micro-structure would likely fail by cracking or delamination. A stress-relief anneal would be recommended. (7 marks) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid** > > 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–2 marks typical loss):** Tensile is positive, compressive is negative. Mixing these up in principal stress calculations is the most common mistake. > > 2. **Unit Conversion Mistakes (–2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $\mu m$ to $m$ and GPa to Pa. KTU examiners specifically deduct heavily for this. > > 3. **Stoney's Formula Misuse (–3 marks):** Using it for films that are NOT much thinner than the substrate. The formula requires $t_f \ll t_s$. > > 4. **Confusing Principal Stress vs. Maximum Shear Stress:** Principal stresses are NORMAL stresses on planes where shear is zero; max shear stress occurs on planes oriented at 45°. > > 5. **Forgetting Symmetry of Stress Tensor:** $\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx}$ — stating otherwise will lose marks on tensor derivation questions. > > 6. **Skipping Geometric Reasoning in Mohr's Circle:** Always draw the Mohr's circle and label $\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$, and $\tau_{max}$ explicitly. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stress in Microrobotics** - ✅ **Stress = Force per unit area** (Pa or N/m²); fundamental to all microscale mechanical analysis. - ✅ **Normal stress** acts perpendicular; **Shear stress** acts tangential. - ✅ **Cauchy's Stress Theorem:** $T_i = \sigma_{ij} n_j$ — the traction on any plane is the stress tensor dotted with the plane's normal. - ✅ **Stress tensor is symmetric:** $\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$ → only 6 independent components in 3D. - ✅ **Principal stresses** are eigenvalues where shear vanishes; obtained from the cubic characteristic equation. - ✅ **Hydrostatic stress** $\sigma_m$ causes volume change; **Deviatoric stress** causes shape distortion. - ✅ **Residual stress** is THE critical microrobotics concept — comes from fabrication, not loading. - ✅ **Stoney's formula:** $\sigma_f = \dfrac{E_s t_s^2}{6(1-\nu_s) t_f R}$ — for thin films on thick substrates. - ✅ **Thermal stress:** $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ — drives residual stress in bi-material MEMS. - ✅ **Bending stress in cantilever:** $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{Mc}{I}$ — maximum at the fixed end, top and bottom fibers. - ✅ **Stress concentration factor** $K_t$ must be minimized at micro-structural corners and notches. - ✅ **Stress at microscale is amplified** because cross-sectional areas are vanishingly small — even micro-Newtons produce mega-Pascals. - ✅ **Always state units explicitly** in KTU answers; always show the substitution step before computing the final numerical answer. - ✅ **Drawing a free-body diagram (FBD) or Mohr's circle** in long answers scores extra presentation marks. ---

topics.title: Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of Strain

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Continuum Mechanics** (the foundational discipline for microrobotic design), **Strain** is a dimensionless, mathematical measure of deformation representing the *relative displacement* between particles in a material body, normalized with respect to a reference configuration.

In simpler KTU-board language:
> **Strain = Change in dimension / Original dimension**

It quantifies *how much* and *in what manner* a body deforms under an applied load, thermal field, or actuation stimulus. Since microrobots operate at sub-millimetre scales, strain governs everything from the deflection of micro-cantilevers to the curling of shape-memory-alloy (SMA) micro-grippers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight (CO1 / Module 1):** Strain is NOT a material property — it is a *geometric* response quantity. It depends purely on displacement gradients and is therefore fundamental to kinematics of micro-actuators.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you pinch a **rubber eraser** between your fingers and stretch it:

- The original length = $L_0$
- The new (deformed) length = $L$
- The amount you stretched = $\Delta L = L - L_0$

If you stretched it by **1 mm** on an original **10 mm**, the strain is $0.1$ or **10 %**.

> **Analogy for Microrobotics:** Think of a MEMS cantilever beam used as a micro-switch. When a voltage is applied to the underlying piezoelectric layer, the *tiny elongation* (a few micrometres) over its original length (say, 200 µm) gives the strain that physically bends the beam. This strain-induced bending is what closes the switch.

So strain is the **"fractional change in length"** — and it is *always* relative (not absolute).

---

## 1.3 Classification of Strain (Broad Categories)

| Category | Description |
|---|---|
| **Normal / Axial Strain** | Change along one axis (tension or compression) |
| **Shear Strain** | Change in angle between originally perpendicular lines |
| **Volumetric Strain** | Relative change in volume |
| **Engineering Strain** | Small-deformation approximation: $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ |
| **True (Logarithmic) Strain** | Large-deformation measure: $\varepsilon_{true} = \ln(L/L_0)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering strain** is the KTU syllabus default for microrobotics problems because most MEMS deformations are *infinitesimal* ($\varepsilon < 0.01$). However, **SMA actuators** in micro-grippers can exhibit $\varepsilon > 5\%$, demanding true-strain treatment.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Strain is **dimensionless** (m/m or no units).
- Often expressed in **microstrain** ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$), e.g. $\mathbf{1000 \; \mu\varepsilon = 0.1\%}$.
- Typical MEMS strains: **$10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$**.
- Standard reference temperature (for thermal strain): $\mathbf{20^\circ C}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Displacement Field

Let a point $P$ in the undeformed body have position vector $\mathbf{X}$. After deformation it moves to $P'$ with position vector $\mathbf{x}$.

The **displacement vector** is:

$$\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{x} - \mathbf{X}$$

In 2D Cartesian coordinates:

$$\mathbf{u} = u_x \,\hat{i} + u_y \,\hat{j}$$

The derivatives of these displacements define strain. This is the *core insight* of the strain concept.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

**Step 1 — Consider a small line element** $d\mathbf{X}$ in the reference configuration.
After deformation it becomes $d\mathbf{x}$.

**Step 2 — Use the chain rule** to relate $d\mathbf{x}$ to $d\mathbf{X}$ via the **deformation gradient tensor** $\mathbf{F}$:

$$d\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{F} \, d\mathbf{X}, \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{F}_{ij} = \frac{\partial x_i}{\partial X_j}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the change in squared length** of this line:

$$ds^2 - dS^2 = d\mathbf{x}^T d\mathbf{x} - d\mathbf{X}^T d\mathbf{X} = 2 \, d\mathbf{X}^T \mathbf{E} \, d\mathbf{X}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the strain tensor** $\mathbf{E}$ that captures all second-order length information.

---

## 2.3 The Three Principal Strain Tensors (High-Yield for KTU)

| Tensor Name | Formula | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Infinitesimal (Cauchy) Strain** | $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\nabla\mathbf{u} + \nabla\mathbf{u}^T)$ | Small deformations, MEMS, linear beam theory |
| **Green-Lagrangian Strain** | $\mathbf{E} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{F}^T\mathbf{F} - \mathbf{I})$ | Finite rotations & strains (SMA actuators) |
| **Euler-Almansi Strain** | $\mathbf{e} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{F}^{-T}\mathbf{F}^{-1})$ | Strain measured w.r.t. *current* configuration |

For a microrobot designer, the **Cauchy infinitesimal strain tensor** is used 95 % of the time.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars `|` are used inside table cells to preserve Markdown rendering. Use `\vert` if needed.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Engineering normal strain (1D) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | True (logarithmic) strain | $\varepsilon_{true} = \displaystyle\int_{L_0}^{L}\dfrac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)$ | Dimensionless, large deformations |
| 3 | Shear strain (engineering) | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} - \theta'$ (rad) | Angle change in rad |
