|----------|--------|----------------|------|
| Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | **1.380649 × 10⁻²³** | J/K |
| Dynamic viscosity of water (20°C) | $\mu$ | **1.002 × 10⁻³** | Pa·s |
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **998.2** | kg/m³ |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |
| Hamaker constant (typical) | $A_H$ | **10⁻¹⁹ to 10⁻²⁰** | J |
| Surface tension of water | $\gamma$ | **0.0728** | N/m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Scaling Law:**
> A scaling law in microrobotics describes how physical quantities (force, mass, time, energy) change as a characteristic length $L$ of a system is reduced. It is derived from dimensional analysis of governing physical laws.

### 1.4 Why "Dynamics" is Different at the Micro-Scale

At the macro-scale, **Newton's Second Law** ($F = ma$) dominates the analysis. At the micro-scale, multiple physical effects alter the dynamic behavior:

1. **Low Reynolds Number Flow** — viscous forces overwhelm inertial forces.
2. **Surface-to-Volume Ratio Increases** — surface effects (adhesion, friction, capillarity) become dominant.
3. **Brownian Motion** — random thermal kicks of fluid molecules push the microrobot.
4. **Electrostatic Charging** — tribocharging or contact electrification causes sticking.
5. **Stiction** — static friction at the micro-scale prevents release of components.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Scaling Law Framework

The fundamental principle of microrobotic dynamics begins with **geometric scaling**. Consider a microrobot of characteristic length $L$. As $L$ is reduced by a scaling factor $s$ (where $s \ll 1$), every physical property scales predictably.

Let the original (macro) characteristic length be $L_0$ and the scaled (micro) length be $L = s \cdot L_0$.

**Derived scaling relationships:**

- **Linear dimension:** $L \propto s$
- **Area:** $A \propto s^2$
- **Volume:** $V \propto s^3$
- **Mass:** $m \propto s^3$ (assuming constant density)
- **Force (gravitational/inertial):** $F_{body} \propto s^3$ (proportional to mass)
- **Force (surface, e.g., surface tension):** $F_{surf} \propto s^1$ (proportional to length)
- **Pressure (force per area):** $P \propto s^1$ for body forces, $P \propto s^{-1}$ for surface forces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:**
> As $s$ becomes very small (micro/nano regime), the **surface force to body force ratio** scales as $s^{-2}$. This means **surface forces become 1,000,000× more dominant** when a robot is shrunk by a factor of 1000. This is the **mathematical origin of why microrobotics is fundamentally different** from macro-robotics.

### 2.2 Dominant Forces in Microrobotic Dynamics

The microrobot experiences a combination of the following forces, which must all be included in the dynamic equation of motion:

**(a) Van der Waals Force (Adhesion)**

The van der Waals force between a spherical particle of radius $R$ and a flat surface is given by:

$$F_{vdW} = \frac{A_H \cdot R}{6 \cdot D^2}$$

where:
- $A_H$ = Hamaker constant (≈ **10⁻¹⁹ J** for typical materials)
- $R$ = radius of the spherical contact
- $D$ = separation distance (typically **0.2–1 nm**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** Van der Waals force is the **quantum mechanical attraction** between induced dipoles in atoms/molecules. It is the dominant adhesion force at the micro-scale for dry contacts.

**(b) Capillary Force**

When a thin liquid film is present between the microrobot and the surface (e.g., due to humidity), capillary force arises from surface tension:

$$F_{cap} = 4 \pi R \gamma \cos\theta$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = surface tension of the liquid
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $R$ = radius of the meniscus

**(c) Electrostatic Force**

For a charged particle near a grounded surface:

$$F_{elec} = \frac{Q^2}{16 \pi \varepsilon_0 R^2}$$

or for a charge $Q$ near a grounded conductor at distance $D$:

$$F_{elec} \approx \frac{1}{16 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{D^2}$$

**(d) Viscous Drag Force (Stokes Drag)**

For a sphere of radius $R$ moving in a viscous fluid at velocity $v$:

$$F_{drag} = 6 \pi \mu R v = b \cdot v$$

where $b = 6\pi\mu R$ is the **drag coefficient** and $\mu$ is the dynamic viscosity.

**(e) Brownian Random Force**

The stochastic force from thermal molecular bombardment is characterized by the **fluctuation-dissipation theorem**:

$$\langle F_B(t) \rangle = 0, \quad \langle F_B(t) F_B(t') \rangle = 2 b k_B T \, \delta(t - t')$$

where $k_B T$ is the thermal energy.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol/Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Reynolds number $Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Inertial/viscous ratio | dimensionless | Determines flow regime |
| 2 | Stokes drag $F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$ | Linear viscous drag | N | Low-Re fluid dynamics |
| 3 | Van der Waals $F_{vdW} = \frac{A_H R}{6 D^2}$ | Molecular adhesion | N | Dry contact adhesion |
| 4 | Capillary $F_{cap} = 4\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | Surface tension force | N | Humid environment adhesion |
| 5 | Scaling: Mass $\propto L^3$ | Mass vs length | kg | Geometric scaling |
| 6 | Scaling: Surface force $\propto L^1$ | Surface vs body | N | Force scaling |
| 7 | Ratio $F_{surf}/F_{body} \propto L^{-2}$ | Dominance ratio | dimensionless | Why micro differs |
| 8 | Thermal energy $E_{th} = k_B T$ | Brownian energy | J | Random motion scale |
| 9 | Diffusion coeff. $D = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi\mu R}$ | Einstein relation | m²/s | Brownian dynamics |
| 10 | Equation of motion $m\ddot{x} = F_{ext} - b\dot{x} + F_B(t)$ | Langevin equation | N | Full microrobot dynamics |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** In KTU answer sheets, **always define symbols before use**. Examiners specifically check for symbol definitions in the first use. A formula written without preceding symbol definitions will lose 1–2 marks even if the formula is correct.

### 2.4 Reynolds Number — The Master Non-Dimensional Number

The Reynolds number is defined as:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}}$$

**Physical interpretation:**

| $Re$ Range | Regime | Dominant Force | Application |
|------------|--------|----------------|-------------|
| $Re \ll 1$ | Stokes (creeping flow) | Viscous | **Microrobotics, microfluidics** |
| $Re \sim 1$ | Transitional | Mixed | Boundary cases |
| $Re \gg 1$ | Inertial (turbulent/laminar) | Inertial | Macro robotics, aerodynamics |

> [!NOTE]
> **For microrobots in fluid:** $Re$ is typically in the range **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻²**. The flow is **strictly reversible and time-symmetric** (a concept called *scallop theorem*). This means **reciprocal motion cannot produce net displacement** — you cannot swim with a simple back-and-forth motion at the micro-scale!

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The dynamics framework is essential for:
1. **Design of micro-swimmers** for drug delivery in bloodstream.
2. **MEMS actuator dynamics** (comb drives, cantilever beams).
3. **Cell manipulation robots** (e.g., optical tweezers feedback control).
4. **Micro-aerial vehicles (MAVs)** hovering via flapping wings at low Re.
5. **Microfluidic pumping** using ciliary-motion-inspired actuators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Scaling Law for Force Dominance

**Starting Premise:** Consider a cube of side $L$ with constant density $\rho$. Compute the ratio of surface force to body (gravitational) force as $L$ is varied.

**Step 1 — Express the gravitational body force:**

The mass of the cube is $m = \rho L^3$. The gravitational body force is:

$$F_{body} = m g = \rho L^3 g$$

**Step 2 — Express a typical surface force:**

A surface force (e.g., surface tension, friction at the base) acts along an area $A = L^2$, with a typical surface stress $\sigma_{surf}$ (constant):

$$F_{surf} = \sigma_{surf} \cdot L^2$$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$\frac{F_{surf}}{F_{body}} = \frac{\sigma_{surf} \cdot L^2}{\rho L^3 g} = \frac{\sigma_{surf}}{\rho g L}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the scaling:**

$$\frac{F_{surf}}{F_{body}} \propto \frac{1}{L}$$

More rigorously (for a thin film or contact where $F_{surf} \propto L$), we obtain the stronger $L^{-2}$ scaling, because surface force itself reduces slower than body force.

$$\boxed{\frac{F_{surf}}{F_{body}} \propto L^{-2}}$$

**Conclusion:** As $L$ decreases by a factor of 10, the surface-to-body force ratio increases by a factor of 100. This is the **fundamental derivation** showing why micro-robotics differs from macro-robotics.

### 3.2 Derivation: Terminal Velocity of a Microrobot Under Gravity in a Viscous Fluid

**Starting Premise:** A spherical microrobot of radius $R$ and density $\rho_p$ falls through a fluid of density $\rho_f$ and viscosity $\mu$ under gravity.

**Step 1 — Identify forces acting:**
- Gravitational force: $F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \rho_p g$
- Buoyancy force: $F_b = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \rho_f g$
- Viscous drag (Stokes): $F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$

**Step 2 — At terminal velocity, net force = 0:**

$$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve for $v_t$:**

$$\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 6\pi\mu R v_t$$

$$v_t = \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{6\pi\mu R}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$v_t = \frac{2 R^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{9 \mu}$$

**Step 5 — Confirm the scaling:**

$$v_t \propto R^2$$

This is a beautiful result: **terminal velocity scales with $R^2$**, so a 10× smaller sphere falls 100× slower. For a 1 µm particle, terminal velocity in water is on the order of **µm/s**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Characteristic Relaxation Time (Time Constant) of Microrobot Motion

**Starting Premise:** A microrobot of mass $m$ and drag coefficient $b$ is released with initial velocity $v_0$ in a fluid. Find the time for velocity to decay to $1/e$ of $v_0$.

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law with drag:**

$$m \frac{dv}{dt} = -b v$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**

$$\frac{dv}{v} = -\frac{b}{m} dt$$

**Step 3 — Integrate from $v_0$ to $v(t)$:**

$$\int_{v_0}^{v(t)} \frac{dv}{v} = -\frac{b}{m} \int_0^t dt$$

**Step 4 — Obtain the exponential decay:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{v(t)}{v_0}\right) = -\frac{b}{m} t$$

$$v(t) = v_0 \, e^{-t/\tau}, \quad \text{where} \quad \tau = \frac{m}{b}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $m$ and $b$ for a sphere:**

$$\tau = \frac{\rho \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3}{6\pi\mu R} = \frac{2 \rho R^2}{9 \mu}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{2 \rho R^2}{9 \mu}}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret:**

The relaxation time $\tau \propto R^2$. For a 1 µm sphere in water, $\tau \approx 0.1 \,\mu s$. For a 1 mm sphere, $\tau \approx 0.1 \,s$. The **smaller the microrobot, the more rapidly it forgets its initial velocity** — meaning momentum is essentially irrelevant in micro-scale dynamics.

### 3.4 Derivation: Mean Square Displacement Due to Brownian Motion

**Starting Premise:** A particle of radius $R$ in a fluid of temperature $T$ experiences random forces. Find the mean square displacement after time $t$.

**Step 1 — Einstein's Relation (Fluctuation-Dissipation):**

The diffusion coefficient is related to drag and thermal energy:

$$D = \frac{k_B T}{b} = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi\mu R}$$

**Step 2 — Diffusion equation in 1D:**

The mean square displacement for free diffusion is:

$$\langle x^2(t) \rangle = 2 D t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $D$:**

$$\langle x^2(t) \rangle = \frac{k_B T}{3\pi\mu R} \cdot t$$

**Step 4 — Numerical estimate for a 1 µm sphere in water at 300 K:**

$$\langle x^2 \rangle = \frac{(1.38\times10^{-23})(300)}{3\pi(10^{-3})(10^{-6})} t \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-13} \cdot t \, \text{m}^2$$

For $t = 1$ s: $\langle x^2 \rangle \approx 4.4 \times 10^{-13}$ m², so $\sqrt{\langle x^2 \rangle} \approx 21 \,\mu m$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** A 1 µm microrobot **diffuses ~21 µm every second** purely from thermal noise. This is a HUGE displacement relative to its own size. **Brownian motion is a major design constraint** in microrobotic dynamics and must be counteracted by closed-loop control or high-frequency actuation.

### 3.5 Summary of Derived Results (Analytical Matrix)

| Quantity | Formula | Scaling with $R$ | Scaling with $L$ |
|----------|---------|------------------|------------------|
| Gravitational force | $F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \rho g$ | $R^3$ | $L^3$ |
| Van der Waals force | $F_{vdW} = \frac{A_H R}{6D^2}$ | $R^1$ | $L^1$ |
| Capillary force | $F_{cap} = 4\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | $R^1$ | $L^1$ |
| Stokes drag | $F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$ | $R^1$ | $L^1$ |
| Terminal velocity | $v_t = \frac{2R^2 \Delta\rho g}{9\mu}$ | $R^2$ | $L^2$ |
| Relaxation time | $\tau = \frac{2\rho R^2}{9\mu}$ | $R^2$ | $L^2$ |
| Diffusion coefficient | $D = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi\mu R}$ | $R^{-1}$ | $L^{-1}$ |
| Reynolds number | $Re = \frac{2R\rho v}{\mu}$ | $R^1$ | $L^1$ |
| Surface/Body ratio | $F_{surf}/F_{body}$ | $R^{-2}$ | $L^{-2}$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Force Interactions on a Microrobot

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Drive] --> B[Drive Force F_drive]
    C[Gravity Field] --> D[Body Force F_g]
    E[Surface Contact] --> F[Van der Waals F_vdW]
    G[Humidity Film] --> H[Capillary F_cap]
    I[Charging / Static] --> J[Electrostatic F_elec]
    K[Fluid Surrounding] --> L[Stokes Drag F_drag]
    M[Thermal Bath T] --> N[Brownian Force F_B]
    B --> O[Equation of Motion]
    D --> O
    F --> O
    H --> O
    J --> O
    L --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[m d2x/dt2 = F_net]
    P --> Q[Microrobot Position x t]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Scaling Law Dominance Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Macro Scale L > 1 cm] --> B{Re > 1000}
    B --> C[Inertial Dominant]
    B --> D[Surface Force Negligible]
    B --> E[Newtonian Dynamics F=ma]
    F[Micro Scale L = 1 to 1000 um] --> G{Re 0.001 to 1}
    G --> H[Viscous Dominant]
    G --> I[Surface Force Significant]
    G --> J[Stokes Dynamics F = -bv]
    K[Nano Scale L < 1 um] --> L{Re < 0.001}
    L --> M[Brownian Dominant]
    L --> N[Molecular Adhesion Dominant]
    L --> O[Stochastic Langevin Dynamics]
    C --> P[Classic Macrorobot Theory]
    H --> Q[Continuum Low Re Microrobot Theory]
    M --> R[Molecular Dynamics Kinetics Theory]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture: Dynamic Equation Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Microrobot Dynamic Analysis] --> Input[Inputs Geometry Material Environment]
    Input --> Mass[m effective m]
    Input --> Drive[F drive from actuator]
    Input --> Fluid[F drag b v from fluid]
    Input --> Adhesion[F adhesion F_vdW F_cap F_elec]
    Input --> Random[F Brownian from thermal]
    Mass --> EqForm
    Drive --> EqForm
    Fluid --> EqForm
    Adhesion --> EqForm
    Random --> EqForm
    EqForm[Equation m x double dot plus b x dot equals F drive minus F adhesion plus F Brownian] --> Solve{Solve Type}
    Solve -->|Deterministic| ODE[ODE x of t]
    Solve -->|Stochastic| SDE[SDE Langevin Simulation]
    ODE --> Output1[Position Velocity Trajectory]
    SDE --> Output2[Statistical Mean Variance]
    Output1 --> Design[Design Feedback Controller]
    Output2 --> Design
```

### 4.4 Diagram: Reynolds Number Regime Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Re less than 0.001] --> RegA[Cilia Bacteria Realm]
    R1[Re 0.001 to 1] --> RegB[Microrobot Realm Target]
    R2[Re 1 to 100] --> RegC[Small Insect Realm]
    R3[Re 100 to 10000] --> RegD[Small Fish Realm]
    R4[Re greater than 10000] --> RegE[Macro Vehicle Realm]
    RegA --> Note1[Brownian Motion Non Negligible]
    RegB --> Note2[Stokes Flow Reversible]
    RegC --> Note3[Transitional Inertial Effects Begin]
    RegD --> Note4[Inertial Dominant]
    RegE --> Note5[Turbulent Boundary Layer Effects]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**

**Define Reynolds number for a microrobotic system. Why is it critical in micro-scale dynamics?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Reynolds number is a dimensionless quantity that expresses the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

where $\rho$ = fluid density, $v$ = characteristic velocity, $L$ = characteristic length, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity.

**Significance in microrobotics (2 marks):**
- Microrobots operate at extremely low $Re$ (typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$).
- Viscous forces **completely dominate** inertial forces.
- Flow is **reversible and time-symmetric** (scallop theorem applies).
- **Reciprocal motion** (e.g., opening/closing a paddle) cannot produce net propulsion.
- The dynamics must be analyzed using the **Stokes equation** rather than the **Navier-Stokes** with full inertia.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often state "Reynolds number measures turbulence." While true at high Re, in microrobotics, the **focus is on the viscous dominance and reversibility** — not turbulence. Examiners specifically look for the word "reversibility" or "scallop theorem."

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**

**List any three dominant forces at the micro-scale and explain why surface forces dominate body forces using scaling laws.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Three dominant forces at the micro-scale:**

1. **Van der Waals force** — molecular adhesion, $F_{vdW} \propto L^1$
2. **Capillary force** — surface tension, $F_{cap} \propto L^1$
3. **Viscous drag force** — Stokes drag, $F_{drag} \propto L^1 v$

**Why surface forces dominate (2 marks):**
- Body forces (gravity, inertia) scale as $L^3$ (because they depend on mass/volume).
- Surface forces scale as $L^1$ (because they depend on contact length or area).
- The ratio $F_{surf}/F_{body} \propto L^{-2}$.
- As $L$ decreases (micro/nano regime), this ratio **increases dramatically**, making surface forces dominant.
- **Example:** If $L$ is reduced by 1000×, surface forces become 10⁶× more dominant relative to body forces.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — ESE Module Internal Choice

---

## Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP]**

### (a) Derive the scaling law showing the dominance of surface forces over body forces in microrobotics. Show that the ratio scales as $L^{-2}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the microrobot geometry:**
Consider a microrobot of characteristic length $L$, with surface area $A \propto L^2$ and volume $V \propto L^3$.

**Step 2 — Express a generic body force:**
A body force such as gravity or inertia is proportional to the mass (and thus volume):
$$F_{body} \propto \rho V g \propto L^3$$

**Step 3 — Express a generic surface force:**
A surface force (e.g., surface tension, adhesion) acts over the surface and is proportional to the characteristic length or area:
$$F_{surf} \propto \gamma L \propto L^1$$
(using a contact-line surface force model)

**Step 4 — Form the ratio:**
$$\frac{F_{surf}}{F_{body}} \propto \frac{L^1}{L^3} = L^{-2}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret physically:**
$$\boxed{\frac{F_{surf}}{F_{body}} = \frac{C}{L^2}}$$
where $C$ is a material/geometry constant.

**Step 6 — Numerical illustration:**
- For $L = 1$ m: ratio = $C$ (baseline)
- For $L = 10^{-3}$ m (1 mm): ratio = $10^6 C$ — surface forces are **1 million times more dominant**.
- For $L = 10^{-6}$ m (1 µm): ratio = $10^{12} C$ — surface forces are **trillion times more dominant**.

**Step 7 — Engineering consequence:**
At the micro-scale, designers must prioritize surface treatments, lubrication, anti-stiction coatings, and electrostatic discharge protection, rather than focusing solely on weight reduction (which is the macro-scale approach).

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Setup & definitions — 2 marks
- Body force expression — 1 mark
- Surface force expression — 1 mark
- Ratio derivation — 2 marks
- Physical interpretation — 1 mark

---

### (b) A spherical microrobot of radius $R = 10 \,\mu m$ and density $\rho = 2000 \,kg/m^3$ moves through water ($\mu = 10^{-3} \,Pa\cdot s$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \,kg/m^3$). Compute the terminal velocity and the relaxation time under Stokes flow. **(7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $R = 10 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\rho_{particle} = 2000$ kg/m³
- $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $\rho_{fluid} = 1000$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Compute the effective body force (gravity − buoyancy):**

$$\Delta F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

$$\Delta F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi (10^{-5})^3 (2000 - 1000)(9.81)$$

$$\Delta F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi \times 10^{-15} \times 1000 \times 9.81$$

$$\Delta F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi \times 9.81 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$\Delta F_g \approx 4.11 \times 10^{-11} \,N$$

**Step 2 — Use the terminal velocity formula:**

$$v_t = \frac{2 R^2 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{9 \mu}$$

$$v_t = \frac{2 \times (10^{-5})^2 \times 1000 \times 9.81}{9 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_t = \frac{2 \times 10^{-10} \times 9810}{9 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_t = \frac{1.962 \times 10^{-6}}{9 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.18 \times 10^{-4} \,m/s$$

$$\boxed{v_t \approx 218 \, \mu m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the drag coefficient:**

$$b = 6 \pi \mu R = 6 \pi \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{-5} = 1.885 \times 10^{-7} \,N\cdot s/m$$

**Step 4 — Compute the effective mass:**

$$m = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \rho_p = \frac{4}{3}\pi \times 10^{-15} \times 2000 = 8.38 \times 10^{-12} \,kg$$

**Step 5 — Compute the relaxation time:**

$$\tau = \frac{m}{b} = \frac{8.38 \times 10^{-12}}{1.885 \times 10^{-7}} = 4.44 \times 10^{-5} \,s$$

$$\boxed{\tau \approx 44.4 \,\mu s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Reynolds number at terminal velocity:**

$$Re = \frac{2R \rho_f v_t}{\mu} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5} \times 1000 \times 2.18 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = 4.36 \times 10^{-3} \ll 1$$

This confirms the Stokes flow assumption is valid. **(Verification step — 1 mark bonus awarded in KTU valuation for explicit verification.)**

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Force calculation setup — 1 mark
- Terminal velocity derivation — 2 marks
- Numerical value with units — 1 mark
- Relaxation time — 2 marks
- Reynolds number verification — 1 mark

---

## Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

### (a) Explain the Langevin equation for a microrobot in a fluid. Define the diffusion coefficient and estimate the Brownian displacement of a 1 µm sphere in water at 300 K over 1 second. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Langevin equation:**

For a microrobot of mass $m$ moving in a viscous fluid under an external force $F_{ext}$, the equation of motion is:

$$m \frac{d^2 x}{dt^2} = -b \frac{dx}{dt} + F_{ext}(t) + F_B(t)$$

where:
- $b = 6\pi\mu R$ is the Stokes drag coefficient
- $F_B(t)$ is the **Brownian (stochastic) force** with statistical properties:
  - Mean: $\langle F_B(t) \rangle = 0$
  - Autocorrelation: $\langle F_B(t) F_B(t') \rangle = 2 b k_B T \, \delta(t - t')$

**Step 2 — Overdamped limit:**
At low Reynolds number, the inertial term $m\ddot{x}$ is negligible, giving:
$$b \frac{dx}{dt} = F_{ext}(t) + F_B(t)$$

This is the **overdamped Langevin equation**.

**Step 3 — Diffusion coefficient from Einstein relation:**

$$D = \frac{k_B T}{b} = \frac{k_B T}{6\pi\mu R}$$

**Step 4 — Mean square displacement in 1D:**

$$\langle x^2(t) \rangle = 2 D t = \frac{k_B T}{3\pi\mu R} \cdot t$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:**

Given:
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $T = 300$ K
- $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $R = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}$ m (radius of 1 µm sphere, i.e., diameter)
- $t = 1$ s

$$D = \frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{6\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$D = \frac{4.14 \times 10^{-21}}{9.42 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.39 \times 10^{-13} \,m^2/s$$

$$\langle x^2 \rangle = 2 \times 4.39 \times 10^{-13} \times 1 = 8.78 \times 10^{-13} \,m^2$$

$$\sqrt{\langle x^2 \rangle} \approx 2.96 \times 10^{-6} \,m \approx 3 \,\mu m$$

$$\boxed{\langle x^2 \rangle \approx 8.78 \times 10^{-13} \,m^2, \quad \text{RMS displacement} \approx 3 \,\mu m \text{ per second}}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering significance:**
A 1 µm sphere drifts ~3 µm in 1 second **without any external actuation**. For precision positioning tasks, the microrobot controller must either (a) apply feedback at frequencies > 1 Hz, or (b) operate in a medium where Brownian motion is suppressed (e.g., higher viscosity, smaller temperature, or vacuum environment).

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Langevin equation statement — 2 marks
- Statistical properties of $F_B$ — 1 mark
- Diffusion coefficient — 1 mark
- Numerical computation — 2 marks
- Engineering interpretation — 1 mark

---

### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain how surface forces (van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic) collectively affect the dynamic stability of a microrobot resting on a substrate. How does this impact the design of micro-actuators? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Force balance for a microrobot at rest on a substrate:**

A microrobot resting on a flat surface experiences:
- **Adhesion (downward, holding it to the surface):** $F_{adh} = F_{vdW} + F_{cap} + F_{elec}$
- **Restoring force from actuator (upward, lifting it off):** $F_{act}$

**Condition for release/lift-off:**
$$F_{act} > F_{adh} \Rightarrow \text{Motion initiated}$$

**Step 2 — Each force in detail:**

(a) **Van der Waals:**
$$F_{vdW} = \frac{A_H R}{6 D^2}$$
For $R = 10 \,\mu m$, $A_H = 10^{-19}$ J, $D = 0.5$ nm:
$$F_{vdW} \approx 6.67 \times 10^{-7} \,N = 0.667 \,\mu N$$

(b) **Capillary (with humidity film):**
$$F_{cap} = 4 \pi R \gamma \cos\theta$$
For $R = 10 \,\mu m$, $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m, $\theta = 0°$:
$$F_{cap} \approx 9.05 \times 10^{-6} \,N = 9.05 \,\mu N$$

(c) **Electrostatic:**
$$F_{elec} = \frac{1}{16\pi\varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q^2}{D^2}$$
For $Q = 10^{-13}$ C, $D = 1$ µm:
$$F_{elec} \approx 7.2 \times 10^{-9} \,N = 7.2 \,nN$$

**Step 3 — Total adhesion:**
$$F_{adh} \approx F_{vdW} + F_{cap} + F_{elec} \approx 0.67 + 9.05 + 0.0072 \approx 9.73 \,\mu N$$

**Step 4 — Dynamic stability condition:**

For static stability: $F_{adh} \geq F_{gravity}$ (always true at micro-scale).
For release: Actuator must provide $F_{act} > 9.73 \,\mu N$.

**Step 5 — Design implications for micro-actuators:**

1. **Actuator must overcome adhesion** — typically 1–100 µN range for 10 µm robots.
2. **Use compliant mechanisms** to store elastic energy and release suddenly.
3. **Reduce adhesion by:**
   - Surface roughening (reduce $R$ effective)
   - Hydrophobic coatings (reduce $F_{cap}$)
   - Anti-stiction SAM (self-assembled monolayers)
4. **Use vibration/dithering** to break stiction during initial release.
5. **Operating environment:** Dry, low-humidity, controlled charge.

**Step 6 — Block diagram (textual representation):**

```
Microrobot Substrate
        |
        v
[Surface Contact] --> F_vdW -->|
[Humidity Film]   --> F_cap -->|----> [F_adhesion total] --> [Force Comparison]
[Tribocharging]   --> F_elec-->|
                              ^
[Actuator Voltage] --> F_act --|--> If F_act > F_adhesion: Release
                              |
                       Else: Stuck (Stiction Failure)
```

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Force balance concept — 1 mark
- Three force expressions with values — 3 marks
- Total adhesion calculation — 1 mark
- Design implications — 1 mark
- Block diagram — 1 mark

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in Dynamics Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the Stokes flow assumption** (Re ≪ 1) before using $F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$. Examiners deduct 1 mark if this verification is missing.
> 2. **Unit mistakes in Stokes drag** — the drag coefficient $b$ has units of N·s/m, not N/m. Always check units before plugging into equations.
> 3. **Confusing Hamaker constant $A_H$ with Boltzmann constant $k_B$** — they are completely different physical quantities with different orders of magnitude ($10^{-19}$ J vs $10^{-23}$ J/K).
> 4. **In scaling law derivations, students often write $F_{surf}/F_{body} \propto L^{-1}$ instead of $L^{-2}$** — this is a critical error. The $L^{-2}$ scaling arises from the ratio of $L^1$ (surface) to $L^3$ (body), and the result must be explicitly derived, not assumed.
> 5. **In Brownian motion problems, students forget the factor of 2** in $\langle x^2 \rangle = 2Dt$ for 1D motion (and $6Dt$ for 3D). Examiners specifically test this.
> 6. **Not mentioning the scallop theorem / time-reversibility** when discussing low-Re swimming — this is a favorite KTU question topic.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Scaling law derivation** — $F_{surf}/F_{body} \propto L^{-2}$ is the cornerstone of microrobotic dynamics; commit this to memory.
- **Reynolds number $Re = \rho v L / \mu$** — at the micro-scale, $Re \ll 1$, leading to **Stokes flow**, **time-reversibility**, and the **scallop theorem**.
- **Three dominant surface forces:** van der Waals ($F \propto L$), capillary ($F \propto L$), electrostatic ($F \propto L$).
- **Stokes drag:** $F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$ — linear in velocity, valid only for low Re.
- **Terminal velocity:** $v_t = 2R^2 \Delta\rho g / (9\mu) \propto R^2$.
- **Relaxation time:** $\tau = m/b = 2\rho R^2 / (9\mu) \propto R^2$ — momentum is forgotten in microseconds.
- **Brownian mean square displacement:** $\langle x^2 \rangle = 2Dt$ in 1D, with $D = k_B T / (6\pi\mu R)$.
- **Einstein relation** $D = k_B T / b$ links thermal energy to drag — a fundamental result.
- **Langevin equation** — overdamped form governs microrobot dynamics in fluid: $b\dot{x} = F_{ext} + F_B(t)$.
- **Scallop theorem** — reciprocal motion (e.g., back-and-forth paddle) cannot produce net propulsion at low Re; this drives the design of **non-reciprocal** micro-swimmers.
- **Hamaker constant** typical value: $A_H \sim 10^{-19}$ to $10^{-20}$ J for most material pairs.
- **Anti-stiction design** is mandatory in micro-actuator engineering due to dominant adhesion.
- **KTU 2024 weightage:** Dynamics typically carries 15–20% of Module 1 marks; expect at least one Part A (3 marks) and likely one sub-part of a Part B (7 marks) question on this topic in ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear and Angular Momentum
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Linear and Angular Momentum

# Linear and Angular Momentum

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Linear Momentum (𝒑)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Momentum** of a particle is defined as the product of its mass and instantaneous velocity. It is a **vector quantity** whose direction is identical to the direction of velocity.

Mathematically:

$$\vec{p} = m \vec{v}$$

Where:
- $m$ = mass of the particle (in **kg**)
- $\vec{v}$ = instantaneous velocity (in **m/s**)
- $\vec{p}$ = linear momentum (in **kg·m/s** or **N·s**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Linear momentum is fundamentally a measure of "quantity of motion" possessed by a moving body. A heavy truck moving slowly can have the same momentum as a light bullet moving very fast.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine two people running toward you — one is a sprinter (light, fast) and the other is a sumo wrestler (heavy, slow). Even though their speeds differ drastically, if both are hard to stop, they have comparable linear momentum. Momentum captures *both* mass and motion.

### 1.2 Angular Momentum (𝐿)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Angular Momentum** about a point O of a particle with linear momentum $\vec{p}$ is defined as the cross product of the position vector $\vec{r}$ (from O to the particle) and the linear momentum $\vec{p}$.

$$\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p} = \vec{r} \times (m\vec{v})$$

Where:
- $\vec{r}$ = position vector of particle relative to O (in **m**)
- $\vec{L}$ = angular momentum (in **kg·m²/s** or **J·s**)
- The direction of $\vec{L}$ is **perpendicular** to the plane formed by $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{p}$ (given by the right-hand rule).

### 1.3 Newton's Second Law in Momentum Form

> [!NOTE]
> The **net external force** acting on a particle equals the **time rate of change of its linear momentum**:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = \frac{d(m\vec{v})}{dt}$$

If mass is constant:

$$\vec{F}_{\text{net}} = m\vec{a}$$

**Conceptual Analogy:** Angular momentum is the rotational equivalent of linear momentum. Just as a fast-moving object tends to keep moving in a straight line (linear inertia), a spinning object tends to keep spinning (rotational inertia). A spinning figure skater pulling in their arms spins faster — angular momentum is conserved while moment of inertia decreases.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Linear Momentum — Theoretical Framework

1. **Definition Stage:** $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$ — captures translational inertia.
2. **Newton's Formulation:** Force equals rate of change of momentum.
3. **Impulse:** The integral of force over the time interval during which it acts:

$$\vec{J} = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F}\, dt = \Delta \vec{p}$$

4. **Conservation Principle:** If $\vec{F}_{\text{net,ext}} = 0$, then $\vec{p}$ is conserved (constant in magnitude and direction).

### 2.2 Angular Momentum — Theoretical Framework

1. **Particle Form:** $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p} = m(\vec{r} \times \vec{v})$
2. **Rigid Body Form:** For a body rotating about a symmetry axis with moment of inertia $I$ and angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$:

$$\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}$$

3. **Torque–Angular Momentum Relation:**

$$\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$

4. **Conservation Principle:** If $\vec{\tau}_{\text{net,ext}} = 0$, then $\vec{L}$ is conserved.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility in Microrobotics

- **Linear momentum control** governs the locomotion of swimming/crawling microrobots in viscous fluid (low Reynolds number regimes).
- **Angular momentum conservation** underlies reaction-wheel and gyroscope-based **attitude control** in micro-satellites and flapping-wing MAVs.
- The **impulse** concept is used in designing micro-actuators that deliver controlled momentum pulses to manipulate micro-objects.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---------|----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Linear Momentum | $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$ | kg·m/s | Vector quantity |
| 2 | Newton's 2nd Law (momentum form) | $\vec{F} = d\vec{p}/dt$ | N | General form |
| 3 | Impulse–Momentum Theorem | $\vec{J} = \int \vec{F}\, dt = \Delta \vec{p}$ | N·s | Time-integrated force |
| 4 | Conservation of Linear Momentum | $\sum \vec{p}_{\text{initial}} = \sum \vec{p}_{\text{final}}$ | kg·m/s | Closed, isolated system |
| 5 | Angular Momentum (particle) | $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p}$ | kg·m²/s | Vector, perpendicular to $\vec{r}$–$\vec{p}$ plane |
| 6 | Angular Momentum (rigid body) | $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ | kg·m²/s | Scalar form for fixed axis |
| 7 | Torque–Angular Momentum | $\vec{\tau} = d\vec{L}/dt$ | N·m | Rotational equivalent of $\vec{F}=d\vec{p}/dt$ |
| 8 | Conservation of Angular Momentum | $\sum \vec{L}_{\text{initial}} = \sum \vec{L}_{\text{final}}$ | kg·m²/s | If $\vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = 0$ |
| 9 | Relation: $\vert \vec{L} \vert$ from $mvr$ | $L = mvr\sin\theta$ | kg·m²/s | $\theta$ is angle between $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{v}$ |
| 10 | Angular Impulse | $\int \vec{\tau}\, dt = \Delta \vec{L}$ | N·m·s | Time-integrated torque |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In KTU Board exams, when asked for "magnitude of angular momentum" of a particle moving in a circle, always use $L = mvr$ because $\vec{r} \perp \vec{v}$ (i.e., $\theta = 90°$, $\sin\theta = 1$).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Impulse–Momentum Theorem

We begin with Newton's second law in momentum form.

$$\vec{F} = \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** Multiply both sides by $dt$.

$$\vec{F}\, dt = d\vec{p}$$

**Step 2:** Integrate both sides over a finite time interval from $t_1$ to $t_2$.

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F}\, dt = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} d\vec{p}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the right-hand side (it becomes the change in momentum).

$$\int_{t_1}^{t_2} \vec{F}\, dt = \vec{p}_2 - \vec{p}_1 = \Delta \vec{p}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the left-hand side is the **impulse** $\vec{J}$.

$$\boxed{\vec{J} = \Delta \vec{p}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This theorem is fundamental in designing microrobotic impact-actuators (e.g., piezoelectric impulse hammers) where the impulse delivered is what matters, not the peak force.

### 3.2 Derivation of Torque as the Time Derivative of Angular Momentum

We start with angular momentum defined as:

$$\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p} = \vec{r} \times (m\vec{v})$$

**Step 1:** Take the time derivative of both sides. Apply the product rule for cross products:

$$\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} \times \vec{p} + \vec{r} \times \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate each term.
- The first term: $\dfrac{d\vec{r}}{dt} = \vec{v}$, and $\vec{v} \times \vec{p} = \vec{v} \times (m\vec{v}) = m(\vec{v} \times \vec{v}) = \vec{0}$ (cross product of parallel vectors is zero).

- The second term: $\dfrac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = \vec{F}_{\text{net}}$.

**Step 3:** Substitute back.

$$\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \vec{0} + \vec{r} \times \vec{F}_{\text{net}}$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $\vec{r} \times \vec{F}_{\text{net}}$ is the definition of **net torque** about point O.

$$\boxed{\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** This result is critical in microrobot joint control — to achieve a desired angular acceleration, the controller computes the required torque from the rate of change of angular momentum.

### 3.3 Derivation: Angular Momentum of a Rigid Body Rotating About a Fixed Axis

Consider a rigid body rotating about the z-axis with angular velocity $\vec{\omega} = \omega\hat{k}$. It consists of $n$ particles.

**Step 1:** The linear momentum of the $i$-th particle (mass $m_i$, at perpendicular distance $r_i$ from axis) is:

$$\vec{p}_i = m_i \vec{v}_i = m_i (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_i)$$

**Step 2:** The angular momentum of the $i$-th particle about the rotation axis is:

$$\vec{L}_i = \vec{r}_i \times \vec{p}_i = \vec{r}_i \times m_i (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_i)$$

**Step 3:** Using the BAC–CAB identity: $\vec{r}_i \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_i) = \vec{\omega}(\vec{r}_i \cdot \vec{r}_i) - \vec{r}_i(\vec{r}_i \cdot \vec{\omega})$. Since $\vec{r}_i \perp \vec{\omega}$ (for a particle at perpendicular distance $r_i$), $\vec{r}_i \cdot \vec{\omega} = 0$.

Thus:

$$\vec{r}_i \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}_i) = \omega r_i^2 \hat{k}$$

**Step 4:** Therefore:

$$\vec{L}_i = m_i \omega r_i^2 \hat{k}$$

**Step 5:** Sum over all particles (definition of moment of inertia $I = \sum m_i r_i^2$):

$$\vec{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{L}_i = \left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i^2\right) \omega \hat{k}$$

$$\boxed{\vec{L} = I \vec{\omega}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** In micro-gyroscopes and reaction wheels, the moment of inertia $I$ is engineered (mass distribution) so that a small angular velocity yields a precisely controllable angular momentum for satellite attitude control.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Conceptual Relationship Between Momentum Quantities

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Newton's Second Law of Motion] --> node2[Linear Form: F = m a]
    node1 --> node3[Momentum Form: F = dP/dt]
    node3 --> node4[Linear Momentum P = m v]
    node4 --> node5[Impulse J equals Delta P]
    node4 --> node6[Conservation of P when Fext = 0]
    node1 --> node7[Rotational Analogue: Torque equals dL/dt]
    node7 --> node8[Angular Momentum L = r cross P]
    node7 --> node9[Angular Momentum L = I omega]
    node8 --> node10[Particle in Circular Motion: L = m v r]
    node9 --> node11[Rigid Body Rotation]
    node8 --> node12[Conservation of L when Torque_ext = 0]
    node9 --> node12
    node10 --> node13[Microrobot Gyroscope Attitude Control]
    node11 --> node13
    node5 --> node14[Microrobot Impulse Actuator Design]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Momentum-Based Microrobot Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA[Command Input] --> blockB[Trajectory Planner]
    blockB --> blockC[Momentum Controller]
    blockC --> blockD[Linear Momentum Loop: F = m a]
    blockC --> blockE[Angular Momentum Loop: Torque = I alpha]
    blockD --> blockF[Micro Actuator Driver]
    blockE --> blockF
    blockF --> blockG[Microrobot Hardware]
    blockG --> blockH[Sensor Feedback: Position and Orientation]
    blockH --> blockC
    blockG --> blockI[Environment: Fluid or Air]
    blockI --> blockG
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Vector Relationship for Angular Momentum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR[Position Vector r] --> nodeP[Linear Momentum p = m v]
    nodeR --> nodeL[Angular Momentum L = r cross p]
    nodeP --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeDir[Direction: Perpendicular to r-p plane, given by right-hand rule]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Description — Microrobot Reaction Wheel System

```
+----------------------------------+
|  MICROSATELLITE / ROBOT BODY     |
|                                  |
|    [Payload]    [Reaction Wheel] |
|        |             |           |
|        +---- Spindle / Motor ----+
|                    |              |
|              Angular Momentum Lw  |
|                    |              |
|        Conservation: L_total = 0  |
|        => Body rotates opposite   |
+----------------------------------+
```

> [!NOTE]
> The reaction wheel stores angular momentum $L_w = I_w \omega_w$. When spun up, conservation forces the satellite body to rotate in the opposite direction — a textbook application of angular momentum conservation in microrobotics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q1. Define linear momentum. State its SI unit.** `[3 Marks] [CO1, Remember]`

**Model Answer:**
Linear momentum of a particle is defined as the product of its mass and instantaneous velocity. It is a vector quantity having the same direction as velocity.

$$\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$$

**SI unit: kg·m/s** (also expressed as **N·s**).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question | Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q2. State the law of conservation of angular momentum. Mention one practical application in microrobotics.** `[3 Marks] [CO2, Understand]`

**Model Answer:**
The law of conservation of angular momentum states that *the total angular momentum of an isolated system (no external torque) remains constant in both magnitude and direction.*

**Mathematically:** If $\vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = 0$, then $\vec{L} = \text{constant}$.

**Application:** Used in **reaction-wheel attitude control** of micro-satellites and in **gyroscopic stabilization** of microrobotic platforms.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Module Internal Choice — Select either Question A OR Question B.**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question | May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the relation $\vec{\tau} = \dfrac{d\vec{L}}{dt}$ starting from Newton's second law. Explain each step clearly. **[7 Marks] [CO2, Apply]**

**(b)** A uniform solid disc of mass **5 kg** and radius **0.2 m** rotates about its central axis at **10 rad/s**. Compute its angular momentum. If the disc's radius is reduced (mass conserved) to **0.1 m** while angular momentum is conserved, find the new angular velocity. **[7 Marks] [CO3, Apply]`

**Model Solution (a):**
Starting with $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p}$, differentiate w.r.t. time using product rule:

$$\frac{d\vec{L}}{dt} = \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} \times \vec{p} + \vec{r} \times \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt}$$

The first term vanishes because $\vec{v} \times m\vec{v} = \vec{0}$. The second term gives $\vec{r} \times \vec{F} = \vec{\tau}$.

$$\boxed{\vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{L}}{dt}}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Moment of inertia of solid disc: $I_1 = \dfrac{1}{2} M R_1^2$.

$$I_1 = 0.5 \times 5 \times (0.2)^2 = 0.1 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Initial angular momentum:

$$L_1 = I_1 \omega_1 = 0.1 \times 10 = 1.0 \text{ kg·m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 3:** New moment of inertia at $R_2 = 0.1$ m (mass conserved):

$$I_2 = 0.5 \times 5 \times (0.1)^2 = 0.025 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 4:** Apply conservation of angular momentum: $L_1 = L_2 \Rightarrow I_1\omega_1 = I_2\omega_2$.

$$\omega_2 = \frac{I_1 \omega_1}{I_2} = \frac{1.0}{0.025} = 40 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Final Answer:** $L_1 = 1.0$ kg·m²/s, $\omega_2 = 40$ rad/s.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question | May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the impulse–momentum theorem. A **0.2 kg** ball strikes a wall perpendicular at **15 m/s** and rebounds at **10 m/s**. Find the impulse and average force if the contact time is **0.05 s**. **[7 Marks] [CO2, Apply]**

**(b)** Explain the conservation of linear momentum with reference to a collision in two dimensions. A **2 kg** particle moving at **3 m/s** along the x-axis collides with a **3 kg** particle at rest. After collision, the 2 kg particle moves at **2 m/s** at **60°** to x-axis. Find the velocity of the 3 kg particle. **[7 Marks] [CO3, Apply]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Derivation:** Starting from $\vec{F} = d\vec{p}/dt$, integrating gives:

$$\int \vec{F}\, dt = \Delta \vec{p} = \vec{J}$$

**Numerical computation:**
Take the wall-normal as positive direction.

- Initial momentum: $p_i = 0.2 \times 15 = 3.0$ kg·m/s.
- Final momentum: $p_f = 0.2 \times (-10) = -2.0$ kg·m/s (rebound reverses direction).

$$\vec{J} = p_f - p_i = -2.0 - 3.0 = -5.0 \text{ N·s}$$

$$\vert \vec{J} \vert = 5.0 \text{ N·s (directed back into the wall)}$$

Average force:

$$F_{\text{avg}} = \frac{J}{\Delta t} = \frac{5.0}{0.05} = 100 \text{ N}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

Conservation in x-direction:

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 \cos\theta_1 + m_2 v_2 \cos\theta_2$$

$$2(3) + 0 = 2(2)\cos 60° + 3 v_2 \cos\theta_2$$

$$6 = 2 + 3 v_2 \cos\theta_2 \Rightarrow 3 v_2 \cos\theta_2 = 4 \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Conservation in y-direction (initial y-momentum = 0):

$$0 = m_1 v_1 \sin\theta_1 + m_2 v_2 \sin\theta_2$$

$$0 = 2(2)\sin 60° + 3 v_2 \sin\theta_2$$

$$0 = 2\sqrt{3} + 3 v_2 \sin\theta_2 \Rightarrow v_2 \sin\theta_2 = -\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3} \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

Squaring (i) and (ii) and adding:

$$9 v_2^2 (\cos^2\theta_2 + \sin^2\theta_2) = 16 + \frac{4 \cdot 3}{9} = 16 + \frac{12}{9} = \frac{156}{9}$$

$$v_2^2 = \frac{156}{81} = 1.926$$

$$v_2 \approx 1.388 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\theta_2 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{-\frac{2\sqrt{3}}{3}}{\frac{4}{3}}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \approx -40.9°$$

**Final Answer:** $v_2 \approx 1.39$ m/s at $-40.9°$ to x-axis (i.e., below the x-axis).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Examiners Penalize:**
>
> 1. **Direction Confusion (–2 marks):** Forgetting the negative sign in rebound problems when computing $\Delta \vec{p}$. Always define a positive axis and be consistent.
> 2. **Wrong Vector vs. Scalar (–2 marks):** Writing $L = mvr$ without checking whether $\vec{r}$ and $\vec{v}$ are perpendicular. In 2-D problems with arbitrary angles, use $L = mvr\sin\theta$.
> 3. **Units (–1 mark):** Writing angular momentum in **N·m** instead of **kg·m²/s**. The SI unit of torque is N·m, but angular momentum is kg·m²/s.
> 4. **Conservation Misuse (–3 marks):** Applying conservation of linear momentum in problems where external forces (e.g., gravity, friction) act — conservation applies ONLY to isolated systems OR when net external force is zero.
> 5. **Moment of Inertia Errors (–2 marks):** Using $I = MR^2$ for a disc instead of $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$. Always recall the standard formula for the geometry in question.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Linear momentum** is $\vec{p} = m\vec{v}$; **angular momentum** is $\vec{L} = \vec{r} \times \vec{p}$ for a particle and $\vec{L} = I\vec{\omega}$ for a rigid body.
- The **time derivative** of momentum gives **force**; the time derivative of angular momentum gives **torque**.
- The **impulse–momentum theorem** connects a time-integrated force to a change in momentum: $\vec{J} = \Delta \vec{p}$.
- The **angular impulse–angular momentum theorem** connects a time-integrated torque to a change in angular momentum: $\int \vec{\tau}\, dt = \Delta \vec{L}$.
- **Conservation of linear momentum** applies when $\sum \vec{F}_{\text{ext}} = 0$.
- **Conservation of angular momentum** applies when $\sum \vec{\tau}_{\text{ext}} = 0$ — a foundational principle in microrobot reaction wheels, gyroscopes, and attitude control.
- **SI Units to Memorize:**
  - Linear momentum: kg·m/s or N·s.
  - Angular momentum: kg·m²/s or J·s.
  - Impulse: N·s.
  - Angular impulse: N·m·s.
- **Key Geometric Moment-of-Inertia Values** (for KTU problems):
  - Solid disc: $I = \tfrac{1}{2}MR^2$
  - Solid sphere: $I = \tfrac{2}{5}MR^2$
  - Thin rod (about center): $I = \tfrac{1}{12}ML^2$
- Always **draw a free-body or vector diagram** before applying conservation laws.
- **Microrobot context:** Most microrobot locomotion occurs in low-Reynolds-number regimes where viscous drag dominates — this affects how linear and angular momentum are transferred to the fluid environment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equation of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equation of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Microrobotics**, the **Equation of Motion (EOM)** is the fundamental second-order differential equation that mathematically describes the dynamic response of a microrobotic system subjected to external actuation forces and environmental disturbances. It is derived from **Newton's Second Law of Motion** and is typically expressed as:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F_{ext}(t)$$

where:
- $m$ = effective mass of the microrobot (kg)
- $b$ = viscous damping coefficient (N·s/m)
- $k$ = elastic/stiffness constant (N/m) (if spring-coupled)
- $F_{ext}(t)$ = resultant external actuation force (N)
- $\ddot{x}$, $\dot{x}$, $x$ = acceleration, velocity, and displacement respectively

At the **microscale**, the equation of motion becomes dominated by **viscous forces** rather than inertial forces due to the extremely low **Reynolds Number ($Re \ll 1$)**, fundamentally altering the dynamic behavior compared to macro-scale robotics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**: In PERUT864 Microrobotics Module 1, the Equation of Motion is examined in the context of low Reynolds number fluid dynamics, magnetic/electrostatic actuation, and the design implications for sub-millimeter robotic swimmers, crawlers, and flyers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey vs Water" Intuition

Imagine pushing a small marble through **water** versus through **honey**.

- In **water**, the marble coasts for a long time after you stop pushing — inertia dominates because the fluid offers little resistance. The marble *remembers* its previous motion.
- In **honey**, the marble stops almost the instant you stop pushing — viscosity dominates because the fluid "grabs" onto the marble. The marble *forgets* its motion instantly.

A microrobot swimming in a fluid medium (like blood, water, or air at sub-millimeter scales) behaves much more like the marble in **honey**. The equation of motion at microscale thus essentially reduces to:

$$b\dot{x} \approx F_{ext}(t)$$

This is known as the **overdamped regime** or **quasi-static regime** of motion.

## 1.3 Critical Physical Constants at the Microscale

| Parameter | Symbol | Approximate Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dynamic viscosity of water | $\mu$ | **1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | Dominates drag forces |
| Density of water | $\rho$ | **1.0 × 10³ kg/m³** | Sets inertial scale |
| Kinematic viscosity of water | $\nu = \mu/\rho$ | **1.0 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s** | Appears in Reynolds number |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Often negligible vs surface forces |
| Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0$ | **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** | Used in electrostatic EOM |
| Permeability of free space | $\mu_0$ | **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m** | Used in magnetic EOM |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: At length scales below ~1 mm, surface forces (van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary) and viscous forces dominate over volume forces (gravity, inertia). This is why microrobot EOMs must include these surface/volume ratio effects.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — Force Decomposition

The general form of the equation of motion for a microrobot is built from a **force balance** on the body. The standard decomposition is:

$$\sum F_i = m\vec{a}$$

Which expands into:

$$F_{drag} + F_{actuation} + F_{elastic} + F_{gravity} + F_{surface} + F_{Brownian} = m\vec{a}$$

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic Decomposition

**Step 1: Identify the dominant force regime**
- Compute Reynolds number $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$
- If $Re \ll 1$ → **Stokes regime** (viscous dominates)
- If $Re \gg 1$ → **Inertial regime** (rare at microscale)

**Step 2: Select appropriate drag model**
- Spherical body → Stokes drag: $F_d = 6\pi\mu r v$
- Non-spherical → Modified drag with shape factor
- Near surface → Wall-corrected drag (Faxén's law)

**Step 3: Identify the actuation mechanism**
- Magnetic field → $\vec{F}_m = \nabla(\vec{m}\cdot\vec{B})$
- Electric field → $\vec{F}_e = q\vec{E}$
- Optical → Radiation pressure
- Chemical → Bubble propulsion, surface tension gradients

**Step 4: Include environmental and surface forces**
- Brownian motion → Stochastic forcing
- Van der Waals → $F_{vdW} \propto 1/d^3$
- Electrostatic double layer → DLVO theory

**Step 5: Combine into the master EOM**

## 2.2 Why and How — The Physics Behind Each Term

| Term | Physical Origin | Scaling with Size ($L$) |
|---|---|---|
| Inertia ($m\ddot{x}$) | Resistance to acceleration | $\propto L^3$ |
| Viscous drag ($b\dot{x}$) | Fluid friction | $\propto L$ |
| Elastic force ($kx$) | Material stiffness | $\propto L$ |
| Magnetic force | Field gradient | $\propto L^3$ |
| Electrostatic force | Charge-field interaction | $\propto L^2$ (surface) or $L^3$ (volume) |
| Surface tension | Interfacial energy | $\propto L$ |
| Gravity ($mg$) | Earth attraction | $\propto L^3$ |

**The "Why"**: Since inertia scales as $L^3$ and drag scales as $L$, the ratio $F_{inertia}/F_{drag} \propto L^2$. As $L \to 10^{-6}$ m, this ratio becomes negligible, making drag the dominant force.

**The "How"**: This scaling behavior forces microrobot designers to use **continuous oscillatory actuation** (rather than single impulses) because momentum is dissipated instantly.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F_{ext}(t)$ | Master EOM (general) | N |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number | dimensionless |
| 3 | $F_d = 6\pi\mu r v$ | Stokes Drag (sphere) | N |
| 4 | $b = 6\pi\mu r$ | Damping coefficient (sphere) | N·s/m |
| 5 | $\tau_{relax} = \dfrac{m}{b}$ | Velocity relaxation time | s |
| 6 | $F_m = \nabla(\vec{m}\cdot\vec{B})$ | Magnetic force on dipole | N |
| 7 | $\vec{T}_m = \vec{m} \times \vec{B}$ | Magnetic torque | N·m |
| 8 | $F_e = q\vec{E}$ | Electrostatic force | N |
| 9 | $F_g = mg$ | Gravitational force | N |
| 10 | $F_{cap} = \gamma L \cos\theta$ | Capillary force | N |
| 11 | $\tau_p = \dfrac{6\pi\mu r^3 L_p}{k_B T}$ | Brownian rotational diffusion | s |
| 12 | $m\ddot{x} \approx 0$ (when $Re \ll 1$) | Overdamped/quasi-static limit | — |
| 13 | $F_{tot} = F_{actuation} - F_{drag}$ | Steady-state force balance | N |
| 14 | $v_{ss} = \dfrac{F_{actuation}}{6\pi\mu r}$ | Steady-state velocity (sphere) | m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Note**: For KTU examinations, Equation (1), (2), (3), and (6) form the **core quartet** that appears in 90% of numerical problems. Master their derivation and physical meaning.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The equation of motion is the **backbone of microrobot design** in applications such as:
- **Targeted drug delivery**: Predicting trajectory of magnetic nanocarriers in bloodstream
- **Microsurgery**: Controlling microgrippers for cellular manipulation
- **Biofilm removal**: Designing swimming microrobots with optimal actuation frequency
- **Lab-on-a-chip systems**: Predicting particle motion in microfluidic channels
- **Environmental remediation**: Swimming microrobots for pollutant capture

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Master Equation of Motion from Newton's Second Law

Starting from Newton's Second Law applied to a microrobot of mass $m$ moving in a viscous fluid:

$$F_{net} = m\vec{a} = m\ddot{\vec{x}}$$

**Step 1**: Identify all forces acting on the body:

$$\vec{F}_{net} = \vec{F}_{actuation} + \vec{F}_{drag} + \vec{F}_{elastic} + \vec{F}_{other}$$

**Step 2**: For the drag force in the Stokes regime (low Re), apply the linear viscous model:

$$\vec{F}_{drag} = -b\dot{\vec{x}}$$

where $b$ is the **viscous damping coefficient** (positive scalar; the negative sign indicates the drag opposes motion).

**Step 3**: For elastic restoring forces (e.g., flexible cilia, tethered microrobots):

$$\vec{F}_{elastic} = -k\vec{x}$$

**Step 4**: Substituting into Newton's law:

$$m\ddot{\vec{x}} = \vec{F}_{actuation} - b\dot{\vec{x}} - k\vec{x}$$

**Step 5**: Rearranging to the standard form:

$$\boxed{m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F_{ext}(t)}$$

This is the **canonical second-order linear ODE** for a microrobot's 1-D translational motion.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Stokes Drag Force on a Spherical Microbot

Starting from the **Navier-Stokes equation** for incompressible flow:

$$\rho\left(\frac{\partial \vec{v}}{\partial t} + \vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}\right) = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^2\vec{v} + \vec{f}$$

**Step 1**: At the microscale, the Reynolds number $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} \ll 1$, so the inertial term $\rho(\vec{v}\cdot\nabla\vec{v}) \to 0$:

$$0 = -\nabla p + \mu\nabla^2\vec{v} + \vec{f}$$

**Step 2**: For a sphere of radius $r$ translating with velocity $U$ in an unbounded quiescent fluid:

$$\mu\nabla^2\vec{v} = \nabla p$$

**Step 3**: Solve in **spherical coordinates** with boundary conditions:
- At $r = R$ (sphere surface): $\vec{v} = U\hat{x}$
- At $r \to \infty$: $\vec{v} \to 0$

**Step 4**: The pressure field is obtained as:

$$p = p_\infty - \frac{3\mu U R}{2 r^2}\cos\theta$$

**Step 5**: The velocity field is:

$$\vec{v} = -\frac{3R U}{4r}(2 + \frac{R^2}{r^2})\cos\theta \,\hat{r} + \frac{3R U}{4r}(\frac{R^2}{r^2} - 1)\sin\theta \,\hat{\theta}$$

**Step 6**: Integrate the stress tensor over the sphere surface to obtain total drag:

$$F_d = \int \tau_{r\theta} \vert_{r=R} \, dA$$

**Step 7**: Performing the surface integration yields the famous result:

$$\boxed{F_d = 6\pi\mu R U}$$

where $R$ is the sphere radius, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $U$ is the translational velocity.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Velocity Relaxation Time at Microscale

Starting from the overdamped equation of motion (setting $F_{ext} = 0$):

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} = 0$$

**Step 1**: For a sphere, $m = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \rho_p$ and $b = 6\pi\mu R$.

**Step 2**: Divide through by $m$:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{b}{m}\dot{x} = 0$$

**Step 3**: Define the relaxation time constant:

$$\tau_{relax} = \frac{m}{b} = \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \rho_p}{6\pi\mu R}$$

**Step 4**: Simplify:

$$\tau_{relax} = \frac{2 R^2 \rho_p}{9 \mu}$$

**Step 5**: For a typical microrobot ($R = 10\,\mu\text{m} = 10^{-5}$ m, $\rho_p = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s):

$$\tau_{relax} = \frac{2 \cdot (10^{-5})^2 \cdot 1000}{9 \cdot 10^{-3}} = \frac{2 \cdot 10^{-7}}{9 \cdot 10^{-3}} \approx 2.22 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s}$$

This extremely short relaxation time (~22 microseconds) confirms that inertia is **negligible** at microscale.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Steady-State Velocity for a Magnetically Actuated Microrobot

Starting with the overdamped equation of motion for 1-D translation:

$$b\dot{x} = F_{magnetic}$$

**Step 1**: For a magnetic dipole $\vec{m}$ in a field gradient $\nabla \vec{B}$:

$$F_{magnetic} = (\vec{m} \cdot \nabla)\vec{B}$$

**Step 2**: For a permanent magnet microrobot with constant dipole moment aligned with $\hat{x}$:

$$F_{magnetic} = m_{mag}\frac{\partial B_x}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3**: For a sphere in Stokes flow, $b = 6\pi\mu R$, so:

$$6\pi\mu R \cdot v_{ss} = m_{mag}\frac{\partial B_x}{\partial x}$$

**Step 4**: Solve for steady-state velocity:

$$\boxed{v_{ss} = \frac{m_{mag}}{6\pi\mu R}\frac{\partial B_x}{\partial x}}$$

**Step 5**: Special case — uniform field with no gradient yields $v_{ss} = 0$. This shows that **field gradient is essential** for translational motion of magnetic microrobots (gradient pulling).

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Dimensionless Form and Reynolds Number Analysis

Starting from the master EOM:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} = F_{ext}$$

**Step 1**: Non-dimensionalize using characteristic length $L$ and characteristic time $T = L/V$:

$$x^* = \frac{x}{L}, \quad t^* = \frac{t}{T} = \frac{Vt}{L}$$

**Step 2**: Derivatives transform as:

$$\dot{x} = V\frac{dx^*}{dt^*}, \quad \ddot{x} = \frac{V^2}{L}\frac{d^2 x^*}{dt^{*2}}$$

**Step 3**: Substituting:

$$m\frac{V^2}{L}\frac{d^2 x^*}{dt^{*2}} + bV\frac{dx^*}{dt^*} = F_{ext}$$

**Step 4**: Divide by $bV$:

$$\frac{mV}{bL}\frac{d^2 x^*}{dt^{*2}} + \frac{dx^*}{dt^*} = \frac{F_{ext}}{bV}$$

**Step 5**: Recognize the dimensionless coefficient as the inverse of a Reynolds-like number. Using $b = 6\pi\mu R$ and $m \sim \rho_p L^3$:

$$\frac{mV}{bL} \sim \frac{\rho_p L^2 V}{\mu} = Re$$

**Step 6**: Therefore:

$$Re\cdot \ddot{x}^* + \dot{x}^* = \frac{F_{ext}}{bV}$$

At microscale, $Re \ll 1$, confirming the overdamped limit:

$$\dot{x}^* \approx \frac{F_{ext}}{bV}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip**: This dimensionless analysis is a favorite KTU question type — examiners often ask students to "justify why inertia is neglected at microscale using Reynolds number analysis."

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Influence Summary

| Parameter | Increase → Effect on EOM | Physical Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Robot size $L$ ↑ | Inertia grows as $L^3$ vs drag as $L$ | Volume scales faster than surface |
| Fluid viscosity $\mu$ ↑ | Drag dominates more strongly | Higher momentum diffusion |
| Velocity $V$ ↑ | Reynolds number ↑ | More inertial effects |
| Magnetic moment $m_{mag}$ ↑ | Higher $v_{ss}$ | Stronger actuation |
| Field gradient $\nabla B$ ↑ | Higher $v_{ss}$ (linear) | Direct force amplification |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates the functional architecture of the equation of motion, showing how various physical forces combine into the master EOM:

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Equation of Motion for a microrobot. Why is the inertial term often neglected at the microscale?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The Equation of Motion (EOM) for a microrobot in 1-D is the second-order ODE:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F_{ext}(t)$$

where $m$ is mass, $b$ is viscous damping, $k$ is stiffness, and $F_{ext}$ is the actuation force.

**(1 mark for definition)**

The inertial term $m\ddot{x}$ is neglected at microscale because the **Reynolds number** $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu} \ll 1$ for typical microrobots ($L < 1$ mm). Since $F_{inertia}/F_{drag} \propto L^2$, as $L \to 10^{-6}$ m, inertial forces become negligible compared to viscous forces.

**(2 marks for justification with Reynolds number argument)**

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the Stokes drag formula for a spherical microrobot.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):

The Stokes drag formula for a sphere of radius $R$ moving with velocity $U$ in a viscous fluid of dynamic viscosity $\mu$ is:

$$F_d = 6\pi\mu R U$$

**(1 mark for statement)**

This formula is valid only in the low Reynolds number regime ($Re \ll 1$), where inertial terms in the Navier-Stokes equation are negligible. The corresponding viscous damping coefficient is $b = 6\pi\mu R$.

**(2 marks for explanation and validity conditions)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the equation of motion for a magnetic microrobot in a viscous fluid, clearly showing all force components and assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical magnetic microrobot of radius $R = 50\,\mu\text{m}$ and density $\rho_p = 7000$ kg/m³ is placed in water ($\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s). Compute: (i) the damping coefficient, (ii) the velocity relaxation time, and (iii) comment on the validity of neglecting inertia. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 (1 mark)**: Apply Newton's Second Law to the microrobot:
$$F_{net} = m\vec{a}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark)**: Identify force components:
- Magnetic actuation: $F_m = m_{mag}\,\partial B_x/\partial x$
- Viscous drag (Stokes): $F_d = -6\pi\mu R \dot{x}$
- Brownian stochastic force: $F_B(t)$
- Gravity/buoyancy: often negligible

**Step 3 (1 mark)**: Write the force balance:
$$m\ddot{x} = F_m - 6\pi\mu R \dot{x} + F_B(t)$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)**: Rearrange to standard form:
$$m\ddot{x} + 6\pi\mu R \dot{x} = m_{mag}\frac{\partial B_x}{\partial x} + F_B(t)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: State assumptions:
- Spherical geometry
- Low Reynolds number (Stokes flow)
- Negligible inertia at steady state
- No elastic coupling (free body)

**Step 6 (1 mark)**: State the overdamped limit:
$$6\pi\mu R \dot{x} \approx m_{mag}\frac{\partial B_x}{\partial x}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark)**: Solve for steady-state velocity:
$$v_{ss} = \frac{m_{mag}}{6\pi\mu R}\frac{\partial B_x}{\partial x}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**Given**: $R = 50\,\mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $\rho_p = 7000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

**(i) Damping coefficient (2 marks)**:
$$b = 6\pi\mu R = 6\pi \times 10^{-3} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$b = 6 \times 3.1416 \times 5 \times 10^{-8} \approx 9.42 \times 10^{-7} \text{ N·s/m}$$

**(ii) Velocity relaxation time (3 marks)**:
Mass: $m = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3 \rho_p = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi (50 \times 10^{-6})^3 \times 7000$

$$m = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \times 1.25 \times 10^{-13} \times 7000 \approx 3.665 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg}$$

$$\tau_{relax} = \frac{m}{b} = \frac{3.665 \times 10^{-9}}{9.42 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 3.89 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 3.89 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) Validity of neglecting inertia (2 marks)**:
The relaxation time of ~3.89 ms is much shorter than typical actuation periods (~100 ms). Therefore, the microrobot reaches terminal velocity almost instantly after force application. Inertia can be safely neglected, justifying the overdamped approximation.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the significance of the Reynolds number in microrobot dynamics. Show that at $Re \ll 1$, the equation of motion reduces to a first-order ODE. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a non-dimensionalization procedure, derive the dimensionless form of the microrobot equation of motion and identify the conditions under which inertial effects can be neglected. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 (1.5 marks)**: Define Reynolds number:
$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$
where $\rho$ = fluid density, $v$ = characteristic velocity, $L$ = characteristic length, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks)**: Physical meaning:
- $Re$ represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces.
- High $Re$ → inertia dominates (turbulence, swimming fish).
- Low $Re$ → viscosity dominates (microswimmers, bacteria).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks)**: Microrobot case:
For a microrobot of size $L = 10\,\mu\text{m}$, $v = 100\,\mu\text{m/s}$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s:
$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-3}} = 10^{-3} \ll 1$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks)**: Reduction to first-order ODE:
Starting from $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} = F_{ext}$, divide by $b$:
$$\frac{m}{b}\ddot{x} + \dot{x} = \frac{F_{ext}}{b}$$

Since $\dfrac{m}{b} = \tau_{relax} \to 0$ at microscale:
$$\dot{x} \approx \frac{F_{ext}}{b}$$

This is a **first-order ODE** — velocity responds instantaneously to applied force.

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Implication: Microrobots require **time-asymmetric** (non-reciprocal) motion patterns to achieve net displacement (Scallop theorem).

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:

**Step 1 (1.5 marks)**: Start with dimensional EOM:
$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} = F_{ext}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks)**: Introduce dimensionless variables:
$$x^* = \frac{x}{L}, \quad t^* = \frac{Vt}{L}$$
$$\dot{x} = V\frac{dx^*}{dt^*}, \quad \ddot{x} = \frac{V^2}{L}\frac{d^2x^*}{dt^{*2}}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks)**: Substitute:
$$m\frac{V^2}{L}\frac{d^2x^*}{dt^{*2}} + bV\frac{dx^*}{dt^*} = F_{ext}$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks)**: Divide by $bV$:
$$\frac{mV}{bL}\frac{d^2x^*}{dt^{*2}} + \frac{dx^*}{dt^*} = \frac{F_{ext}}{bV}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)**: Identify dimensionless coefficients:
The coefficient $\dfrac{mV}{bL} \sim \dfrac{\rho_p L^2 V}{\mu} \sim Re$

Therefore:
$$Re \cdot \ddot{x}^* + \dot{x}^* = \frac{F_{ext}}{bV}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning**: Students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. Forgetting to **state units** explicitly in the final answer.
> 2. Confusing **dynamic viscosity $\mu$** with **kinematic viscosity $\nu$** in the Reynolds number formula.
> 3. **Not justifying** why inertia is neglected (must explicitly mention $Re \ll 1$).
> 4. Forgetting to **identify boundary conditions** in derivation problems.
> 5. Mixing up $b = 6\pi\mu R$ (sphere) with general drag $b = c_d \cdot$ Area $\cdot \rho/2$ (used at high Re).

**Step 6 (0.5 marks)**: Condition for neglecting inertia:
$$Re \ll 1 \implies \text{overdamped, first-order ODE regime applies}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Equation of Motion in Microrobotics**

- **Master EOM**: $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F_{ext}(t)$ — always start here
- **Reynolds Number**: $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ — the gatekeeper to regime selection
- **Microscale regime**: $Re \ll 1$ always → Stokes flow assumption valid
- **Stokes drag (sphere)**: $F_d = 6\pi\mu R v$ — memorize and derive
- **Damping coefficient**: $b = 6\pi\mu R$ — for spherical geometry
- **Relaxation time**: $\tau = \dfrac{2R^2\rho_p}{9\mu}$ — typically microseconds to milliseconds
- **Overdamped limit**: $b\dot{x} = F_{ext}$ — used for 90% of microrobot problems
- **Steady-state velocity**: $v_{ss} = \dfrac{F_{act}}{6\pi\mu R}$ — direct proportionality
- **Magnetic actuation**: $F_m = (\vec{m}\cdot\nabla)\vec{B}$ — gradient pulling needed
- **Scaling laws**:
  - Inertia $\propto L^3$ (volume)
  - Drag $\propto L$ (linear dimension)
  - Surface tension $\propto L$
  - Van der Waals $\propto L$
- **Always**: Justify every term you include or exclude with a scaling/Reynolds argument
- **Units check**: Force [N], damping [N·s/m], mass [kg], velocity [m/s]
- **Common pitfall**: Never write $F = ma$ and call it done — must decompose forces
- **Scallop Theorem**: At $Re \ll 1$, reciprocal motion gives zero net displacement
- **Exam mantra**: "At microscale, viscosity reigns, inertia forgets."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multi-Body Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Multi-Body Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Multi-Body System (MBS)** in the context of microrobotics refers to a mechanical assembly composed of two or more rigid or flexible bodies interconnected by joints, constraints, and contact elements, whose kinematic and dynamic behaviour must be analyzed as a coupled entity rather than as isolated components. In microrobotics, MBS theory is the mathematical and physical framework used to describe the motion, interaction, and energy transfer between the micro-scale links, actuators, sensors, and end-effectors that constitute a microrobot.

In the KTU 2024 scheme (PERUT864 Module 1), the study of multi-body systems encompasses:

- **Generalized coordinates** and **degrees of freedom (DOF)** determination
- **Holonomic** and **non-holonomic constraints**
- **Kinematic analysis** — forward and inverse
- **Dynamic modeling** — Newton–Euler and Lagrangian formulations
- **Constraint equations** and the **Jacobian matrix**
- **Coupled equations of motion** in matrix form: $\mathbf{M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PERUT864 Module 1):** Multi-body systems form the foundational backbone for analyzing every subsequent module — from micro-actuators to swarm coordination. Mastering DOF counting and Lagrangian formulation is non-negotiable for the ESE.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The Human Finger:**
> Imagine your index finger. It has 3 bones (phalanges), connected by 3 joints. Each joint allows motion in specific directions. If you wanted to touch your fingertip to a precise point, your brain doesn't command each bone individually — it solves a **coupled multi-body problem** automatically. The bones are the *bodies*, the joints are the *constraints*, the tendon forces are the *generalized forces*, and the fingertip position is computed via a *kinematic chain*.

Now, **shrink that finger to 100 micrometres**. The bones are now silicon micro-links, the joints are flexure hinges, the tendons are electrostatic or piezoelectric actuators, and surface forces (van der Waals, capillary, friction) become dominant. The math is identical — the physics is dramatically different. This is the essence of *multi-body microrobotics*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and scale-dependent metrics are critical for multi-body analysis at the micro-scale:

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\rho$ | Density of silicon | $2.33 \times 10^{3} \ \text{kg/m}^3$ |
| $E$ | Young's modulus (silicon) | $1.69 \times 10^{11} \ \text{Pa}$ |
| $G$ | Shear modulus (silicon) | $6.5 \times 10^{10} \ \text{Pa}$ |
| $\nu$ | Poisson's ratio | $0.28$ |
| $\mu$ | Coefficient of friction (micro) | $0.1 - 0.6$ |
| $\gamma$ | Surface tension (water) | $0.072 \ \text{N/m}$ |
| $L_c$ | Capillary length | $2.7 \ \text{mm}$ |
| $Re$ | Reynolds number (micro) | $< 1$ |
| $Kn$ | Knudsen number | $0.001 - 10$ |
