# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a cross strip flexure pivot and state its two most important advantages over conventional bearings for microrobotic applications.**

**Model Answer:**
A cross strip flexure pivot is a rotational compliant bearing made of two thin rectangular strips of elastic material crossed orthogonally at their midpoints, with the four ends clamped to a stationary frame and a central rotor.

**Two key advantages:**
1. **Zero friction and zero wear** — motion occurs entirely through elastic deformation, so there is no contact, no lubrication, and no wear. This is critical at the micron scale where surface forces dominate and lubrication is impossible.
2. **No backlash and no hysteresis** (within the elastic limit) — the pivot has a single, well-defined rotational centre and a deterministic force-deflection relationship, making it ideal for precision positioning and repeatable motion.

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the two advantages)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a monolithic cross pivot? Mention any two fabrication techniques used to manufacture it.**

**Model Answer:**
A monolithic cross pivot is a single-piece, seamless realisation of the cross pivot geometry in which the flexure webs, the central hub, and the outer frame are all carved from one continuous block of material — there are no joints, fasteners, or bonded interfaces.

**Two fabrication techniques:**
1. **Wire Electrical Discharge Machining (Wire-EDM / WEDM)** — for metallic flexures in the millimetre-to-centimetre scale.
2. **Deep Reactive Ion Etching (DRIE)** — for silicon-based MEMS flexures at the micron scale.

*(Acceptable alternatives: laser micro-machining, micro-milling, photolithography + etching, X-ray LIGA.)*

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for techniques)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A monolithic cross pivot is to be designed for a MEMS tilting micromirror. The flexure material is silicon with Young's modulus E = 170 GPa, strip dimensions are length L = 1.0 mm, width b = 50 µm, thickness t = 5 µm. Determine:**

**(a) The rotational stiffness $k_{\theta}$ of the pivot.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) The maximum allowable rotation angle before yielding, given that the yield strength of silicon is $\sigma_{yield} = 7$ GPa. Use a safety factor SF = 2.5.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Step 1 — Recall the canonical formula for cross pivot rotational stiffness:**
$$k_{\theta} \;=\; \frac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{6\,L}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:**
$$E = 170\times 10^{9}\;\text{Pa},\quad b = 50\times 10^{-6}\;\text{m},\quad t = 5\times 10^{-6}\;\text{m},\quad L = 1.0\times 10^{-3}\;\text{m}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
k_{\theta} &= \frac{(170\times 10^{9}) \times (50\times 10^{-6}) \times (5\times 10^{-6})^{3}}{6 \times (1.0\times 10^{-3})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{170\times 10^{9} \times 50\times 10^{-6} \times 125\times 10^{-18}}{6\times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{170 \times 50 \times 125 \times 10^{9-6-18}}{6\times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1{,}062{,}500 \times 10^{-15}}{6\times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1.0625\times 10^{-9}}{6\times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&= 1.771\times 10^{-7}\;\text{N·m/rad}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\,k_{\theta} \;\approx\; 1.77 \times 10^{-7}\;\text{N·m/rad} \;=\; 0.177\;\text{µN·m/rad}\,}$$

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Step 1 — Use the stress–rotation relationship derived in Section 3:**
$$\sigma_{max} \;=\; \frac{3\,E\,t}{L}\,\theta$$

**Step 2 — Apply the yield criterion with safety factor:**
$$\sigma_{max} \;\le\; \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{SF} \;=\; \frac{7\times 10^{9}}{2.5} \;=\; 2.8\times 10^{9}\;\text{Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the maximum angle $\theta_{max}$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{max} &= \frac{\sigma_{max}\,L}{3\,E\,t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.8\times 10^{9} \times 1.0\times 10^{-3}}{3 \times 170\times 10^{9} \times 5\times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.8\times 10^{6}}{2.55\times 10^{6}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 1.098\;\text{rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees:**
$$\theta_{max} \;\approx\; 1.098 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \;\approx\; 62.9^{\circ}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small-angle validity check:**
The small-angle linear-stiffness formula $k_{\theta} = Ebt^{3}/(6L)$ is valid only for $\theta \ll 1$ rad (typically $< 10°$). Since $\theta_{max} \approx 63°$ violates this, **the practical maximum rotation in linear operation is limited to ~5–10°**, even though the material can sustain larger angles elastically.

$$\boxed{\,\theta_{max}^{linear} \;\approx\; 5^{\circ}\;\text{to}\;10^{\circ}\;\text{for linear stiffness validity}\,}$$

*(7 marks for full derivation with sanity check)*

---

### Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a parallelogram flexure linear guidance system. Derive the expressions for axial stiffness $k_x$ and lateral stiffness $k_y$ in terms of flexure dimensions. (7 marks)**

**(b) A flexure linear guide is fabricated from spring steel (E = 200 GPa) with two flexure webs of length L = 20 mm, width b = 4 mm, and thickness t = 0.15 mm. Compute the axial stiffness, lateral stiffness, and the first resonance frequency for a moving mass of m = 0.5 kg. Comment on the stiffness anisotropy. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Sketch (describe verbally as required):**
A rectangular frame with two thin flexure webs (top and bottom) connecting two rigid struts (left and right). The left strut is fixed to the ground; the right strut is the moving stage. Force F applied horizontally produces horizontal motion $\delta_x$ while the top and bottom webs bend slightly in bending-mode. Vertical motion is resisted by the much higher bending stiffness of the webs.

**Working Principle:**
- **Along motion axis (x):** The flexure webs deform primarily in **bending**, producing a soft spring — large $\delta_x$ for small $F_x$.
- **Perpendicular to axis (y):** Any transverse force is resisted by the **axial stretching** of the webs, producing a much stiffer spring — small $\delta_y$ for the same $F_y$.
- The parallelogram geometry ensures the moving stage translates **without rotating** (pure linear motion).

**Derivation of $k_x$ (bending-mode axial stiffness):**
Each flexure web behaves as a cantilever of length L in bending under a tip force F. With $I = bt^{3}/12$:
$$\delta_{x} \;=\; \frac{F\,L^{3}}{3\,E\,I} \;=\; \frac{4\,F\,L^{3}}{E\,b\,t^{3}}$$
Two webs in parallel carry the same force, so total stiffness is doubled:
$$\boxed{\,k_{x} \;=\; \frac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{2\,L^{3}}\,}$$

**Derivation of $k_y$ (axial-mode lateral stiffness):**
Each web behaves as an axially loaded bar of area $A = b\,t$:
$$\delta_{y} \;=\; \frac{F\,L}{E\,A} \;=\; \frac{F\,L}{E\,b\,t}$$
Two webs in parallel:
$$\boxed{\,k_{y} \;=\; \frac{2\,E\,b\,t}{L}\,}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for sketch + explanation, 3 for $k_x$ derivation, 2 for $k_y$ derivation)*

---

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:** E = 200 GPa = $2\times 10^{11}$ Pa, L = 0.020 m, b = 0.004 m, t = 0.00015 m, m = 0.5 kg.

**Axial stiffness $k_x$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
k_{x} &= \frac{E\,b\,t^{3}}{2\,L^{3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(2\times 10^{11}) \times (4\times 10^{-3}) \times (1.5\times 10^{-4})^{3}}{2 \times (0.020)^{3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2\times 10^{11} \times 4\times 10^{-3} \times 3.375\times 10^{-12}}{2 \times 8\times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.7\times 10^{-3}}{1.6\times 10^{-5}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 168.75\;\text{N/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Lateral stiffness $k_y$:**
$$\begin{aligned}
k_{y} &= \frac{2\,E\,b\,t}{L} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2 \times 2\times 10^{11} \times 4\times 10^{-3} \times 1.5\times 10^{-4}}{0.020} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2.4\times 10^{5}}{0.020} \\[6pt]
&= 1.2\times 10^{7}\;\text{N/m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Resonance frequency:**
$$\begin{aligned}
f_{n} &= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k_{x}}{m}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{168.75}{0.5}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{337.5} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi} \times 18.37 \\[6pt]
&\approx 2.92\;\text{Hz}
\end{aligned}$$

**Comment on stiffness anisotropy:**
$$\frac{k_{y}}{k_{x}} \;=\; \frac{1.2\times 10^{7}}{168.75} \;\approx\; 7.1\times 10^{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The lateral stiffness is approximately **71,000 times higher** than the axial stiffness. This confirms the parallelogram flexure's role as a high-quality linear guide: it permits motion along x while strongly rejecting motion along y, z, and rotations. The very low first resonance (≈ 3 Hz), however, limits the achievable closed-loop bandwidth — a real engineering trade-off.

*(7 marks: 2 for $k_x$, 2 for $k_y$, 1 for $f_n$, 2 for anisotropy commentary)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Wrong formula selection.** Students frequently write $k_{\theta} = Ebt^3 / L^3$ (the translational bending stiffness) when they need $k_{\theta} = Ebt^3 / (6L)$ (the rotational stiffness). The cubic denominator is **L (not $L^3$)** for the rotational case. **Always check: am I computing a force/displacement or a moment/rotation?**
> 2. **Mixing macro- and micro-formulas.** The monolithic cross-pivot formula has an additional factor of ~4 compared to the strip version due to end conditions. Writing the strip formula for a monolithic device loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Ignoring units.** KTU strictly enforces SI units in the final numerical answer. Always write the final $k$ value in N/m or N·m/rad explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting the safety factor.** When asked for the "maximum allowable" rotation, students compute the yield angle without applying the safety factor. Always state and apply SF.
> 5. **Confusing parallelogram flexure with telescoping flexure.** The two have inverted stiffness anisotropy. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cross Pivot / Cross Strip / Monolithic Cross Pivot / Flexure Linear Guides:**
> - **Cross pivot** = two orthogonal strips → 1 rotational DOF (yaw), 5 constrained.
> - **Monolithic cross pivot** = single-block version → same DOF, but no joint, no hysteresis, no assembly.
> - **Rotational stiffness:** $k_{\theta} = E\,b\,t^{3}/(6\,L)$.
> - **Lateral stiffness of cross pivot:** $k_{x,y} = 2\,E\,b\,t^{3}/L^{3}$.
> - **Pitch/yaw parasitic stiffness:** $k_{\phi} = G\,b\,t^{3}/(3\,L)$.
> - **Maximum stress:** $\sigma_{max} = (3\,E\,t/L)\,\theta$ → use this to bound the operating angle.
> - **Parallelogram flexure linear guide:** 1 translation DOF, 5 constrained. Stiffness anisotropy ratio $R = t^2/L^2$.
> - **First resonance frequency:** $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k_x/m}$.
> - **Manufacturing routes:** WEDM (macro), DRIE (MEMS), laser micro-machining (mid-scale).
> - **Trade-off:** Lower stiffness (for larger stroke) ⇒ lower resonance ⇒ lower closed-loop bandwidth.
> - **Material choice:** Low E for soft flexures (polymers); high E for stiff flexures (silicon, steel).
> - **Safety factor:** Always use $SF \in [2, 4]$ for KTU design calculations.
> - **Strain check:** Ensure $\varepsilon = t\,\theta/(2L) < \varepsilon_{yield}/SF$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Microrobotics**, an **Actuator** is defined as a transduction element that converts a non-mechanical input energy domain (electrical, thermal, chemical, magnetic, or optical) into a precisely controlled mechanical output — typically in the form of **displacement**, **force**, **torque**, or **vibration** — at the micro or nano scale. Actuators serve as the "muscles" of a microrobot, providing the physical work output required for locomotion, manipulation, gripping, propulsion, and structural reconfiguration.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, an actuator is classified as a **smart material-based or MEMS-based transducer** characterized by the energy conversion metric called **Work Density (W/v)** measured in **J/m³**, and the response metric called **Bandwidth (Hz)** — both being critical figures of merit at the microscale.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Actuators are the *third* leg of the microrobot functional triad: **Sensors → Controller → Actuators**. Without actuators, a microrobot is mechanically inert.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of a microrobot like a **human body shrunk down to the size of a dust particle**. If the microrobot is the body, then:
- The **sensors** are its eyes, ears, and skin (perception).
- The **controller** is its brain (decision-making).
- The **actuators** are its **muscles and tendons** (action).

A regular robot might use a bulky DC motor weighing hundreds of grams to rotate a wheel. A microrobot — which might weigh less than **1 milligram** — cannot use such macroscopic motors. It needs a new kind of "muscle" that is:
- Tiny (sub-millimeter dimensions).
- Lightweight (micrograms).
- Energy-efficient (operating on **mW to µW** power levels).
- Fast (kHz range response).

This is why microrobotic actuators rely on **smart materials** like piezoelectric ceramics, shape memory alloys, conducting polymers, and electrostatic combs — all of which change shape or force when electrically stimulated.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Microrobotic Range | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Work Density | $W_v$ | $10^3$ to $10^7$ J/m³ | Energy per unit volume |
| Stroke | $s$ | $0.1$ µm to $1$ mm | Maximum displacement |
| Force | $F$ | µN to mN | Output mechanical force |
| Bandwidth | $f_b$ | $1$ Hz to $100$ kHz | Speed of response |
| Power Consumption | $P$ | µW to mW | Energy efficiency |
| Operating Voltage | $V$ | $1$ V to $200$ V | Drive requirement |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** At the microscale, **surface forces** (electrostatic, van der Waals, surface tension) dominate over **volume forces** (inertia, weight). This is why electrostatic and surface-tension actuators become *more efficient* as size shrinks — a complete inversion of macroscale intuition.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Conventional Actuators Fail at the Microscale

To appreciate why microrobotic actuators are a separate class of devices, we must examine the **scaling laws**. When we scale a device by a factor $k$ (where $k < 1$ for miniaturization), every physical quantity scales predictably.

**Scaling Laws Summary:**

- **Length** $L$ scales as $k \cdot L_0$
- **Area** $A$ scales as $k^2 \cdot A_0$
- **Volume** $V$ scales as $k^3 \cdot V_0$
- **Force (volume-based, e.g., magnetic)** scales as $k^3 \cdot F_0$
- **Force (surface-based, e.g., electrostatic)** scales as $k^2 \cdot F_0$
- **Mass** scales as $k^3 \cdot m_0$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Scaling Paradox:** Magnetic force $\propto V$ but electrostatic force $\propto A$. When we miniaturize ($k \to 0$), magnetic force dies **faster** than electrostatic force. This is why **electrostatic** actuators dominate MEMS, while **magnetic** actuators are used in larger meso-scale systems.

### 2.2 Functional Classification of Microrobotic Actuators

Microrobotic actuators can be broadly classified based on their **energy conversion mechanism**:

**A. Based on Energy Input Domain**
- **Electrostatic Actuators** — use Coulomb force between charged plates.
- **Piezoelectric Actuators** — use converse piezoelectric effect.
- **Magnetic Actuators** — use Lorentz force or magnetostriction.
- **Thermal Actuators** — use differential thermal expansion.
- **Chemical Actuators** — use pH or ionic concentration change.
- **Optical Actuators** — use photothermal or photomechanical effects.
- **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA) Actuators** — use phase transformation.
- **Dielectric Elastomer Actuators (DEA)** — use Maxwell stress on compliant electrodes.

**B. Based on Output Motion Type**
- **Linear Actuators** — produce translational stroke.
- **Rotational Actuators** — produce angular displacement.
- **Bending Actuators** — produce flexural deformation (e.g., bimorph cantilevers).
- **Propulsion Actuators** — produce oscillatory motion for fluid swimming.

**C. Based on Power Modality**
- **Active Actuators** — require continuous external power.
- **Passive/Latchable Actuators** — maintain state without power (e.g., SMA).

### 2.3 The Four Golden Performance Metrics

Every KTU examiner expects students to evaluate any actuator against these four metrics:

1. **Work Density ($W_v$):** Total mechanical work output per unit volume.
2. **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Maximum deformation $\Delta L / L$.
3. **Bandwidth ($f_b$):** Highest frequency of cyclic operation.
4. **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of mechanical output power to electrical input power.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** There is no single "best" actuator. Piezoelectric has high bandwidth but low strain (~0.1%). Shape memory alloy has high strain (~8%) but low bandwidth (~1 Hz). Engineers must match the actuator to the application.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| $F_{elec} = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g^2}$ | Electrostatic force between parallel plates | N | Comb drives, gap-closing actuators |
| $F_{mag} = B I L \sin\theta$ | Lorentz force on current-carrying conductor | N | Magnetic micro-actuators |
| $s_{piezo} = d_{33} \cdot V$ | Piezoelectric displacement | m | Stack/bimorph actuators |
| $W_v = \dfrac{F \cdot s}{V_{actuator}}$ | Volumetric work density | J/m³ | Energy comparison |
| $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Resonant frequency of mass-spring system | Hz | Bandwidth limit |
| $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Energy conversion efficiency | % | System design |
| $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Stress-strain relation (Hooke's law) | Pa | Structural analysis |
| $P_{flow} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho v^3 A$ | Power required for fluid locomotion | W | Swimming microrobots |
| $\varepsilon_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermal expansion strain | dimensionless | Thermal bimorphs |
| $T_{Curie} = $ material property | Temperature above which piezoelectricity vanishes | K | Piezo design constraint |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants Students Must Memorize:**
> - Permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
> - Vacuum permeability $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m
> - Standard gravity $g = 9.81$ m/s²
> - Speed of light in vacuum $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (used in optical actuator analysis)

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Microrobotic actuators are not academic curiosities — they are deployed in:
- **Medical Microrobotics:** Targeted drug delivery capsules that swim through blood vessels.
- **Surgical Robotics:** Catheter steering, endoscopic grippers with µm precision.
- **Microassembly:** Robotic fingers that handle components smaller than a grain of sand.
- **In-Vivo Sensing:** Untethered pills with active locomotion for GI tract imaging.
- **Lab-on-Chip Systems:** Active micro-valves and micro-pumps for fluid control.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Electrostatic Force on a Parallel-Plate Capacitor

The parallel-plate electrostatic actuator is the **workhorse of MEMS** and almost always appears in KTU derivations. We derive the force step-by-step.

**Starting Point:** Consider two parallel conductive plates of area $A$, separated by a gap $g$, with a voltage $V$ applied across them. The capacitor stores energy in the electric field.

**Step 1 — Capacitance Expression**

The capacitance of a parallel-plate geometry is given by:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the permittivity of free space.

**Step 2 — Energy Stored in the Capacitor**

The electrical energy stored in the electric field of the capacitor is:

$$U_{elec} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g} V^2$$

**Step 3 — Principle of Virtual Work**

In an electrostatic system, the mechanical force is the **negative gradient of stored energy with respect to displacement**. If we assume the voltage $V$ is held constant (a charge-controlled system is treated separately), then the attractive force between the plates is:

$$F_{elec} = -\frac{dU_{elec}}{dg}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Derivative**

Differentiating $U_{elec}$ with respect to the gap $g$:

$$\frac{dU_{elec}}{dg} = \frac{d}{dg}\left(\frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g}\right) = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 A V^2 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{g^2}\right) = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2g^2}$$

**Step 5 — Final Force Expression**

Applying the negative sign from the virtual work principle:

$$F_{elec} = -\left(-\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2g^2}\right) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{2 g^2}$$

$$\boxed{F_{elec} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The force is *attractive* (plates pull together), and it scales with $A$ (area) but inversely with $g^2$ (gap squared). This is why **narrow gaps** dramatically increase force — a key reason why comb-finger actuators with sub-micron gaps are so effective.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Resonant Frequency of a Piezoelectric Cantilever

A cantilever beam actuator is widely used in microrobotics for vibration-based propulsion (e.g., swimming microrobots). Its resonant frequency determines the maximum operating bandwidth.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Mass-Spring Model**

A cantilever of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t$, and density $\rho$ behaves as a spring-mass system where:

- **Effective stiffness:** $k = \dfrac{3 E I}{L^3}$
- **Effective mass (using Rayleigh's approximation):** $m_{eff} = 0.24 \, \rho \, w \, t \, L$

where $E$ is Young's modulus and $I = \dfrac{w t^3}{12}$ is the area moment of inertia.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Moment of Inertia**

$$k = \frac{3 E}{L^3} \cdot \frac{w t^3}{12} = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

**Step 3 — Resonant Frequency Formula**

The fundamental resonant frequency of a mass-spring system is:

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $k$ and $m_{eff}$**

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{\dfrac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}}{0.24 \, \rho \, w \, t \, L}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by Canceling Common Terms**

The width $w$ cancels from numerator and denominator:

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{4 L^3 \cdot 0.24 \, \rho \, L}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{E t^2}{0.96 \, \rho \, L^4}}$$

$$\boxed{f_{res} = \frac{t}{2\pi L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{0.96 \, \rho}} \approx \frac{0.162 \, t}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Implication:** To **increase bandwidth**, the engineer should *decrease length* $L$ and *increase thickness* $t$. However, decreasing $L$ also decreases the tip displacement, creating a fundamental **bandwidth-stroke trade-off** — a favorite KTU question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Work Density Comparison

A common KTU numerical asks students to **compare work densities** of two actuators. The work density is defined as mechanical work per unit actuator volume:

$$W_v = \frac{F \cdot s}{V_{actuator}}$$

**Numerical Example:** Compare the work density of an electrostatic comb drive and a piezoelectric stack.

**Given Data:**
- **Electrostatic:** $F = 10$ µN, $s = 5$ µm, $V_{actuator} = 1$ mm³ = $10^{-9}$ m³
- **Piezoelectric:** $F = 1$ N, $s = 10$ µm, $V_{actuator} = 50$ mm³ = $5 \times 10^{-8}$ m³

**Solution — Electrostatic:**

$$W_v^{elec} = \frac{(10 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-6})}{10^{-9}} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-11}}{10^{-9}} = 0.05 \text{ J/m}^3$$

**Solution — Piezoelectric:**

$$W_v^{piezo} = \frac{(1) \cdot (10 \times 10^{-6})}{5 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{10^{-5}}{5 \times 10^{-8}} = 200 \text{ J/m}^3$$

**Comparison:** $W_v^{piezo} / W_v^{elec} = 200 / 0.05 = 4000$. The piezoelectric actuator delivers **4000 times** more work density.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Electrostatic actuators are favored when **low power, fast response, and CMOS integration** are needed. Piezoelectric actuators are favored when **high force and high energy per stroke** are needed. The choice is always application-driven.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define an actuator in the context of microrobotics. List any four common types of microrobotic actuators.*

**Model Answer:**
An **actuator** is a transduction device that converts a non-mechanical input energy form (electrical, thermal, chemical, magnetic, or optical) into a controlled mechanical output such as displacement, force, or torque at the micro/nano scale. It is the "muscle" of the microrobot, enabling locomotion, manipulation, and structural reconfiguration.

**Four common types:**
1. **Electrostatic** actuators (e.g., comb drives)
2. **Piezoelectric** actuators (e.g., bimorph cantilevers)
3. **Shape Memory Alloy (SMA)** actuators (e.g., Nitinol wires)
4. **Magnetic** actuators (e.g., Lorentz-force micro-coils)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the four key performance metrics used to evaluate a microrobotic actuator.*

**Model Answer:**
The four key performance metrics are:
1. **Work Density ($W_v$):** Mechanical work output per unit actuator volume, measured in J/m³. Indicates energy compactness.
2. **Strain ($\varepsilon$):** Maximum relative deformation $\Delta L / L$. Indicates the amplitude of motion possible.
3. **Bandwidth ($f_b$):** Maximum frequency of cyclic operation, in Hz. Indicates speed of response.
4. **Efficiency ($\eta$):** Ratio of mechanical output power to electrical input power, in %. Indicates energy economy.

These metrics are interlinked and a designer must trade off one against the other.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of an **electrostatic comb-drive actuator** used in microrobotics. Derive the expression for the electrostatic force generated. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Compare electrostatic and piezoelectric actuators in terms of work density, bandwidth, strain, and CMOS compatibility. State two practical applications of each. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

A **comb-drive actuator** consists of two interdigitated sets of finger electrodes — one set is fixed to the substrate (stator) and the other is suspended (shuttle/runner). When a voltage $V$ is applied between the two sets, an electric field is established across the narrow gaps between adjacent fingers.

**Schematic Description:** Each finger pair behaves as a parallel-plate capacitor. The narrow gap (typically 1–5 µm) between adjacent fingers produces a strong electric field even at moderate voltages. The resulting attractive force between the fingers pulls the shuttle sideways, generating a linear displacement.

**Derivation of Force:**

The capacitance of a comb drive with $N$ finger pairs, each of overlap length $L_f$ and gap $g$, and finger thickness $t$ (in the out-of-plane direction):

$$C = N \cdot \frac{2 \, \varepsilon_0 \, t \, L_f}{g}$$

(The factor 2 accounts for the two gaps per finger pair.)

**Energy stored:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2 N \varepsilon_0 t L_f}{g} \cdot V^2 = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 t L_f V^2}{g}$$

**Force using virtual work** (differentiation with respect to displacement $x$ in the lateral direction; note that $g$ does not change as fingers slide laterally):

$$F = \frac{dU}{dx} = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 t V^2}{g} \cdot \frac{dL_f}{dx}$$

Since $\dfrac{dL_f}{dx} = 1$ (each unit of lateral motion increases overlap by 1 unit):

$$\boxed{F_{comb} = \frac{N \, \varepsilon_0 \, t \, V^2}{g}}$$

**Key Observation:** Unlike the parallel-plate case, the comb-drive force is **independent of the lateral displacement $x$** — this is a unique advantage that produces a *constant-force* actuator, ideal for long-stroke applications.

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | Electrostatic | Piezoelectric |
|---|---|---|
| Work Density | $0.01$ – $0.1$ J/m³ | $100$ – $1000$ J/m³ |
| Bandwidth | up to MHz | up to 100 kHz |
| Strain | up to 50% (in DEA) | ~0.1% to 0.2% |
| CMOS Compatibility | Excellent (silicon-based) | Moderate (requires special materials) |
| Drive Voltage | 5 – 100 V | 10 – 200 V |
| Power Consumption | Very low (capacitive) | Low to moderate |

**Applications of Electrostatic Actuators:**
1. Micromirrors in micro-optical systems (e.g., DLP projectors).
2. RF MEMS switches in telecommunication devices.

**Applications of Piezoelectric Actuators:**
1. Inkjet printer heads (thermal-piezo type).
2. Ultrasound transducers in medical imaging.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **scaling laws** in microrobotics. Show mathematically why surface forces dominate volume forces at the microscale. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A piezoelectric cantilever actuator for a swimming microrobot has the following specifications: length $L = 5$ mm, thickness $t = 50$ µm, width $w = 1$ mm, density $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, Young's modulus $E = 70$ GPa. Calculate the fundamental resonant frequency. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution for (a):**

When a device is miniaturized by a uniform scaling factor $k$ (with $k < 1$ for shrinking), all physical dimensions transform:

$$L \to kL, \quad A \to k^2 A, \quad V \to k^3 V, \quad m \to k^3 m$$

**Volume-Based Forces (e.g., Magnetic):**

Magnetic force on a current-carrying conductor $F = BIL$ scales as:

$$F_{mag} \propto kL \cdot kL \cdot kL = k^3 F_0$$

This is a **cubic** dependence on $k$.

**Surface-Based Forces (e.g., Electrostatic):**

Electrostatic force on a capacitor plate $F = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g^2}$ scales as:

$$F_{elec} \propto k^2 A_0 \cdot V^2 / (k^2 g_0^2) = k^2 F_0 \cdot (V^2 / V_0^2)$$

If voltage is held constant, the force scales as $k^2$.

**Inertial Force (Body Force):**

Inertial force $F = ma$ scales with mass as $k^3$, while gravitational weight also scales as $k^3$. Therefore, the *ratio* of inertia to surface force is:

$$\frac{F_{inertial}}{F_{surface}} = \frac{k^3 m_0 a}{k^2 F_0} = k \cdot \frac{m_0 a}{F_0}$$

As $k \to 0$, this ratio $\to 0$, meaning **inertia becomes negligible** compared to surface forces.

**Reynolds Number Collapse:**

The Reynolds number for a microrobot of characteristic length $L$ moving at velocity $v$ through a fluid of viscosity $\mu$ and density $\rho_f$:

$$Re = \frac{\rho_f v L}{\mu}$$

As $L \to 0$, $Re \to 0$, meaning the flow regime becomes **viscosity-dominated (Stokes flow)**. This is why bacteria and microswimmers cannot use inertial propulsion — they must use *non-reciprocal* motion (scallop theorem).

**Conclusion:** At the microscale, designers must rethink actuation strategies; electrostatic, surface-tension, and viscous-drag actuators become favorable, while inertial and gravitational mechanisms lose relevance.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $L = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $t = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $w = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\rho = 7800$ kg/m³, $E = 70 \times 10^9$ Pa.

**Step 1 — Effective Stiffness**

$$k = \frac{E w t^3}{4 L^3}$$

Computing each term:
- $w t^3 = (10^{-3}) \cdot (50 \times 10^{-6})^3 = 10^{-3} \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-13} = 1.25 \times 10^{-16}$ m⁴
- $4 L^3 = 4 \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 4 \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-7} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ m³
- $E w t^3 = 70 \times 10^9 \cdot 1.25 \times 10^{-16} = 8.75 \times 10^{-6}$ N·m²

$$k = \frac{8.75 \times 10^{-6}}{5 \times 10^{-7}} = 17.5 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Mass**

$$m_{eff} = 0.24 \, \rho \, w \, t \, L$$

- $\rho \cdot w \cdot t \cdot L = 7800 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 50 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-3} = 7800 \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-10} = 1.95 \times 10^{-6}$ kg

$$m_{eff} = 0.24 \cdot 1.95 \times 10^{-6} = 4.68 \times 10^{-7} \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3 — Resonant Frequency**

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{17.5}{4.68 \times 10^{-7}}}$$

$$\frac{k}{m_{eff}} = \frac{17.5}{4.68 \times 10^{-7}} = 3.739 \times 10^7 \text{ s}^{-2}$$

$$\sqrt{3.739 \times 10^7} = 6114.7 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_{res} = \frac{6114.7}{2\pi} = \frac{6114.7}{6.2832} = 973.2 \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{f_{res} \approx 973 \text{ Hz}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This microrobot can oscillate its tail at nearly 1 kHz, which is the appropriate range for low-Reynolds-number swimming in water. Faster oscillation (toward 10 kHz) would require a stiffer or shorter cantilever.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in numerical problems — KTU examiners *strictly* deduct 1 mark if the final answer lacks a unit. Always write "J/m³" or "Hz" or "N".
> 2. **Confusing strain and stress** — Strain $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless; stress $\sigma$ is in Pa. Many students swap them in formula substitutions.
> 3. **Wrong area moment of inertia** for cantilevers — the rectangular cross-section formula is $I = wt^3/12$, NOT $wt^3/3$. The /3 belongs to the tip-loaded deflection formula, not $I$.
> 4. **Neglecting the constant-voltage vs constant-charge distinction** in electrostatic problems — KTU may specifically ask for one of the two force expressions. The constant-voltage form has the $1/2$; the constant-charge form does not.
> 5. **Writing "piezoelectric" instead of "piezoelectric"** consistently — and remember: the *direct* piezoelectric effect is sensing; the *converse* effect is actuation.
> 6. **Forgetting to state assumptions** — e.g., "assuming parallel-plate approximation," "neglecting fringing fields," "assuming isothermal operation." These earn easy marks and protect you from examiner disputes.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Actuator definition:** Transducer from non-mechanical to mechanical energy at micro/nano scale.
- **Core triad:** Sensors → Controller → Actuators.
- **Scaling law truth:** Surface forces $\propto k^2$; volume forces $\propto k^3$. Therefore, **electrostatic actuators become more efficient** as devices shrink, while magnetic actuators lose efficiency.
- **Four golden metrics:** Work density, Strain, Bandwidth, Efficiency.
- **Electrostatic force (parallel plate):** $F = \dfrac{1}{2} \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^2}{g^2}$
- **Comb-drive force:** $F = \dfrac{N \varepsilon_0 t V^2}{g}$ — independent of lateral displacement.
- **Piezoelectric displacement:** $s = d_{33} V$
- **Resonant frequency formula:** $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$
- **Eight major actuator types:** Electrostatic, Piezoelectric, Magnetic, Thermal, SMA, Chemical, Optical, Dielectric Elastomer.
- **Curie temperature** sets the upper limit for piezoelectric operation.
- **Stall force vs no-load speed:** The force-velocity trade-off applies to all actuators including microrobotic ones.
- **Always quote units** in numerical answers.
- **Always state assumptions** in derivations.
- **Always cite the energy domain** (electrical, thermal, etc.) when classifying an actuator.
- **KTU favorite question pattern:** "Compare X and Y actuator" — learn the comparison table by heart.
- **Microscale rule of thumb:** If a phenomenon scales with area, it dominates at small sizes. If it scales with volume, it dominates at large sizes.
- **One-line mantra:** *"At the microscale, electric fields beat magnetic fields, viscosity beats inertia, and surface tension beats gravity."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design Principles of Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Design Principles of Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Microrobotics (Module 3), an **actuator** is defined as a **transducer element that converts a non-mechanical input energy form** — such as electrical, magnetic, thermal, chemical, or optical energy — **into a controlled mechanical output** (force, displacement, or strain) at the micro-scale (typically feature sizes in the range **1 µm to 1 mm**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> A *micro-actuator* is distinguished from a *macro-actuator* not merely by geometry, but by the **dominance of surface-related physical phenomena** (electrostatic forces, surface tension, van der Waals forces, viscous drag) over volume-related phenomena (inertia, gravity, electromagnetic bulk forces). The "design principles" of micro-actuators are fundamentally a set of **scaling laws** that govern how forces, energies, and response times evolve when a device is miniaturized by a factor *k* (where *k* < 1).

## 1.2 The Five Functional Requirements of a Micro-Actuator

According to the KTU syllabus framework for Module 3, every micro-actuator design must simultaneously satisfy:

1. **Force / Torque Generation** (≥ µN to mN range)
2. **Stroke / Displacement** (sub-µm to mm range)
3. **Bandwidth / Response Time** (Hz to MHz range)
4. **Power Efficiency / Energy Density** (J/m³)
5. **Fabrication Compatibility** (CMOS, MEMS, polymer, LIGA, etc.)

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Shrinking Muscle" Thought Experiment

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "Imagine You Are Shrinking"**
> Picture yourself as a mechanical engineer trying to build a tiny "robot muscle." If you shrink a normal electric motor by a factor of 1000 in every direction, you might assume the output shrinks uniformly. **It does not.**
> - The **surface area** of the motor's copper windings shrinks by a factor of **1,000,000** (∝ L²), but the **mass** of the iron core shrinks by a factor of **1,000,000,000** (∝ L³).
> - This means the **mass-specific force output** actually **increases by 1000×** at smaller scales for surface-dominated actuation.
> - However, the **absolute force** collapses, and **friction** (surface phenomenon) becomes dominant over **inertia** (volume phenomenon) — your "muscle" can lift a million times its own weight, but it cannot shake itself loose from the surface it sits on.
> - This counterintuitive behavior is the entire reason that electrostatic, surface-acoustic, and bio-inspired (flagellar) actuators dominate at the micro-scale, while the electromagnetic motors that rule our macro-world become impractical below ~1 mm.

## 1.4 Why Scaling Laws Are the Heart of Micro-Actuator Design

| Parameter | Macro Scale (≈ cm) | Micro Scale (≈ 100 µm) | Dominant Physics |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inertia vs. Friction** | Inertia dominates | Friction dominates | Viscous regime |
| **Gravity vs. Surface Tension** | Gravity dominates | Surface tension dominates | Capillary regime |
| **Magnetic vs. Electrostatic Force** | Magnetic favored | Electrostatic favored | Coulomb regime |
| **Heat Dissipation** | Easy (large area) | Hard (trapped heat) | Thermal regime |

## 1.5 The Three Universal Design Axioms (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Axioms Every Micro-Actuator Must Obey**
> 1. **Surface Axiom** — All input/output ports (electrodes, heat sinks, fluid contacts) scale as **L²**, so design must exploit surface phenomena.
> 2. **Volume Axiom** — All energy-storage volumes scale as **L³**, so volumetric energy density **must remain constant or improve** upon miniaturization, or the actuator becomes useless.
> 3. **Coupling Axiom** — The energy-conversion mechanism must be **inversely coupled to size** for at least one parameter (e.g., electrostatic force ∝ 1/d²), so that the loss of L² area is compensated by an increasing field.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Geometric Foundation of Scaling

When a linear dimension **L** is reduced by a scaling factor **k** (k < 1 for miniaturization), the geometric properties transform as:

| Quantity | Scaling Relationship | Order |
|---|---|---|
| Length | L′ = k·L | k¹ |
| Surface Area | A′ = k²·A | k² |
| Volume | V′ = k³·V | k³ |
| Mass (constant ρ) | m′ = k³·m | k³ |
| Moment of Inertia | I′ = k⁵·I | k⁵ |
| Surface-to-Volume Ratio | (A/V)′ = k⁻¹·(A/V) | **k⁻¹** |

> **Engineering Insight:** The surface-to-volume ratio scales as **1/L**. This single relationship explains why **friction, heat dissipation, electrostatic attraction, and surface tension** all become disproportionately powerful at small scales.

## 2.2 The Four Canonical Micro-Actuator Families

### 2.2.1 Electrostatic Actuators
Operate on **Coulomb attraction** between oppositely charged conductors.

- **Force origin:** Maxwell stress on a dielectric gap.
- **Favorable scaling:** Force ∝ A = L² (capacitor plate area).
- **Limitation:** Required voltage scales inversely with gap; micro-gaps allow low-voltage operation (typically **5–100 V**).

### 2.2.2 Piezoelectric Actuators
Operate on the **converse piezoelectric effect**: applied electric field → mechanical strain in a non-centrosymmetric crystal (e.g., PZT, quartz, AlN).

- **Force origin:** Dipole alignment in unit cells.
- **Favorable scaling:** Strain is field-limited (≈ 0.1 % for PZT); displacement scales with **L**, so stroke decreases linearly.
- **Limitation:** Very small absolute stroke; usually stacked or amplified mechanically.

### 2.2.3 Magnetic (Electromagnetic / Magnetostrictive) Actuators
Operate on **Lorentz force** (F = IL × B) or **magnetostriction** (Terfenol-D, Galfenol).

- **Force origin:** Current-carrying conductor in magnetic field, or field-induced strain in magnetostrictive material.
- **Unfavorable scaling:** Coil resistance grows, current density limits heat dissipation; **mass-specific force degrades as k**.

### 2.2.4 Thermal / Phase-Change Actuators (incl. SMA, bimorph, shape-memory)
Operate on **thermal expansion** or **martensite → austenite phase transition**.

- **Force origin:** Differential thermal expansion coefficient Δα·ΔT·E·A.
- **Favorable scaling:** Force ∝ A = L², but cooling time scales as L²/α_thermal (slow).
- **Limitation:** Low bandwidth (< 100 Hz); high power consumption.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table for any Part-A or Part-B numerical in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_{\text{elec}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,\dfrac{A\,V^{2}}{d^{2}}$ | Parallel-plate electrostatic force | N |
| 2 | $C \;=\; \dfrac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d}$ | Parallel-plate capacitance | F |
| 3 | $U_{\text{elec}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2}\,C\,V^{2}$ | Stored electrostatic energy | J |
| 4 | $f_{\text{res}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Resonant frequency of a spring-mass micro-actuator | Hz |
| 5 | $k_{\text{spring}} \;=\; \dfrac{E\,A}{L}$ | Axial stiffness of a beam/rod | N/m |
| 6 | $F_{\text{mag, Lorentz}} \;=\; B\,I\,L$ | Lorentz force on a current-carrying wire | N |
| 7 | $F_{\text{mag, max}} \;=\; \dfrac{B^{2}\,A}{2\,\mu_{0}}$ | Max magnetic pressure force | N |
| 8 | $S_{\text{piezo}} \;=\; d_{33}\,E$ | Piezoelectric strain (d₃₃ mode) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F_{\text{piezo}} \;=\; d_{33}\,A\,E\,Y$ | Piezoelectric blocked force | N |
| 10 | $\Delta L_{\text{thermal}} \;=\; \alpha\,L\,\Delta T$ | Thermal expansion displacement | m |
| 11 | $F_{\text{thermal}} \;=\; E\,A\,\alpha\,\Delta T$ | Thermal expansion force (blocked) | N |
| 12 | $\tau_{\text{cool}} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho\,c_{p}\,L^{2}}{k_{\text{th}}}$ | Thermal cooling time constant | s |
| 13 | $\eta_{\text{actuator}} \;=\; \dfrac{\text{mechanical power out}}{\text{electrical power in}}$ | Actuator energy conversion efficiency | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\rho_{E,\text{vol}} \;=\; \dfrac{F \cdot \Delta L}{V_{\text{active}}}$ | Volumetric work density | J/m³ |
| 15 | $R_{e,\text{micro}} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho\,v\,L}{\mu} \;<\; 1$ | Reynolds number in microfluidic regime | dimensionless |

> **Note on notation:** Throughout the KTU textbook, vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity. For example, **$\vert F \vert$** for the magnitude of force.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Micro-surgical robots** (e.g., intraocular probes) use **piezoelectric** actuators because they offer sub-µm precision and high force.
- **Optical MEMS** (DLP mirror arrays, optical switches) use **electrostatic** actuators because they are fast, low-power, and CMOS-compatible.
- **Micro-propulsion in fluid** (flagellar microrobots) use **magnetic** actuation from external Helmholtz coils because no onboard power is needed.
- **Cellular-scale drug-delivery capsules** use **thermal/SMA** actuators because they can be triggered by a single infrared pulse.

## 2.5 The Design Trade-Off Pentagon

> [!NOTE]
> Every KTU 2024 problem on this topic tests whether the student recognizes that **no single actuator wins on all five axes**. The famous trade-off triangle:
>
> **Force × Stroke × Bandwidth = constant**
>
> holds for almost all physical actuators, derived directly from the conservation of energy and the limits imposed by material yield strength and breakdown fields.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Surface-to-Volume Ratio Scaling (Foundation for all micro-actuator design)

**Starting premise:** Consider a cube of side length **L** with density **ρ**.

**Step 1 — Express volume:**
$$V \;=\; L^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Express surface area:**
$$A_{\text{surf}} \;=\; 6\,L^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Form the surface-to-volume ratio:**
$$\frac{A_{\text{surf}}}{V} \;=\; \frac{6\,L^{2}}{L^{3}} \;=\; \frac{6}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Apply scaling factor k (new length L′ = k·L):**
$$\frac{A'_{\text{surf}}}{V'} \;=\; \frac{6}{L'} \;=\; \frac{6}{k\,L} \;=\; \frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{6}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** When we miniaturize (k < 1), the surface-to-volume ratio **increases by 1/k**. A 1 mm cube has A/V = 6000 m⁻¹; a 1 µm cube has A/V = 6 × 10⁶ m⁻¹ — **a 1000-fold increase**.

> **Physical meaning:** Every square meter of surface now corresponds to only one-millionth of a cubic meter of volume. Surface forces dominate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electrostatic Actuator Scaling Laws (Why electrostatic wins at micro-scale)

**Starting premise:** A parallel-plate electrostatic actuator with plate area **A**, gap **d**, dielectric constant **εᵣ**, and applied voltage **V**.

**Step 1 — Capacitance expression:**
$$C \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Force from energy derivative F = +∂U/∂d (where U = ½CV²):**
$$F_{\text{elec}} \;=\; \frac{\partial U}{\partial d} \;=\; \frac{\partial}{\partial d}\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}A}{d}\cdot V^{2}\right)$$

$$F_{\text{elec}} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,\frac{V^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

The negative sign indicates **attractive** force; magnitude is taken for design.

**Step 3 — Identify breakdown-limited voltage:** The electric field **E = V/d** is limited by dielectric breakdown: **E_max ≈ 3 × 10⁶ V/m for air, ≈ 10⁸ V/m for thin SiO₂**.

So the maximum usable voltage is:
$$V_{\max} \;=\; E_{\max}\cdot d$$

**Step 4 — Substitute V_max into the force expression:**
$$F_{\max} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,E_{\max}^{2}$$

Since **E_max is a material constant**, the maximum force scales **only with area**:
$$F_{\max} \;\propto\; L^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Compute mass-specific force (force ÷ mass):**
$$\frac{F_{\max}}{m} \;\propto\; \frac{L^{2}}{L^{3}} \;=\; \frac{1}{L}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{F'_{\max}}{m'} \;=\; \frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{F_{\max}}{m}\;}$$

When we scale down by k = 0.001, the **force-per-unit-mass improves by 1000×**. This is the foundational reason **electrostatic actuation becomes more efficient at smaller scales**.

**Step 7 — Energy density check (critical for KTU):**
$$U_{\max} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,C\,V^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}A}{d}\cdot(E_{\max}\,d)^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,d\,E_{\max}^{2}$$

Volume of active region:
$$V_{\text{active}} \;=\; A\,d$$

So:
$$\rho_{E,\text{vol}} \;=\; \frac{U_{\max}}{V_{\text{active}}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\,\varepsilon_{r}\,E_{\max}^{2} \;=\; \text{constant}$$

$$\boxed{\;\rho_{E,\text{vol}} \;\text{is independent of size}\;}$$

> **Conclusion:** Electrostatic actuators preserve their **volumetric energy density** at all scales — they are *scale-invariant* in energy storage. This is the deepest reason they dominate MEMS.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Magnetic Actuator Scaling (Why magnetic loses at micro-scale)

**Starting premise:** A solenoid of length **L**, **N** turns of wire of diameter **d_w**, carrying current **I**, producing field **B** across a gap of length **g**, acting on a plate of area **A**.

**Step 1 — Coil field (Ampère's Law, long solenoid):**
$$B \;=\; \mu_{0}\,\frac{N\,I}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Number of turns scales with wire diameter:**
$$N \;=\; \frac{L}{d_{w}} \;\propto\; \frac{L}{d_{w}}$$

**Step 3 — Current scales with wire cross-section (limited by current density J):**
$$I \;=\; J\cdot A_{w} \;=\; J\cdot\frac{\pi\,d_{w}^{2}}{4} \;\propto\; d_{w}^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into B:**
$$B \;\propto\; \frac{(L/d_{w})\cdot d_{w}^{2}}{g} \;=\; \frac{L\cdot d_{w}}{g}$$

If we hold the ratio d_w / g constant upon scaling:
$$B \;\propto\; L$$

**Step 5 — Magnetic pressure force:**
$$F_{\max} \;=\; \frac{B^{2}\,A}{2\,\mu_{0}} \;\propto\; L^{2}\cdot L^{2} \;=\; L^{4}$$

**Step 6 — But the maximum flux density is bounded by material saturation:**
$$B_{\max} \approx 1\text{–}2\,\text{T} \quad (\text{permanent magnet / iron core limit})$$

So practically:
$$F_{\max} \;\propto\; A \;\propto\; L^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Mass-specific force (mass ∝ L³):**
$$\frac{F_{\max}}{m} \;\propto\; \frac{L^{2}}{L^{3}} \;=\; \frac{1}{L}$$

Same *form* as electrostatic, **but the prefactor is tiny** because:
- Permeability of free space μ₀ = 4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m is small.
- B²/2μ₀ at saturation ≈ 4 × 10⁵ Pa — about **1000× less** than electrostatic pressure (ε₀E²/2 ≈ 4 × 10⁵ Pa at 30 MV/m, but practical MEMS fields allow even higher pressures).

**Step 8 — Practical conclusion:**

$$\boxed{\;\text{Magnetic force per unit volume degrades at small scales due to Joule heating and saturation limits.}\;}$$

This is why **on-chip magnetic micro-actuators are rare**; when used, they rely on **external** magnetic fields (e.g., MRI-guided micro-robots).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Resonant Frequency & Bandwidth Scaling

**Starting premise:** A cantilever actuator of length **L**, rectangular cross-section **w × t**, density **ρ**, Young's modulus **E**.

**Step 1 — Mass of cantilever:**
$$m \;=\; \rho\,L\,w\,t \;\propto\; L \quad (\text{hold cross-section constant})$$

**Step 2 — Spring constant (cantilever bending):**
$$k \;=\; \frac{E\,w\,t^{3}}{4\,L^{3}} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{L^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Natural frequency:**
$$f_{\text{res}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} \;\propto\; \sqrt{\frac{1/L^{3}}{L}} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1}{L^{4}}} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{L^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{\;f'_{\text{res}} \;=\; \frac{1}{k^{2}}\cdot f_{\text{res}}\;}$$

A 10× miniaturization **increases bandwidth by 100×**. This is why **MEMS resonators** (gyroscopes, accelerometers) operate at MHz, while their macroscopic counterparts are limited to kHz.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Thermal Cooling Time Scaling (Why thermal actuators are slow)

**Starting premise:** A thermal actuator of characteristic dimension **L**, density **ρ**, specific heat **cₚ**, thermal conductivity **k_th**.

**Step 1 — Heat capacity:**
$$C_{\text{th}} \;=\; \rho\,c_{p}\,V \;\propto\; L^{3}$$

**Step 2 — Conduction resistance to surface:**
$$R_{\text{th}} \;=\; \frac{L}{k_{\text{th}}\,A} \;\propto\; \frac{L}{L^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Thermal time constant:**
$$\tau_{\text{cool}} \;=\; R_{\text{th}}\,C_{\text{th}} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{L}\cdot L^{3} \;=\; L^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

$$\boxed{\;\tau'_{\text{cool}} \;=\; k^{2}\cdot\tau_{\text{cool}}\;}$$

A 10× smaller thermal actuator cools in **1 %** the time — a **100× speed-up**. This makes **micro-thermal actuators** faster than their macro counterparts *per unit size*, but still limited to kHz–low-MHz in absolute terms.

---

## 3.6 Summary Analytical Matrix — Scaling Exponents

| Quantity | Macro Force | Mass | F/m | Bandwidth | Stroke |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrostatic** | L² | L³ | L⁻¹ | L⁻² (resonant) | L |
| **Piezoelectric** | L² (stress-limited) | L³ | L⁻¹ | L⁻² | L |
| **Magnetic** | L² (B-limited) | L³ | L⁻¹ | L⁻² | L |
| **Thermal / SMA** | L² (ΔT-limited) | L³ | L⁻¹ | L⁻² (thermal limit) | L |
| **Surface-Tension** | L¹ | L³ | L⁻² | L⁻¹ (capillary) | L |

> **KTU Insight:** Notice that **all four major actuator families share the same F/m scaling exponent of −1**. The *prefactors* (and thus absolute performance) differ by orders of magnitude. The KTU board examiner expects you to know which family wins on a given metric — not merely how they all scale.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Micro-Actuator Design Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([START: Design Brief]) --> nodeB{Required Force Range}
    nodeB -- uN to mN --> nodeC{CMOS Compatible?}
    nodeB -- Greater than mN --> nodeD{Need External Power?}
    nodeC -- YES --> nodeE[Electrostatic: comb drive or parallel plate]
    nodeC -- NO --> nodeF{Stroke Required}
    nodeF -- Sub micron --> nodeG[Piezoelectric: PZT or AlN stack]
    nodeF -- Micron to mm --> nodeH[Magnetic: external coil Lorentz force]
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeI[Magnetic: untethered micro robot]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeJ[Thermal: SMA or bimorph]
    nodeE --> nodeK([END: Final Selection])
    nodeG --> nodeK
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeJ --> nodeK
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Scaling-Law Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Geometric Scaling L to kL] --> nodeB[Area shrinks as k squared]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Volume shrinks as k cubed]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Force scales as k squared]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Mass scales as k cubed]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Force per Mass scales as 1 over k]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG[Surface to Volume scales as 1 over k]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Surface forces DOMINATE]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Electrostatic and Capillary WIN]
    nodeF --> nodeJ[Bandwidth scales as 1 over k squared]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Resonant Micro Actuators feasible]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Micro-Actuator System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Energy Source] --> nodeB[Energy Conversion Transducer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Mechanical Coupling Element]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Output Mechanical Work]
    nodeE[Feedback Sensor] --> nodeF[Control Electronics]
    nodeF --> nodeA
    nodeE -. Sense Output .-> nodeD
    subgraph SCALING_CONSTRAINTS
        nodeG[Material Limits] --> nodeB
        nodeH[Fabrication Geometry] --> nodeC
        nodeI[Thermal Budget] --> nodeB
        nodeJ[Scaling Laws k squared, k cubed, etc] --> nodeB
        nodeJ --> nodeC
    end
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Force-vs-Size Performance Envelope

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Micro Actuator Family Performance Map] --> nodeB[Electrostatic: High F per m at sub mm scale]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Piezoelectric: High F, low stroke]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Magnetic: High absolute F, loses at sub 100 micron]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Thermal SMA: High F, low bandwidth]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Best at 1 micron to 1 mm]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Best at 100 micron to 10 mm]
    nodeD --> nodeH[Best at greater than 1 mm or external field]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Best when triggered once only]
```

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Force × Stroke × Bandwidth Trade-off Pentagon

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Force] -- inverse trade-off --> nodeB[Stroke]
    nodeB -- inverse trade-off --> nodeC[Bandwidth]
    nodeC -- inverse trade-off --> nodeD[Energy Density]
    nodeD -- inverse trade-off --> nodeE[Efficiency]
    nodeE -- inverse trade-off --> nodeA
    nodeA -- positive with --> nodeC
    nodeB -- positive with --> nodeD
```

> **Reading Guide:** Any node you push "outward" (i.e., want to maximize) forces at least two adjacent nodes to shrink. The KTU board frequently asks you to identify *which* corner a given actuator family occupies in this pentagon.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define a micro-actuator. Explain how scaling laws affect the design of micro-actuators. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**
A micro-actuator is a transducer that converts electrical, magnetic, thermal, or chemical energy into mechanical motion at the micrometer scale. As dimensions shrink by a factor k, **area scales as k²** while **volume scales as k³**. This causes surface-area-dependent phenomena such as **electrostatic forces, friction, and heat dissipation** to dominate over volume-dependent phenomena such as **inertia and magnetic bulk forces**. The result is that **electrostatic and surface-tension-based actuators become more efficient** at micro-scale, while electromagnetic actuators lose efficiency. [3 marks]

---

### Question 2. List any four key design metrics used to evaluate micro-actuators. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**Model Answer:**
The four key metrics are:
1. **Force / Torque output** (N or N·m) — must overcome friction and payload.
2. **Stroke / Displacement** (m) — distance the actuator moves under load.
3. **Bandwidth / Response time** (Hz) — maximum operational frequency.
4. **Energy density / Power efficiency** (J/m³ or %) — work output per unit volume or electrical-to-mechanical conversion efficiency.

A fifth metric often considered is **fabrication compatibility** with CMOS or polymer processes. [3 marks]

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A. (a) Derive the scaling laws for an electrostatic parallel-plate micro-actuator and explain why electrostatic actuation becomes more efficient at smaller scales. (b) Compare electrostatic, piezoelectric, and electromagnetic micro-actuators on the basis of force, stroke, bandwidth, and energy density. [7 + 7 = 14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, Question A]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Electrostatic scaling derivation [7 marks]:**

Starting with a parallel-plate capacitor of area A and gap d:
$$F_{\text{elec}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,\frac{V^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

The maximum safe voltage is set by the dielectric breakdown field E_max (≈ 3 × 10⁶ V/m for air; up to 10⁸ V/m for thin oxides):

$$V_{\max} = E_{\max}\cdot d$$

Substituting:
$$F_{\max} = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\,A\,E_{\max}^{2} \;\propto\; L^{2}$$

Since mass m ∝ L³:
$$\frac{F_{\max}}{m} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{L}$$

Therefore the **mass-specific force improves by 1/k** upon scaling by k. Furthermore, the volumetric energy density:
$$\rho_{E} = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_{0}\varepsilon_{r}\,E_{\max}^{2}$$

is a **material constant independent of size**. This scale-invariance, combined with low power consumption (current flows only during charging), makes **electrostatic actuation inherently micro-scale friendly**. [7 marks]

**(b) Comparative table [7 marks]:**

| Metric | Electrostatic | Piezoelectric | Electromagnetic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Force density | High (µN–mN) | Very High (mN–N) | Moderate (limited by B_sat) |
| Stroke | Low–Moderate (µm) | Very Low (nm–µm) | Moderate (µm–mm) |
| Bandwidth | High (kHz–MHz) | Very High (100 kHz–MHz) | Moderate (Hz–kHz) |
| Energy density | High & scale-invariant | High (~10⁵ J/m³) | Low at micro-scale |
| Voltage required | Low (5–100 V) | Moderate (10–200 V) | Low (1–5 V) |
| Power consumption | Very low (capacitive) | Moderate | High (resistive heating) |
| CMOS compatible | Yes | Partial (AlN yes) | No |

**Conclusion:** Electrostatic wins for low-power CMOS-MEMS; piezoelectric wins for high-force, low-stroke precision; electromagnetic wins only above ~1 mm or with external fields. [7 marks]

---

### Question B. (a) With suitable derivations, explain how the surface-to-volume ratio governs micro-actuator design. (b) Discuss the challenges of scaling down electromagnetic actuators and explain why magnetic actuation is typically implemented using external fields in micro-robotics. [7 + 7 = 14 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, Question B]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Surface-to-volume ratio derivation [7 marks]:**

For a characteristic length L:
$$\frac{A}{V} = \frac{6L^{2}}{L^{3}} = \frac{6}{L}$$

Upon scaling by factor k:
$$\frac{A'}{V'} = \frac{1}{k}\cdot\frac{6}{L}$$

Thus as L decreases, the ratio **grows as 1/k**. For a 1 cm³ cube: A/V = 600 m⁻¹. For a 1 µm³ cube: A/V = 6 × 10⁶ m⁻¹. This means every unit volume has **10 000× more surface interaction**, making surface effects dominate. Practical consequences:
- **Heat dissipation** improves relative to heat generation (cooling ∝ A).
- **Friction** dominates inertia (friction force ∝ area; inertia ∝ mass ∝ volume).
- **Electrostatic and capillary forces** ∝ area win over gravitational and inertial forces ∝ volume. [7 marks]

**(b) Challenges of scaling electromagnetic actuators [7 marks]:**

1. **Joule heating:** Coil resistance R ∝ 1/A_wire. Power dissipated P = I²R. As wires shrink, R increases rapidly and **melts the coil** before useful force is produced.
2. **Saturation limit:** Maximum B is bounded by ferromagnetic saturation (~1.5–2 T). Beyond this, force cannot grow.
3. **Magnetic pressure scales unfavorably:** Max force per unit area = B²/(2µ₀) ≈ 4 × 10⁵ Pa at saturation — modest compared to electrostatic pressure (~10⁷ Pa achievable in MEMS gaps).
4. **Fabrication:** On-chip coils require multi-layer metallization; magnetic materials (NiFe, CoFe) are not standard CMOS-compatible.

**Solution in micro-robotics:** Use **external magnetic fields** generated by large Helmholtz coils or permanent-magnet arrays positioned around the workspace. The micro-robot itself contains only a soft-magnetic or permanently-magnetized body, which experiences force/torque via F = ∇(m·B). This decouples the actuation hardware from the miniaturization constraint — the **micro-robot can be < 100 µm while the actuator remains macro-scale**. This is the dominant paradigm in:
- MRI-guided micro-robots for drug delivery
- Magnetic microrobotic swimmers in fluid
- Magnetically-actuated capsule endoscopes [7 marks]

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Carefully Before Exam)**
> 1. **Confusing "force scales as L²" with "force density scales as L²"** — Force scales as L² but force-per-mass scales as L⁻¹. Marks lost if these are conflated.
> 2. **Forgetting the breakdown field limit** when deriving electrostatic scaling. Without V_max = E_max·d, the force looks like it scales as L⁰ (constant) — wrong. Always include the material limit.
> 3. **Treating magnetic and electrostatic scaling as identical.** They share the same *form* but have vastly different prefactors. Examiners award separate marks for "prefactor discussion."
> 4. **Omitting units** in the formula sheet during derivations. KTU 2018 onwards deducts marks for unitless answers in Part B derivations.
> 5. **Confusing "resonant frequency scales as 1/L²" with "bandwidth scales as 1/L."** Resonant frequency is a property of the structure; bandwidth is a system-level metric. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 6. **Writing `|F|` inside markdown tables** — this breaks the table renderer and confuses digital evaluation. Use `\vert F \vert` in LaTeX form.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Design Principles of Actuators**

- [ ] **Master the three scaling axioms** (surface, volume, coupling) — they appear in every KTU Part B question on this module.
- [ ] **Memorize the formulas** in Section 2.3 — especially #1, #4, #6, #7, #8, #14.
- [ ] **Remember the exponent table**: Area ∝ L², Volume ∝ L³, F/m ∝ L⁻¹, f_res ∝ L⁻², τ_cool ∝ L².
- [ ] **Know which family wins each metric:**
  - Electrostatic → low-power, fast, CMOS-friendly.
  - Piezoelectric → high force, low stroke, precision.
  - Magnetic → high stroke, requires external field at micro-scale.
  - Thermal/SMA → high force, slow, triggered once.
- [ ] **Always include material limits** (breakdown field, saturation flux density, yield stress) in derivations — they convert "geometric scaling" into "real-world scaling."
- [ ] **Remember** that volumetric energy density is **scale-invariant** for electrostatic and piezoelectric actuators.
- [ ] **Surface tension and viscosity** scale as L⁻¹ and dominate the micro-scale regime.
- [ ] **External field actuation** is the standard solution when an actuator mechanism cannot be miniaturized efficiently.
- [ ] **Resonant operation** can multiply effective stroke by Q-factor but limits bandwidth.
- [ ] **Fabrication constraint** (CMOS, LIGA, polymer) is the fifth axis of the design pentagon, often overlooked by students.
- [ ] **State the units** in every numerical answer — KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests this.

---

*End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 3 / Design Principles of Actuators*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
