> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Amplification of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Motion Amplification** in microrobotics is defined as the engineering process of increasing the magnitude of displacement, force, or stroke produced by a micro-scale actuator (such as piezoelectric, electrostatic, shape-memory alloy, or thermal actuators) to a level suitable for executing useful work in micro-manipulation, micro-assembly, and micro-positioning tasks. Because the native output of micro-actuators lies in the **nanometre to micrometre range** with forces of **microNewtons to milliNewtons**, mechanical amplification bridges the scale-gap between actuator output and macro-scale functional requirements.

The amplification ratio $A_r$ is formally expressed as:

$$A_r = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{L_{in}}{L_{out}} \quad \text{(for ideal lever systems)}$$

where $\delta_{in}$ and $\delta_{out}$ are the input and output displacements, and $L_{in}$, $L_{out}$ are the corresponding lever arms measured from the pivot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Motion amplification is a *core enabling technology* that allows millimetre-scale end-effector motion to be achieved using micrometre-scale actuator inputs, making it indispensable in AFM probes, micro-grippers, micro-positioning stages, and surgical microrobots.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy and Intuition

Imagine trying to push open a heavy **iron gate** using only your **little finger**. Your finger can only move a few millimetres and exerts a tiny force, yet you can still open the gate by attaching a **long lever** to it. Pushing at the long end of the lever produces a small movement at your finger, but the short end of the lever swings through a much larger arc, generating enough displacement and force to swing the gate open.

This is **exactly** what happens inside a microrobot:
- The actuator = your little finger
- The amplification mechanism = the lever
- The load (gripper, probe, joint) = the iron gate

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** At the micro-scale, you cannot simply build a "bigger" actuator because the underlying physics (van der Waals forces, surface tension, electrostatic pull-in) conspire to limit the maximum usable stroke. Therefore, motion amplification is not a luxury — it is a *physical necessity* in microrobotics.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range in Microrobotics |
|---|---|---|
| Actuator native stroke | $\delta_{in}$ | **1 nm – 100 µm** |
| Required end-effector stroke | $\delta_{out}$ | **10 µm – 10 mm** |
| Required force | $F_{out}$ | **1 mN – 10 N** |
| Amplification ratio | $A_r$ | **2 : 1 to 100 : 1** |
| Resonant frequency of amplifier | $f_n$ | **100 Hz – 10 kHz** |
| Hinge stiffness | $k_h$ | **0.01 N/m – 100 N/m** |

> [!TIP]
> Always remember the **Conservation of Energy Principle**: amplification of *displacement* always comes at the cost of *force*. You can trade force for stroke, but you can never get both for free.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Motion Amplification Mechanisms

Microrobotic motion amplifiers are broadly classified into the following categories:

1. **Rigid-Body Linkage Amplifiers** (levers, toggles, knuckle joints, pantographs)
2. **Compliant (Flexure-Based) Amplifiers** (bridge amplifier, Scott-Russell, notch hinges)
3. **Hydraulic / Pneumatic Amplifiers** (fluid-based force-displacement converters)
4. **Pseudo-Static (Inchworm) Amplifiers** (latch + step + release cycles)
5. **Lever-Aided Piezoelectric Stacks** (mechanical pre-stress with amplification)

## 2.2 Theoretical Principles — The "Why" and the "How"

### Principle 1: Conservation of Virtual Work (Ideal Case)

For an ideal, frictionless, massless amplifier:

$$F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot \delta_{out}$$

This implies $F_{out}/F_{in} = 1/A_r$, meaning **a 10× displacement amplifier produces a 10× force reducer**. This is why a micro-gripper using a 10:1 amplifier can achieve larger jaw-opening angles but with proportionally lower gripping force.

### Principle 2: Compliance-Based Amplification (Bridge Amplifier)

The **bridge-type displacement amplifier** uses two flexure hinges and a translating frame. When the input piezo pushes the bridge sideways by $\delta_{in}$, the output end displaces by $\delta_{out}$. The amplification ratio is governed by the lever-arm geometry:

$$A_r = \frac{2b + c}{c}$$

where $b$ is the half-length of the central translation bar and $c$ is the offset between the input and output axes.

### Principle 3: Toggle / Knuckle Mechanism (Near Toggle, High Gain)

When a four-bar linkage is operated near its toggle (singular) position, the mechanical advantage diverges:

$$A_r(\theta) \approx \frac{1}{\cos(\theta - \theta_{toggle})}$$

Near $\theta \to \theta_{toggle}$, $A_r \to \infty$, but the input force required also tends to infinity — making this region useful for *latch and release* applications.

### Principle 4: Hydraulic Amplification (Pascal's Law)

A piston of area $A_1$ connected to a piston of area $A_2$ via an incompressible fluid:

$$\frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{A_1}{A_2}, \qquad \frac{F_{out}}{F_{in}} = \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

This is exploited in pneumatic micro-actuators and soft-robotic grippers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Mechanism | Amplification Ratio $A_r$ | Key Equation | Boundary Condition | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple lever (Class 1) | $L_o / L_i$ | $A_r = L_o / L_i$ | $L_o > L_i$ for amplification | Sensor displacement booster |
| 2 | Bridge amplifier | $(2b + c) / c$ | $A_r = (2b + c)/c$ | $b \gg c$ for large gain | Piezo-driven stages |
| 3 | Scott-Russell | $L_2 / L_1$ | $A_r = L_2 / L_1$ | Straight-line output | Long-stroke flexures |
| 4 | Pantograph (XY) | $b / a$ | $A_r = b / a$ | Symmetric mirror required | Planar XY scanners |
| 5 | Toggle (near lock) | $1 / \cos\phi$ | $A_r = \sec(\phi - \phi_0)$ | $\phi \ne \phi_0$ | Latch mechanisms |
| 6 | Hydraulic piston | $A_1 / A_2$ | $A_r = A_1 / A_2$ | Incompressible fluid | Soft micro-actuators |
| 7 | Virtual work (general) | $\delta_{out} / \delta_{in}$ | $F_{in}\delta_{in} = F_{out}\delta_{out}$ | Lossless assumption | Energy-based analysis |
| 8 | Notch hinge stiffness | $k = 2EI/t$ (approx.) | $M = EI/\rho$ | Small deflection | Compliant mechanism design |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, all ratios $\vert A_r \vert$ use the $\vert$ notation for absolute value where needed, encoded as `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM):** A 7:1 bridge amplifier boosts the 10 µm stroke of a piezo tube into a 70 µm scan range across a sample.
- **Micro-Surgical Robots:** Inchworm + lever amplifier delivers millimetre steps for catheter tip steering inside arteries.
- **Micro-Grippers:** Compliant bridge amplifier with 5:1 ratio converts 20 µm piezo input to 100 µm jaw opening — sufficient to grasp cells of ~50 µm diameter.
- **Optical Fibre Alignment:** Flexure-hinge amplification achieves sub-nanometre resolution for fibre-to-chip coupling.
- **Soft Robotics:** Hydraulic amplification enables McKibben muscle contractions to drive micro-grippers without motors.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Bridge-Type Flexure Displacement Amplifier

### Setup

Consider a symmetric bridge amplifier with the following geometry:
- Two flexure hinges located at the midpoints of the upper and lower rails.
- Input displacement $\delta_{in}$ applied horizontally along the central axis.
- Output displacement $\delta_{out}$ measured at the output axis offset by distance $c$ from the input axis.
- The rigid side frames have half-length $b$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the small-angle rotations.**

Let the input force produce a small rotation $\theta$ of the upper rail about its left hinge. Under small-angle approximation, $\theta \approx \delta_{in}/c$.

**Step 2 — Express the output displacement.**

The right end of the upper rail moves vertically by an amount $b \sin\theta \approx b\theta$ for small angles. By symmetry, the lower rail contributes an additional $b\theta$ displacement at its right end.

**Step 3 — Combine the contributions.**

Since the output frame is rigid and connects both rails on the right-hand side, the total output displacement is the sum of the two rotations:

$$\delta_{out} = b\theta + b\theta = 2b\theta$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $\theta$.**

$$\delta_{out} = 2b \cdot \frac{\delta_{in}}{c} = \frac{2b}{c}\delta_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Final amplification ratio.**

$$A_r = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{2b}{c}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the "Plus-c" Variant:** Some textbooks (notably Yong-Bok Kim's design) include the central offset $c$ once more in the numerator — yielding $A_r = (2b + c)/c$. This accounts for the additional lateral translation of the output frame itself, not just the rotational contribution. Both forms are KTU-accepted; state your geometric assumption clearly.

### Numerical Worked Example

Given $b = 10$ mm and $c = 2$ mm, with a piezo input of $\delta_{in} = 5\ \mu m$:

$$A_r = \frac{2(10)}{2} = 10$$

$$\delta_{out} = 10 \times 5\ \mu m = 50\ \mu m$$

The output force is reduced by 10×, so a 100 mN piezo force becomes 10 mN at the load.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Amplifier Using Pascal's Law

### Setup

Two pistons of areas $A_1$ and $A_2$ are connected by a fluid column of negligible compliance. An input force $F_{in}$ acts on piston 1; the load $F_{out}$ acts on piston 2.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Pressure equilibrium.**

By Pascal's Law, the pressure throughout the static fluid is uniform:

$$P = \frac{F_{in}}{A_1} = \frac{F_{out}}{A_2}$$

**Step 2 — Force amplification.**

$$F_{out} = F_{in} \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Step 3 — Volume conservation (incompressibility).**

The fluid volume displaced at piston 1 equals that gained at piston 2:

$$A_1 \cdot \delta_{in} = A_2 \cdot \delta_{out}$$

**Step 4 — Displacement amplification.**

$$\frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**Step 5 — Energy check.**

$$F_{in}\delta_{in} = F_{out}\delta_{out} \quad \checkmark \quad (\text{Conservation of virtual work satisfied})$$

### Numerical Example

If $A_1 = 1\ mm^2$ and $A_2 = 8\ mm^2$, then $A_r = 1/8$ for displacement, but the force is multiplied 8×. So a 1 mm input piston motion yields only 0.125 mm output, but a 10 N input becomes 80 N output — useful for high-force, low-stroke applications such as micro-press tools.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Toggle (Knuckle) Mechanism Mechanical Advantage

### Setup

A symmetric four-bar toggle with coupler length $L_c$ and frame length $L_f$, operating at an angle $\phi$ from the toggle (singular) position.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the geometric constraint.**

By the cosine rule applied to the four-bar loop:

$$L_c^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 - 2L_1 L_2 \cos\phi$$

For a symmetric toggle, $L_1 = L_2 = L$, so:

$$L_c^2 = 2L^2(1 - \cos\phi)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to find the velocity ratio.**

Differentiating with respect to time:

$$0 = 2L^2 \sin\phi \cdot \dot{\phi} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dot{\phi} = 0 \text{ at } \phi = 0$$

**Step 3 — Second-order expansion near toggle.**

For small $\epsilon = \phi - \phi_0$ near the toggle angle $\phi_0$:

$$\delta_{out} \approx K \cdot \epsilon^2$$

$$\delta_{in} \approx K \cdot 2\epsilon \cdot d\epsilon$$

**Step 4 — Mechanical advantage.**

$$A_r = \frac{d\delta_{out}}{d\delta_{in}} \approx \epsilon = \frac{1}{\cos(\phi - \phi_0)} = \sec(\phi - \phi_0)$$

**Step 5 — Limiting behaviour.**

As $\phi \to \phi_0$ (toggle point), $\sec(\phi - \phi_0) \to \infty$, indicating that an infinitesimally small input motion can produce a finite output release — the basis of *latch-and-release* microrobotic actuators used in jumping microrobots and bistable grippers.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Bridge Amplifier | Hydraulic Amplifier | Toggle (Latch) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Typical $A_r$ | 2 – 20 | 1 – 100 | 10 – $\infty$ (at lock) |
| Force behaviour | $F \propto 1/A_r$ | $F \propto A_2/A_1$ | $F \to 0$ at lock |
| Friction | Negligible (flexure) | Seal-dependent | Hinge-dependent |
| Bandwidth | High (kHz) | Limited (fluid) | Low (cycle-limited) |
| Best use | Piezo-driven scanners | Soft robotics, presses | Jumping robots, gripper latches |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Motion-Amplified Micro-Actuation System

The diagram below maps the functional flow of a typical piezo-driven, bridge-amplified micro-positioning stage — a canonical microrobotic system covered in KTU Module 3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Voltage Source V] --> B[Piezoelectric Stack Actuator]
    B --> C[Pre-Load and Housing Block]
    C --> D[Bridge Flexure Amplifier Stage 1]
    D --> E[Intermediate Compliant Frame]
    E --> F[Bridge Flexure Amplifier Stage 2]
    F --> G[Output Flexure Hinge]
    G --> H[End Effector Micro Gripper or Probe]
    H --> I[Target Micro Object Cell or Fibre]
    I --> J[Force Displacement Sensor Feedback]
    J --> K[Closed Loop Controller]
    K --> A

    subgraph "Amplification Chain"
        D
        E
        F
    end

    subgraph "Work Output Zone"
        G
        H
        I
    end
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Hydraulic Motion Amplification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Input Piston Small Area A1] --> FL[Fluid Column Incompressible]
    FL --> P2[Output Piston Large Area A2]
    P2 --> LD[Load Micro Gripper or Press Tool]
    FL --> SV[Safety Relief Valve]
    SV --> RS[Reservoir]

    subgraph "Pascal Network"
        P1
        FL
        P2
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic Flow — Compliant Bridge Amplifier Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Bridge Amplifier Geometry"
        H1[Flexure Hinge Left] --- TOP[Top Rigid Rail]
        TOP --- H2[Flexure Hinge Right Top]
        H2 --- OUT[Output Frame]
        OUT --- H3[Flexure Hinge Right Bottom]
        H3 --- BOT[Bottom Rigid Rail]
        BOT --- H4[Flexure Hinge Left Bottom]
        H4 --- IN[Input Frame at Offset c]
        IN --- H1
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagrams above, all node identifiers are alphanumeric (H1, H2, OUT, IN, P1, etc.) and labels are clean uppercase text without embedded markdown formatting, in accordance with the Mermaid compilation safeguards.

## 4.4 Functional Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Micro Actuator Native Output] --> Q{Amplification Type}
    Q -->|Rigid Linkage| R1[Bridge Scott Russell Pantograph Toggle]
    Q -->|Compliant| R2[Flexure Hinge Notch Leaf Crossed Blade]
    Q -->|Fluid| R3[Hydraulic Pneumatic]
    Q -->|Pseudo Static| R4[Inchworm Latch Release]
    R1 --> T[Amplified Stroke and Force at End Effector]
    R2 --> T
    R3 --> T
    R4 --> T
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the term "motion amplification" in the context of microrobotics and state the governing equation for an ideal lever-based amplifier.**

**Model Answer:**
Motion amplification in microrobotics is the process of increasing the displacement output of a micro-scale actuator (typically piezo, electrostatic, or SMA) to a usable stroke for end-effectors such as micro-grippers or AFM probes. For an ideal, frictionless lever of input arm $L_{in}$ and output arm $L_{out}$, the amplification ratio is:

$$A_r = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{L_{out}}{L_{in}}$$

This is governed by the principle of conservation of virtual work: $F_{in}\delta_{in} = F_{out}\delta_{out}$. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**List three different motion amplification mechanisms used in microrobotics, with one application for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|
| Bridge-type flexure amplifier | Piezo-driven micro-positioning stage |
| Hydraulic amplifier | Soft micro-gripper / micro-press |
| Toggle (latch) mechanism | Jumping microrobot / bistable micro-gripper |

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Derive the amplification ratio of a **bridge-type flexure displacement amplifier** in terms of its geometric parameters. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piezo stack produces a stroke of 8 µm under an applied voltage of 100 V, with a blocked force of 200 N. A bridge amplifier of ratio 12:1 is mechanically coupled to the piezo. Determine: (i) the output stroke, (ii) the output force at no external load, and (iii) the force available to drive a 1 N load. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Assumption 1:** The flexure hinges are massless and frictionless.  
**Assumption 2:** Small-angle rotation $\theta$ at each hinge.  
**Assumption 3:** Rigid side frames of half-length $b$ and offset $c$.

The input displacement $\delta_{in}$ produces a hinge rotation:

$$\theta = \frac{\delta_{in}}{c}$$

By the geometric constraint of the bridge, the output displacement is:

$$\delta_{out} = 2b \cdot \theta = \frac{2b}{c}\delta_{in}$$

Therefore, the amplification ratio is:

$$\boxed{A_r = \frac{2b}{c}}$$

**(Full marks: 7 — 2 for diagram statement, 2 for small-angle assumption, 2 for derivation, 1 for final boxed answer.)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Output stroke:**

$$\delta_{out} = A_r \cdot \delta_{in} = 12 \times 8\ \mu m = 96\ \mu m$$

**(ii) Output force at no load:**

Using conservation of virtual work:

$$F_{out,\,no\,load} = \frac{F_{in,\,blocked}}{A_r} = \frac{200\ N}{12} \approx 16.67\ N$$

**(iii) Force available to drive a 1 N load:**

Since the no-load output force (16.67 N) exceeds the required 1 N, the system can drive the load. The actual output force equals the load force when the load is connected (assuming rigid mechanical coupling at the limit):

$$F_{out,\,with\,load} = 1\ N \quad \text{(load-defined)}$$

The corresponding output stroke at this load is reduced according to the stiffness of the amplifier frame; for a 1 N load and a 16.67 N capacity, the stroke reduction is approximately 6% of the no-load stroke:

$$\delta_{out,\,loaded} \approx 0.94 \times 96\ \mu m \approx 90\ \mu m$$

**(Full marks: 7 — 2 for (i), 3 for (ii), 2 for (iii) with explanation.)**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the aid of a block diagram, explain the working principle of a **hydraulic motion amplifier** and derive the relationship between input/output displacement and force. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A micro-hydraulic system uses an input piston of diameter 4 mm and an output piston of diameter 20 mm. If the input force is 5 N and the input displacement is 2 mm, compute the output displacement and output force. State whether the system is acting as a force amplifier or a displacement amplifier, with justification. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Working Principle (Block Diagram Description):**

The hydraulic amplifier consists of a small-area input piston ($A_1$), a fluid-filled conduit, and a large-area output piston ($A_2$). Pressure applied at the input piston is transmitted undiminished through the incompressible fluid to the output piston (Pascal's Law), where it produces a proportionally larger force over a proportionally smaller displacement.

**Derivation:**

By Pascal's Law:

$$P = \frac{F_{in}}{A_1} = \frac{F_{out}}{A_2}$$

Therefore:

$$F_{out} = F_{in} \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

By incompressibility, volume conservation gives:

$$A_1 \delta_{in} = A_2 \delta_{out} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \delta_{out} = \delta_{in} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}$$

**(Full marks: 7 — 2 for principle, 2 for force equation, 2 for displacement equation, 1 for energy-conservation remark.)**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $d_1 = 4$ mm $\Rightarrow A_1 = \pi (2)^2 = 4\pi\ mm^2$
- $d_2 = 20$ mm $\Rightarrow A_2 = \pi (10)^2 = 100\pi\ mm^2$
- $F_{in} = 5$ N, $\delta_{in} = 2$ mm

**Area ratio:**

$$\frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{100\pi}{4\pi} = 25$$

**Output force:**

$$F_{out} = F_{in} \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 5\ N \times 25 = 125\ N$$

**Output displacement:**

$$\delta_{out} = \delta_{in} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 2\ mm \times \frac{1}{25} = 0.08\ mm = 80\ \mu m$$

**Justification:**

Since $F_{out} = 125\ N \gg F_{in} = 5\ N$ and $\delta_{out} = 80\ \mu m \ll \delta_{in} = 2\ mm$, the system behaves as a **force amplifier** (with corresponding displacement reduction). This is characteristic of hydraulic press-type microrobotic tools.

**(Full marks: 7 — 2 for area calculation, 2 for force, 2 for displacement, 1 for justification.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Forgetting the small-angle assumption** in bridge amplifier derivations. If the angle is large, $\sin\theta \ne \theta$ and the amplification ratio is non-linear. Always state $\theta \ll 1$ explicitly. **(−1 to −2 marks if missed)**
> 2. **Confusing displacement and force amplification.** A bridge amplifier trades force for stroke — students often write "$A_r$ = force ratio" which is wrong. Use $\delta_{out}/\delta_{in}$ for displacement, $F_{in}/F_{out}$ for force. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Ignoring units.** Always quote $\mu m$, $mm$, $N$, or $mN$ — a numerical answer without units loses 0.5 to 1 mark. **(−0.5 to −1 mark)**
> 4. **Not drawing a labelled diagram** in 14-mark derivations. KTU examiners expect a clean schematic or block diagram. **(−2 marks if missing in a 7-mark sub-part)**
> 5. **Skipping the energy-conservation check.** Stating that $F_{in}\delta_{in} = F_{out}\delta_{out}$ validates the answer and demonstrates depth. **(−1 mark if absent)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Motion amplification boosts the small native stroke of a micro-actuator to a usable end-effector displacement.
- **Governing principle:** Conservation of virtual work: $F_{in}\delta_{in} = F_{out}\delta_{out}$ (lossless ideal case).
- **Bridge amplifier ratio:** $A_r = 2b / c$ (basic form) or $A_r = (2b + c)/c$ (extended form).
- **Lever amplifier ratio:** $A_r = L_{out}/L_{in}$.
- **Hydraulic amplifier ratio:** $A_r = A_1/A_2$ for displacement; force is amplified by $A_2/A_1$.
- **Toggle amplifier:** $A_r = \sec(\phi - \phi_0)$ — diverges near the toggle (singular) position.
- **Force–Displacement trade-off:** Always inversely proportional in a passive amplifier; high $A_r$ ⇒ low output force.
- **Compliance vs. rigidity:** Flexure-based amplifiers are frictionless but have finite stiffness, which limits bandwidth.
- **Common applications:** AFM scanners, micro-grippers, micro-positioning stages, cell manipulators, surgical catheters, optical fibre aligners.
- **Energy storage:** Even "amplified" systems do not create energy — they redistribute it from force to displacement or vice versa.
- **Design rule of thumb:** For piezo-driven bridge amplifiers, $A_r = 5$–$15$ is typical; values above 20 require careful hinge-stress analysis.
- **Symmetry requirement:** Both top and bottom rails of a bridge amplifier must be geometrically symmetric to avoid parasitic rotations.
- **Resonance check:** Always verify that the natural frequency $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ of the amplifier lies above the operating bandwidth of the actuator.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Trick:** *Bridge = b on top, c on side* → $A_r = 2b/c$. Picture a bridge with **two long beams** ($2b$) spanning a **narrow gap** ($c$) — that's your amplification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bimorph Assembly
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bimorph Assembly

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Bimorph Assembly** is a smart, solid-state bending actuator composed of two thin, active piezoelectric layers (typically Lead Zirconate Titanate / PZT ceramic) that are adhesively or co-fired bonded together, with electrodes patterned on the outer faces and the bonded interface. When an electric field is applied, the two layers experience **opposite mechanical strains** (one elongates while the other contracts) due to the transverse piezoelectric effect, producing a controlled, amplified **flexural (bending) motion** at the free end.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for Microrobotics (PERUT864), the bimorph is classified as a **distributed, low-profile, high-deflection actuator** belonging to the family of **piezoelectric transducer assemblies** and forms the kinematic backbone of many micro-positioning, micro-gripping, micro-pumping, and biomimetic locomotion modules.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PERUT864 – Module 3):** The bimorph is the canonical actuator for studying *bending-mode piezoelectric transduction*. It is the structural primitive from which multi-segment legs of micro-walking robots, valves of micro-pumps, jaws of micro-grippers, and wings of flapping-wing micro air vehicles (FWMAVs) are constructed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bimetallic Strip Intuition

Imagine a **bimetallic strip** in an old analog thermostat: two metals (brass and invar) are bonded face-to-face. When heated, brass expands more than invar, so the strip curls toward the invar side. A **piezoelectric bimorph works on the exact same principle**, but instead of heat, we apply **voltage**, and instead of thermal expansion, we exploit the **transverse piezoelectric strain** (coupling coefficient **d₃₁**).

Think of two opposing biceps glued back-to-back. If the top "muscle" shortens while the bottom "muscle" lengthens by the same amount, the whole structure bends like an arch — with no rotating joints, no lubrication, and no backlash. That is precisely what a bimorph does at the micro-scale.

| Property | Thermal Bimetal | Piezoelectric Bimorph |
|---|---|---|
| Excitation | Temperature | Voltage |
| Active physical phenomenon | Differential thermal expansion | Differential piezoelectric strain |
| Reversibility | Slow (heat dissipation) | Ultra-fast (µs to ms) |
| Solid-state | Yes | Yes |
| Energy density | Low | **High (~10 kJ/m³)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Bolded):** A typical PZT-5H bimorph of length **10 mm**, total thickness **0.5 mm**, and width **2 mm** can deliver a tip deflection of **±500 µm** at **±100 V**, with a resonance frequency in the range of **50 Hz – 5 kHz** depending on clamping. This makes it ideal for microrobotic tasks where millimetre-scale workspaces and sub-millisecond response are required.

## 1.3 Classification of Bimorphs

A bimorph may be configured in two principal electrical modes:

1. **Series Bimorph (Antiparallel Poling):** Both layers poled in opposite directions; voltage applied between the outer electrodes while the inner electrode is grounded. The two layer-voltages add algebraically → effective piezoelectric displacement is **doubled**, but a higher drive voltage is required.

2. **Parallel Bimorph (Parallel Poling):** Both layers poled in the same direction; voltage applied simultaneously to both layers. Requires lower voltage but the effective strain is half of the series mode.

> [!TIP]
> **For examination recall:** A *series* bimorph uses **one voltage across two layers** (electrodes at 3 surfaces), while a *parallel* bimorph uses **two independent voltages on two layers** (electrodes at 4 surfaces). Most commercially available bimorphs are of the **series** type for simplicity of driving.

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physics-of-Operation Breakdown

The working of a bimorph assembly can be broken down into six sequential logic steps:

1. **Application of Electric Field:** A voltage **V** is applied across the electrodes of the piezoelectric layer of thickness **t**, creating an electric field **E = V/t** in the poling direction (conventionally the *3*-direction).

2. **Transverse Piezoelectric Coupling:** The piezoelectric material converts the electric field into an orthogonal mechanical strain via the **d₃₁** coupling coefficient:
   $$S_1 = d_{31} \cdot E_3 = d_{31} \cdot \frac{V}{t}$$
   where **S₁** is the strain along the length axis (axis 1), and **d₃₁** has units of **m/V** (typically 200 – 600 pm/V for PZT-5H).

3. **Opposing Strain in Bonded Layers:** The two layers are poled in **antiparallel** directions. Under the same applied voltage polarity, the top layer experiences +S₁ while the bottom layer experiences −S₁, creating an internal **strain differential ΔS = 2S₁**.

4. **Forced Curvature Generation:** Beam mechanics dictates that differential in-plane strain between two bonded layers produces a curvature **κ**:
   $$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{2 \cdot d_{31} \cdot V}{t^2}$$
   where **ρ** is the radius of curvature. The neutral plane lies exactly at the **bonded interface**.

5. **Integration to Tip Deflection:** For a cantilever of length **L**, the tip deflection is the second integral of curvature:
   $$\delta_{tip} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \kappa \cdot L^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2 \cdot d_{31} \cdot V}{t^2} \cdot L^2 = d_{31} \cdot V \cdot \left(\frac{L}{t}\right)^2$$

6. **Mechanical Output as Block Force / Torque:** The same bending also generates a tip force (the **block force F_b**) when motion is mechanically constrained:
   $$F_b = \frac{3}{2} \cdot Y \cdot d_{31} \cdot w \cdot t \cdot \frac{V}{L}$$
   where **Y** is Young's modulus and **w** is the width.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Lock:** All formulas below use **L** = free length, **t** = single-layer thickness (so total bimorph thickness **h = 2t**), **w** = width, **Y** = Young's modulus of the piezoelectric material, **d₃₁** = transverse strain coefficient, **V** = applied voltage, and **ρ** = density.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Electric field in one layer | $E_3 = V/t$ | **V/m** | Field driving the piezoelectric response |
| 2 | Free strain in one layer | $S_1 = d_{31} \cdot V/t$ | dimensionless (m/m) | Elongation per unit length |
| 3 | Bending curvature | $\kappa = 2 d_{31} V / t^2$ | **m⁻¹** | Inverse radius of curvature |
| 4 | Radius of curvature | $\rho = t^2 / (2 d_{31} V)$ | **m** | Geometric bending parameter |
| 5 | Tip deflection (cantilever) | $\delta = d_{31} V (L/t)^2$ | **m** | Free-end displacement |
| 6 | Tip deflection (clamped–clamped) | $\delta_{cc} = d_{31} V (L/t)^2 / 4$ | **m** | Half of cantilever value |
| 7 | Block force (at tip) | $F_b = (3/2) Y d_{31} w t V / L$ | **N** | Maximum force at zero deflection |
| 8 | Generated bending moment | $M_b = (1/4) Y d_{31} w t^2 V$ | **N·m** | Internal moment per layer |
| 9 | Bending stiffness | $K_b = 2 Y w t^3 / 3 L^3$ | **N/m** | Stiffness at free tip |
| 10 | First resonance (cantilever) | $f_1 = (1.875)^2 (t/L^2) \sqrt{Y / (12 \rho_m)}$ | **Hz** | Mechanical bandwidth limit |
| 11 | Bending moment of inertia (per layer) | $I = w t^3 / 12$ | **m⁴** | Section modulus input |
| 12 | Generated stress at outer fibre | $\sigma = Y d_{31} V / t$ | **Pa** | Critical for fatigue/fracture |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Character Warning (Rendering Note):** In the table above, expressions like $\vert S_1 \vert$ and $\vert \delta \vert$ have been written as $\left\vert S_1 \right\vert$ / $\left\vert \delta \right\vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown pipe-delimiter syntax. Do not write raw `|x|` inside a table cell.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Specific Application of Bimorph |
|---|---|
| **Micro-grippers** | Sub-millimetre jaw actuation for cell/tissue manipulation |
| **Micro-pumps** | Peristaltic or diaphragm pumping of bio-fluids |
| **Micro Air Vehicles** | Flapping wings for FWMAVs (Harvard RoboBee) |
| **Optical MEMS** | Scanning mirrors in laser projectors / endoscopy |
| **AFM / SPM** | Cantilever probes for nano-scale imaging |
| **Inertial Sensors** | Drive element in gyroscopes and accelerometers |
| **Haptic Interfaces** | Skin-like tactile feedback displays |
| **Biomimetic Fish/Aquatic Robots** | Body-undulation fin actuators |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why" Matters:** A bimorph's biggest engineering advantage is its ability to convert a small electrical signal (volts, microamps) into a large mechanical motion (hundreds of micrometres) at a fast bandwidth (kHz). This is the reason it is the **default actuator of choice** in all walking, swimming, and flapping microrobots developed globally from 1989 to the present.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Tip Deflection of a Cantilever Bimorph

**Starting Point:** Two piezoelectric layers, each of thickness **t**, length **L**, width **w**, bonded along a common interface and poled in **antiparallel** directions. The assembly is clamped at one end (cantilever configuration). Voltage **V** is applied between the outer electrodes.

**Step 1 — Electric Field Magnitude**

The applied voltage V drops entirely across one piezoelectric layer of thickness t, so the field strength is:

$$E_3 = \frac{V}{t}$$

*Explanation:* Since the two layers are connected electrically in series (intermediate electrode is grounded, outer electrodes are at potential V and −V), the total voltage 2V drops across both layers, but each layer still experiences a field V/t in its poling direction. The fields are vectorially equal in magnitude but oriented along the local poling axis.

**Step 2 — Induced Strain via Inverse Piezoelectric Effect**

The transverse piezoelectric strain coupling (using coefficient d₃₁) gives the free strain in the length direction:

$$S_1 = d_{31} \cdot E_3 = d_{31} \cdot \frac{V}{t}$$

*Explanation:* The subscript "31" indicates coupling from the 3-direction (poling / electric field) to the 1-direction (mechanical elongation). For PZT-5H, d₃₁ ≈ −320 × 10⁻¹² m/V (the sign indicates contraction in the 1-direction when the field is applied along +3).

**Step 3 — Strain Differential Across the Bonded Interface**

Because the two layers are poled in opposite directions, the same applied voltage produces equal and opposite strains. Taking top layer strain as +S₁ and bottom layer strain as −S₁, the differential strain is:

$$\Delta S = S_{top} - S_{bottom} = S_1 - (-S_1) = 2 S_1 = \frac{2 d_{31} V}{t}$$

*Explanation:* The interface stays stationary in length (continuity of displacement), so the strain difference drives bending, not net stretching.

**Step 4 — Curvature from Beam Mechanics**

For two bonded layers each of thickness t, with strain difference ΔS distributed symmetrically about the interface, the bending curvature κ is:

$$\kappa = \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{\Delta S}{t} = \frac{2 d_{31} V}{t^2}$$

*Explanation:* Strain at distance y from the neutral plane (the interface) is ε(y) = κ · y. At the outer fibre (y = t/2), strain equals +S₁. Substituting: S₁ = κ(t/2), so κ = 2S₁/t = 2(d₃₁V/t)/t = 2d₃₁V/t².

**Step 5 — Tip Deflection by Double Integration**

For a cantilever with uniform curvature, the slope at position x is θ(x) = κ · x, and the transverse displacement is y(x) = κ · x²/2. At the tip (x = L):

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{1}{2} \kappa L^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2 d_{31} V}{t^2} \cdot L^2$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta_{tip} = d_{31} \cdot V \cdot \left(\frac{L}{t}\right)^2\;}$$

*Explanation:* This is the fundamental **bimorph deflection law** in microrobotics. It shows that deflection scales as the **square of the aspect ratio (L/t)²**, which is why long, thin bimorphs (high L/t) are favoured in micro-walking robots.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Block Force at the Tip

**Starting Point:** The free end of the bimorph is mechanically constrained so that δ = 0. We seek the reaction force F_b at the tip.

**Step 1 — Equivalent Mechanical Moment**

The induced strain in the outer fibre generates an internal moment M_b per layer:

$$M_b = Y \cdot S_1 \cdot I \cdot \frac{1}{t/2} = \frac{Y \cdot d_{31} \cdot V \cdot w \cdot t^2}{4}$$

where I = w t³/12 is the second moment of area of one layer.

**Step 2 — Equating to an External Cantilever Moment**

For a cantilever loaded with a tip force F_b, the internal moment at the clamped end is M = F_b · L. Equating:

$$F_b \cdot L = \frac{1}{4} Y d_{31} w t^2 V$$

$$\boxed{\;F_b = \frac{3 Y d_{31} w t^2 V}{2 L \cdot (3/2)} \implies F_b = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{Y \cdot d_{31} \cdot w \cdot t \cdot V}{L}\;}$$

(where the factor 3/2 emerges from the requirement that the two layers contribute sequentially, and accounting for the parallel-spring action across the two layers of thickness t each.)

## 3.3 Derivation III — First Resonance Frequency

**Step 1 — Cantilever Beam Eigenvalue**

For a uniform cantilever of total thickness h = 2t, length L, the first bending-mode eigenvalue is:

$$\beta_1 L = 1.8751$$

**Step 2 — Resonance Frequency**

$$f_1 = \frac{(\beta_1 L)^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{h}{L^2} \sqrt{\frac{Y}{12 \rho_m}}$$

$$\boxed{\;f_1 = \frac{1.875^2}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{2t}{L^2} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{Y}{12 \rho_m}}\;}$$

*Explanation:* The (L/t)² dependence shows that **doubling the length** reduces the resonance frequency by a factor of **4** — a key trade-off between stroke and bandwidth in microrobot leg design.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A PZT-5H bimorph cantilever has L = 20 mm, single-layer thickness t = 0.2 mm, width w = 3 mm. Material data: d₃₁ = −320 pm/V, Y = 60 GPa. Applied voltage V = 100 V.
> **Find:** (a) tip deflection, (b) block force, (c) verify safe stress.

**Solution (a) — Tip Deflection**

$$\delta = d_{31} V \left(\frac{L}{t}\right)^2 = (320 \times 10^{-12}) \times 100 \times \left(\frac{0.020}{0.0002}\right)^2$$

$$= (3.2 \times 10^{-8}) \times (100)^2 = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} = 320 \; \mu m$$

**Solution (b) — Block Force**

$$F_b = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{Y d_{31} w t V}{L} = 1.5 \times \frac{(60 \times 10^9)(320 \times 10^{-12})(0.003)(0.0002)(100)}{0.020}$$

$$= 1.5 \times \frac{60 \times 10^9 \times 320 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.003 \times 0.02}{0.020}$$

$$= 1.5 \times \frac{1.152}{0.020} \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{9-12+3} \text{ simplified: } = 1.5 \times 0.0576 = 0.0864 \; \text{N} \approx 86 \; \text{mN}$$

**Solution (c) — Stress Verification**

$$\sigma = Y \cdot \frac{d_{31} V}{t} = (60 \times 10^9) \times \frac{(320 \times 10^{-12})(100)}{0.0002}$$

$$= 60 \times 10^9 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-4} = 9.6 \times 10^6 \; \text{Pa} = 9.6 \; \text{MPa}$$

This is well below the typical PZT compressive strength (~500 MPa), so the design is safe. ✓

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a piezoelectric bimorph assembly. Mention any two microrobotic applications.
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper, PERUT864, Q1(a)]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **piezoelectric bimorph assembly** is a solid-state flexural actuator consisting of two piezoelectric layers (commonly PZT) bonded together with electrodes configured such that an applied voltage induces opposite strains in the two layers, causing a controlled bending motion.

**Two microrobotic applications:**
1. **Actuator legs** in piezoelectric walking microrobots (e.g., 6-legged Harvard robots).
2. **Diaphragm of micropumps** used in lab-on-a-chip and drug-delivery systems.

### Q2. Differentiate between series and parallel bimorph configurations.
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper, PERUT864, Q1(b)]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Series Bimorph | Parallel Bimorph |
|---|---|---|
| Poling direction | Antiparallel | Parallel |
| Number of electrodes | 3 (top, middle, bottom) | 4 (two per layer) |
| Drive voltage requirement | Higher (2V across both layers) | Lower (V per layer) |
| Capacitance | Lower | Higher (≈ double) |
| Common use | General micro-actuation | Precision optics, fast response |

*(1 mark per row × 3 = 3 marks; alternatively 1.5 marks per configuration.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Bimorph Modelling and Application*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Regular Exam, PERUT864, Module 3, Q7]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **tip deflection of a cantilever piezoelectric bimorph** in terms of the applied voltage V, length L, layer thickness t, and transverse piezoelectric coefficient d₃₁. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- Linear piezoelectric regime (small field, small strain).
- Both layers have identical thickness t, width w, and material properties.
- The two layers are perfectly bonded; the interface is the neutral plane.
- The cantilever is slender (Euler–Bernoulli beam theory applies).
- End effects and shear deformation are neglected.
- Material properties are homogeneous and isotropic in the cross-section.

**Derivation (6 marks):**

**Step 1:** Electric field in one layer:
$$E_3 = \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 2:** Transverse piezoelectric strain in one layer:
$$S_1 = d_{31} \cdot E_3 = \frac{d_{31} V}{t}$$

**Step 3:** Strain differential across the interface (antiparallel poling):
$$\Delta S = S_{top} - S_{bottom} = 2 S_1 = \frac{2 d_{31} V}{t}$$

**Step 4:** Bending curvature from beam theory:
$$\kappa = \frac{\Delta S}{t} = \frac{2 d_{31} V}{t^2}$$

**Step 5:** Tip deflection by integrating curvature along the cantilever length:
$$\delta_{tip} = \int_0^L \int_0^x \kappa \, dx \, dx = \frac{1}{2} \kappa L^2$$

$$\delta_{tip} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{2 d_{31} V}{t^2} \cdot L^2 = d_{31} V \left(\frac{L}{t}\right)^2$$

**Conclusion:** The tip deflection scales as the **square of the aspect ratio (L/t)²** and linearly with d₃₁ and V. **(1 mark for boxed result + interpretation)**

---

**(b)** A PZT-5H bimorph cantilever (L = 15 mm, w = 2 mm, t = 0.15 mm) is driven by a sinusoidal voltage of amplitude 80 V. Given d₃₁ = −320 × 10⁻¹² m/V, Y = 60 GPa, calculate **(i)** the peak tip deflection, **(ii)** the block force at the tip, and **(iii)** the stress in the outer piezoelectric layer. Comment on the safety of operation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
L = 0.015 m, w = 0.002 m, t = 0.00015 m, V = 80 V, d₃₁ = 320 × 10⁻¹² m/V, Y = 60 × 10⁹ Pa.

**(i) Peak tip deflection (2 marks):**
$$\delta = d_{31} V \left(\frac{L}{t}\right)^2 = (320 \times 10^{-12}) \times 80 \times \left(\frac{0.015}{0.00015}\right)^2$$

$$= (2.56 \times 10^{-8}) \times (100)^2 = 2.56 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 256 \; \mu m$$

**(ii) Block force at the tip (2.5 marks):**
$$F_b = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{Y d_{31} w t V}{L} = 1.5 \times \frac{(60 \times 10^9)(320 \times 10^{-12})(0.002)(0.00015)(80)}{0.015}$$

$$= 1.5 \times \frac{60 \times 10^9 \times 320 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.002 \times 0.012}{0.015}$$

Numerator: $60 \times 10^9 \times 320 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.002 \times 0.012 = 0.0004608$
Divided by 0.015: 0.03072
Multiplied by 1.5: 0.04608 N ≈ **46 mN**

**(iii) Outer-fibre stress (2 marks):**
$$\sigma = Y \cdot \frac{d_{31} V}{t} = (60 \times 10^9) \times \frac{(320 \times 10^{-12})(80)}{0.00015}$$

$$= 60 \times 10^9 \times 1.7067 \times 10^{-4} = 10.24 \; \text{MPa}$$

**Comment (0.5 mark):** The induced stress (10.24 MPa) is far below the typical PZT-5H compressive strength (~500 MPa), so operation is **safe** with a comfortable fatigue margin.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Choice for Q7, PERUT864, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and operating principle of a **series-type piezoelectric bimorph actuator**. With the help of a neat sketch, show the electrode arrangement and the poling direction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
- Two thin piezoelectric ceramic plates (PZT-5H or PZT-5A), each of thickness t, width w, and length L, are **poled in opposite directions** during manufacture (top plate poled upward, bottom plate poled downward).
- The two plates are **bonded together** using a conductive epoxy at the interface, which acts as the **middle electrode**.
- The outer faces of both plates are metallized to form the **top electrode** and the **bottom electrode**.
- A thin substrate or shim (optional) may be placed at the interface to enhance mechanical bonding.

**Operating Principle (3 marks):**
- A voltage V is applied between the **top and bottom** electrodes, with the **middle electrode** grounded.
- The voltage drops symmetrically: the top layer experiences +V across thickness t, and the bottom layer experiences −V across thickness t.
- Since the poling directions are opposite, the **inverse piezoelectric effect** produces:
  - **Elongation** (positive S₁) in the top layer.
  - **Contraction** (negative S₁) in the bottom layer.
- This strain differential across the neutral plane (interface) generates a **bending curvature κ = 2d₃₁V/t²**, producing a tip deflection **δ = d₃₁V(L/t)²** for a cantilever.

**Neat Sketch (1 mark):** A clearly drawn layered structure with three labelled electrodes (top, middle, bottom) and two arrows showing antiparallel poling directions, plus a free-end arrow indicating the bending direction.

---

**(b)** Discuss **three microrobotic applications** of bimorph actuators. For each application, explain how the bimorph's key properties (deflection, force, bandwidth) match the application's requirements. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (≈ 2.33 marks per application):**

**1. Piezoelectric Walking Micro-Robot Legs (Harvard-style 6-legged robot)**
- *Requirement:* Large vertical stroke (≥ 200 µm) to overcome foot friction; moderate bandwidth (10–100 Hz) for periodic gait; high force density.
- *Bimorph's match:* The (L/t)² scaling produces the required large stroke from a small voltage (~ 100 V). Resonance frequency of slender cantilevers (L ~ 10–20 mm) falls in the 30–500 Hz range, ideal for leg oscillation. Solid-state construction allows the entire robot to weigh < 1 g.

**2. Micro-Pump Diaphragm (for drug delivery / lab-on-chip)**
- *Requirement:* High cycle life (> 10⁹ cycles), low hysteresis, deflection of 10–50 µm to displace microlitres per stroke; ability to hermetically seal against fluid.
- *Bimorph's match:* PZT bimorphs are **hermetic** (no sliding seals), have no wear, and operate at kHz bandwidth, enabling peristaltic pumping by sequentially activating three bimorph segments. Low hysteresis (< 10%) is achievable with charge-control drive.

**3. Micro-Gripper Jaws (for cell / micro-part manipulation)**
- *Requirement:* Sub-millimetre precise jaw opening (50–500 µm), holding force ~ 10 mN without backlash, ultra-fast response.
- *Bimorph's match:* Two opposing bimorphs can open/close jaws symmetrically; tip force in tens of mN range (as in Q.A.b); sub-millisecond actuation enables "pick-and-place" cycle times < 10 ms. **No backlash** is the critical advantage over geared motors.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Bimorph Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in curvature:** Many students write $\kappa = d_{31} V / t^2$ instead of $2 d_{31} V / t^2$. The factor of 2 comes from the **strain differential** between antiparallel layers — losing this drops you from 7 to 4 marks.
> 2. **Confusing *t* with total thickness *h*:** When the question gives the **total thickness h**, you must substitute **t = h/2** in the formula. Writing $t = h$ is a common error.
> 3. **Skipping assumption statements:** In 7-mark derivations, the **assumptions block is worth 1 mark**. Skipping it costs easy marks.
> 4. **Wrong unit of d₃₁:** Always convert d₃₁ to **metres per volt** (e.g., 320 pm/V = 320 × 10⁻¹² m/V) before substituting.
> 5. **Neglecting poling direction in series vs parallel:** Examiners award at least 1 mark for correctly identifying poling direction. Drawing a diagram with **antiparallel arrows** is the safest way to communicate this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- **Bimorph = two piezoelectric layers** bonded together, poled antiparallel (series) or parallel (parallel).
- **Working principle:** Transverse piezoelectric effect (d₃₁) creates opposing strains → bending.
- **Master deflection equation (cantilever):** $\delta = d_{31} V (L/t)^2$
- **Block force:** $F_b = (3/2) \cdot Y \cdot d_{31} \cdot w \cdot t \cdot V / L$
- **Curvature:** $\kappa = 1/\rho = 2 d_{31} V / t^2$
- **Neutral plane:** Located at the **bonded interface** for symmetric bimorphs.
- **Resonance frequency:** Scales as $t/L^2 \cdot \sqrt{Y/\rho_m}$ → longer = slower.
- **Three design levers:** (i) **Material choice** (PZT vs PMN-PT vs PVDF), (ii) **Geometry** (L, t, w), (iii) **Drive signal** (V amplitude, frequency, waveform).
- **Material benchmark:** PZT-5H gives d₃₁ ≈ −320 pm/V; PMN-PT gives d₃₁ ≈ −1000 pm/V (3× higher stroke).
- **Series vs parallel:** Series = fewer electrodes, higher drive voltage. Parallel = lower voltage, higher capacitance.
- **Applications in microrobotics:** Walking robot legs, micro-pumps, micro-grippers, flapping-wing MAVs, scanning mirrors, AFM probes.
- **Key advantage:** Solid-state, no backlash, sub-ms response, high force density.
- **Key limitation:** Small stroke (sub-millimetre) at safe voltages, requires HV amplifier, brittle ceramic prone to fatigue.
- **Exam formula must include:** $\delta$, $F_b$, $\kappa$, $f_1$, $\sigma$ — and the **factor of 2** in curvature.
- **Assumptions to always state:** Linear piezo regime, Euler–Bernoulli beam, perfect bonding, neutral plane at interface, identical layers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Motion Amplification in Plane
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Motion Amplification in Plane

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Motion Amplification in Plane** is the mechanism by which a small input displacement (typically at the micron-to-nanometer scale, generated by an actuator such as a piezoelectric stack, shape-memory alloy wire, or electrostatic comb drive) is converted into a larger, usable output displacement *within a single planar workspace* (the X–Y plane), **without the use of conventional rolling or sliding bearings**, by exploiting the elastic deformation of flexure hinges and compliant elements.

In the context of **KTU PERUT864 (Microrobotics)**, this term specifically addresses **compliant amplification topologies** — geometric arrangements of thin flexible segments (notches, beams, leaf springs) that magnify stroke via:
- **Geometric (kinematic) advantage** — analogous to a lever.
- **Buckling-mode interaction** — preloaded compliant segments that snap to amplify.
- **Bending-mode accumulation** — multiple small rotations summing into a large translation.

The defining performance metric is the **Displacement Amplification Ratio**, denoted:

$$R_a = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}}$$

For precision microrobotic positioners, a typical **R_a** ranges from **2× to 20×** for in-plane amplifiers; cascaded stages can reach **R_a > 100×**, but at the cost of lateral stiffness and bandwidth.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine you are trying to push open a heavy, stiff door. If you press right next to the hinges, you must push very hard and travel only a short distance. But if you push at the far edge of the door (away from the hinges), the door swings open easily and the far edge moves through a much larger arc. The hinge acts like a pivot (flexure), and the long door acts like the amplifier arm.

**Mapping the analogy to microrobotics:**

| Door Concept | Microrobotic Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Door (the lever) | Rigid compliant arm / amplifier link |
| Hinges | **Flexure notches** (cartilever / notch hinges) |
| Your push (small, near hinge) | Actuator input displacement (e.g., piezo stack) |
| Door's far edge (large arc) | Amplified output displacement |
| Heavy door (resistance) | Output load / stiffness to overcome |

The **single most important insight** is this: motion amplification in plane **does not create energy**. It simply *trades force for displacement*. The output force is reduced by the same factor as the displacement is magnified (minus losses).

$$F_{out} \approx \frac{F_{in}}{R_a} \quad \text{(ideal, lossless)}$$

This is the **conservation of virtual work** principle applied to compliant amplifiers, and it is the reason a piezo actuator (high force, small stroke ≈ **0.1 % strain**) can be coupled to an amplifier to drive a large microrobotic joint.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Young's Modulus of Silicon (Si): E ≈ 130–190 GPa** (orientation dependent).
- **Young's Modulus of Steel (typical flexure material): E ≈ 200 GPa**.
- **Yield strain for elastic flexure design: ε_y ≈ 0.1 % – 0.3 %**.
- **Maximum piezo free strain: ≈ 0.1 %** (thus a 10 mm stack yields ≈ 10 µm).
- **Typical notch-filleted flexure radius: r = 0.1 mm – 1 mm**.
- **Standard amplification ratios in literature: R_a = 2, 3, 5, 10, 20**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why In-Plane Motion Amplification? — The Engineering Driver

In microrobotics, the bottleneck is **actuator stroke**. Almost every micro-actuator (piezo, SMA, electrostatic, thermal) produces only **sub-100 µm displacement**. Yet many tasks (e.g., pick-and-place of a 1 mm die, optical fiber alignment over 200 µm range) require **hundreds of microns to millimeters** of motion. **Motion amplification in plane solves this mismatch** without resorting to:
- *Sliding bearings* — which introduce stiction, friction, wear, and particle generation (fatal in MEMS).
- *Gear trains* — which are extremely difficult to fabricate at the micro-scale and have backlash.

Instead, the entire amplifier is a **single monolithic flexure structure** — typically wire-EDM cut, laser-micromachined, or DRIE-etched — with no moving contacts.

## 2.2 The Three Classical In-Plane Amplifier Topologies

### Topology 1 — Lever Amplifier (Cartesian Bridge)

A simple rigid lever pivoted on a **flexure hinge** at one end. Input is applied near the pivot; output is taken at a longer moment arm.

**Geometry:**
- Input moment arm: $a$ (short).
- Output moment arm: $b$ (long).
- Amplification ratio:

$$R_a = \frac{b}{a}$$

**Stiffness (output side):**

$$k_{out} = \frac{E \cdot t \cdot h^3}{4 \cdot L^3} \quad \text{(for end-loaded cantilever of length L)}$$

### Topology 2 — Diamond / Rhombus Amplifier (Parallelogram)

Two flexure hinges connected by rigid links forming a parallelogram. As one diagonal shortens by $\delta_{in}$, the perpendicular diagonal extends by $\delta_{out}$.

$$R_a = \frac{L_{out}}{L_{in}} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos\theta}$$

where $\theta$ is the half-angle of the diamond at equilibrium.

### Topology 3 — Bridge-Type (Scott-Russell) Amplifier

A symmetric arrangement with **two pairs of flexures**. Inputs push inward on a central node; outputs move outward perpendicular to the input axis. This is the **most common topology in micromanipulators**.

For a symmetric bridge amplifier with horizontal flexures of length $L_f$ and vertical amplification arms of length $L_a$:

$$R_a = \frac{L_a}{L_f} \quad \text{(first-order approximation)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU board examinations, the **bridge amplifier (Topology 3)** is the most frequently tested. Memorize its kinematic relation.

---

## 2.3 The Pseudorigid-Body Model (PRBM) — Howland's Method

To analyze flexure hinges analytically, KTU examiners expect familiarity with the **Pseudorigid-Body Model** (Howell & Midha, 1994, codified as a standard tool). Each small-angle flexure is replaced by:
- A rigid link of length $\gamma \cdot L$ pivoted at the notch,
- With a **characteristic radius factor** $\gamma \approx 0.85$ for small-filleted hinges.

This converts a complex continuum-mechanics problem into a **planar linkage problem** solvable by standard kinematics.

$$R_a^{PRBM} = f(\gamma, L_1, L_2, \theta_1, \theta_2)$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Amplification Ratio | $R_a$ | $\delta_{out} \,/\, \delta_{in}$ | dimensionless | Primary design metric |
| 2 | Lever Amplifier Ratio | $R_{a,\text{lever}}$ | $b \,/\, a$ | dimensionless | $b$ = output arm, $a$ = input arm |
| 3 | Bridge Amplifier Ratio (1st order) | $R_{a,\text{bridge}}$ | $L_a \,/\, L_f$ | dimensionless | Most-tested in KTU |
| 4 | Diamond Amplifier Ratio | $R_{a,\text{dia}}$ | $(L_{out}/L_{in}) \cdot (1/\cos\theta)$ | dimensionless | $\theta$ = half-angle |
| 5 | Virtual-work force relation | $F_{out}$ | $F_{in} \,/\, R_a$ | N | Lossless ideal |
| 6 | Cantilever tip deflection | $\delta$ | $F L^3 \,/\, (3 E I)$ | m | Euler-Bernoulli |
| 7 | Second moment of area (rectangular) | $I$ | $b h^3 \,/\, 12$ | m⁴ | $b$ = out-of-plane width, $h$ = thickness |
| 8 | Notch hinge rotational stiffness | $K_\theta$ | $2 E b t^{5/2} \,/\, (9 \sqrt{\pi} r^{1/2})$ | N·m/rad | $t$ = notch thickness, $r$ = fillet radius |
| 9 | Compliance matrix entry | $C_{ij}$ | $\partial u_i \,/\, \partial F_j$ | m/N | Symmetric: $C_{ij} = C_{ji}$ (Maxwell-Betti) |
| 10 | Buckling load (Euler) | $P_{cr}$ | $\pi^2 E I \,/\, (K L)^2$ | N | $K$ = effective length factor |
| 11 | Natural frequency (1st mode) | $\omega_n$ | $\sqrt{k \,/\, m}$ | rad/s | Sets amplifier bandwidth |
| 12 | PRBM radius factor | $\gamma$ | $\approx 0.85$ | dimensionless | Small-angle flexure approximation |
| 13 | Strain energy stored | $U$ | $(1/2) k \delta^2$ | J | Useful for sizing actuators |
| 14 | Lateral stiffness ratio | $k_L \,/\, k_A$ | $R_a^{-2}$ (ideal) | dimensionless | Lateral collapse with amplification |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions Checklist (KTU expects):**
> 1. Input and output stages must be **kinematically grounded** (anchored to a fixed frame).
> 2. Flexure thickness must satisfy $t \geq 2r$ for valid notch theory.
> 3. Maximum principal strain $\sigma_{max}/E \leq \varepsilon_{yield}$.
> 4. Buckling load $P_{cr} \geq 3 \times$ applied axial preload.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In-plane motion amplification is the **backbone of every modern micromanipulator**:
- **Micro-assembly robots** for watch / MEMS component placement.
- **Scanning probe microscopes (AFM/STM)** — flexure scanners provide sub-nm resolution over tens of microns.
- **Surgical robotics at the laryngeal / ophthalmic scale** — piezo-driven flexure stages.
- **Optical fiber aligners** — require micron-level travel over millimeters of workspace.
- **KTU Lab Context:** the piezoelectric-actuated 2-DOF flexure stage typically demonstrated in the Microrobotics / Precision Engineering lab module.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amplification Ratio of the Symmetric Bridge Amplifier

**Problem Setup:** A symmetric bridge amplifier has two horizontal flexures (length $L_f$, stiffness $k_f$ each) and two vertical amplification arms (length $L_a$, treated as rigid). The input force $F_{in}$ is applied horizontally at the central node; the output displacement $\delta_{out}$ is measured vertically at the output tip.

**Assumptions:**
- Small-angle rotations ($\theta \ll 1$ rad).
- Flexure notches act as ideal rotational springs of stiffness $K_\theta$.
- Rigid links carry only axial force (no bending).

### Step 1 — Free-Body of the Central Input Node

The input node is in horizontal equilibrium under $F_{in}$ and the two horizontal restoring forces from the flexures.

Each flexure rotates by $\theta_f$ under input displacement $\delta_{in}$:

$$\theta_f = \frac{\delta_{in}}{L_f}$$

### Step 2 — Moment Equilibrium of the Amplification Arm

Each rigid amplification arm of length $L_a$ rotates by the same small angle $\theta_f$ (since it is rigidly attached to the flexure pivot). The vertical tip displacement is therefore:

$$\delta_{out} = L_a \cdot \theta_f = L_a \cdot \frac{\delta_{in}}{L_f}$$

### Step 3 — Amplification Ratio

$$\begin{aligned}
R_a &= \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} \\
&= \frac{L_a}{L_f}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Including Flexure Compliance (Refined Model)

If the flexures themselves have finite rotational stiffness $K_\theta$, the input displacement is **shared** between flexure rotation and a rigid-body shift $x_{rigid}$. Let $\delta_{in} = x_{rigid} + L_f \theta_f$.

Moment equilibrium at the input node gives:

$$F_{in} \cdot L_f = 2 K_\theta \cdot \theta_f$$

Solving for $\theta_f$:

$$\theta_f = \frac{F_{in} \cdot L_f}{2 K_\theta}$$

Thus:

$$\delta_{out} = L_a \cdot \theta_f = \frac{F_{in} \cdot L_a \cdot L_f}{2 K_\theta}$$

The effective input stiffness at the input port is:

$$k_{in} = \frac{F_{in}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{2 K_\theta}{L_f^2}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** Higher flexure stiffness $K_\theta$ → stiffer amplifier → less stroke consumed by flexure bending → more stroke transmitted to output.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force Transmission & Virtual Work

For any lossless compliant amplifier, virtual work gives:

$$F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot \delta_{out}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{out} &= F_{in} \cdot \frac{\delta_{in}}{\delta_{out}} \\
&= \frac{F_{in}}{R_a}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):**

A piezo stack provides $F_{in} = 1000$ N and $\delta_{in} = 10\ \mu\text{m}$. A bridge amplifier has $L_a = 30$ mm, $L_f = 6$ mm.

Step 1 — Compute ratio:

$$R_a = \frac{30}{6} = 5$$

Step 2 — Compute output stroke:

$$\delta_{out} = 5 \times 10\ \mu\text{m} = 50\ \mu\text{m}$$

Step 3 — Compute output force:

$$F_{out} = \frac{1000}{5} = 200\ \text{N}$$

> **Note for students:** This lossless assumption is *ideal*. In practice, flexure bending absorbs **10 %–30 %** of input work as elastic strain energy. So a more realistic expression is:

$$F_{out} = \eta \cdot \frac{F_{in}}{R_a}, \quad \eta \approx 0.70 \text{ to } 0.90$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lateral Stiffness Collapse (Why Amplifiers Get Squirrely)

The lateral (off-axis) stiffness of an in-plane amplifier **decreases with the square of the amplification ratio**. Derivation via the compliance matrix:

For an ideal symmetric amplifier:

$$\begin{aligned}
k_{axial} &= k_0 \quad \text{(input axis stiffness)} \\
k_{lateral} &= \frac{k_0}{R_a^{2}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Proof by energy equivalence:**

Total strain energy at axial input $\delta_{in}$:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} k_{axial} \delta_{in}^{2}$$

The same energy expressed in terms of lateral output $\delta_{lat} = \delta_{in} / R_a$:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} k_{lateral} \delta_{lat}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} k_{lateral} \frac{\delta_{in}^{2}}{R_a^{2}}$$

Equating the two expressions:

$$\begin{aligned}
k_{axial} \delta_{in}^{2} &= k_{lateral} \frac{\delta_{in}^{2}}{R_a^{2}} \\
k_{lateral} &= k_{axial} \cdot R_a^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wait — sign correction.** The lateral stiffness of an amplifier **decreases**, not increases, with $R_a$. The correct statement is: the **lateral displacement for a given lateral force** increases with $R_a^{2}$, i.e., $k_{lateral} = k_0 / R_a^{2}$. This is because lateral motion of the output corresponds to a *parasitic* rotation of the flexures which is amplified by the long arms. **This is the most-asked KTU conceptual trap.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Compliance Matrix for a 2-DOF Bridge Amplifier

For a planar 2-DOF compliant amplifier with input axes $x$ and $y$ at the central node, the compliance matrix is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} \delta_x \\ \delta_y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} C_{xx} & C_{xy} \\ C_{yx} & C_{yy} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} F_x \\ F_y \end{bmatrix}$$

For a symmetric bridge:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_{xx} &= \frac{2 L_a^{2}}{K_\theta} + \frac{L_f}{E A_f} \\
C_{yy} &= \frac{2 L_f^{2}}{K_\theta} + \frac{L_a}{E A_a} \\
C_{xy} &= C_{yx} = 0 \quad \text{(ideal symmetry)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Maxwell-Betti reciprocity:** $C_{xy} = C_{yx}$ is **always true** for any linear elastic system and is a frequent KTU true/false or 2-mark question.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Full KTU-Style Problem

> A symmetric bridge-type in-plane motion amplifier has horizontal flexure length $L_f = 8$ mm, vertical amplification arm $L_a = 40$ mm, flexure notch thickness $t = 0.4$ mm, fillet radius $r = 0.5$ mm, out-of-plane width $b = 10$ mm, and Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa.
>
> **Compute:**
> (a) The displacement amplification ratio.
> (b) The flexure rotational stiffness $K_\theta$.
> (c) If the piezo input provides 15 µm, what is the output displacement?

**Solution:**

**(a) Amplification Ratio**

$$R_a = \frac{L_a}{L_f} = \frac{40}{8} = 5$$

**(b) Rotational Stiffness** (using the standard notch-hinge formula):

$$\begin{aligned}
K_\theta &= \frac{2 E b t^{5/2}}{9 \sqrt{\pi}\, r^{1/2}} \\
&= \frac{2 \times (200 \times 10^{9}) \times (10 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.4 \times 10^{-3})^{5/2}}{9 \sqrt{\pi}\, (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{1/2}}
\end{aligned}$$

Compute each factor:

$$t^{5/2} = (0.4 \times 10^{-3})^{2.5} = 1.016 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m}^{5/2}$$

$$r^{1/2} = (0.5 \times 10^{-3})^{0.5} = 2.236 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m}^{1/2}$$

$$\sqrt{\pi} = 1.7725$$

Numerator:

$$2 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 10 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.016 \times 10^{-8} = 40.64$$

Denominator:

$$9 \times 1.7725 \times 2.236 \times 10^{-2} = 0.3567$$

$$\boxed{K_\theta = \frac{40.64}{0.3567} \approx 113.9\ \text{N·m/rad}}$$

**(c) Output Displacement**

$$\delta_{out} = R_a \times \delta_{in} = 5 \times 15\ \mu\text{m} = 75\ \mu\text{m}$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Diagram — Functional Architecture of an In-Plane Motion Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Actuator Layer] -->|delta_in small, F_in large| B[Preload Stage]
    B --> C[Flexure Hinge Bank]
    C -->|rotates by theta_f| D[Rigid Amplification Arm Bank]
    D -->|delta_out = R_a times delta_in| E[Output Workspace Plane XY]
    E --> F[Microrobotic End Effector]

    subgraph G[Boundary Conditions]
        G1[Anchored Frame] -.-> C
        G2[Strain Limit Guard] -.-> D
        G3[Buckling Guard] -.-> C
    end

    subgraph H[Performance Monitors]
        H1[Amplification Ratio R_a] -.-> D
        H2[Lateral Stiffness k_lat] -.-> D
        H3[Natural Frequency omega_n] -.-> E
    end
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Bridge Amplifier Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Frame[Fixed Anchored Frame]
        F1[Top Left Anchor]
        F2[Top Right Anchor]
        F3[Bottom Left Anchor]
        F4[Bottom Right Anchor]
    end

    subgraph FlexSet[Flexure Hinge Set]
        H1[Hinge A top]
        H2[Hinge B top]
        H3[Hinge C bottom]
        H4[Hinge D bottom]
    end

    subgraph AmpSet[Amplification Arm Set]
        R1[Left Arm Length La]
        R2[Right Arm Length La]
    end

    subgraph InputNode[Input Node]
        I[Central Input Force F_in delta_in]
    end

    subgraph OutputNode[Output Node]
        O[Output Tip delta_out]
    end

    F1 --> H1
    F2 --> H2
    F3 --> H3
    F4 --> H4

    H1 --> R1
    H2 --> R2
    H3 --> R1
    H4 --> R2

    R1 --> I
    R2 --> I
    I --> O
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flow — Design Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Specify Required Output Stroke delta_out] --> Q1{Amplification Ratio Target}
    Q1 -->|R_a between 2 and 5| T1[Use Lever Amplifier]
    Q1 -->|R_a between 5 and 20| T2[Use Bridge Amplifier]
    Q1 -->|R_a greater than 20| T3[Cascade Two Stages]
    T1 --> C1[Check Lateral Stiffness k_lat]
    T2 --> C1
    T3 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Check Strain Limit epsilon less than 0.3 percent]
    C2 --> C3[Check Buckling Load P_cr]
    C3 --> D[Final Geometry La, Lf, t, r]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]`**

### Q1. Define motion amplification in plane as applied to compliant microrobotic mechanisms. Mention any two amplifier topologies.

**Model Answer:**

Motion amplification in plane refers to the technique of magnifying a small input displacement (generated by a micro-actuator) into a larger output displacement along a planar axis, **using only elastic deformation of flexure hinges**, without sliding or rolling contacts.

Two common topologies are:
1. **Lever (Cartesian) amplifier** — uses a moment-arm ratio $R_a = b/a$.
2. **Bridge (Scott-Russell) amplifier** — uses $R_a = L_a / L_f$ geometry.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse *in-plane* amplification with *out-of-plane* (Z-axis) amplification. In-plane = motion remains in the X–Y plane. Out-of-plane = motion leaves the plane (vertical amplification). KTU explicitly tests this distinction.

---

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]`**

### Q2. State the virtual work principle as applied to motion amplification. Derive the relation between input and output force.

**Model Answer:**

The virtual work principle states that for a lossless compliant amplifier:

$$F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot \delta_{out}$$

Dividing by $\delta_{in}$ on both sides and using $R_a = \delta_{out}/\delta_{in}$:

$$F_{in} = F_{out} \cdot R_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{out} = \frac{F_{in}}{R_a}$$

Hence, **force is reduced by the same factor as displacement is magnified**. *(3 marks: 1 for statement, 1 for derivation, 1 for concluding remark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a **symmetric bridge-type in-plane motion amplifier**. Derive the expression for the displacement amplification ratio in terms of the geometric parameters. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A bridge amplifier has $L_f = 6$ mm, $L_a = 36$ mm. A piezoelectric actuator provides $F_{in} = 800$ N and $\delta_{in} = 12\ \mu\text{m}$. Compute (i) the amplification ratio, (ii) the output displacement, and (iii) the theoretical output force. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle & Derivation**

A symmetric bridge-type amplifier consists of two horizontal flexure hinges of length $L_f$ anchored at their outer ends to a fixed frame. Between their inner ends, a rigid amplification arm of length $L_a$ is mounted. When the input node is pushed inward by $\delta_{in}$, each flexure rotates by a small angle $\theta_f$. Since the amplification arm is rigid and rigidly connected to the flexure, it rotates by the same angle. The output tip thus sweeps through:

$$\delta_{out} = L_a \cdot \theta_f = L_a \cdot \frac{\delta_{in}}{L_f}$$

$$\boxed{R_a = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{L_a}{L_f}}$$

**Schematic (text-representation):**

```
       [Anchor]----L_f----[Input]
                    Hinge     |
                              |  Rigid arm L_a (rotates by theta_f)
                              |
                            [Output]
                            delta_out
```

The geometry allows the **input force** to be applied along the short axis while the **output displacement** is taken along the long arm, giving mechanical advantage in displacement.

**(b) Numerical Computation**

Given: $L_f = 6$ mm, $L_a = 36$ mm, $F_{in} = 800$ N, $\delta_{in} = 12\ \mu\text{m}$.

**(i) Amplification Ratio:**

$$R_a = \frac{L_a}{L_f} = \frac{36}{6} = 6$$

**(ii) Output Displacement:**

$$\delta_{out} = R_a \times \delta_{in} = 6 \times 12\ \mu\text{m} = 72\ \mu\text{m}$$

**(iii) Theoretical Output Force:**

$$F_{out} = \frac{F_{in}}{R_a} = \frac{800}{6} \approx 133.3\ \text{N}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Students frequently **omit units** in numerical answers → -1 mark.
> 2. Students may **invert the ratio** ($L_f / L_a$ instead of $L_a / L_f$) → full 7-mark part loss.
> 3. Forgetting to mention the **small-angle assumption** → -1 mark.
> 4. Forgetting that virtual-work force reduction assumes **lossless** behavior → -1 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **pseudorigid-body model (PRBM)** approach to analyzing flexure hinges. How does it simplify motion amplification analysis? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Discuss the **trade-off between amplification ratio and lateral stiffness** in in-plane motion amplifiers. A microrobotic stage requires 100 µm output stroke. The available piezo provides 5 µm. What minimum single-stage amplification ratio is required, and what is the impact on lateral stiffness if the original lateral stiffness was 5 N/µm? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**(a) Pseudorigid-Body Model (PRBM)**

The PRBM, formalized by Howell and Midha, replaces each continuum flexure hinge with:
- A **rigid link** of length $\gamma \cdot L$, pivoted at the notch.
- A **rotational spring** of stiffness $K_\theta$ at the pivot.
- A characteristic radius factor $\gamma \approx 0.85$ for small-filleted hinges.

**Simplifications gained:**
- The structure becomes a **planar rigid-body linkage** with springs at joints.
- Standard kinematics (loop-closure equations) can be applied directly.
- No partial differential equations (continuum mechanics) needed.
- Closed-form expressions for $R_a$ as a function of link lengths and angles are easily derived.
- Computational cost drops from FEM hours to **algebra seconds**.

**Limitation:** PRBM is accurate only for **small rotations** ($\theta < 10°$). For large deflections, FEM or nonlinear PRBM (nPRBM) is required.

**(b) Trade-off and Numerical Analysis**

**Required amplification ratio:**

$$R_a = \frac{\delta_{out}}{\delta_{in}} = \frac{100\ \mu\text{m}}{5\ \mu\text{m}} = 20$$

A single-stage amplifier with $R_a = 20$ is feasible but requires careful geometric proportions. Alternatively, **two cascaded stages** with $R_a = \sqrt{20} \approx 4.47$ each would be more robust.

**Impact on Lateral Stiffness:**

For an ideal lossless amplifier:

$$k_{lateral} = \frac{k_{lateral,0}}{R_a^{2}}$$

Substituting:

$$k_{lateral} = \frac{5\ \text{N}/\mu\text{m}}{20^{2}} = \frac{5}{400} = 0.0125\ \text{N}/\mu\text{m}$$

**Engineering implication:** the lateral stiffness drops from **5 N/µm to 12.5 mN/µm** — a **400× reduction**. This makes the stage highly susceptible to off-axis disturbance forces (tooling weight, cable drag). Practical design must either:
- Use cascaded stages (each with lower $R_a$),
- Add **lateral stiffness reinforcement** (e.g., nested parallelogram guides),
- Or accept reduced off-axis load capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Q2):**
> 1. Students often state "$k_{lateral}$ increases with $R_a$" — **wrong**; it decreases as $1/R_a^{2}$.
> 2. PRBM factor $\gamma = 0.85$ must be **cited explicitly**; do not write "approximately 1."
> 3. Always specify the **range of validity** (small angle) for PRBM.
> 4. In trade-off discussions, mention **bandwidth** also collapses with $R_a$ (since $k$ drops but $m$ stays ≈ same, $\omega_n \propto \sqrt{k/m}$ falls).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Motion Amplification in Plane)**

- [ ] **Definition:** Amplification of small input displacement into larger output displacement **within the X-Y plane**, using only elastic flexure deformation — no sliding/rolling bearings.
- [ ] **Three core topologies:** Lever amplifier, Bridge (Scott-Russell) amplifier, Diamond/Rhombus amplifier.
- [ ] **Primary metric:** Displacement amplification ratio $R_a = \delta_{out}/\delta_{in}$.
- [ ] **Bridge amplifier formula:** $R_a = L_a / L_f$ (most-tested).
- [ ] **Lever amplifier formula:** $R_a = b / a$.
- [ ] **Diamond amplifier formula:** $R_a = (L_{out}/L_{in}) \cdot (1/\cos\theta)$.
- [ ] **Virtual work principle:** $F_{in} \cdot \delta_{in} = F_{out} \cdot \delta_{out}$ ⇒ $F_{out} = F_{in}/R_a$ (lossless).
- [ ] **Flexure notch rotational stiffness:** $K_\theta = 2 E b t^{5/2} / (9\sqrt{\pi}\,r^{1/2})$.
- [ ] **Cantilever tip deflection:** $\delta = F L^{3}/(3 E I)$, with $I = b h^{3}/12$.
- [ ] **Lateral stiffness collapse:** $k_{lateral} = k_{lateral,0} / R_a^{2}$ — **the amplifier becomes laterally soft.**
- [ ] **Buckling guard:** axial preload $P \leq P_{cr}/3$, with $P_{cr} = \pi^{2} E I / (K L)^{2}$.
- [ ] **PRBM approximation:** small-filleted flexure ↔ rigid link of length $0.85 L$ with rotational spring $K_\theta$.
- [ ] **Compliance matrix symmetry:** $C_{ij} = C_{ji}$ (Maxwell-Betti reciprocity — always true, often tested).
- [ ] **Energy conservation:** amplifier does not create energy; it *trades force for displacement*.
- [ ] **Efficiency:** real amplifiers lose **10 %–30 %** of input work as elastic strain energy in flexures.
- [ ] **Typical $R_a$ range:** single-stage 2–20; cascaded up to 100+.
- [ ] **Design constraint:** maximum principal strain $\varepsilon_{max} \leq 0.3\,\%$ for elastic operation.
- [ ] **Bandwidth impact:** $\omega_n \propto \sqrt{k_{eff}/m}$; as $k_{eff}$ falls with $R_a^{2}$, bandwidth also drops.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrostatic Actuator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrostatic Actuator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Electrostatic Actuator** is a class of microactuator that converts electrical energy into controlled mechanical displacement (force or motion) by exploiting the **Coulomb attraction/repulsion** between electrically charged conductors separated by a dielectric medium (typically air or vacuum). In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Microrobotics (Module 3), electrostatic actuation is treated as the **most widely exploited transduction mechanism in MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems)** because the underlying electrostatic force scales **inversely with characteristic dimension squared**, making it inherently favorable at micro- and nano-scales.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> "Electrostatic actuators generate motion through the attractive force developed between oppositely charged electrodes. They form the building block of micromotors, comb drives, and scratch-drive actuators used in walking microrobots."

The generalized expression for the electrostatic force on a movable electrode in a lossless system is given by:

$$F = +\frac{1}{2} V^{2} \frac{dC}{dx}$$

where $V$ is the applied voltage and $dC/dx$ is the rate of change of capacitance with respect to displacement.
