### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two flat plastic rulers. You rub them and bring the charged sides close — they snap toward each other. Now, instead of plastic, take two **microscopic metal plates** separated by an air gap the width of a hair, connect a battery, and the plates pull together with a precisely controllable force. That "snap" is the **electrostatic actuator** in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** A *parallel-plate capacitor* is the heart of an electrostatic actuator. The capacitor wants to maximize its stored energy by *minimizing its air gap*, just like a spring wants to relax. We control this "relaxation" electronically using a voltage source.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following values are mandatory for KTU board numerical problems:

| Symbol | Quantity | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\varepsilon_0$ | Permittivity of free space | $8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m |
| $\varepsilon_r$ | Relative permittivity (air) | $\approx 1.0006$ | dimensionless |
| $E_{bd}$ | Dielectric breakdown of air | $3 \times 10^{6}$ | V/m |
| $E_{bd,SiO_2}$ | Breakdown field of SiO₂ | $\approx 10^{9}$ | V/m |

The breakdown voltage sets a **hard upper limit** on the operating voltage and therefore on the maximum achievable force density. This is a critical KTU design constraint.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Charge accumulation:** A potential difference $V$ applied between two conductors separated by a dielectric produces surface charges of opposite sign on the facing electrodes.
2. **Field generation:** These charges create an electric field $E$ in the gap, with energy density $u = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$ per unit volume.
3. **Coulomb force:** The oppositely charged surfaces attract, producing a normal (and in some geometries, tangential) force proportional to $V^2$.
4. **Mechanical response:** The movable electrode deflects against an elastic restoring force (e.g., a folded flexure spring of stiffness $k$).
5. **Equilibrium:** At steady state, electrostatic force = spring restoring force, defining the operating point.

> [!NOTE]
> **Bistable note for KTU:** Because the force is proportional to $V^2$, electrostatic actuators respond identically to $+V$ and $-V$. They cannot, by themselves, produce a *directionally* biased motion in DC mode — that is why stepping actuators (inchworm, SDA) need a mechanical ratchet or friction asymmetry.

### 2.2 Why Electrostatics Favour the Microscale

The force density on a parallel plate is:

$$P = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^2}{2 g^{2}}$$

Although this expression does **not** explicitly contain a length scale, the breakdown field $E_{bd} = V/g$ imposes a fixed maximum $V \propto g$. Substituting:

$$P_{max} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 E_{bd}^{2}}{2} \approx 40 \text{ kPa (air gap)}$$

Since $P_{max}$ is **size-independent**, but the *absolute* force $F = P \cdot A$ decreases as $A \sim L^2$, scaling down is acceptable. Meanwhile, the *spring stiffness* of flexures also scales as $L$, so the **deflection $\delta = F/k$** remains comparable — but the **speed** improves because mass $\sim L^3$ while force $\sim L^2$, giving acceleration $\sim 1/L$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the single most important KTU conceptual takeaway: **Electrostatic actuation is one of the few mechanisms whose performance per unit volume actually *improves* as device size shrinks.**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No | Parameter | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitance (parallel plate) | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g}$ | F | $A$ = overlap area, $g$ = gap |
| 2 | Stored energy | $W = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} Q V$ | J | Energy method basis |
| 3 | Electrostatic force | $F = \tfrac{1}{2} V^{2} \, dC/dx$ | N | Sign convention: $+x$ is attractive direction |
| 4 | Attractive pressure | $P = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 V^{2}}{2 g^{2}}$ | Pa | Pressure between parallel plates |
| 5 | Comb-drive force | $F = \dfrac{N \, t \, \varepsilon_0 V^{2}}{d}$ | N | $N$ = number of finger gaps, $t$ = thickness, $d$ = gap |
| 6 | Energy density | $u = \tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^{2}$ | J/m³ | Field-energy picture |
| 7 | Pull-in gap (parallel plate) | $g_{pi} = \dfrac{2}{3} g_0$ | m | $g_0$ is the zero-voltage gap |
| 8 | Pull-in voltage | $V_{pi} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k g_0^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$ | V | Depends on spring stiffness $k$ |
| 9 | SDA step size | $\Delta x \approx \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 L V^{2}}{2 E t^{2}}$ | m | Per driving pulse |
| 10 | Speed scaling | $a \sim 1/L$, $\omega \sim \sqrt{k/m} \sim \sqrt{1/L}$ | m/s², rad/s | Acceleration up, natural freq up |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In all KTU board answers, **never** quote the force as $F = V^{2} \, dC/dx$ — it is always $\tfrac{1}{2}V^{2} \, dC/dx$. The factor of $1/2$ is the most commonly missed half-mark.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **RF MEMS switches:** Electrostatic actuation gives sub-microsecond switching.
- **Micromirrors (DLP):** Texas Instruments' DMD chip uses electrostatic tilting mirrors.
- **Walking microrobots:** Scratch-drive and inchworm actuators move silicon walkers across a substrate.
- **Probe-based data storage (IBM Millipede):** Electrostatic cantilever array for thermomechanical writing.
- **Micro-grippers and micro-valves:** Precise force control with negligible power consumption in the static state.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Force on a Parallel-Plate Capacitor (Energy Method)

**Given:** Two parallel plates of area $A$, initial gap $g$, applied voltage $V$.

**Step 1 — Capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g}$$

**Step 2 — Energy stored in the capacitor at constant voltage:**

$$W_e = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{g}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical work extracted when gap decreases by $dg$:** As $g$ shrinks, $C$ increases, so the source delivers additional charge to keep $V$ constant. The total mechanical work done by the field equals the *decrease* in co-energy (or equivalently, the negative gradient of stored energy with respect to displacement at constant $V$):

$$F = +\left.\frac{\partial W_e}{\partial (-g)}\right|_{V} = -\left.\frac{\partial W_e}{\partial g}\right|_{V}$$

Taking the derivative:

$$\frac{\partial W_e}{\partial g} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 A V^{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{g^{2}}\right) = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 g^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Attractive force (positive value pulling the plates together):**

$$F = - \frac{\partial W_e}{\partial g} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 g^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Pressure form (force per unit area):**

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^{2}}{2 g^{2}} = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^{2}$$

where $E = V/g$ is the inter-plate electric field.

> [!NOTE]
> The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$ is identical to the energy density of the field. Physically, the attractive pressure equals the energy density stored in the gap — this is a Maxwell stress-tensor result for an ideal parallel-plate geometry.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pull-In Instability Condition

**Setup:** A movable plate of mass $m$ suspended by a linear spring of stiffness $k$, opposing the electrostatic attraction.

**Equation of motion (1-D, static balance):**

$$k \, \delta = F_{elec} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 (g_0 - \delta)^{2}}$$

**Step 1 — Non-dimensionalize:** Let $x = \delta / g_0$, so $g = g_0(1-x)$:

$$k \, g_0 x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 g_0^{2} (1-x)^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Define the normalized voltage** $\alpha = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 k g_0^{3}}$:

$$\alpha = x(1-x)^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium extrema:** Differentiate with respect to $x$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{d\alpha}{dx} = (1-x)^{2} + x \cdot 2(1-x)(-1) = (1-x)\big[(1-x) - 2x\big] = (1-x)(1-3x) = 0$$

This yields $x = 1$ (trivial: plates collapsed) and $x = 1/3$.

**Step 4 — Stable equilibrium exists only for $x \le 1/3$**, i.e., when the gap has closed by at most one-third. Beyond that, the electrostatic force grows faster than the linear spring can compensate → **pull-in instability**.

**Step 5 — Pull-in gap and voltage:**

$$g_{pi} = g_0 \left(1 - \tfrac{1}{3}\right) = \tfrac{2}{3} g_0$$

$$V_{pi} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^{3}}{27 \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common student error is to state "the plates touch at pull-in." They do *not* — pull-in occurs when the gap closes to $2/3$ of the original value. Beyond this, no static equilibrium exists.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Force in a Lateral Comb-Drive Actuator

**Geometry:** Two interdigitated comb fingers, $N$ gaps, finger thickness $t$ (out-of-plane), in-plane gap $d$, applied voltage $V$.

**Step 1 — Capacitance of one finger pair (side-view, length overlap $L$):**

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 t L}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Force per unit overlap length (lateral direction $x$):**

$$F_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} V^{2} \frac{dC_1}{dx} = \tfrac{1}{2} V^{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 t}{d}$$

Note: the derivative is **independent of $x$** because moving the fingers sideways does not change the gap — only the overlap changes the *engaged* length. (The engaged portion contributes a constant derivative; the disengaged portion contributes zero.)

**Step 3 — Total force for $N$ gaps in parallel:**

$$F_{comb} = N \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 t V^{2}}{2 d} = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 t V^{2}}{2 d}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU key insight:** Unlike a parallel-plate actuator, the comb-drive force is **independent of displacement** — it is a "constant-force" device over a large travel range. This is why comb drives are the workhorse of long-stroke linear micromotors.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Scratch-Drive Actuator (SDA) Step Size

**Geometry:** A flexible plate of length $L$, thickness $t$, Young's modulus $E$, pressed against a substrate by a normal force $F_N$, with an electrostatic bushing driving it sideways by a voltage pulse $V$.

**Step 1 — Tangential electrostatic force per unit length:**

$$f_t = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^{2}}{2 d}$$

where $d$ is the bushing-substrate gap.

**Step 2 — Bending curvature from moment equilibrium** (treated as a beam on elastic foundation with stiffness $K_f$):

$$\frac{d^{2} y}{dx^{2}} = \frac{M}{E I}, \quad I = \frac{t^{3} w}{12} \text{ (w = width)}$$

**Step 3 — Net tangential step per pulse:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{\varepsilon_0 L V^{2}}{2 E t^{2}}$$

The plate deflects, the leading edge latches by friction, and when voltage is removed, the plate relaxes forward — yielding a **per-pulse step** that can be controlled in the **nanometre range**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering significance:** SDAs are used in **walking microrobots** (e.g., the Berkeley micro-hoe and CMU's micro-walker). They can achieve step sizes of 20–100 nm with 100 V pulses, making them the highest-resolution stepping actuator in microscale robotics.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Electrostatic Actuator System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    VCTRL[Voltage Source V] --> CONV[Charge Controller]
    CONV --> ELEC[Electrode Pair]
    ELEC --> GAP[Air Gap Capacitor Cg]
    GAP --> COUL[Coulomb Force Generator]
    COUL --> SPRG[Mechanical Spring k]
    SPRG --> MASS[Movable Mass m]
    MASS --> OUT[Motion Output x]
    FB[Position Sensor] --> MASS
    FB --> CONV
    COUL --> FFE[Force Feq 0.5 V2 dC/dx]
    FFE --> PMAX[Pull-In Check gpi 2 over 3 g0]
    PMAX --> SAFE{Safe Operating Region}
    SAFE -->|Yes| OUT
    SAFE -->|No| ALARM[Pull-In Alarm]
    PMAX --> ENRG[Energy Density 0.5 eps0 E2]
    ENRG --> SCL[Scaling Law F scales as L2]
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Mapping — Comb-Drive Linear Micromotor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VIN[DC Voltage Input] --> MUX[Phase Sequencer]
    MUX --> P1[Stator Phase A]
    MUX --> P2[Stator Phase B]
    MUX --> P3[Stator Phase C]
    P1 --> F1[Comb Fingers Group 1]
    P2 --> F2[Comb Fingers Group 2]
    P3 --> F3[Comb Fingers Group 3]
    F1 --> R[Slider Rotor with NFingers]
    F2 --> R
    F3 --> R
    R --> OUT[Linear Displacement]
    R --> FB2[Position Feedback]
    FB2 --> MUX
    R --> FO[Force Output Fcomb N eps0 t V2 over 2d]
```

### 4.3 Concept Map: Electrostatic Actuator Family in KTU Module 3

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Electrostatic Actuator Family]
    ROOT --> A1[Parallel Plate]
    ROOT --> A2[Comb Drive]
    ROOT --> A3[Scratch Drive SDA]
    ROOT --> A4[Inchworm Motor]
    ROOT --> A5[Torsional Mirror]
    A1 --> D1[Pull-In Instability]
    A1 --> D2[Energy Method Force]
    A2 --> D3[Long Stroke Constant Force]
    A2 --> D4[Rotational and Linear Variants]
    A3 --> D5[Nanometre Step Resolution]
    A3 --> D6[Walking Microrobots]
    A4 --> D7[Clamp and Push Cycle]
    A4 --> D8[Open Loop Position Control]
    A5 --> D9[Optical MEMS Scanners]
    A5 --> D10[DLP Projection]
```

### 4.4 Schematic Representation (ASCII Schematic)

```
   Fixed Electrode (Top)         ___________
   ─────────────────────         |          |  Voltage V
   |    Air gap g      |   <--->|  +Q  -Q  |
   |____________________|        |__________|
   Movable Electrode (Bottom)
            |
            |  Displacement δ
            v
   ──────[ Spring k ]──────  →  Wall (ground)

   F_electrostatic = ε₀ A V² / (2 g²)        (attractive, downward)
   F_spring        = k δ                      (restoring, upward)
   At equilibrium:  F_electrostatic = F_spring
   Pull-in occurs at:  δ = g₀ / 3   →   g_pi = (2/3) g₀
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define an electrostatic actuator. Why is it preferred over electromagnetic actuation at the microscale?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An electrostatic actuator is a device that produces mechanical motion using the Coulomb force between oppositely charged electrodes. It is preferred at the microscale because **(1)** the electrostatic force density is **size-independent** (it depends only on the electric field squared, and breakdown field does not depend on geometry), while **(2)** electromagnetic forces scale poorly (magnetic field $\sim I$ and current density limits scale unfavorably), and **(3)** electrostatic devices are **CMOS-compatible**, requiring only conductive layers and a dielectric gap, with no need for coils or magnetic materials. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*State the expression for the electrostatic force in terms of capacitance. What does the factor ½ represent physically?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The general expression is $F = \tfrac{1}{2}V^{2} (dC/dx)$ for operation at **constant voltage**, and $F = \tfrac{1}{2}Q^{2} (d(1/C)/dx)$ for operation at **constant charge**. The factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ arises from the energy-method derivation: at constant voltage, when capacitance increases by $dC$ due to a small displacement, the voltage source delivers additional energy $\tfrac{1}{2}V^{2} dC$ that is converted into mechanical work. Physically, it represents the **co-energy contribution** of the field. (3 marks)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the electrostatic force between two parallel plates of area $A$ separated by an air gap $g$ when a voltage $V$ is applied across them. Use the **energy method**. Show all steps clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A parallel-plate electrostatic actuator has plate area $A = 250 \times 250 \; \mu m^{2}$ and an initial air gap of $g_0 = 4 \; \mu m$. The suspension spring stiffness is $k = 1 \; N/m$. Calculate: (i) the pull-in voltage, (ii) the maximum stable displacement, and (iii) the electrostatic force at half the pull-in voltage. Take $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; F/m$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

We consider the capacitor's stored electrostatic energy:

$$W = \frac{1}{2}CV^{2} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{g}V^{2}$$

The mechanical work done by the field when the gap reduces by an infinitesimal $dg$ equals the decrease in co-energy at constant $V$:

$$F = -\left.\frac{\partial W}{\partial g}\right|_{V} = -\frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 A V^{2} \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{g^{2}}\right) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A V^{2}}{2 g^{2}}$$

This is the **attractive force** between the plates. The corresponding **pressure** is:

$$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 V^{2}}{2g^{2}} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^{2}$$

(7 marks: 2 marks for setup, 3 marks for differentiation, 2 marks for final form and physical interpretation)

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $A = 250 \times 250 \; \mu m^{2} = 6.25 \times 10^{-8} \; m^{2}$, $g_0 = 4 \; \mu m = 4 \times 10^{-6} \; m$, $k = 1 \; N/m$.

**(i) Pull-in voltage** using the standard formula:

$$V_{pi} = \sqrt{\frac{8 k g_0^{3}}{27 \, \varepsilon_0 A}}$$

Substituting:

$$V_{pi} = \sqrt{\frac{8 \times 1 \times (4 \times 10^{-6})^{3}}{27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-8}}}$$

Compute numerator: $8 \times 64 \times 10^{-18} = 5.12 \times 10^{-16}$.

Compute denominator: $27 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-8} = 27 \times 55.34 \times 10^{-20} = 1.494 \times 10^{-17}$.

Ratio: $5.12 \times 10^{-16} \; / \; 1.494 \times 10^{-17} = 34.27$.

$$\boxed{V_{pi} = \sqrt{34.27} \approx 5.85 \; V}$$

**(ii) Maximum stable displacement:**

$$\delta_{max} = \frac{g_0}{3} = \frac{4}{3} \; \mu m \approx 1.33 \; \mu m$$

**(iii) Force at $V = V_{pi}/2 = 2.93 \; V$:**

At half the pull-in voltage, the equilibrium gap is found from $k\delta = \varepsilon_0 A V^{2}/(2(g_0-\delta)^{2})$. Using $\alpha = x(1-x)^{2}$ with $\alpha = (V/V_{pi})^{2} \cdot 8/27 \cdot ...$, we solve iteratively or note that at $V = V_{pi}/2$ the displacement is approximately $\delta \approx 0.13 \, g_0 = 0.52 \; \mu m$. Then:

$$F = k\delta = 1 \times 0.52 \times 10^{-6} = 0.52 \; \mu N$$

(7 marks: 2 marks for (i), 1 mark for (ii), 4 marks for (iii))

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a **lateral comb-drive actuator**. Derive the expression for the force produced. Why is this force independent of displacement? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A comb-drive actuator has $N = 100$ finger gaps, finger thickness $t = 25 \; \mu m$, gap between fingers $d = 3 \; \mu m$. If a voltage of $V = 50 \; V$ is applied, compute: (i) the lateral force produced, and (ii) the energy stored in the engaged capacitance. Comment on why this actuator can produce a long stroke. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

A lateral comb-drive consists of two sets of interdigitated "fingers" — one set fixed (stator), the other movable (rotor). When voltage is applied between stator and rotor, an in-plane electric field is established across each gap $d$. The capacitance per unit engaged length is $C' = \varepsilon_0 t / d$, and the force per finger pair is:

$$F_1 = \frac{1}{2} V^{2} \frac{dC_1}{dx} = \frac{1}{2} V^{2} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 t}{d}$$

For $N$ gaps in parallel:

$$F_{comb} = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 t V^{2}}{2 d}$$

The force is independent of $x$ (the lateral displacement) because the gap $d$ does not change as the rotor slides — only the **engaged overlap** changes, and the engaged fingers contribute a constant $dC/dx = \varepsilon_0 t/d$ regardless of position. The disengaged portion contributes zero. This is why the comb-drive produces a **constant force over a large travel range**, enabling strokes of tens of micrometres. (7 marks: 2 marks for sketch, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for displacement-independence explanation)

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:** $N = 100$, $t = 25 \; \mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6} \; m$, $d = 3 \; \mu m = 3 \times 10^{-6} \; m$, $V = 50 \; V$.

**(i) Lateral force:**

$$F_{comb} = \frac{N \varepsilon_0 t V^{2}}{2 d} = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 50^{2}}{2 \times 3 \times 10^{-6}}$$

Compute numerator: $100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 2500 = 100 \times 8.854 \times 25 \times 2500 \times 10^{-18} = 5.534 \times 10^{-11}$.

Denominator: $6 \times 10^{-6}$.

$$F_{comb} = \frac{5.534 \times 10^{-11}}{6 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.22 \times 10^{-6} \; N \approx 9.22 \; \mu N$$

**(ii) Energy stored** (per engaged finger pair length $L$, summing over $N$):

Total capacitance: $C = N \varepsilon_0 t L / d$. Assuming a typical overlap $L = 50 \; \mu m = 50 \times 10^{-6} \; m$:

$$C = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}}{3 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$C = \frac{100 \times 8.854 \times 25 \times 50 \times 10^{-24}}{3 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.107 \times 10^{-18}}{3 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.69 \times 10^{-13} \; F$$

$$W = \frac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3.69 \times 10^{-13} \times 2500 = 4.61 \times 10^{-10} \; J = 0.461 \; nJ$$

**Comment:** Because the force is independent of displacement, the actuator delivers constant thrust over its full stroke (limited only by the finger length). This is a major advantage over parallel-plate designs, which suffer from pull-in at one-third of the gap. (7 marks: 3 marks for (i), 2 marks for (ii), 2 marks for comment)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgot the factor ½:** Many students write $F = V^{2} dC/dx$. Always include $\tfrac{1}{2}$ for the constant-voltage case. **−1 mark** every time.
> 2. **Confusing pull-in gap with zero gap:** Pull-in occurs at $g = (2/3) g_0$, **not** at $g = 0$. Writing "plates touch at pull-in" costs full credit on that part.
> 3. **Units in comb-drive formula:** Mixing $\mu m$ and $m$ — always convert to SI **before** substituting. Board examiners will not grant method marks if the final numerical answer is off by $10^{6}$ or $10^{12}$.
> 4. **Wrong sign convention:** Electrostatic force on a movable plate is *attractive* (reduces the gap). State this explicitly.
> 5. **Energy-method vs. virtual-work confusion:** Use co-energy (with the $V$ held constant) for force, not stored energy. At constant $Q$, use the *reciprocal* formula.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- [x] **Core equation:** $F = \tfrac{1}{2} V^{2} \, dC/dx$ (constant-V operation).
- [x] **Parallel-plate force:** $F = \varepsilon_0 A V^{2}/(2 g^{2})$, pressure $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 E^{2}$.
- [x] **Comb-drive force:** $F = N \varepsilon_0 t V^{2}/(2d)$ — independent of displacement.
- [x] **Pull-in instability:** occurs at $g_{pi} = (2/3) g_0$, with $V_{pi} = \sqrt{8 k g_0^{3}/(27 \varepsilon_0 A)}$.
- [x] **Scaling:** electrostatic force density is size-independent → **favours microscale**.
- [x] **Energy density of the field:** $u = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^{2}$ — this equals the attractive pressure in a parallel plate.
- [x] **Stepping actuators:** SDA delivers nm-scale steps; inchworm uses clamp-push-unclamp cycle.
- [x] **Speed advantage:** mass $\sim L^{3}$ while force $\sim L^{2}$ → acceleration $\sim 1/L$ improves as devices shrink.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** high voltage needed (tens to hundreds of V), small absolute forces, pull-in limits travel.
- [x] **Energy method trick:** Differentiate $W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^{2}$ with respect to gap to get force at constant $V$.
- [x] **Boundary condition:** Do not exceed dielectric breakdown: $E \le 3 \times 10^{6}$ V/m in air.
- [x] **Always state units** explicitly: $F$ in N, $V$ in V, $g$ in m, $C$ in F.
- [x] **CMOS compatibility** is the chief reason electrostatic actuation dominates commercial MEMS.
- [x] **Compare with magnetic actuation** for KTU essays: magnetic $\propto L^{4}$ (poor at small scale), electrostatic $\propto L^{2}$ (favourable at small scale).
- [x] **Applications to remember:** DLP micromirrors, RF switches, scratch-drive walkers, IBM Millipede, AFM cantilever arrays, micromotors in optical scanners.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Piezoelectric Actuators

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Piezoelectric Actuator:** A solid-state transduction device that converts a directly applied electrical voltage into a precisely controlled mechanical displacement (sub-nanometre to tens of micrometres) by exploiting the *converse piezoelectric effect* in non-centrosymmetric crystalline materials. In the KTU 2024 microrobotics framework, it is classified as a **high-resolution, high-bandwidth, hysteresis-affected smart-material actuator** used primarily for positioning, walking-leg actuation, and active gripper elements in meso- and micro-scale robotic platforms.

The **direct piezoelectric effect** (discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie, **1880**) is the linear coupling where mechanical stress $T$ applied to a piezoelectric crystal produces an electric displacement $D$ (or surface charge). The **converse (or inverse) piezoelectric effect** is the dual coupling where an applied electric field $E$ produces a mechanical strain $S$. Actuators exploit only the *converse* branch; sensors exploit only the *direct* branch; many microrobotic elements (e.g., AFM cantilevers, SAW motors) exploit both.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Nano-Inchworm"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Imagine a stack of 1000 identical rubber discs, each of which is normally $1\ \mu m$ thick. The moment you plug a $1.5\ V$ battery across the stack, every single disc "squishes" by a tiny amount — say $0.0003\ \mu m$. Because the discs are mechanically in series, the total shortening of the whole stack is the sum of every disc's squish — yielding a motion of $\sim 0.3\ \mu m$ that you can move **backwards** by reversing polarity. The rubber discs are replaced by PZT (lead-zirconate-titanate) ceramic wafers, and the battery by a high-voltage amplifier delivering $0$ to $\pm 200\ V$. That summed sub-micrometre motion is exactly what a piezo *stack actuator* does in a microrobot leg or microgripper.

A second intuitive picture is the **bimorph "fish tail"**: glue two piezo plates back-to-back with opposite poling; when voltage is applied, one plate expands while the other contracts, producing a macroscopic bending — the same way a bimetallic strip bends under heat. This bending is the working principle of piezo *benders* and *walking microrobots*.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard quantitative benchmarks used throughout this module:
> - Operating electric field for PZT: $E \approx 1$ to $3\ \mathrm{kV/mm}$
> - Coercive field of soft PZT: $E_c \approx 0.5$ to $1\ \mathrm{kV/mm}$
> - Curie temperature of PZT5H: $T_C \approx 200\ ^\circ \mathrm{C}$ (above which piezoelectricity is permanently lost)
> - Strain coefficient $d_{33}$ of PZT5H: $d_{33} \approx 5.93 \times 10^{-10}\ \mathrm{m/V} = 593\ \mathrm{pm/V}$
> - Curie piezoelectric coefficient of quartz: $d_{11} \approx 2.3\ \mathrm{pm/V}$
> - Typical unloaded stroke of a 10 mm stack of 100 layers: $\Delta L \approx 10\ \mu m$ at $100\ V$

### 1.4 Material Taxonomy Used in Piezoelectric Microrobotics

| Class | Example | $d_{33}$ (pm/V) | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single crystal | Quartz (SiO₂) | 2.3 | Low coupling, very stable, sensors |
| Single crystal | LiNbO₃ | 6 | Optical-grade, SAW devices |
| Soft ceramic | PZT-5H | 593 | Workhorse of stack actuators |
| Hard ceramic | PZT-8 | 225 | High-power ultrasound |
| Polymer | PVDF | ~33 | Flexible, large-area, low mass |
| Relaxor single crystal | PMN-PT | >2000 | Highest known $d_{33}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For microrobot *actuation* (not sensing), PZT-5H and PMN-PT dominate. For *sensing + flexing* (bio-inspired skins, cilia), PVDF is preferred because of mechanical compliance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Fundamental Coupling Equations (IEEE Linear Standard)

The piezoelectric medium is described by four canonical variables: mechanical **stress** $T$ (N/m²), mechanical **strain** $S$ (dimensionless), electric **field** $E$ (V/m), and electric **displacement** $D$ (C/m²). Linear coupling is captured by two equations — the "sensor" and the "actuator" forms. In actuator notation (using strain-charge form):

$$S = s^E \, T + d_t \, E$$

$$D = d \, T + \varepsilon^T \, E$$

- $s^E$ — elastic compliance at **constant electric field** (m²/N).
- $d$ — **piezoelectric strain coefficient matrix** (m/V); transpose $d_t$ appears in the strain equation.
- $\varepsilon^T$ — dielectric permittivity at **constant stress** (F/m).

### 2.2 Why the Off-Diagonal Coupling Exists — Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Crystal asymmetry:** A piezoelectric unit cell (e.g., PZT tetragonal phase) has a permanent dipole along a polar axis; the positive and negative charge centroids do *not* coincide.
- **Step 2 — Mechanical strain re-shapes the dipole:** When the crystal is compressed along the polar axis, the dipole moment changes; bound surface charge appears, producing measurable $D$. This is the **direct effect**.
- **Step 3 — Electric field re-shapes the unit cell:** Applying $E$ parallel to the polar axis physically shifts the central cation with respect to the oxygen octahedra. Because the unit cell is asymmetric, this ionic shift produces a net lattice strain $S$. This is the **converse effect** — the operational basis of every piezo actuator.
- **Step 4 — Tensor reduction in 3-1 / 3-3 mode:** Poling aligns most dipoles along the *3-axis*. For thin disks poled through thickness and energised through thickness, only the $d_{33}$ component matters and the constitutive scalar equation reduces to $S_3 = d_{33} \, E_3$.

### 2.3 The Two Most Important Boundary Conditions for Actuators

**Condition A — Free displacement (zero stress, $T = 0$):**
$$S_3 = d_{33} \, E_3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V}$$

where $n$ is the number of active layers, $V$ is applied voltage, and $\Delta L$ is the stack's free elongation.

**Condition B — Blocked force (zero strain, $S_3 = 0$):**
$$0 = s^E \, T + d_{33} \, E_3 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{F_{\text{blk}} = \dfrac{A \, d_{33}}{s^E} \, E_3 = A \, d_{33} \, Y \, E_3}$$

where $A$ is the electrode area, $Y = 1/s^E$ is Young's modulus ($\sim 60\ \mathrm{GPa}$ for PZT-5H).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The product $d_{33} \cdot Y$ is called the **blocking-stress coefficient** $e_{33}$ (often written $e_{33} = d_{33} Y$ in C/m²). It is the figure of merit that determines the *mechanical authority* of a stack actuator.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Equation | Symbol | Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free stroke of stack | $\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V$ | m | Leg displacement budget |
| 2 | Electric field in dielectric | $E = V / t$ | V/m | Must stay below $\sim 2\ \mathrm{kV/mm}$ |
| 3 | Blocked force | $F_{\text{blk}} = A \, d_{33} \, Y \, E$ | N | Gripper clamping force |
| 4 | Strain in $3-3$ mode | $S_3 = d_{33} \, E_3$ | — | Direct displacement design |
| 5 | Strain in $3-1$ mode (tube) | $S_1 = d_{31} \, E_3$ | — | Lateral scan of piezo tube |
| 6 | Lateral scan of tube scanner | $\Delta X = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \, \dfrac{L^2}{t} \, d_{31} \, V$ | m | AFM/STM tube scanner geometry |
| 7 | Curie temperature limit | $T_C$ | $^\circ$C | Depoling must be avoided |
| 8 | Resonant frequency of stack | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{Y}{\rho}}$ | Hz | Sets the actuator bandwidth |
| 9 | Hysteresis loss (PZT) | $H \approx 10$ to $15\%$ | — | Charge drive reduces it to <2% |
| 10 | Coupling factor | $k_{33} = \dfrac{d_{33}}{\sqrt{s^E \, \varepsilon^T}}$ | — | Material FOM, $k_{33} \approx 0.7$ for PZT-5H |

> [!NOTE]
> Note on equation 6: $\Delta X = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \frac{L^2}{t} d_{31} V$ assumes an axially poled tube scanned by a *quartered* outer electrode; $L$ = tube length, $t$ = wall thickness. This is the textbook formula for piezo-tube scanners in AFMs and is a frequent KTU Part B question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **AFM/STM nanopositioners:** A single piezo tube provides sub-Å positioning in $X$, $Y$, $Z$ simultaneously — the most precise robot in the world.
- **Walking microrobots (e.g., 1 g Harvard robot):** Each leg is driven by a bimorph cantilever that bends up to $200\ \mu m$ at $\pm 10\ V$.
- **Microgrippers for cells:** Two opposing stack actuators close a compliant flexure hinge with sub-micrometre precision.
- **Inertial drive (stick-slip motors):** A piezo stack excites a slow ramp + sudden snap to step a runner forward by tens of mm at nm resolution.
- **Ultrasonic motors (USM):** A travelling wave on a piezo ring drives a rotor in contact with it — used in camera autofocus and surgical microrobot joints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The unifying engineering insight: **piezo actuators are voltage-controlled, very stiff, extremely fast, but their stroke is inherently small (micrometres) and non-linear (hysteresis).** Most of microrobotic design is therefore devoted to *amplifying* stroke (lever, bimorph, tube) and *correcting* hysteresis (charge drive, Preisach model, feed-forward inversion).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free Displacement of a Piezo Stack Actuator

> **Problem context:** A PZT-5H stack actuator consists of $n$ ceramic layers, each of thickness $t = 100\ \mu m$, connected mechanically in series and electrically in parallel. The total length is $L = n t$. A voltage $V$ is applied across every layer. Derive the free stroke $\Delta L$ in terms of $n$, $d_{33}$, and $V$.

**Step 1 — Write the field inside one layer:**
The voltage is applied across a layer of thickness $t$, so the electric field magnitude is

$$E_3 = \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Write the strain in a single layer at zero external stress** ($T = 0$):

$$S_3 = s^E \cdot 0 + d_{33} \, E_3 = d_{33} \, \frac{V}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply strain by the layer thickness to obtain that layer's individual displacement:**

$$\delta = S_3 \, t = d_{33} \, \frac{V}{t} \cdot t = d_{33} \, V$$

**Step 4 — Add displacements of all $n$ layers (mechanical series summation):**

$$\Delta L = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta_i = n \, d_{33} \, V$$

**Step 5 — In terms of total stack length $L = n t$:**

$$\Delta L = \frac{L}{t} \, d_{33} \, V = d_{33} \, \frac{V}{t} \, L = d_{33} \, E_3 \, L$$

This is the famous result $\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V$ (or equivalently $\Delta L = d_{33} \, E_3 \, L$). **Interpretation:** increasing the number of layers linearly increases stroke at fixed voltage, but the cost in capacitance (drive current) also rises linearly. The free strain $\Delta L / L = d_{33} E_3$ is *always* limited to roughly $0.1\%$ for PZT-5H before depoling begins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** For $n = 100$, $d_{33} = 593 \times 10^{-12}\ \mathrm{m/V}$, $V = 100\ \mathrm{V}$:
> $\Delta L = 100 \cdot 593 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 100 = 5.93 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m} \approx 5.93\ \mu m$. Matches the canonical $10\ \mu m$ stroke for a 200 V / 10 mm stack.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Blocked Force of a Piezo Stack

> **Problem context:** With the stack constrained between two rigid walls (no displacement allowed), find the internal stress $T$ that develops when a voltage $V$ is applied.

**Step 1 — Set $S_3 = 0$ (constrained, no motion) in the constitutive relation:**

$$0 = s^E \, T_3 + d_{33} \, E_3$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the internal stress:**

$$T_3 = - \frac{d_{33}}{s^E} \, E_3$$

The negative sign indicates a *compressive* reaction stress — the actuator pushes back on the wall with that magnitude.

**Step 3 — Compute the equivalent reaction force using $F = - T_3 \, A$:**

$$F_{\text{blk}} = \frac{d_{33}}{s^E} \, E_3 \, A$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Y = 1 / s^E$ and $E_3 = V / t$:**

$$F_{\text{blk}} = d_{33} \, Y \, \frac{V}{t} \, A$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** PZT-5H: $d_{33} = 593 \times 10^{-12}\ \mathrm{m/V}$, $Y = 6 \times 10^{10}\ \mathrm{N/m^2}$, $V/t = 10^6\ \mathrm{V/m}$ (i.e., $100\ \mathrm{V}$ over $100\ \mu m$), $A = 10^{-4}\ \mathrm{m^2}$ (a $10 \times 10\ \mathrm{mm}$ footprint):
> $F_{\text{blk}} = 593 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 6 \times 10^{10} \cdot 10^6 \cdot 10^{-4} \approx 3.56 \times 10^2\ \mathrm{N} \approx 36\ \mathrm{kgf}$. A modest-sized stack can hold tens of kilograms — which is why piezo stages feel "stiff".

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Lateral Deflection of an Axially-Poled Piezo Tube Scanner

> **Problem context:** A piezo tube of length $L$, wall thickness $t$, mean radius $R$, is poled along its axis (the $z$ direction). The outer electrode is split into four equal quadrants; the inner electrode is grounded. A voltage $V$ is applied to the $+X$ quadrant. Derive the lateral tip deflection $\Delta X$.

**Step 1 — Identify the field direction.** Field is radial, the poling is axial; the relevant coupling is the *transverse* coefficient $d_{31}$, giving a circumferential strain $S_\theta = d_{31} E_r$.

**Step 2 — Replace the radial field with a voltage across the wall** $E_r \approx V/t$.

**Step 3 — Convert the strain into an effective bending moment.** A half-section of the tube of length $L$ and width $R$ behaves like a bimorph beam; the bending curvature is

$$\kappa \approx \frac{d_{31} \, V}{t \, R}$$

**Step 4 — Tip deflection of a uniformly curved cantilever of length $L$:**

$$\Delta X = \frac{\kappa L^2}{2} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{d_{31} V}{t R} \cdot L^2 = \frac{d_{31} \, V \, L^2}{2 \, t \, R}$$

For a quartered (quadrant) electrode, the effective moment arm is the standard textbook factor of $\sqrt{2}/2$ in the $X$ direction (the resultant of two half-quadrants in the $+X$ side):

$$\boxed{\Delta X = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{d_{31} \, V \, L^2}{t \, R}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** stroke scales as $L^2$ — longer tubes give *quadratically* more scan, which is why commercial AFM tubes are made as long as stiffness allows. Resonant frequency of the first lateral mode scales as $1/L^2$, so the design is a fundamental stroke vs. bandwidth trade-off.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bimorph Bending Cantilever Deflection

> **Problem context:** A piezoelectric bimorph is a sandwich of two $d_{31}$ polarised layers, poled in opposite directions, with a central shim. When a voltage $V$ is applied, one layer expands, the other contracts, producing a bending moment. Find the tip deflection of a cantilever bimorph of length $L$, total thickness $h$, and width $b$.

**Step 1 — Mismatch strain between layers** (using antisymmetric poling and same field):

$$\Delta S = 2 \, d_{31} \, E_3 = 2 \, d_{31} \frac{V}{h/2} = \frac{4 \, d_{31} \, V}{h}$$

**Step 2 — Treat the bimorph as a composite beam with a moment per unit width:**

$$M = \frac{Y \, I \, \Delta S}{h} = \frac{Y \, (b h^3 / 12) \, \Delta S}{h} = \frac{Y \, b \, h^2}{12} \, \Delta S$$

**Step 3 — Curvature from beam-bending theory:**

$$\kappa = \frac{M}{Y \, I} = \frac{\Delta S}{h} = \frac{4 \, d_{31} \, V}{h^2}$$

**Step 4 — Tip deflection of a uniform-curvature cantilever:**

$$\Delta z = \frac{\kappa L^2}{2} = \frac{2 \, d_{31} \, V \, L^2}{h^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check:** $d_{31} = -320\ \mathrm{pm/V}$ (PZT-5H), $L = 20\ \mathrm{mm}$, $h = 0.6\ \mathrm{mm}$, $V = 100\ \mathrm{V}$:
> $\Delta z = 2 \cdot 320 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 100 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-3})^2 / (0.6 \times 10^{-3})^2 \approx 7.1 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{m} = 71\ \mu m$. This is why *bimorphs* are the workhorse of $\sim 100\ \mu m$ walking microrobot legs.

## 3.5 Summary Matrix of Derived Quantities

| Configuration | Governing Coefficient | Free Stroke | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single-layer slab | $d_{33}$ | $d_{33} V$ | $\sim 0.06\ \mu m$ at 100 V |
| Stack (n layers) | $d_{33}$ | $n d_{33} V$ | $\sim 6\ \mu m$ at 100 V |
| Tube scanner (lateral) | $d_{31}$ | $\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} d_{31} V L^2 / (t R)$ | $\sim 10\ \mu m$ at 200 V |
| Bimorph cantilever | $d_{31}$ | $2 d_{31} V L^2 / h^2$ | $\sim 70\ \mu m$ at 100 V |
| Shear actuator (fibre) | $d_{15}$ | $d_{15} V L$ | $\sim 30\ \mu m$ at 100 V |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Piezo Actuator Subsystem in a Microrobot

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Controller MCU] --> B[DAC or PWM Modulator]
    B --> C[High Voltage Amplifier x20 to x40]
    C --> D[Charge Drive or Voltage Drive Stage]
    D --> E[Piezo Stack or Bimorph]
    E --> F[Mechanical Coupling Flexure Hinge]
    F --> G[Microrobot End Effector Leg or Gripper]
    H[Hysteresis Observer] --> C
    H --> I[Preisach or Bouc Wen Inverse Model]
    I --> D
    J[Position Sensor LVDT or Capacitive] --> G
    J --> K[Closed Loop PID or Sliding Mode]
    K --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The block chain shows the canonical signal-flow for a closed-loop piezo-driven microrobot joint. The key engineering feature is the **hysteresis observer** loop — without this, the open-loop piezo will produce 10–15% position error. Adding a charge drive *and* a Preisach inverse model typically reduces positioning error to <0.5%.

## 4.2 Architecture — Material-Property Map for Piezo Actuation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Voltage V] --> B[Poled Piezoelectric Ceramic PZT or PMN PT]
    B --> C[Inverse Piezoelectric Strain S equals d33 E3]
    C --> D[Mechanical Output: Free Stroke Delta L]
    B --> E[Stiffness Y plus Coupling d33]
    E --> F[Mechanical Output: Blocked Force Fblk]
    B --> G[Non-idealities: Hysteresis H, Creep C, Depoling at T greater than TC]
    G --> H[Drives Design: Charge Drive, Closed Loop, Thermal Management]
```

## 4.3 Architecture — Operating-Mode Decision Tree for Designers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Start: Pick a Piezo Mode] --> B{Required Stroke}
    B -->|less than 1 micrometre| C[Single Layer d33 Slab]
    B -->|1 to 20 micrometre| D[Stack Actuator]
    B -->|20 to 200 micrometre| E[Bimorph Cantilever]
    B -->|hundreds of micrometre to mm| F[Amplified Piezo or Inertial Stick Slip]
    C --> G{Bandwidth Requirement}
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G -->|greater than 1 kHz| H[Consider Tube or Shear Mode]
    G -->|less than 1 kHz| I[Use Stack with Charge Drive]
    H --> J[End]
    I --> J
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design takeaway for the KTU exam:** A common Part-B question asks you to "select a piezo configuration for a leg producing 100 µm vertical motion at 50 Hz". The decision tree above shows the answer is a **bimorph cantilever**, not a stack — stacks saturate at ~10 µm. Mention *both* the choice *and* the reason (stroke-budget vs. voltage budget) to score full marks.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block — Signal-Energy Flow Inside a Stack Actuator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V[Voltage Source V] --> E[Electric Field E3 equals V over t in PZT Layer]
    E --> S[Strain S3 equals d33 times E3]
    S --> D[Displacement Delta L equals n times S3 times t]
    S -.conjugate.-> F[Blocked Force F equals A times d33 times Y times E3]
    D --> W[Mechanical Work W equals F times Delta L]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate clearly between the *direct* and *converse* piezoelectric effects. State one microrobotic application that uses each.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Direct piezoelectric effect** (Curie brothers, 1880): Application of mechanical stress $T$ on a non-centrosymmetric crystal produces an electric displacement $D$ on its surfaces. Mathematically: $D = d \, T + \varepsilon^T E$. Used in **sensors** — e.g., a PVDF tactile skin on a microrobot finger detects contact force (1 mark).
> - **Converse piezoelectric effect**: Application of an external electric field $E$ produces a mechanical strain $S$. Mathematically: $S = s^E T + d_t E$. Used in **actuators** — e.g., the PZT-5H stack driving a microgripper jaw (1 mark).
> - *Coupling symmetry:* The same $d$-matrix governs both effects, ensuring they are thermodynamic duals (1 mark).

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Q2.** What is *hysteresis* in a piezoelectric actuator? Mention two engineering techniques used to mitigate it.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - Definition: The output displacement of a piezo actuator for a *given voltage* depends on the *history* of previously applied voltages, producing a butterfly-shaped loop in the displacement–voltage plane. For soft PZT, the loop width is typically $10$–$15\%$ of full-scale stroke (1 mark).
> - Technique 1: **Charge drive** — replacing voltage control with charge ($Q = C V$) control reduces loop width to <2% by eliminating the displacement-dependent capacitance variation (1 mark).
> - Technique 2: **Feed-forward inversion** with a Preisach or Bouc–Wen model that pre-distorts the voltage command; often combined with closed-loop PID using a strain gauge or capacitive sensor (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B — Module-Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Stack Actuator Design for a Walking Microrobot

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **(a)** A microrobot leg is driven by a PZT-5H piezo stack of $n = 120$ layers, each of thickness $t = 80\ \mu m$. The footprint area is $A = 5 \times 5\ \mathrm{mm^2}$. Material parameters: $d_{33} = 5.93 \times 10^{-10}\ \mathrm{m/V}$, $Y = 6 \times 10^{10}\ \mathrm{N/m^2}$. Drive voltage $V = 150\ \mathrm{V}$. Calculate:
>  (i) the free stroke $\Delta L$, (ii) the blocked force $F_{\text{blk}}$, and (iii) the maximum safe operating temperature if the Curie temperature of PZT-5H is $T_C = 200\ ^\circ \mathrm{C}$ and a $30\ ^\circ \mathrm{C}$ safety margin is required.
>
> **(b)** Discuss why piezo stack actuators must be **preloaded** in microrobotic applications. With a neat sketch, describe the construction of a **multilayer co-fired stack actuator** and explain the role of internal electrodes.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) (i) Free stroke** (4 marks):

$$\Delta L = n \, d_{33} \, V = 120 \cdot 5.93 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 150$$

$$\Delta L = 120 \cdot 8.895 \times 10^{-8}\ \mathrm{m} = 1.0674 \times 10^{-5}\ \mathrm{m} \approx 10.67\ \mu m$$

**(a) (ii) Blocked force** (3 marks):

$$E_3 = \frac{V}{t} = \frac{150}{80 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.875 \times 10^{6}\ \mathrm{V/m}$$

$$F_{\text{blk}} = d_{33} \, Y \, A \, E_3 = 5.93 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 6 \times 10^{10} \cdot 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1.875 \times 10^{6}$$

$$F_{\text{blk}} = 5.93 \cdot 6 \cdot 25 \cdot 1.875 \cdot 10^{-10+10-6+6} = 1668\ \mathrm{N} \approx 1.67\ \mathrm{kN}$$

(Accept answers in the range $1600$–$1700\ \mathrm{N}$; a common error is forgetting the $A$ term — see warning below.)

**(a) (iii) Safe operating temperature** (1 mark):

$$T_{\max} = T_C - 30 = 200 - 30 = 170\ ^\circ \mathrm{C}$$

**(b) Preload and construction discussion** (6 marks):
- *Why preload:* Piezo ceramics are brittle (tensile strength $\sim 30$ MPa, compressive $\sim 500$ MPa). Actuation motion is *expansion*; if the driven mass provides tension on the stack, tensile stress combined with the operational stress can crack the ceramic. A mechanical spring (or Belleville washer stack) keeps the stack permanently in compression (2 marks).
- *Construction of multilayer co-fired stack:* Alternating PZT-5H sheets ($t \sim 50$–$100\ \mu m$) are screen-printed with Ag/Pd internal electrodes, stacked, laminated, and co-fired at $\sim 1100\ ^\circ \mathrm{C}$. Every alternate electrode is brought out to one of two external side electrodes, so that all layers are energised in **parallel** electrically but remain in **series** mechanically. This gives the full stroke $n \, d_{33} V$ at a *low* drive voltage ($\sim 100$–$200\ \mathrm{V}$) suitable for microrobotic electronics (3 marks).
- *Role of internal electrodes:* (1) Provide uniform field through every layer; (2) connect all layers in parallel electrically so the whole stack runs at the same low voltage; (3) reduce capacitive charging time (capacitance scales as $n^2$, improving high-frequency response) (1 mark).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Piezo Tube Scanner and Hysteresis Compensation

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
> **(a)** A piezo tube scanner used in an AFM head has length $L = 25\ \mathrm{mm}$, mean radius $R = 6.35\ \mathrm{mm}$, wall thickness $t = 0.5\ \mathrm{mm}$, and the outer electrode is split into four equal quadrants. With $d_{31} = -2.0 \times 10^{-10}\ \mathrm{m/V}$ (PZT-5A) and a drive voltage $V = 250\ \mathrm{V}$ applied to the $+X$ quadrant, calculate the lateral tip deflection $\Delta X$ in the $X$ direction. Comment on the units and sign of $d_{31}$.
>
> **(b)** With a block diagram, explain the **Preisach-model-based feed-forward hysteresis compensation** for a piezo actuator. How does this differ from a *charge drive* in principle and in performance?

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lateral deflection** (7 marks):

Use the quartered-electrode formula derived in Section 3.3:

$$\Delta X = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{d_{31} \, V \, L^2}{t \, R}$$

Substitute values (SI units):

$$\Delta X = \frac{1.414}{2} \cdot \frac{(-2.0 \times 10^{-10}) \cdot 250 \cdot (25 \times 10^{-3})^2}{0.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 6.35 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Step-wise:

- Numerator: $-2.0 \times 10^{-10} \cdot 250 = -5.0 \times 10^{-8}$
- $(L^2)$ factor: $(25 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-4}$
- Numerator total: $-5.0 \times 10^{-8} \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-4} = -3.125 \times 10^{-11}$
- Denominator: $0.5 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 6.35 \times 10^{-3} = 3.175 \times 10^{-6}$
- $\dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 0.707$

$$\Delta X = 0.707 \cdot \frac{-3.125 \times 10^{-11}}{3.175 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.707 \cdot (-9.84 \times 10^{-6})\ \mathrm{m}$$

$$\Delta X \approx -6.96 \times 10^{-6}\ \mathrm{m} \approx -6.96\ \mu m$$

*Sign commentary (1 mark):* The negative sign means that for a *positive* $V$ on the $+X$ quadrant, the tube bends *inward* along $-X$ — a consequence of $d_{31} < 0$ (PZT contracts laterally when its thickness is extended). In AFM operation, drive electronics invert the command to obtain the desired positive scan.

**(b) Preisach compensation and comparison with charge drive** (7 marks):

- *Hysteresis as a sum of relays:* The **Preisach model** represents hysteresis as the weighted superposition of independent non-ideal relays (hysterons) parameterised by $(\alpha, \beta)$ switching thresholds. The output displacement is

$$\Delta L(V) = \iint_{\alpha \ge \beta} \mu(\alpha, \beta) \, \hat{\gamma}_{\alpha, \beta}(V) \, d\alpha \, d\beta$$

where $\hat{\gamma}_{\alpha, \beta}$ is 0 or 1 depending on the past input (3 marks).

- *Feed-forward inversion:* A measured or identified density $\mu(\alpha, \beta)$ is integrated to obtain the displacement surface; the *inverse* of that surface is computed, and the drive voltage is *pre-distorted* through it so that the *output* tracks the desired linear command. Typical open-loop residual error drops from $10$–$15\%$ to $0.5$–$2\%$ (2 marks).
- *Block diagram of compensation loop:* [Reference command] $\rightarrow$ [Preisach inverse model] $\rightarrow$ [HV amplifier] $\rightarrow$ [Piezo + Hysteresis plant] $\rightarrow$ [Output displacement]. In practice a *parallel* closed-loop PID using a strain-gauge or capacitive sensor is added.
- *Comparison with charge drive:*

| Aspect | Voltage drive (open loop) | Charge drive | Preisach feed-forward |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hysteresis | 10–15% | 1–3% | 0.5–2% |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate (charge amp) | High (model ID + DSP) |
| Bandwidth | Limited by amp | Limited by current | Limited by ID accuracy |
| Principle | $V$→$Q$→$S$ non-linear | $Q$→$S$ linear | Pre-distort $V$ to cancel $S$–$V$ loop |

(2 marks for the comparison.)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting $A$ in blocked force:** many students write $F_{\text{blk}} = d_{33} Y E$ without multiplying by the electrode area $A$. This loses 2 of 3 marks in Part (a) (ii).
> - **Wrong sign of $d_{31}$:** the value is *negative* for all standard PZT. Carrying it correctly through a piezo-tube derivation is required for full credit.
> - **Confusing $d$ and $e$:** $d$ is strain/field (m/V); $e$ is stress/field (C/m²) = $d Y$. Mixing these up is the most common 1-mark penalty.
> - **Open-loop hysteresis claim:** asserting that "piezo actuators are perfectly linear" is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction. Always *mention* the 10–15% loop and how your design compensates it.
> - **Stating stack stroke without $n$:** A frequent error is to quote the single-layer result $d_{33} V$ for a stack — losing the multiplier $n$ and 1–2 marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist (rapid-fire for the night before the exam):**
> - **Piezoelectric effect** is reversible; both direct and converse are governed by the same $d_{ij}$ tensor.
> - **Constitutive equations** in actuator form: $S = s^E T + d_t E$, $D = d T + \varepsilon^T E$.
> - **Free stroke of stack:** $\Delta L = n \, d_{33} V$. The stroke scales with $n$ and $V$, *not* with the area $A$.
> - **Blocked force of stack:** $F_{\text{blk}} = d_{33} \, Y \, A \, (V/t)$. The force scales with $A$, *not* with $n$ or $V/t$ alone.
> - **Piezo tube lateral scan:** $\Delta X = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{d_{31} V L^2}{t R}$ (quartered electrode, axially poled).
> - **Bimorph cantilever tip:** $\Delta z = \frac{2 d_{31} V L^2}{h^2}$ — the chosen configuration when 50–200 µm strokes are needed.
> - **Five actuator modes:** (i) stack ($d_{33}$), (ii) tube ($d_{31}$ lateral), (iii) bimorph ($d_{31}$ bending), (iv) shear ($d_{15}$), (v) amplified (lever or shell).
> - **Material hierarchy** (by $d_{33}$): quartz $\ll$ PZT-5H $< $ PMN-PT (relaxor single crystal, $d_{33} > 2000$ pm/V).
> - **Three non-idealities:** (1) **hysteresis** 10–15% (mitigation: charge drive, Preisach inversion); (2) **creep** 1–3% over minutes (mitigation: closed-loop); (3) **depoling** above $T_C$ or under reverse bias $> E_c$ (mitigation: thermal management, polarity-aware drive).
> - **Coupling factor $k_{33}$** is the material FOM: $k_{33} = d_{33}/\sqrt{s^E \varepsilon^T}$, equals ~0.7 for PZT-5H.
> - **Resonant frequency** of a stack scales as $1/L$; the bandwidth of an inertial drive scales with the *excitation* frequency of the piezo (kHz to MHz).
> - **Always preload** a stack in compression (typical preload = $0.1 \times F_{\text{blk}}$) to avoid tensile fracture.
> - **Charge drive** is the single most effective hardware fix for hysteresis; **Preisach inversion** is the most effective software fix.
> - **Curie temperature** is a *hard* limit — operation above $T_C$ permanently destroys piezoelectricity.
> - **In microrobots, the right configuration is rarely a bare stack:** almost always it is a stack *plus* a flexure hinge, a bimorph *plus* a leg, or a tube *plus* a quadrant driver.
> - **Two equations to remember verbatim for Part B:** (a) $\Delta L = n d_{33} V$, (b) $\Delta X_{\text{tube}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} d_{31} V L^2 / (t R)$.
> - **One rule of thumb for design:** every 1 µm of free stroke at 100 V needs ~170 active PZT layers in a stack, OR a bimorph of aspect ratio $L/h \approx 30$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the five modes — "STBSS"** — **S**tack, **T**ube, **B**imorph, **S**hear, amplified **S**hell. Recite before you open the question paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Piezoelectric Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Piezoelectric Motors ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Piezoelectric Motor** is a class of electromechanical actuator that converts electrical energy into mechanical motion (linear or rotary) through the **inverse (converse) piezoelectric effect** in piezoelectric ceramic crystals (typically Lead Zirconate Titanate — **PZT**). Unlike conventional electromagnetic motors, piezoelectric motors operate without magnetic fields, without commutation, and without gear reduction — instead exploiting the sub-nanometer lattice deformation of polarized ferroelectric domains to generate precise, high-force, low-speed actuation. In the KTU 2024 microrobotics context, piezoelectric motors are classified as **stick-slip actuators**, **inchworm actuators**, and **ultrasonic motors (USMs)**, with operating frequencies typically in the range of **20 kHz to 10 MHz** and displacement resolutions down to **sub-nanometer (≈ 0.1 nm)** levels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PERUT864 Module 3):** > Piezoelectric motors form the **core actuation paradigm** for sub-millimeter microrobots (e.g., endoscopic capsule robots, AFM nanopositioners, micro-grippers). The KTU board explicitly tests understanding of the **converse piezoelectric coupling**, **resonance amplification**, and **inertial/elliptical drive mechanisms**. ### 1.2 The Two Sister Effects | Effect | Direction | Used In | Symbol | |---|---|---|---| | **Direct Piezoelectric Effect** | Mechanical stress → Electrical charge | Sensors, force probes | $d$ (d_{ij}) | | **Converse (Inverse) Piezoelectric Effect** | Electric field → Mechanical strain | **Actuators / Motors** | $d$ (same coefficient) | > [!NOTE] > **Maxwell's Symmetry Principle:** The same piezoelectric coefficient tensor $[d]$ (in units of C/N or m/V) governs BOTH effects. This is why the same PZT crystal can be a sensor AND a motor — only the boundary conditions (charge-measuring vs voltage-driving) differ. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Electrified Spring" Imagine a row of **millions of microscopic spring-loaded slinkies** packed tightly inside a ceramic disc. Each slinky is a polarized electric dipole. When no voltage is applied, the slinkies are randomly arranged, and the disc keeps its original shape. The moment you apply a voltage across the disc, every slinky **simultaneously stretches or compresses** along the electric field direction — and the entire disc elongates or shrinks by a few nanometers. Now imagine making **millions of these discs do this 20,000 times a second** in a coordinated wave pattern — and you can push a tiny rotor into smooth, silent rotation. That is exactly how a **piezoelectric motor** works at the atomic scale. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Piezoelectric strain coefficient $d_{33}$ (PZT-5H):** ≈ **650 × 10⁻¹² m/V = 650 pm/V** - **Coupling factor $k_{33}$:** ≈ **0.75 (75 %)** — measures electromechanical energy conversion efficiency - **Curie Temperature $T_C$ (PZT):** ≈ **200 °C** — above this, piezoelectricity is permanently lost - **Young's Modulus $Y$ (PZT):** ≈ **5 × 10¹⁰ N/m²** - **Dielectric constant $\epsilon_r$ (PZT-5H):** ≈ **3400** - **Mechanical Quality Factor $Q_m$:** ≈ **65–80** at resonance > [!TIP] > **Why PZT and not Quartz?** Quartz has $d_{33} \approx 2.3$ pm/V, while PZT-5H has $d_{33} \approx 650$ pm/V — nearly **300× larger strain** for the same voltage. PZT is the **de facto** choice for piezoelectric motors in microrobotics, despite its lower Curie temperature compared to quartz. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Constitutive Equations of Piezoelectricity The electromechanical behavior of any piezoelectric material is fully captured by **two coupled linear constitutive equations** (IEEE Standard 1987): $$S_i = s^E_{ij} \, T_j + d_{ik} \, E_k$$ $$D_k = d_{jk} \, T_j + \epsilon^T_{km} \, E_m$$ Where: - $S_i$ = Mechanical **strain** tensor (m/m) — dimensionless - $T_j$ = Mechanical **stress** tensor (N/m²) - $E_k$ = Applied **electric field** (V/m) - $D_k$ = **Electric displacement** (charge per unit area, C/m²) - $s^E_{ij}$ = Elastic **compliance** at constant electric field (m²/N) - $d_{ik}$ = **Piezoelectric strain coefficient** tensor (m/V or C/N) - $\epsilon^T_{km}$ = **Permittivity** at constant stress (F/m) > [!IMPORTANT] > The first equation is the **motor equation** — it shows that strain $S$ is produced by stress $T$ AND/OR electric field $E$. When used as a motor, we set $T \to 0$ (free body) and the strain is purely electrically induced: $S = d \cdot E$. ### 2.2 The Three Key Piezoelectric Coefficients (For Motor Designers) | Coefficient | Symbol | Units | Physical Meaning | Typical PZT-5H Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | Piezoelectric strain constant | $d_{33}$ | pm/V (or m/V) | Strain produced per unit field | **650 pm/V** | | Piezoelectric voltage constant | $g_{33}$ | mV·m/N | Electric field produced per unit stress | $g_{33} = d_{33}/\epsilon^T \approx 21.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Vm/N | | Electromechanical coupling | $k_{33}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of electrical ↔ mechanical energy converted | **0.75** | ### 2.3 The Three Working Principles of Piezoelectric Motors #### Principle 1 — Standing-Wave Ultrasonic Motor (SWUSM) - A piezoelectric ring is excited at its **mechanical resonance frequency** $f_r$. - The standing wave creates stationary **nodes and antinodes** of displacement. - A rotor pressed against the ring is **friction-driven** at the antinodes. - Used in camera autofocus lenses (e.g., Canon EOS, Nikon AF-S). #### Principle 2 — Traveling-Wave Ultrasonic Motor (TWUSM) - Two orthogonal standing waves, phase-shifted by **90° (π/2 rad)**, are superposed to form a **traveling flexural wave**. - The elliptical motion of surface particles drives a rotor in a chosen direction. - Reverse the 90° phase shift → reverse rotation. - Used in **NASA's Mars Rover wheels** and **Canon EOS USM lenses**. #### Principle 3 — Stick-Slip / Inertial Inchworm (Linear Stepping) - The piezo first **slowly deforms** ("stick" phase) dragging a slider via static friction. - Then **snaps back rapidly** ("slip" phase) — the inertial slider does not follow. - Net displacement = slow-stroke length. Repeating produces unlimited travel with **nm resolution**. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Equation | Application | Unit Checks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $S = d_{33} \cdot E = d_{33} \cdot \dfrac{V}{t}$ | Free strain of piezo stack | dimensionless | | 2 | $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V$ | Total elongation of stack | m | | 3 | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Longitudinal resonance frequency | Hz | | 4 | $v_{sound} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E}{\rho}}$ | Acoustic velocity in piezo | m/s | | 5 | $F_{block} = d_{33} \cdot Y \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{V}{t}$ | Blocking force of piezo stack | N | | 6 | $k_{33}^2 = \dfrac{d_{33}^2}{s^E_{33} \cdot \epsilon^T_{33}}$ | Electromechanical coupling | dimensionless | | 7 | $Q_m = \dfrac{f_r}{\Delta f}$ | Mechanical quality factor | dimensionless | | 8 | $P_{out} = F \cdot v = F \cdot 2\pi f \cdot A_{vib}$ | Mechanical power output | W | | 9 | $T = \dfrac{F \cdot \mu \cdot r}{G}$ | Torque from friction (stick-slip) | N·m | | 10 | $v_{rotor} = 2\pi f \cdot A_{vib} \cdot \mu$ | Rotor tangential velocity (USM) | m/s | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse $d$ (piezoelectric strain constant, m/V) with $g$ (piezoelectric voltage constant, Vm/N). They are related by $g = d/\epsilon^T$, so numerical values differ by a factor of $\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r \approx 3 \times 10^{-8}$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Medical microrobotics:** Capsule endoscopes (PillCam) use piezoelectric ultrasonic motors for self-propulsion through the GI tract. - **AFM / STM nanopositioning:** Stick-slip inchworm stages achieve **0.01 nm** closed-loop resolution. - **Aerospace:** Space-qualified piezoelectric motors (New Scale, PI) operate in vacuum with zero outgassing. - **Haptic feedback:** Smartphone vibrators use linear piezo motors with $f \approx 200$ Hz. - **Optical systems:** Piezoelectric motor-driven deformable mirrors for adaptive optics in telescopes. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Free Strain of a Piezoelectric Stack Actuator **Problem (KTU typical 7-mark):** A PZT-5H piezo stack has $N = 100$ layers, each of thickness $t = 80 \,\mu m$. The piezoelectric coefficient is $d_{33} = 650 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V. A driving voltage $V = 100$ V is applied. Calculate (a) the free strain in each layer, (b) the total elongation $\Delta L$ of the stack, and (c) the resonance frequency if the total stack length is $L = N \cdot t$ and $E/\rho = 30 \times 10^6$ (m/s)². **Step 1 — Electric Field in Each Layer** The voltage $V$ is applied across the entire stack of $N$ layers, so each layer sees a voltage $V_{layer} = V/N$. $$E_{layer} = \dfrac{V_{layer}}{t} = \dfrac{V}{N \cdot t}$$ Substituting values: $$E_{layer} = \dfrac{100}{100 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{100}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.25 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/m}$$ **Step 2 — Free Strain in Each Layer** Using the converse piezoelectric relation $S_3 = d_{33} \cdot E_3$: $$S_3 = d_{33} \cdot E_{layer} = 650 \times 10^{-12} \times 1.25 \times 10^{4}$$ $$S_3 = 8.125 \times 10^{-6} = 8.125 \,\mu\text{strain}$$ This is equivalent to a relative elongation of **0.0008125 % per layer**. **Step 3 — Total Elongation** Total strain of the stack equals strain per layer (they all stretch in series). Total displacement: $$\Delta L = S_3 \cdot L = S_3 \cdot N \cdot t = 8.125 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$\Delta L = 6.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = \boxed{65 \text{ nm}}$$ **Alternative direct form:** $$\Delta L = N \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = 100 \times 650 \times 10^{-12} \times 100 = 6.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 65 \text{ nm} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Resonance Frequency** For a slender rod in longitudinal vibration: $$f_r = \dfrac{v_{sound}}{2L} = \dfrac{\sqrt{E/\rho}}{2L}$$ With $L = 100 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m and $v_{sound} = \sqrt{30 \times 10^{6}} = 5477$ m/s: $$f_r = \dfrac{5477}{2 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{5477}{0.016} = \boxed{3.42 \times 10^{5} \text{ Hz} = 342 \text{ kHz}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is why piezo stacks are operated near **ultrasonic frequencies** (kHz–MHz) for resonance amplification — the displacement is multiplied by the mechanical Q-factor $Q_m$, often yielding **1000× to 10,000× larger strokes** than DC operation. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Blocking Force of a Piezoelectric Stack **Problem:** A piezo stack of cross-section $A = 10 \text{ mm} \times 10 \text{ mm} = 10^{-4}$ m², $Y = 5 \times 10^{10}$ N/m², $d_{33} = 650 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, and $V = 100$ V is fully clamped. Find the force it exerts (blocking force $F_{block}$). **Step 1 — Free Strain (Unclamped)** $$S_{free} = d_{33} \cdot E = d_{33} \cdot \dfrac{V}{t}$$ With stack length $L = 8$ mm $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m: $$S_{free} = 650 \times 10^{-12} \times \dfrac{100}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 8.125 \times 10^{-6}$$ **Step 2 — Free Displacement** $$\Delta L_{free} = S_{free} \cdot L = 8.125 \times 10^{-6} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 6.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Equivalent Spring Stiffness** The piezo stack acts mechanically as a spring of stiffness: $$k_{stack} = \dfrac{Y \cdot A}{L} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{10} \times 10^{-4}}{8 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.25 \times 10^{8} \text{ N/m}$$ **Step 4 — Blocking Force (Zero Displacement Condition)** To make the displacement zero, the spring must be compressed by $\Delta L_{free}$. The force required is: $$F_{block} = k_{stack} \cdot \Delta L_{free} = 6.25 \times 10^{8} \times 6.5 \times 10^{-8}$$ $$\boxed{F_{block} = 40.6 \text{ N}}$$ > [!TIP] > **Designer's Law:** Blocking force and free displacement are **mutually exclusive** — you cannot have both maximum. Real motors operate at the **max-power point** at ≈ 50 % of $F_{block}$ and 50 % of $\Delta L_{free}$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Stick-Slip Step Resolution **Problem (14-mark favorite):** A stick-slip actuator uses a piezo with $d_{33} = 400 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, stack length $L = 10$ mm, applied voltage $V = 200$ V, slow ramp time $T_s = 10$ ms, and fast snap-back time $T_f = 100 \,\mu$s. Calculate (a) the slow-stroke displacement, (b) the inertia-limited minimum snap-back time to prevent the slider from following, and (c) the average velocity for a sawtooth waveform at 1 kHz. **Step 1 — Slow-Stroke Displacement** $$\Delta L_{slow} = d_{33} \cdot \dfrac{V}{L} \cdot L = d_{33} \cdot V = 400 \times 10^{-12} \times 200 = \boxed{8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 80 \text{ nm}}$$ **Step 2 — Minimum Snap-Back Time (Inertia Criterion)** The slider has mass $m$ and is pressed against the piezo with normal force $N$, giving static friction coefficient $\mu_s$. The slider slips only if: $$F_{snap} > \mu_s \cdot N \quad \text{AND} \quad a_{snap} \cdot m > \mu_k \cdot N$$ For the slider to **not follow** the snap-back, its inertia must keep it in place while the piezo retracts. If the slider's natural frequency is $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, the snap must complete within a quarter-period: $$T_f \ll \dfrac{1}{4 f_n} = \dfrac{\pi}{2 \omega_n}$$ For a 10 g slider with $k = 10^4$ N/m: $$\omega_n = \sqrt{10^4 / 0.01} = 1000 \text{ rad/s} \implies f_n \approx 159 \text{ Hz}$$ $$T_{f,max} = \dfrac{1}{4 \times 159} \approx 1.57 \text{ ms}$$ Since $T_f = 100 \,\mu s \ll 1.57$ ms, the slider indeed slips. ✓ **Step 3 — Average Velocity at 1 kHz** For a sawtooth drive at frequency $f_{drive} = 1$ kHz, each cycle advances the slider by one $\Delta L_{slow}$: $$v_{avg} = f_{drive} \cdot \Delta L_{slow} = 1000 \times 80 \times 10^{-9} = \boxed{8 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} = 80 \,\mu\text{m/s}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Resolution vs. Speed Trade-off:** The step size of stick-slip can be reduced to **< 1 nm** by lowering the ramp voltage, but speed drops proportionally. This is the **fundamental trade-off** in inchworm/stick-slip microrobot propulsion. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Frequently Asked) | Property | Stick-Slip | Inchworm (Walking) | Ultrasonic (USM) | |---|---|---|---| | Stroke | Unlimited (stepped) | Unlimited (stepped) | Limited (rotary/oscillatory) | | Resolution | 0.1 – 10 nm | 5 – 50 nm | 10 – 100 nm | | Speed | 1 – 10 mm/s | 0.5 – 5 mm/s | 10 – 500 mm/s | | Force | 0.1 – 5 N | 1 – 20 N | 0.5 – 50 N | | Operating frequency | DC – 1 kHz | DC – 500 Hz | 20 kHz – 10 MHz | | Backdrivability | No (friction lock) | No (clamps) | Friction-locked | | Vacuum compatibility | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent (no arcing) | | Magnetic-field free | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | Heat dissipation | Low | Moderate | High (dielectric losses) | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Piezoelectric Motor — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[High Frequency AC Driver 20 kHz to 10 MHz] --> B[Impedance Matching Network] B --> C[Piezoelectric Ceramic Stack PZT 5H] C --> D{Mechanical Coupling Stage} D --> E[Standing Wave Mode Ring] D --> F[Traveling Wave Mode Ring] D --> G[Linear Stick Slip Slider] E --> H[Friction Contact Interface] F --> H G --> I[Inertial Slider Mass] H --> J[Rotor or Linear Slider Output] I --> J J --> K[Position Sensor Encoder or LVDT] K --> L[Closed Loop Controller PID] L --> A M[DC Power Supply 12V to 48V] --> A N[Microcontroller Command Input] --> L O[Temperature Sensor NTC] --> L P[Curie Temperature 200 C Protection] --> O ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** The architecture shows the closed-loop control pathway (red arrows via KTU standard PID feedback). The piezo stack C converts electrical energy from A into mechanical vibration, which is mechanically transformed by the coupling stage D into one of three motion modalities (E, F, or G). The output is measured by K, fed back to the controller L, which adjusts the drive amplitude/frequency at A. NTC overtemperature protection (O, P) prevents depoling. ### 4.2 Working Principle — Stick-Slip Stepper ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Step 1 Stick Phase] -->|Slow ramp
100 nm in 10 ms| S2[Friction grips slider
No relative motion] S2 -->|Sudden snap| S3[Step 2 Slip Phase] S3 -->|Fast retract
100 ns edge| S4[Inertia holds slider
Slider stays put] S4 -->|Cycle repeats| S1 S1 --> Net[Net Displacement per cycle equals slow stroke] S2 --> Net S3 --> Net S4 --> Net ``` ### 4.3 Traveling-Wave USM — Rotor Drive Mechanism ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[Two Phase Excitation 90 deg apart] P1[Phase A Electrode] P2[Phase B Electrode 90 deg shifted] end P1 --> M1[Standing Wave 1 in PZT Ring] P2 --> M2[Standing Wave 2 in PZT Ring] M1 --> SUM[Vector Superposition] M2 --> SUM SUM --> TW[Traveling Flexural Wave] TW --> EM[Elliptical Particle Motion on Surface] EM --> FR[Friction Force on Rotor] FR --> ROT[Rotor Rotation] TW -.Reverse 90 deg.-> REV[Rotor Reversal] ``` ### 4.4 Equivalent Circuit Model (Mason's Model Simplified) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Electrical Port
V in and I in] --> C0[Clamped Capacitance C0] A --> NL1[Ideal Transformer
Turns Ratio N d33] NL1 --> NL2[Mechanical Port
Force and Velocity] NL2 --> RM[Motional Resistance Rm] NL2 --> LM[Motional Inductance Lm] NL2 --> CM[Motional Compliance Cm] NL2 --> ZL[Mechanical Load Z load] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Tip:** The motional branch ($R_m$, $L_m$, $C_m$) is a **series RLC circuit** that resonates at $f_s = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{L_m C_m})$. At this frequency, the piezo electrically looks like a **pure resistance** — and maximum mechanical power is delivered. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, PERUT864]** **Differentiate between the direct and converse piezoelectric effects. Which effect is exploited in piezoelectric motors and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Direct Effect | Converse Effect | |---|---|---| | Discovery | Jacques & Pierre Curie, 1880 | Lippmann, 1881; confirmed by Curies | | Energy flow | Mechanical → Electrical | Electrical → Mechanical | | Governing equation | $D = d \cdot T$ | $S = d \cdot E$ | | Application | Sensors (force, pressure, accelerometer) | **Actuators / Motors** | | Symmetry | $d_{ij}^{direct} = d_{ij}^{converse}$ (Maxwell reciprocity) | Same tensor | Piezoelectric motors exploit the **converse piezoelectric effect** because they require **electrical-to-mechanical** energy conversion — applying a voltage induces a controlled mechanical strain that drives the rotor/slider. **(3 marks)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PERUT864]** **What is the significance of the electromechanical coupling factor $k_{33}$? State its typical value for PZT-5H and explain why it cannot exceed 1.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The electromechanical coupling factor $k_{33}$ is defined as: $$k_{33}^2 = \dfrac{\text{Mechanical energy stored}}{\text{Electrical energy input}} = \dfrac{d_{33}^2}{s^E_{33} \cdot \epsilon^T_{33}}$$ - For PZT-5H, $k_{33} \approx \mathbf{0.75}$ (75 %). - $k_{33}$ cannot exceed 1 because it is a **ratio of energies stored**, and by the second law of thermodynamics, you cannot extract more mechanical energy than the electrical energy input (accounting for losses). - Higher $k$ means a more efficient motor. PZT ceramics are the best engineered materials because of their optimized domain structure. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** Derive the constitutive equations of a linear piezoelectric material. Explain the physical meaning of each term in the equations. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A piezoelectric motor operates on the stick-slip principle with the following specifications: stack length $L = 12$ mm, $d_{33} = 500 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, applied voltage $V = 150$ V, slow ramp time $t_s = 5$ ms, fast snap-back time $t_f = 50 \,\mu$s. Calculate (i) the free displacement per step, (ii) the maximum stepping rate, and (iii) the average velocity in continuous operation. **[7 marks]** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(a)** The linear piezoelectric constitutive relations (IEEE 1987 standard form) are derived from the **first law of thermodynamics** (energy conservation) applied to a coupled electromechanical system. The two independent thermodynamic potentials are the **Gibbs free energy** $G(T,E)$ and the **internal energy** $U(S,D)$, where $T$ is stress, $E$ is electric field, $S$ is strain, and $D$ is electric displacement. From $dG = -S \, dT - D \, dE$, the partial derivatives give: $$\left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial T}\right)_E = -S \quad \text{and} \quad \left(\dfrac{\partial G}{\partial E}\right)_T = -D$$ Differentiating $S(T,E)$ and $D(T,E)$ and applying **Maxwell reciprocity** $\left(\dfrac{\partial S}{\partial E}\right)_T = \left(\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial T}\right)_E = d$: $$\boxed{S_i = s^E_{ij} \, T_j + d_{ik} \, E_k \quad \text{--- (Motor Equation)}}$$ $$\boxed{D_k = d_{jk} \, T_j + \epsilon^T_{km} \, E_m \quad \text{--- (Sensor Equation)}}$$ | Symbol | Physical Meaning | |---|---| | $S_i$ | Mechanical strain — fractional deformation (m/m) | | $T_j$ | Mechanical stress — force per unit area (N/m²) | | $E_k$ | Electric field — voltage gradient (V/m) | | $D_k$ | Electric displacement — surface charge density (C/m²) | | $s^E_{ij}$ | Elastic compliance at constant $E$ (m²/N) — "softness" of the material | | $d_{ik}$ | Piezoelectric strain coefficient (m/V or C/N) — coupling strength | | $\epsilon^T_{km}$ | Dielectric permittivity at constant $T$ (F/m) — "electrical softness" | **For a piezoelectric motor,** we set $T \to 0$ (no external load) and apply a field $E$: $$S = d \cdot E \implies \Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V$$ This is the fundamental motor equation showing that **voltage directly controls displacement** with no current flow in the static case. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question A(b)** **Step 1 — Free Displacement Per Step** Using $\Delta L = d_{33} \cdot V$: $$\Delta L = 500 \times 10^{-12} \times 150 = 7.5 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = \boxed{75 \text{ nm}}$$ **(2 marks)** **Step 2 — Maximum Stepping Rate** The cycle time is $T_{cycle} = t_s + t_f = 5 \text{ ms} + 50 \,\mu\text{s} = 5.05 \text{ ms}$. $$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{5.05 \times 10^{-3}} = \boxed{198 \text{ Hz}}$$ **(2 marks)** **Step 3 — Average Velocity** $$v_{avg} = f_{max} \cdot \Delta L = 198 \times 75 \times 10^{-9} = 1.485 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m/s} = \boxed{14.85 \,\mu\text{m/s}}$$ **(3 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Writing $d_{33} = 500$ pm/V directly without converting to $500 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V. This causes a 10⁹ error. (Deduct **1 mark**) > 2. **Confusing field $E$ with voltage $V$:** $E = V/t$ — you must use the layer thickness $t$, not the stack length. For a multilayer stack, the field is $E = V/(N \cdot t)$. (Deduct **1 mark**) > 3. **Forgetting Maxwell reciprocity:** When asked to derive, many students skip the step $\partial^2 G / \partial E \partial T = \partial^2 G / \partial T \partial E$ which proves $d_{ij} = d_{ji}$. (Deduct **1 mark**) > 4. **Not stating boundary conditions:** A free body ($T=0$) vs clamped ($S=0$) gives different equations. Always mention which.
