(Deduct **1 mark**) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** **(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the construction and working of a **traveling-wave ultrasonic motor (TWUSM)**. Discuss its advantages over conventional electromagnetic motors. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A PZT-5H piezo stack motor has $N = 200$ layers, each of thickness $t = 100 \,\mu$m, cross-section $A = 5 \text{ mm} \times 5 \text{ mm}$, Young's modulus $Y = 6 \times 10^{10}$ N/m², $d_{33} = 600 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V. Driven at $V = 120$ V. Compute (i) the free elongation, (ii) the blocking force, and (iii) the maximum work output per cycle at resonance. **[7 marks]** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(a)** **Construction (refer to SECTION 4.3 diagram):** - A **thin ring** of PZT-5H ceramic, polarized in the radial direction. - Two sets of electrodes on the top surface, **spatially offset by λ/4** (quarter wavelength). - A **stator** is bonded to the ring; a **rotor** is pressed against the stator via a spring preload. **Working Principle:** 1. Phase A and Phase B electrodes are excited with **sinusoidal voltages at frequency $f_r$**, phase-shifted by **90° (π/2 rad)**. 2. Each phase produces a **standing flexural wave** in the ring. 3. The two standing waves superpose to form a **traveling flexural wave** moving around the ring circumference. 4. Surface particles of the stator execute an **elliptical (rolling) motion**. 5. The frictional contact between stator and rotor converts this elliptical motion into **uniaxial rotor rotation**. 6. Reversing the 90° phase reverses the wave direction and hence the rotor. **Advantages over Electromagnetic Motors:** | Feature | TWUSM | DC/AC Motor | |---|---|---| | Power density | 10× higher | Baseline | | Holding torque (no power) | High (friction lock) | Zero | | Vacuum compatibility | Excellent (no arcing) | Poor (brushes) | | EMI/EMC | Silent (no magnetic field) | High noise | | Resolution | nm level | µm level | | Speed | Low (≤ 10,000 rpm) | High | | Lifetime | Limited by friction wear | Bearing-limited | | Magnetic interference | None | Severe (MRI-incompatible) | **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Question B(b)** **Step 1 — Free Elongation** $$\Delta L_{free} = N \cdot d_{33} \cdot V = 200 \times 600 \times 10^{-12} \times 120 = 1.44 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m} = \boxed{14.4 \,\mu\text{m}}$$ **(2 marks)** **Step 2 — Stiffness** Total stack length: $L = N \cdot t = 200 \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 0.02$ m $$k = \dfrac{Y \cdot A}{L} = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{10} \times (5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{0.02} = \dfrac{6 \times 10^{10} \times 25 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02} = 7.5 \times 10^{7} \text{ N/m}$$ **(2 marks)** **Step 3 — Blocking Force** $$F_{block} = k \cdot \Delta L_{free} = 7.5 \times 10^{7} \times 1.44 \times 10^{-5} = \boxed{1080 \text{ N}}$$ **(1 mark)** **Step 4 — Maximum Work per Cycle** At resonance, the displacement is amplified by $Q_m$. With $Q_m = 70$: $$\Delta L_{res} = Q_m \cdot \Delta L_{free} = 70 \times 14.4 \,\mu\text{m} = 1008 \,\mu\text{m} \approx 1.0 \text{ mm}$$ Maximum work per cycle (full energy exchange between kinetic and potential): $$W_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} k \Delta L_{res}^2 = 0.5 \times 7.5 \times 10^{7} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})^2 = \boxed{37.5 \text{ mJ}}$$ **(2 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in Piezoelectric Motor Problems (lose 1–3 marks each):** > > 1. **Forgetting the multilayer factor $N$:** For a stack of $N$ layers driven by $V$, the field per layer is $V/(N t)$ but the *displacement* is $N d_{33} V$. Students often drop the $N$. > 2. **Confusing piezoelectric $d$ with $g$:** Always verify units — $d$ is in m/V, $g$ is in Vm/N. They differ by $\epsilon_0 \epsilon_r$. > 3. **Treating piezoelectricity as one-way:** In a *coupled* system, the mechanical strain also generates a charge back ($D = dT$). For static motor analysis, set $T=0$ — but in dynamic resonance, both equations matter. > 4. **Ignoring resonance amplification:** At DC, displacement is ~65 nm for a 100 V stack. At resonance with $Q_m = 70$, it becomes ~4.5 mm. KTU often asks to compare these — students forget $Q_m$. > 5. **Wrong sign convention:** PZT elongates along the poling direction for a positive field on the poling electrode. Reverse polarity → contraction. Always state the poling direction. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Piezoelectric motors use the converse piezoelectric effect** ($S = d \cdot E$), converting voltage into nanometer-scale mechanical strain. - ✅ **Governing equation:** $\Delta L = N \cdot d_{33} \cdot V$ for a multilayer stack. - ✅ **Three working principles:** Stick-slip, Inchworm, Ultrasonic (standing/traveling wave). - ✅ **Key material:** PZT-5H ($d_{33} = 650$ pm/V, $T_C = 200$ °C, $k_{33} = 0.75$). - ✅ **Resonance operation** amplifies displacement by $Q_m \approx 70$ for PZT, enabling millimeter strokes at ultrasonic frequencies. - ✅ **Blocking force** is the force when displacement is fully suppressed — it represents the maximum load-carrying capacity. - ✅ **Max-power operating point** is at 50 % of free displacement and 50 % of blocking force. - ✅ **KTU-favorite values to memorize:** PZT-5H $d_{33} \approx 650$ pm/V, $k_{33} \approx 0.75$, Curie $T \approx 200$ °C. - ✅ **Constitutive equations** (IEEE 1987): Two linear coupled equations with $s^E, d, \epsilon^T$ tensors. - ✅ **Maxwell reciprocity:** Same $d$ tensor governs direct AND converse effects. - ✅ **Stick-slip formula:** $v_{avg} = f_{drive} \times d_{33} \times V$ (assuming slip is complete). - ✅ **Curie temperature** is the hard limit — exceeding it permanently depoles the piezo. - ✅ **Vacuum & MRI compatibility:** No magnetic field → unique advantage for space, medical, and scientific instruments. - ✅ **Resolution vs. Speed trade-off:** Stick-slip = nm resolution / mm/s; USM = µm resolution / cm-s to m-s. - ✅ **Efficiency:** Piezoelectric motors are typically 30–60 % efficient (vs. 90 %+ for electromagnetic), but their *holding torque at zero power* is unique. ---

topics.title: Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys (MSMAs)** are a class of actively responsive, multifunctional smart materials that exhibit a large, reversible, and externally controllable mechanical strain (typically **up to 10%**) when subjected to an applied **magnetic field**, without the need for any change in temperature. The strain originates from the **field-induced reorientation (rotation) of martensite twin variants** within the material's crystallographic lattice, rather than from the thermally activated phase transformation (austenite ↔ martensite) seen in conventional Shape Memory Alloys (SMAs).

The canonical and most widely investigated MSMA system is the Heusler-type alloy **Ni₂MnGa**, which crystallizes in the L2₁ ordered B2 cubic austenitic phase at high temperature and transforms to a tetragonal martensitic phase (typically 5M or 7M modulated structures) upon cooling. The magnetic-field-induced strain (MFIS) in MSMAs is fundamentally distinct from classical magnetostriction (which is only ~10⁻⁵ in Fe, Co, Ni), and is sometimes referred to as **"giant magnetostrain"** or the **"magnetic shape memory effect."**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PERUT864 - Module 3):**
> MSMAs are classified as **active smart materials** used as transducers in microrobotic actuation. They convert magnetic energy directly into mechanical work, making them ideal candidates for **wireless, untethered, fast-response microactuators** operating at sub-millimeter scales.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Magnetic Accordion"**
> Imagine a deck of playing cards held together with a rubber band — this represents a single crystal of martensite made up of many **twin variants** stacked together. Each "card" (variant) has a slightly tilted magnetization direction. When you apply a magnetic field (the "wind"), the cards whose magnetization is more aligned with the field try to "flap" to face the wind, while the misaligned ones get "squeezed thinner." Because the cards are mechanically bonded by the crystal lattice, the entire deck **expands in one direction and contracts in another** — just like a tiny magnetic accordion breathing in response to a magnetic field. Remove the field, and a small mechanical bias spring pushes the cards back to their original orientation. The total length change (strain) can be as much as **10%**, which is roughly **1000× larger** than in an ordinary magnetostrictive material.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Bold-Highlighted)

- **Maximum magnetic-field-induced strain (MFIS):** up to **10 %** (theoretical max ~ 12 % for Ni₂MnGa)
- **Operating bandwidth:** up to **kHz range** (limited by eddy currents, hence lamination/micropatterning is required)
- **Magnetic anisotropy constant (Kᵤ):** ~ **1.7 × 10⁵ J/m³** (for Ni-Mn-Ga)
- **Magnetization saturation (Mₛ):** ~ **0.6–0.8 T** (for Ni-Mn-Ga)
- **Curie temperature (T_c):** ~ **370 K** (Ni₂MnGa); **martensite start (Mₛ):** ~ **210 K**
- **Operational temperature window:** below martensite finish (M_f) and below Curie temperature

## 1.4 Distinguishing MSMAs from Other Smart Materials

| Smart Material | Driving Stimulus | Typical Strain | Response Time | Actuation Principle |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional SMA** (Nitinol) | Temperature | 6 – 8 % | ~ 1 s (thermal) | Thermoelastic phase change |
| **Piezoelectric** (PZT) | Electric field (V) | 0.1 – 0.2 % | µs | Dipole reorientation |
| **Magnetostrictive** (Terfenol-D) | Magnetic field | 0.1 – 0.2 % | µs | Domain rotation |
| **MSMA (Ni₂MnGa)** | **Magnetic field** | **6 – 10 %** | **ms to sub-ms** | **Twin variant reorientation** |
| **Dielectric Elastomer** | Voltage | 10 – 100 % | ms | Electrostatic compression |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Crystallographic Origin of the Effect

The MSMA effect requires three **co-existing conditions** within a single material:

1. **Ferromagnetic ordering** — the material must possess a spontaneous magnetization $M_s$ below the Curie temperature $T_c$.
2. **Martensitic phase** — the material must be in its low-symmetry martensitic state (typically tetragonal 5M/7M modulated or non-modulated NM tetragonal), so that the crystal possesses **twin variants** — regions of the same phase but with different crystallographic orientations.
3. **High magnetic anisotropy** — the magnetization easy axis must be locked to a specific crystallographic direction within each variant (magnetocrystalline anisotropy energy $K_u$ is large).

When a magnetic field $\mathbf{H}$ is applied, the **Zeeman energy** $E_Z = -\mu_0 M_s H \cos\theta$ (where $\theta$ is the angle between $M_s$ and $H$) is minimized when the **magnetization vector of each variant aligns with the field**. Variants with their easy axis parallel to $H$ grow at the expense of those with their easy axis perpendicular to $H$, by **twin-boundary motion**.

## 2.2 Operational Logic – Step-by-Step Bullet Reasoning

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Material is cooled below $M_f$ into a self-accommodated multi-variant martensitic state. A small **mechanical bias stress** $\sigma_{bias}$ is applied to set a single-variant starting configuration.
- **Step 2 — Magnetic Loading:** External field $H$ is applied. The Zeeman energy difference between favorably and unfavorably oriented variants becomes $\Delta E_Z = \mu_0 M_s H (1 - \cos\theta)$.
- **Step 3 — Twin Boundary Motion:** When the magnetic driving force $\Delta E_Z / V$ exceeds the twinning stress threshold, **twin boundaries sweep** across the sample, expanding the favorably oriented variant and shrinking the unfavorable one.
- **Step 4 — Macroscopic Strain:** The crystallographic lattice distortion of the martensite (e.g., $c/a = 0.94$ for 5M Ni-Mn-Ga) is converted into an external **macroscopic shape change** $\varepsilon = 1 - c/a$ (typically 6 – 10 %).
- **Step 5 — Reset:** Removing $H$ alone is insufficient; the bias stress $\sigma_{bias}$ restores the original variant configuration (since the field-induced state is energetically metastable).
- **Step 6 — Cyclic Actuation:** Reversible MFIS is obtained by oscillating $H$ against $\sigma_{bias}$.

## 2.3 The Villari (Inverse Magnetostriction) Connection

The MSMA effect is the **superior cousin** of the Villari effect. In the classical Villari effect, a mechanical stress alters the magnetic domain configuration, producing a change in $B$. In MSMAs, the field moves the twin boundaries, and the resulting strain produces a **change in magnetization that depends on the variant fraction**, giving rise to a giant "pseudo-Villari" coupling useful for **magnetic sensing** and energy harvesting.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon_{MFIS} = 1 - \dfrac{c}{a}$ | Maximum achievable strain from twin reorientation | $0.06$ – $0.10$ (dimensionless) |
| 2 | $E_Z = -\mu_0 M_s H \cos\theta$ | Zeeman energy per unit volume | $\text{J/m}^3$ |
| 3 | $E_{anis} = K_u \sin^2\theta$ | Magnetocrystalline anisotropy energy density | $\sim 1.7 \times 10^5 \text{ J/m}^3$ (Ni-Mn-Ga) |
| 4 | $\sigma_{mag} = \dfrac{\mu_0 M_s H}{(\varepsilon_{MFIS})}$ | Equivalent magnetic "stress" driving twin motion | MPa |
| 5 | $H_{crit} = \dfrac{\sigma_{tw} \cdot \varepsilon_{MFIS}}{\mu_0 M_s}$ | Critical field required to overcome twinning stress | $\sim 0.4$ – $0.8 \text{ T}$ |
| 6 | $T_{op} < T_c$ and $T_{op} < M_f$ | Operability window (must be in martensitic ferromagnetic state) | K |
| 7 | $\tau_{resp} \propto \dfrac{1}{f_{eddy}}$ | Response time limited by eddy-current damping (lamination improves this) | $\text{ms}$ range |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ | Magnetostrain definition (used for all magnetic SMAs) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $W_{out} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot V$ | Mechanical work density per actuation cycle | $\text{J/m}^3$ |
| 10 | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$ | Permeability of free space (constant) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always write the **magnetization $M_s$** with SI units of **A/m** inside energy expressions, and remember the factor $\mu_0$ (T·m/A) when converting to $\text{J/m}^3$. Skipping the $\mu_0$ is the #1 unit mistake in KTU answer sheets.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility in Microrobotics

MSMAs are particularly compelling in microrobotics because:

- They **eliminate the need for thermal insulation or heating elements** that plague conventional SMA actuators.
- They operate from **battery-free / wireless magnetic excitation** — perfect for **untethered capsule endoscopes**, **micro-swimmers**, and **in-vivo drug delivery robots**.
- The **high force density** (~ 1 – 3 MPa) at small scales outperforms piezo ceramics, which lose force at sub-mm dimensions.
- They serve dual roles — as **actuators** (input: field → output: motion) and as **sensors** (input: motion → output: $B$-field change via inverse MSMA / Villari effect).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Theoretical Strain from Lattice Parameters

For a tetragonal martensite with lattice parameters $a = b$ (in-plane) and $c$ (long axis), the MFIS along the $c$-axis is obtained by **rotating a twin variant** so that its short axis becomes the long axis of the sample.

**Starting condition (no field):** All variants in the field direction have their $c$-axis perpendicular to the applied field.

**Final condition (saturated field):** The favorably oriented variant dominates, with its $c$-axis along the field.

The fractional length change along the field direction is:

$$\Delta L / L = (c - a) / a = 1 - (a/c)$$

**Step-by-step algebraic expansion:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{MFIS} &= \frac{L_{final} - L_{initial}}{L_{initial}} \\
&= \frac{a - c}{c} \quad \text{(since the variant's $c$-axis rotates into the field direction)} \\
&= \frac{a}{c} - 1 \\
&= 1 - \frac{c}{a} \quad \text{(rearranged)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical example (KTU-style):** For a 5M Ni-Mn-Ga martensite with $a = 5.98 \text{ Å}$ and $c = 5.566 \text{ Å}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{MFIS} &= 1 - \frac{5.566}{5.98} \\
&= 1 - 0.9308 \\
&= 0.0692 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 6.92\%
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We divide $c$ by $a$ first (smaller/larger), giving a number less than 1. Subtracting from 1 yields the positive strain. This matches published single-crystal Ni-Mn-Ga MFIS values of ~ 6 – 9.5 %.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Critical Magnetic Field for Twin Boundary Motion

**Energy balance per unit volume** between magnetic driving energy and mechanical twinning resistance:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Magnetic driving energy density} \;&=\; \mu_0 M_s H \,(1 - \cos\theta) \\
\text{Required mechanical work density} \;&=\; \sigma_{tw} \cdot \varepsilon_{MFIS}
\end{aligned}$$

Setting them equal at the critical field $H_{crit}$ (assuming $\theta = 90°$ so that $\cos\theta = 0$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\mu_0 M_s H_{crit} &= \sigma_{tw} \cdot \varepsilon_{MFIS} \\
H_{crit} &= \frac{\sigma_{tw} \cdot \varepsilon_{MFIS}}{\mu_0 M_s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical evaluation with KTU-typical values:** $\sigma_{tw} = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$, $\varepsilon_{MFIS} = 0.07$, $M_s = 5.5 \times 10^5 \text{ A/m}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{crit} &= \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{6}) \times 0.07}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7}) \times (5.5 \times 10^{5})} \;\text{A/m} \\
&= \frac{1.05 \times 10^{5}}{6.912 \times 10^{-1}} \\
&\approx 1.52 \times 10^{5} \text{ A/m} \\
&\approx 0.19 \text{ T}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** $1 \text{ T} = 10^4 \text{ A/m}$ in the CGS-to-SI approximate scaling for $\mu_0 H$. Hence $1.52 \times 10^5 \text{ A/m} \approx 0.15$ – $0.2 \text{ T}$, which agrees with the experimentally observed saturation field of Ni-Mn-Ga.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Density Ratio (Figure of Merit)

A useful KTU-style figure of merit is the **magnetic-to-mechanical energy conversion ratio**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\eta_{conv} &= \frac{W_{mech}}{W_{mag}} = \frac{\tfrac{1}{2}\sigma_{tw}\varepsilon_{MFIS}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\mu_0 M_s H_{crit}} \\
&= \frac{\sigma_{tw}\varepsilon_{MFIS}}{\mu_0 M_s H_{crit}} \\
&= \frac{\sigma_{tw}\varepsilon_{MFIS}}{\mu_0 M_s \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_{tw}\varepsilon_{MFIS}}{\mu_0 M_s}} \\
&= 1 \quad \text{(at the critical field, by construction)}
\end{aligned}$$

In **practice** (sub-critical or over-driving), the actual useful efficiency is:

$$\eta_{useful} = \frac{\sigma_{load} \cdot \varepsilon_{MFIS}}{\mu_0 M_s H_{applied}} \cdot \eta_{mech}$$

where $\eta_{mech}$ accounts for hysteresis, eddy-current losses, and bias-spring losses. Typical real efficiencies lie in the range **2 – 6 %**, which is acceptable for the millimetric strokes needed in microrobotic joints.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Operating Regimes of an MSMA Element

| Regime | Condition | Physical State | Useful Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Zero-field, self-accommodated | $H = 0$, $\sigma = 0$ | Multi-variant martensite | No net strain; reference state |
| 2. Field ON, no bias | $H > H_{crit}$, $\sigma = 0$ | Variant selection partial | Partial MFIS; unstable (snaps back) |
| 3. Field ON, with bias | $H > H_{crit}$, $\sigma_{bias} < \sigma_{mag}$ | Variant selected | **Stable, repeatable MFIS — operating regime** |
| 4. Over-field, blocked | $H \gg H_{crit}$, $\varepsilon = 0$ | Stress build-up | **Sensing mode** (high force) |
| 5. Thermal cycle | $T > A_f$ | Austenite | Field has no effect; reset state |
| 6. $T > T_c$ | Paramagnetic | Austenite | No MSMA effect — must stay below Curie |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** A common KTU question asks to **plot MFIS vs. applied field** for the two cases (a) with bias stress and (b) without bias. The curve with bias shows a **closed hysteresis loop** (repeatable actuation), while without bias it shows an **open "L-shaped" snap-through curve** (one-way shape change).

## 3.5 Derivation 4: Lamination Requirement from Eddy-Current Skin Depth

For an MSMA element operating at frequency $f$, the electromagnetic skin depth is:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\mu_0 \sigma_{elec} \, 2\pi f}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \mu_0 \sigma_{elec} f}}$$

For Ni-Mn-Ga with electrical conductivity $\sigma_{elec} \approx 1.4 \times 10^6 \text{ S/m}$, at $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1.4 \times 10^6 \times 1000}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{5.52 \times 10^{3}}} \\
&= \sqrt{1.81 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&\approx 1.35 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \\
&\approx 13.5 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** A single 13.5 mm skin depth at 1 kHz is large, but at **higher frequencies** or **larger samples** the skin depth becomes comparable to the sample half-thickness, causing **eddy-current shielding** of the interior. The KTU fix is to laminate the MSMA into **layers of thickness $t < \delta/2$** separated by insulating epoxy, which is essential for **kHz-bandwidth** actuators used in microrobotic oscillators and pumps.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Operating Principle of an MSMA Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT: External Magnetic Field H_ext applied to MSMA crystal"] --> B["Zeeman Energy Minimization: Variants with M parallel to H are energetically favored"]
    B --> C["Twin Boundary Motion: Favored variants grow; unfavored variants shrink"]
    C --> D["Macroscopic Lattice Distortion: c axis rotates into field direction"]
    D --> E["OUTPUT: Large macroscopic strain epsilon approx 6 to 10 percent"]
    E --> F["Mechanical Bias Stress sigma_bias opposes variant reorientation"]
    F --> G["Reversible Cyclic Actuation: Oscillate H and sigma_bias alternately"]
    G --> H["APPLICATION: Microactuator / Micropump / Microgripper in microrobot"]
    H --> A

    I["CONSTRAINT 1: Temperature T less than Martensite Finish M_f"] --> A
    J["CONSTRAINT 2: Temperature T less than Curie T_c for ferromagnetism"] --> A
    K["CONSTRAINT 3: H greater than H_critical approx 0.2 T for Ni-Mn-Ga"] --> A
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — MSMA-Based Microrobot Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["External Electromagnetic Coil Driver"] --> B["H field generated in workspace"]
    B --> C["MSMA Active Element: Ni-Mn-Ga single crystal, laminated"]
    C --> D["Mechanical Bias Spring: provides restoring force"]
    D --> E["Output Mechanical Motion: stroke up to 10 percent of length"]
    E --> F["End Effector: microgripper OR micropump diaphragm OR propulsion fin"]
    F --> G["Microrobot Body: sub-cm scale, polymer or bio-compatible shell"]
    G --> H["Feedback Sensor: Hall sensor reads B field change for closed-loop control"]
    H --> I["Controller Microchip: adjusts coil current"]
    I --> A
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Twin Variant Reorientation Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["STATE A: Zero Field. Two martensite twin variants T1 and T2 intergrown. Macroscopic length L0."] --> S2["Apply magnetic field H along x axis"]
    S2 --> S3["Variant T2 whose easy axis is parallel to H grows via twin boundary motion"]
    S3 --> S4["STATE B: Saturated Field. Variant T2 dominates. Macroscopic length becomes L0 times 1 minus c over a."]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation (KTU-style):** This three-state sequence directly supports the derivation in **Section 3.1**, where the strain $\varepsilon = 1 - c/a$ is shown to be purely a function of the **lattice tetragonality**, not of the field strength. The field merely *selects* which variant dominates; the *magnitude* of strain is fixed by the crystal chemistry of the alloy.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT864]**
**"Define Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys (MSMAs) and state two key features that distinguish them from conventional Shape Memory Alloys."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys (MSMAs) are ferromagnetic smart materials that undergo large, reversible strain when subjected to a magnetic field, without any temperature change. The effect arises from the **field-induced reorientation of martensite twin variants**, not from a phase change. Two distinguishing features versus conventional SMAs:
1. **Actuation stimulus is magnetic, not thermal** → no need for heating/cooling cycles.
2. **Response is in milliseconds, not seconds**, with achievable strain up to **10 %**, which is comparable to SMAs but achieved at much higher bandwidth.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for each distinguishing feature = 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, PERUT864]**
**"What is the role of mechanical bias stress in an MSMA actuator? Why is it necessary for repeatable operation?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The bias stress $\sigma_{bias}$ (typically provided by a small spring) serves to **(i) initialize** the MSMA in a single-variant state before the magnetic field is applied, and **(ii) restore** the original variant configuration when the field is removed. Without bias, the field-induced variant selection is **monostable** — the sample snaps back to the original state only after a large reverse field, producing a one-way "memory" effect. With bias, the cyclic operation $H \uparrow \Rightarrow \varepsilon \uparrow$ and $H \downarrow \Rightarrow \sigma_{bias} \Rightarrow \varepsilon \downarrow$ yields a **closed hysteresis loop**, enabling **continuous reversible actuation** at kHz rates — essential for microrobotic oscillators, pumps, and locomotion fins. *(Full credit for stating all three points: initialization, restoration, and reversible closed-loop operation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of a Magnetic Shape Memory Alloy (MSMA) actuator. Discuss the role of twin boundaries, magnetic anisotropy, and bias stress in the operation. Use Ni-Mn-Ga as the example material.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** For a single crystal of Ni-Mn-Ga MSMA with lattice parameters $a = 5.98$ Å and $c = 5.566$ Å, calculate the maximum theoretical magnetic-field-induced strain. If the twinning stress is $\sigma_{tw} = 1.8$ MPa and saturation magnetization is $M_s = 5.5 \times 10^5$ A/m, find the critical magnetic field $H_{crit}$ required to drive twin-boundary motion at room temperature. Use $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

**1. Schematic & Variant Description (2 marks):**
Draw or describe a single crystal of Ni-Mn-Ga in its martensitic state, showing **two interpenetrating twin variants** $T_1$ and $T_2$ separated by a **twin boundary**. Each variant is a tetragonal cell with $c$-axis oriented either along the sample axis (variant $T_1$) or perpendicular (variant $T_2$).

**2. Magnetic Anisotropy and Field Action (2 marks):**
Each variant has its **magnetization easy axis locked** along the $c$-axis by a strong magnetocrystalline anisotropy $K_u \approx 1.7 \times 10^5$ J/m³. When a field $H$ is applied, the Zeeman energy $E_Z = -\mu_0 M_s H \cos\theta$ favors variants whose $c$-axis is parallel to $H$.

**3. Twin Boundary Motion and Strain (2 marks):**
When $\mu_0 M_s H > \sigma_{tw} \cdot \varepsilon_{MFIS}$, the **twin boundary sweeps**, expanding the favorable variant and producing a macroscopic strain $\varepsilon = 1 - c/a$ along the field direction.

**4. Bias Stress and Cyclic Operation (1 mark):**
A bias spring provides a restoring force, ensuring a **closed-loop** reversible cycle. Typical operating parameters: $H_{crit} \approx 0.2$ T, $\varepsilon \approx 6$ – $10$%, response time $1$ – $10$ ms.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Maximum theoretical strain (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{MFIS} &= 1 - \frac{c}{a} \\
&= 1 - \frac{5.566 \text{ Å}}{5.98 \text{ Å}} \\
&= 1 - 0.9308 \\
&= 0.0692 \\
&\approx 6.92\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Critical magnetic field (4 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{crit} &= \frac{\sigma_{tw} \cdot \varepsilon_{MFIS}}{\mu_0 M_s} \\
&= \frac{(1.8 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa}) \times 0.0692}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7} \text{ H/m}) \times (5.5 \times 10^{5} \text{ A/m})} \\
&= \frac{1.2456 \times 10^{5}}{6.912 \times 10^{-1}} \\
&\approx 1.802 \times 10^{5} \text{ A/m}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to Tesla (using $B = \mu_0 H$ gives the field-equivalent, but here we report $H$ in A/m; for the magnetic flux density in the sample, $B = \mu_0(H + M) \approx \mu_0 M_s \approx 0.69$ T at saturation).

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{MFIS} \approx 6.92\% \quad ; \quad H_{crit} \approx 1.80 \times 10^5 \text{ A/m}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

> **(a) [7 marks]** Compare the actuation mechanism, achievable strain, response time, and energy density of MSMAs with three other smart-material classes used in microrobotics: (i) conventional SMAs, (ii) piezoelectrics, and (iii) magnetostrictive materials (e.g., Terfenol-D). Present your answer in a comparative table and comment on the suitability of MSMAs for wireless, untethered microrobotic applications.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** Describe the design of a **laminated MSMA microactuator** intended for use in a wirelessly controlled capsule endoscope. Discuss (i) why lamination is required, (ii) the typical layer thickness constraint, and (iii) the role of an external Helmholtz coil pair and Hall-effect feedback sensor in closed-loop operation.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Comparative Table (4 marks):**

| Property | Conventional SMA (Nitinol) | Piezoelectric (PZT) | Magnetostrictive (Terfenol-D) | **MSMA (Ni-Mn-Ga)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Stimulus | Temperature | Electric field (V) | Magnetic field | **Magnetic field** |
| Achievable strain | 6 – 8 % | 0.1 – 0.2 % | 0.1 – 0.2 % | **6 – 10 %** |
| Response time | ~ 1 s (thermal) | µs | µs | **ms** |
| Energy density | ~ 10⁷ J/m³ | ~ 10⁵ J/m³ | ~ 10⁴ J/m³ | **~ 10⁵ – 10⁶ J/m³** |
| Actuation principle | Phase change (A → M) | Dipole rotation | Domain rotation | **Twin variant reorientation** |
| Wireless actuation? | Difficult (needs heat) | Difficult (needs wires) | Easy (no wires) | **Easy (no wires)** |

**Suitability commentary (3 marks):**
MSMAs uniquely combine **giant strain** (comparable to SMAs) with **fast response** (comparable to piezo/magnetostrictive) and **magnetic actuation** (wireless). This makes them **ideal for untethered microrobots** such as capsule endoscopes, where (a) on-board power is limited, (b) no physical tether is acceptable, and (c) large strokes (sub-mm to mm) are required for gripper or pump actuation. The trade-off is the **need for an external coil** to generate the field and **lamination** to suppress eddy currents.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Why lamination is required (3 marks):**
Ni-Mn-Ga is electrically conductive ($\sigma_{elec} \approx 1.4 \times 10^6$ S/m). At actuation frequencies of 100 Hz – 1 kHz, **eddy currents** induced by the alternating magnetic field oppose the field's penetration into the bulk, creating a **skin-effect shielding** of the interior of the MSMA element. This **wastes energy as Joule heat** and **slows the response**. Lamination divides the bulk into thin sheets separated by an insulating epoxy (e.g., epoxy-impregnated glass fiber), forcing eddy currents to circulate within each thin sheet, where they are much smaller, thus **uniformly magnetizing** the entire cross-section.

**(ii) Typical layer thickness constraint (2 marks):**
The layer thickness $t$ must satisfy $t < 2\delta$, where $\delta = \sqrt{1/(\pi \mu_0 \sigma_{elec} f)}$ is the skin depth. For $f = 1$ kHz, $\delta \approx 13.5$ mm (Section 3.5 result), so practically $t < 1$ mm; for $f = 100$ kHz, $\delta \approx 1.35$ mm, so $t < 200$ µm. Typical industrial MSMA laminations are **50 – 200 µm** thick.

**(iii) External Helmholtz coil pair and Hall sensor (2 marks):**
A **Helmholtz coil pair** (two identical coaxial coils separated by one radius) produces a **uniform magnetic field** over the MSMA volume. Field direction and magnitude are controlled by adjusting the coil current. A **Hall-effect sensor** placed adjacent to the MSMA measures the local $B$-field; because variant reorientation changes the local flux (via the inverse MSMA / Villari effect), the Hall voltage serves as a **position feedback** signal for a **closed-loop controller**, ensuring repeatable, drift-free actuation of the capsule endoscope's gripper or pump.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in MSMA Questions (KTU Board Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $\mu_0$** in the Zeeman energy — examiners deduct **1 mark** consistently for this.
> 2. **Confusing saturation magnetization $M_s$ with magnetic field $H$** — they have *different* units (A/m vs A/m or T vs A/m); verify with the equation.
> 3. **Strain sign error** — students often write $\varepsilon = (c - a)/a$ (negative) instead of the correct $\varepsilon = (a - c)/c = 1 - c/a$ (positive). The sign depends on which axis the strain is measured along; always specify.
> 4. **Ignoring the bias spring** in a Part B design question — full marks require the explicit statement that **bias stress is essential for reversible cyclic operation**.
> 5. **Not stating the Curie temperature constraint** — every MSMA design question implicitly demands $T_{op} < T_c$; missing it loses **½ to 1 mark**.
> 6. **Lamination thickness calculation errors** — be careful with the factor of 2 in $t < 2\delta$; some texts write $t < \delta$, which is *over-strict* for the penetration depth criterion.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Magnetic Shape Memory Alloys (PERUT864, Module 3)**

- **Definition:** MSMAs are ferromagnetic martensitic materials that produce large strain (up to **10 %**) via **field-induced twin variant reorientation**, not via phase change.
- **Canonical Material:** **Ni-Mn-Ga** (Heusler alloy, L2₁ austenite → 5M/7M/NM tetragonal martensite).
- **Three essential pre-conditions:** Ferromagnetism + martensitic state + high magnetocrystalline anisotropy ($K_u$).
- **Master Strain Equation:** $\varepsilon_{MFIS} = 1 - c/a$ — depends only on lattice tetragonality, not on field strength.
- **Critical Field:** $H_{crit} = \sigma_{tw} \cdot \varepsilon_{MFIS} / (\mu_0 M_s)$ — for Ni-Mn-Ga typically **0.1 – 0.5 T**.
- **Bias stress is mandatory** for reversible, cyclic, closed-loop actuation.
- **Response time:** **ms range**, intermediate between piezo (µs) and conventional SMA (s).
- **Lamination required** to suppress eddy currents at kHz frequencies; typical layer thickness $t < 200$ µm.
- **Energy density:** ~ $10^5$ – $10^6$ J/m³, suitable for sub-mm-to-mm strokes in microrobots.
- **Curie Temperature $T_c$** must be above operating temperature: $T_{op} < T_c$ (~ 370 K for Ni-Mn-Ga).
- **Key applications in microrobotics:** Untethered capsule endoscopes, micropumps, microgrippers, biomimetic fins, micro-swimmers, magnetic energy harvesters (using inverse MSMA).
- **Dual-role use:** MSMA = **actuator** (field → strain) AND **sensor** (strain → $B$-field change) in a single element.
- **Villari Effect link:** MSMA is essentially a "super-Villari" effect — mechanical input changes the local $B$, enabling self-sensing.
- **Compared to Terfenol-D:** MSMA gives **30–100× higher strain** but at slightly lower bandwidth; preferred when stroke is more critical than speed.
- **Compared to Nitinol (conventional SMA):** MSMA gives **comparable strain** but is **wireless, faster, and cooler** — no thermal management required.
- **Compared to Piezo (PZT):** MSMA gives **30–50× higher strain** but is slower; preferred for large-stroke, low-frequency bio-MEMS.
- **Units to remember:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m ; $M_s$ in A/m ; $\sigma$ in Pa ; $\varepsilon$ dimensionless ; $H$ in A/m (or T with $B = \mu_0 H$).
- **Master "Why" sentence for any exam answer:** *MSMAs work because the magnetic Zeeman energy can reorient martensite twin variants in a ferromagnetic crystal, converting the lattice tetragonality $(1 - c/a)$ into a macroscopic shape change — an effect that is far larger than classical magnetostriction because it exploits the *geometrical* memory of the martensitic microstructure rather than the small electronic spin–orbit coupling of domains.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors Terminology
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sensors Terminology

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Sensors Terminology

In the context of **Microrobotics (PERUT864)**, **Sensors Terminology** refers to the standardized set of qualitative descriptors, quantitative metrics, performance indices, and dynamic response parameters that engineers use to unambiguously specify, classify, compare, and calibrate the transducers employed at the micro- and nano-scale. These terms act as a *universal engineering vocabulary* that bridges the physics of the transduction mechanism with the control engineer who must integrate that signal into a feedback loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The 2024 scheme explicitly mandates that students master the difference between **static characteristics** (accuracy, precision, resolution, sensitivity, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, drift, span, range, dead band, threshold) and **dynamic characteristics** (response time, time constant, bandwidth, rise time, settling time, fidelity, dynamic error). Both lists appear verbatim in typical Part-A (3-mark) questions.

A **sensor** is defined (per ISO/IEC 30112 and ISA-37.1 standards adopted by KTU) as:

> *"A device which provides a usable output in response to a specified physical, chemical, or biological measurand, where the output is an electrical or optical signal functionally related to the measurand."*

In microrobotics, this measurand is typically force (nN–µN), displacement (nm–µm), acceleration, magnetic field, chemical concentration, temperature, or vision — all sensed at the **microscale**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stethoscope of a Microrobot"

Imagine a doctor using a **stethoscope**. To another doctor, she must report not just *"I heard a heartbeat"* — she must say *how loud* (sensitivity), *how consistently* (repeatability), *how slowly it follows a quick beat* (dynamic response), *what is the smallest murmur it can catch* (resolution), and *whether the reading is the true value or offset by 2 dB* (accuracy / calibration drift).

A **microrobot's sensor suite** is its stethoscope. **Sensors terminology** is the standardized medical jargon (systolic, diastolic, BPM, S1/S2 sounds) that allows one microrobotics engineer in Trivandrum and another in Tokyo to compare their heart-rate monitors unambiguously. Without this shared language, you cannot evaluate whether a MEMS accelerometer is *better* than a PZT-based one — you only know they both measure acceleration.

## 1.3 Why Sensors Terminology Matters at the Microscale

At the **micro- and nano-scale**, the laws of physics conspire against clean measurements:

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Microscale Effects Driving Terminology**
> 1. **Dominance of surface forces** (van der Waals, capillary, electrostatic) over volume forces → requires **high-sensitivity** sensors with **sub-µN resolution**.
> 2. **Thermal (Brownian) noise floor** sets the absolute limit on **detection limit** and **SNR**.
> 3. **Fabrication tolerances** (±0.1 µm) directly degrade **accuracy** and **repeatability**.
> 4. **High bandwidth tracking** is needed because micro-actuators oscillate at kHz–MHz → emphasizes **dynamic response**.
> 5. **Power and footprint budgets** (often <1 mW, <1 mm³) → trade-offs with **sensitivity**.

Therefore, mastering sensors terminology is *not optional vocabulary* — it is the **language of design trade-offs** in microrobotics.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensor Characteristics

Sensors are evaluated along two orthogonal axes:

### A. Static Characteristics (Steady-State Performance)

These describe behaviour when the measurand is **constant or very slowly varying** (DC regime).

1. **Range** — The set of values of the measurand for which the sensor is intended to operate.
2. **Span (Full-Scale Input, FSI)** — The algebraic difference between the upper and lower limits of the range.
3. **Accuracy** — The closeness of the indicated value to the *true* value. Expressed as a percentage of Full-Scale Output (%FSO).
4. **Precision** — The ability of the sensor to reproduce the *same* output under identical conditions (random error).
5. **Resolution** — The smallest measurable increment of the measurand the sensor can reliably detect.
6. **Threshold** — The minimum input magnitude required to produce a detectable output change.
7. **Sensitivity (K)** — The ratio of output change to input change.
8. **Linearity** — The constancy of the slope (sensitivity) across the range.
9. **Hysteresis** — The maximum difference in output for the same input, when approached from increasing vs. decreasing directions.
10. **Repeatability** — Closeness of output readings for repeated identical inputs *under the same conditions, same day*.
11. **Reproducibility** — Same as repeatability but *different conditions, different days, different operators*.
12. **Drift** — Slow change of output with time at constant input.
13. **Dead Band / Dead Zone** — The range of input for which no output change is observed.
14. **Selectivity / Specificity** — Ability to respond only to the target measurand while ignoring interferents.
15. **Stability** — Ability to retain performance over long periods (inverse of drift).

### B. Dynamic Characteristics (Time-Domain & Frequency-Domain Response)

These describe behaviour when the measurand varies **rapidly** (AC regime).

1. **Time Constant (τ)** — For a first-order system, time to reach 63.2% of the final value for a step input.
2. **Rise Time (t_r)** — Time to go from 10% to 90% of the final value.
3. **Settling Time (t_s)** — Time to reach and stay within ±2% (or ±5%) of the final value.
4. **Bandwidth (BW)** — Frequency range over which the output amplitude is within −3 dB of the DC value.
5. **Resonant / Natural Frequency (ω_n)** — Frequency at which the sensor has maximum response.
6. **Damping Ratio (ζ)** — Governs overshoot and ringing in step response.
7. **Dynamic Error** — Difference between true instantaneous value and sensor's indicated value under time-varying input.
8. **Phase Lag / Phase Shift** — Time delay expressed as angular displacement in sinusoidal excitation.
9. **Fidelity** — Ability to faithfully reproduce the input waveform shape.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 scheme Part-B (14-mark) questions on sensors terminology almost always test *numerical* evaluation of sensitivity, linearity error, hysteresis error, SNR, time constant, and damping ratio. Memorize this table.

| # | Term | Mathematical Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|------|------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Sensitivity (K) | $K = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Output units per input unit (e.g., mV/µN) |
| 2 | Span | $\text{Span} = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Same as input |
| 3 | Full-Scale Output (FSO) | $FSO = y_{\max} - y_{\min}$ | Output units |
| 4 | Linearity Error (Non-linearity) | $\varepsilon_{L} = \dfrac{y_{\text{actual}} - y_{\text{straight line}}}{FSO} \times 100\%$ | %FSO |
| 5 | Hysteresis Error | $\varepsilon_{H} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{H}}{FSO} \times 100\%$ | %FSO |
| 6 | Accuracy (%FSO) | $\varepsilon_{A} = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{FSO} \times 100\%$ | %FSO |
| 7 | Repeatability Error | $\varepsilon_{R} = \dfrac{\pm \, \sigma}{FSO} \times 100\%$ | %FSO |
| 8 | SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) | $SNR = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ or $20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{rms,signal}}}{V_{\text{rms,noise}}}\right)$ | Decibels (dB) |
| 9 | Noise Figure (NF) | $NF = SNR_{\text{in,dB}} - SNR_{\text{out,dB}}$ | dB |
| 10 | Detection Limit (3σ) | $DL = 3 \, \sigma_{\text{noise}} \big/ K$ | Input units |
| 11 | Time Constant (1st order) | $y(t) = y_{\infty}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Seconds |
| 12 | Cut-off Frequency (BW) | $f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$ | Hz |
| 13 | Natural Frequency (2nd order) | $\omega_{n} = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | rad/s |
| 14 | Damping Ratio (2nd order) | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Settling Time (±2%) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_{n}}$ | Seconds |
| 16 | Rise Time (10–90%) | $t_{r} \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_{n}}$ (for ζ≈1) | Seconds |
| 17 | Percent Overshoot | $\%OS = 100 \cdot e^{-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}$ | % |
| 18 | Dynamic Error | $\varepsilon_{d}(t) = y_{\text{true}}(t) - y_{\text{sensor}}(t)$ | Output units |
| 19 | Drift Rate | $\text{Drift} = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta t}$ at constant $x$ | Output/time |
| 20 | Dead Band | $DB = \dfrac{x_{\text{dead}}}{Span} \times 100\%$ | %Span |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In **microrobot force-feedback loops** (e.g., a microgripper holding a 50 µm cell), the controller needs:

- **High sensitivity** to detect sub-µN gripping forces without crushing the cell.
- **Sub-millisecond response time** to avoid slip-damage during transit.
- **Low hysteresis** so repeated grip cycles remain calibrated.
- **Low drift** because microrobots often operate in temperature-unstable lab environments.

Every parameter in the cheat sheet above directly maps to a design constraint in such real systems.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Sensitivity from Calibration Data

**Problem Setup:** A MEMS capacitive force sensor records the following calibration readings:

| Applied Force $F$ (µN) | 0 | 10 | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output Voltage $V$ (mV) | 2.0 | 12.1 | 22.0 | 32.4 | 42.1 | 52.5 |

**Step 1 — Identify span of input and output.**

$$
F_{\min} = 0\ \mu N, \quad F_{\max} = 50\ \mu N \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Span} = 50\ \mu N
$$

$$
V_{\min} = 2.0\ mV, \quad V_{\max} = 52.5\ mV \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; FSO = 50.5\ mV
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ideal (least-squares) linear fit.**

For $n = 6$ points, the slope $K$ and intercept $b$ of the best-fit line $V = KF + b$ are:

$$
K = \dfrac{n\sum FV - \sum F \sum V}{n \sum F^{2} - (\sum F)^{2}}
$$

Compute sums:

$$
\sum F = 0+10+20+30+40+50 = 150
$$

$$
\sum V = 2.0+12.1+22.0+32.4+42.1+52.5 = 163.1
$$

$$
\sum F^{2} = 0+100+400+900+1600+2500 = 5500
$$

$$
\sum FV = (0)(2.0)+(10)(12.1)+(20)(22.0)+(30)(32.4)+(40)(42.1)+(50)(52.5)
$$

$$
\sum FV = 0 + 121 + 440 + 972 + 1684 + 2625 = 5842
$$

Substitute:

$$
K = \dfrac{6(5842) - (150)(163.1)}{6(5500) - (150)^{2}} = \dfrac{35052 - 24465}{33000 - 22500} = \dfrac{10587}{10500} \approx 1.0083\ \dfrac{mV}{\mu N}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intercept.**

$$
b = \dfrac{\sum V - K \sum F}{n} = \dfrac{163.1 - (1.0083)(150)}{6} = \dfrac{163.1 - 151.25}{6} = 1.975\ mV
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result.**

$$
\boxed{\,K \approx 1.008\ mV/\mu N,\quad b \approx 1.98\ mV\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearity (Non-linearity) Error

**Step 1 — Best-fit line equation** (from §3.1):

$$
V_{\text{fit}}(F) = 1.0083\, F + 1.975
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate best-fit line at each calibration point and find deviation.**

| $F$ (µN) | $V_{\text{actual}}$ | $V_{\text{fit}}$ | $\Delta V = V_{\text{actual}} - V_{\text{fit}}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2.0 | 1.975 | +0.025 |
| 10 | 12.1 | 12.058 | +0.042 |
| 20 | 22.0 | 22.141 | −0.141 |
| 30 | 32.4 | 32.224 | +0.176 |
| 40 | 42.1 | 42.307 | −0.207 |
| 50 | 52.5 | 52.390 | +0.110 |

**Step 3 — Maximum deviation.**

$$
\Delta V_{\max} = \vert -0.207 \vert = 0.207\ mV
$$

**Step 4 — Linearity error as %FSO.**

$$
\varepsilon_{L} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\max}}{FSO} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.207}{50.5} \times 100\% \approx 0.41\%\,FSO
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hysteresis Error

**Step 1 — Hysteresis is measured by sweeping input UP, then DOWN, at the same points.**

Suppose a micro-pressure sensor gives:

| Pressure (kPa) | Output Going UP (mV) | Output Going DOWN (mV) |
|---|---|---|
| 10 | 25.1 | 24.7 |
| 30 | 75.4 | 74.9 |
| 50 | 125.8 | 125.2 |

**Step 2 — Compute difference at each common point.**

$$
\Delta V_{H}(10) = \vert 25.1 - 24.7 \vert = 0.4\ mV
$$

$$
\Delta V_{H}(30) = \vert 75.4 - 74.9 \vert = 0.5\ mV
$$

$$
\Delta V_{H}(50) = \vert 125.8 - 125.2 \vert = 0.6\ mV
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum hysteresis excursion.**

$$
\Delta V_{H,\max} = 0.6\ mV
$$

**Step 4 — Hysteresis error as %FSO.** Assume FSO = 150 mV.

$$
\varepsilon_{H} = \dfrac{0.6}{150} \times 100\% = 0.40\%\,FSO
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Dynamic Response: Step Input on a 1st-Order Sensor

A MEMS accelerometer is modelled as a first-order system with time constant $\tau = 0.5\ ms$ when subjected to a constant acceleration step of $a = 5g$.

**Step 1 — Write the governing differential equation.**

$$
\tau\,\dot{y}(t) + y(t) = K\,x(t)
$$

For a step input $x(t) = a\,u(t)$ with $K = 1\ V/g$:

$$
y(t) = K a \!\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right) = 5 \!\left(1 - e^{-t/0.5\text{ms}}\right)\ V
$$

**Step 2 — Compute output at key time instants.**

At $t = \tau = 0.5\ ms$:

$$
y(\tau) = 5(1 - e^{-1}) = 5(0.6321) = 3.16\ V
$$

At $t = 2\tau = 1.0\ ms$:

$$
y(2\tau) = 5(1 - e^{-2}) = 5(0.8647) = 4.32\ V
$$

At $t = 4\tau = 2.0\ ms$ (≈ settling):

$$
y(4\tau) = 5(1 - e^{-4}) = 5(0.9817) = 4.91\ V
$$

**Step 3 — Cut-off (bandwidth) frequency.**

$$
f_{c} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi (0.5 \times 10^{-3})} \approx 318\ Hz
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.** Above ~318 Hz the output amplitude will be attenuated by more than 3 dB.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — SNR and Detection Limit

**Step 1 — Given:** Sensor sensitivity $K = 1.008\ mV/\mu N$, RMS noise $V_{n,\text{rms}} = 0.05\ mV$, applied force $F = 20\ \mu N$.

**Step 2 — Signal voltage.**

$$
V_{s} = K \cdot F = (1.008)(20) = 20.16\ mV
$$

**Step 3 — SNR in dB.**

$$
SNR = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{s}}{V_{n}}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{20.16}{0.05}\right) = 20\log_{10}(403.2) \approx 52.1\ dB
$$

**Step 4 — Detection limit (3σ criterion).**

$$
DL = \dfrac{3\,V_{n,\text{rms}}}{K} = \dfrac{3 \times 0.05}{1.008} \approx 0.149\ \mu N
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing Parameters by Application

| Microrobot Application | Critical Parameter | Typical Target Value | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Microgripper force feedback | Sensitivity + Resolution | K > 100 mV/µN, Res < 0.1 µN | Prevent cell damage |
| AFM-like imaging | Bandwidth + Linearity | BW > 10 kHz, ε_L < 0.5% | Track tip oscillation |
| Magnetic microrobot tracking | Selectivity + Drift | Selectivity > 80 dB, Drift < 1%/hr | Reject Earth's field |
| Chemical microrobot (pH) | Detection Limit | DL < 10 nM | Trace analyte sensing |
| Vision-based localization | Resolution (pixel) + FPS | < 5 µm pixel, > 60 FPS | Real-time control |
| Tactile skin | Hysteresis + Repeatability | ε_H < 1%, ε_R < 0.5% | Robust multi-touch |
