# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define the following terms related to sensors:**
**(a) Sensitivity**    **(b) Hysteresis**    **(c) Resolution**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Sensitivity (K):** It is defined as the ratio of the change in output (Δy) to the corresponding change in input (Δx) of the sensor. Mathematically, $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$. For a microrobot force sensor, sensitivity is typically expressed in mV/µN.
>
> **(b) Hysteresis:** It is the maximum difference in sensor output for the same value of input when that input is approached first from one direction (increasing) and then from the other (decreasing). It arises from internal friction, plastic deformation, or magnetic hysteresis in the transducer material and is reported as %FSO.
>
> **(c) Resolution:** It is the smallest measurable increment in the input that produces a detectable change in the output. For a digital sensor, resolution is one least-significant bit (LSB); for analog microsensors, it is limited by the noise floor and is given by $DL = 3\sigma_{\text{noise}}/K$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between (i) Accuracy and Precision, and (ii) Repeatability and Reproducibility.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Accuracy vs Precision:**
> - **Accuracy** is the *closeness of a measured value to the true (reference) value*. It quantifies *systematic error* (bias).
> - **Precision** is the *closeness of two or more measurements to each other* under identical conditions. It quantifies *random error* (scatter).
> - A sensor can be precise but inaccurate (well-clustered but offset from true value), or accurate on average but imprecise (scattered around true value).
>
> **(ii) Repeatability vs Reproducibility:**
> - **Repeatability** is the variation in output when the *same* input is applied repeatedly *under the same conditions, by the same operator, on the same day, using the same instrument* (per ISO 5725-1).
> - **Reproducibility** is the variation when the same input is applied *under changed conditions* — different operators, different days, different laboratories, or after recalibration.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** A MEMS piezoresistive pressure sensor is calibrated against a standard reference. The calibration data are:

| Pressure (kPa) | 0 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 | 300 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output (mV) | 0.5 | 30.2 | 60.8 | 90.1 | 121.5 | 150.8 | 181.2 |

Compute the **sensitivity**, the **span of input**, the **FSO**, and the **maximum linearity error in %FSO**.

**(b)** Discuss the **dynamic characteristics** of a sensor with reference to **time constant, rise time, settling time, bandwidth, and damping ratio**. Derive the relationship between **time constant τ and bandwidth f_c** for a first-order system.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Span of input.**

$$
\text{Span} = P_{\max} - P_{\min} = 300 - 0 = 300\ kPa
$$

**Step 2 — FSO.**

$$
FSO = V_{\max} - V_{\min} = 181.2 - 0.5 = 180.7\ mV
$$

**Step 3 — Sensitivity (slope of best-fit line).** Compute sums:

$$
n = 7,\ \sum P = 1050,\ \sum V = 635.1,\ \sum P^{2} = 227500
$$

$$
\sum PV = (0)(0.5)+(50)(30.2)+(100)(60.8)+(150)(90.1)+(200)(121.5)+(250)(150.8)+(300)(181.2)
$$

$$
\sum PV = 0 + 1510 + 6080 + 13515 + 24300 + 37700 + 54360 = 137465
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{n\sum PV - \sum P \sum V}{n\sum P^{2} - (\sum P)^{2}} = \dfrac{7(137465) - (1050)(635.1)}{7(227500) - (1050)^{2}}
$$

$$
K = \dfrac{962255 - 666855}{1592500 - 1102500} = \dfrac{295400}{490000} \approx 0.6029\ \dfrac{mV}{kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Intercept.**

$$
b = \dfrac{\sum V - K\sum P}{n} = \dfrac{635.1 - (0.6029)(1050)}{7} = \dfrac{635.1 - 633.045}{7} = 0.2936\ mV
$$

**Step 5 — Best-fit deviations.**

| $P$ (kPa) | $V_{\text{actual}}$ | $V_{\text{fit}} = 0.6029P + 0.2936$ | $\Delta V$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.5 | 0.294 | +0.206 |
| 50 | 30.2 | 30.439 | −0.239 |
| 100 | 60.8 | 60.584 | +0.216 |
| 150 | 90.1 | 90.729 | −0.629 |
| 200 | 121.5 | 120.874 | +0.626 |
| 250 | 150.8 | 151.019 | −0.219 |
| 300 | 181.2 | 181.164 | +0.036 |

**Step 6 — Maximum deviation.**

$$
\Delta V_{\max} = \vert -0.629 \vert = 0.629\ mV
$$

**Step 7 — Linearity error.**

$$
\varepsilon_{L} = \dfrac{0.629}{180.7} \times 100\% \approx 0.348\%\,FSO
$$

**Final Answer (a):**

$$
\boxed{K \approx 0.603\ mV/kPa,\ \ \text{Span} = 300\ kPa,\ \ FSO = 180.7\ mV,\ \ \varepsilon_{L} \approx 0.35\%\ FSO}
$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**1. Time Constant (τ):** For a first-order sensor obeying $\tau \dot{y} + y = Kx$, the step response is $y(t) = Ka(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$. The **time constant** is the time required for the output to reach $1 - e^{-1} = 63.2\%$ of its final steady-state value.

**2. Rise Time (t_r):** The time interval for the step response to go from 10% to 90% of the final value. For an overdamped first-order system:

$$
t_{r} \approx 2.2\,\tau
$$

**3. Settling Time (t_s):** Time for the response to enter and remain within a tolerance band (commonly ±2% or ±5%) of the final value. For a first-order system:

$$
t_{s} \approx 4\tau \quad (\text{for } \pm 2\% \text{ band})
$$

**4. Bandwidth (BW, f_c):** The frequency at which the output amplitude drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (−3 dB) of the DC value for a sinusoidal input. For a first-order system, the transfer function magnitude is:

$$
\left\vert \dfrac{Y(j\omega)}{X(j\omega)} \right\vert = \dfrac{K}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega \tau)^{2}}}
$$

Setting $|Y/X| = K/\sqrt{2}$:

$$
\sqrt{1 + (\omega_{c}\tau)^{2}} = \sqrt{2} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_{c}\tau = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{c} = \dfrac{\omega_{c}}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}}
$$

**5. Damping Ratio (ζ):** For a second-order sensor, $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$. It governs overshoot (only when $0 < \zeta < 1$):

$$
\%OS = 100\, e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^{2}}}
$$

It also controls settling time:

$$
t_{s} \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_{n}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Dynamic Characteristics**
> 1. **Confusing bandwidth with rise time:** Many students write "$f_c = 0.35/t_r$" for first-order systems but forget the assumption. Verify whether the system is first- or second-order *before* applying any of these rules.
> 2. **Mixing static and dynamic errors:** A sensor may have excellent static accuracy (ε_A = 0.1% FSO) but still exhibit 50% dynamic error at 1 kHz. Both must be specified separately.
> 3. **Forgetting units of τ:** τ must be in **seconds** before substituting into $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$. A common error is to leave τ in ms and get BW in the wrong order of magnitude.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **static characteristics** of a sensor in detail: **range, span, accuracy, precision, sensitivity, resolution, linearity, hysteresis, repeatability, drift, and dead band**. Provide one-line definitions and the mathematical expression (where applicable).

**(b)** A piezoelectric micro-force sensor has a sensitivity of $K = 2.5\ V/N$ and an RMS noise voltage of $V_n = 0.8\ mV$. It is excited in a 1 kHz bandwidth.
(i) Compute the **SNR in dB** for a measurand of $F = 0.5\ N$.
(ii) Compute the **detection limit (DL)** using the 3σ criterion.
(iii) Compute the **resolution** if the ADC has 12 bits over a 5 V range.
(iv) Identify which is the **binding constraint** (noise-limited DL or ADC-limited resolution).

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| # | Term | One-line Definition | Math Expression |
|---|------|---------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Range | Set of input values for which the sensor is rated to operate | $x_{\min} \leq x \leq x_{\max}$ |
| 2 | Span | Difference between max and min input | $S = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ |
| 3 | Accuracy | Closeness of indicated value to true value | $\varepsilon_A = \Delta y_{\max}/FSO \times 100\%$ |
| 4 | Precision | Reproducibility of reading under identical conditions | $\varepsilon_P = \sigma_y/FSO \times 100\%$ |
| 5 | Sensitivity | Ratio of output change to input change | $K = dy/dx$ |
| 6 | Resolution | Smallest detectable input increment | $R = \Delta x_{\min}$ |
| 7 | Linearity | Constancy of sensitivity over the range | $\varepsilon_L = \Delta y_{\max,\text{NL}}/FSO \times 100\%$ |
| 8 | Hysteresis | Output difference for same input from different directions | $\varepsilon_H = \Delta y_{H,\max}/FSO \times 100\%$ |
| 9 | Repeatability | Output agreement on repeated identical inputs (same conditions) | $\varepsilon_{Rp} = \pm\sigma/FSO \times 100\%$ |
| 10 | Drift | Slow change of output at constant input with time | $D = \Delta y/\Delta t$ |
| 11 | Dead Band | Input range producing no output change | $DB = x_{\text{dead}}/\text{Span} \times 100\%$ |

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) SNR in dB:**

$$
V_s = K \cdot F = (2.5\ V/N)(0.5\ N) = 1.25\ V = 1250\ mV
$$

$$
SNR = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1250}{0.8}\right) = 20\log_{10}(1562.5) \approx 63.9\ dB
$$

**(ii) Detection limit (3σ):**

$$
DL = \dfrac{3\,V_n}{K} = \dfrac{3(0.8 \times 10^{-3})}{2.5} = \dfrac{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5} = 9.6 \times 10^{-4}\ N = 0.96\ mN
$$

**(iii) ADC resolution:**

12-bit ADC over 5 V → 1 LSB = $5/2^{12} = 5/4096 \approx 1.22\ mV$.

Corresponding input resolution:

$$
R_{\text{ADC}} = \dfrac{1.22\ mV}{2.5\ V/N} = 4.88 \times 10^{-4}\ N \approx 0.488\ mN
$$

**(iv) Binding constraint:** Compare noise-limited DL = 0.96 mN with ADC resolution = 0.488 mN. Since $R_{\text{ADC}} < DL$, the ADC is the **coarser** limit and hence the **binding constraint** on the smallest detectable input.

$$
\boxed{\text{Binding constraint: ADC resolution (0.488 mN)}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — SNR & Detection Limit**
> 1. **Using $10\log_{10}$ instead of $20\log_{10}$:** Both forms are valid — $10\log_{10}(P_1/P_2)$ for power ratios, $20\log_{10}(V_1/V_2)$ for voltage/current ratios. The question stem usually specifies. KTU expects voltage form for sensor SNR.
> 2. **Mixing 1σ and 3σ criteria:** Detection limit is conventionally 3σ (99.7% confidence). Some texts use 2σ. State the criterion you assume.
> 3. **Forgetting the ADC LSB step:** Many students compute the SNR correctly but then quote ADC resolution in *bits*, not *Newtons*. Always convert to input units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 — Module 4)**

- ✅ **Static vs Dynamic:** Static = steady-state (DC); Dynamic = time-varying (AC).
- ✅ **11 static terms to memorize:** Range, Span, Accuracy, Precision, Sensitivity, Resolution, Linearity, Hysteresis, Repeatability, Drift, Dead Band.
- ✅ **9 dynamic terms to memorize:** Time constant, Rise time, Settling time, Bandwidth, Natural frequency, Damping ratio, Dynamic error, Phase lag, Fidelity.
- ✅ **Sensitivity** $K = \Delta y / \Delta x$ — slope of calibration curve.
- ✅ **All errors (linearity, hysteresis, accuracy)** are expressed as **%FSO** — never as %reading for KTU valuation.
- ✅ **FSO** = $y_{\max} - y_{\min}$, **Span** = $x_{\max} - x_{\min}$.
- ✅ **First-order bandwidth:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.
- ✅ **First-order rise time:** $t_r \approx 2.2\tau$.
- ✅ **First-order settling time (2%):** $t_s \approx 4\tau$.
- ✅ **Second-order overshoot:** $\%OS = 100 \exp\!\left(-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}\right)$.
- ✅ **SNR (voltage):** $20\log_{10}(V_s/V_n)$ dB.
- ✅ **Detection limit (3σ):** $DL = 3\sigma_n / K$.
- ✅ **Accuracy ≠ Precision:** Accuracy = bias; Precision = scatter.
- ✅ **Repeatability ≠ Reproducibility:** Repeatability = same conditions; Reproducibility = changed conditions.
- ✅ **Microscale caveat:** At sub-mm scale, *surface forces* dominate → sensitivity and resolution become the design-limiting parameters.
- ✅ **Examiner always tests numerical evaluation** — practice at least 5 calibration-based numerical problems before the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensing Technology for Displacements - Types of Sensors - Tracking Methods - Implementation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sensing Technology for Displacements - Types of Sensors - Tracking Methods - Implementation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Displacement Sensing** in the context of microrobotics refers to the precise measurement of linear or angular position changes of a micro-scale robotic platform, its end-effector, or its individual micro-components relative to a reference frame, with resolutions typically ranging from **sub-micrometer (10⁻⁶ m)** down to **nanometer (10⁻⁹ m)** and, in advanced nano-robotics, even **picometer (10⁻¹² m)** scales.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, displacement sensing technology for microrobotics encompasses three interlinked pillars:

1. **Sensing Technology for Displacements** — The physical transduction mechanism that converts a positional change into a measurable electrical, optical, or magnetic signal.
2. **Types of Sensors** — The taxonomy of available transducers (contact vs. non-contact, active vs. passive, on-board vs. external).
3. **Tracking Methods & Implementation** — The signal-processing, control, and feedback architectures used to localize the microrobot continuously over time (often called *pose estimation* or *localization*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The 2024 Scheme explicitly combines the *physical transducer* with the *algorithmic tracking loop*. Students must therefore know **both** the sensor physics and the closed-loop feedback strategy that turns raw sensor data into usable robot coordinates.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blindfolded Painter"

Imagine a blindfolded painter trying to draw a perfect line on a canvas.

- **Without sensing** — the painter has no idea where the brush is; the line wanders.
- **With crude sensing** (e.g., feeling the canvas edge with a fingertip) — feedback is coarse; the line wavers by millimeters.
- **With high-resolution sensing** (e.g., a camera above the canvas that reports the brush tip position 1000 times per second) — the line is precise.

A microrobot moving inside a capillary, in an electromagnetic coil, or across a MEMS stage faces exactly the same challenge. The "brush tip" is a 100 µm micro-actuator, and the "canvas" is the operating volume. Without displacement sensors, autonomous closed-loop control is impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric for Displacement Sensors in Microrobotics:**
> - **Resolution** — smallest detectable increment (often **< 1 µm**, ideally **< 100 nm**)
> - **Bandwidth** — speed of measurement, typically **> 1 kHz** for dynamic microrobots
> - **Working Range** — from **±10 µm** (nanopositioning) up to **±50 mm** (milliscale tracking)
> - **Non-invasiveness** — sensor must not disturb the micro-scale forces (**< µN**) being controlled.

### 1.3 Why Displacement Sensing is Unique in Microrobotics

Unlike macro-robotics, microrobotics faces two fundamental constraints:

| Constraint | Macro-Robot | Micro-Robot |
|---|---|---|
| On-board payload | Grams to kilograms | **Milligrams to micrograms** |
| Available sensing volume | Cubic centimeters | **Cubic millimeters or less** |
| Dominant physics | Inertia | **Surface forces (van der Waals, electrostatic)** |
| Required resolution | 0.1 mm | **0.1 µm or less** |

Because of these, **most displacement sensing for microrobots is *external* (off-board)**, using optical, magnetic, or electromagnetic fields to "look at" the robot from outside.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Displacement Sensors

Displacement sensors can be classified along multiple axes. The most KTU-relevant taxonomy is by **physical transduction principle**:

#### A. Resistive / Piezoresistive Sensors
- **Strain Gauges** — Resistance changes with mechanical strain. Used in MEMS force/displacement feedback.
- **Piezoresistors** — Semiconductor version with gauge factor **GF ≈ 100–200** (vs. ~2 for metal).

#### B. Capacitive Sensors
- Plates separated by a small gap; capacitance changes with displacement.
- **Differential capacitive sensors** use a movable central plate between two fixed outer plates — used in MEMS accelerometers and AFM nanopositioners.

#### C. Inductive Sensors
- **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** — gold standard for sub-micron linear measurement.
- **Eddy-Current Probes** — non-contact, immune to dust/oil.
- **Variable Reluctance Sensors** — simple, robust.

#### D. Piezoelectric Sensors
- Generate charge when deformed. Used as dynamic displacement/vibration sensors (e.g., Laser Doppler Vibrometer reference).

#### E. Optical Sensors
- **Laser Triangulation** — position from reflected laser spot angle.
- **Laser Interferometry (Michelson, heterodyne)** — displacement = N·λ/2.
- **Optical Encoders** — linear (glass scale) or rotary (disk).
- **Confocal Chromatic Sensors** — multi-wavelength distance measurement.
- **Position-Sensitive Detectors (PSD)** — lateral-effect photodiodes.

#### F. Vision-Based Sensors
- **CCD/CMOS cameras** + image processing for **2D/3D tracking**.
- **Stereo vision** — two cameras for 3D reconstruction.
- **Microscopy-coupled vision** — optical microscope + high-speed camera for in-vitro microbots.

#### G. Magnetic / Electromagnetic Sensors
- **Hall-Effect Sensors** — detect position of a permanent magnet on the microrobot.
- **Search Coils** — detect magnetic dipole moment of the microrobot in an AC field.
- **Magnetoresistive (AMR/GMR/TMR) Sensors** — ultra-sensitive field detection.

#### H. Ultrasonic Sensors
- **Time-of-Flight (ToF)** ultrasound — distance from echo delay.
- Resolution typically ~10 µm at MHz frequencies; useful for *in-pipeline* microrobots.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Key Sensors

#### 2.2.1 LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)

**Working Principle:**
- Primary coil excited by AC reference voltage $V_p$ (typically 1–10 kHz).
- Two identical secondary coils (S₁ and S₂) are wound in opposite phase.
- A soft ferromagnetic core slides along the coil assembly.
- When the core is centered → outputs cancel → V_out = 0.
- When core moves by $\Delta x$ → one secondary voltage dominates → V_out ≠ 0.

**Step-by-step signal flow:**
1. AC excitation $V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$ applied to primary.
2. Mutual inductances $M_1$ and $M_2$ vary linearly with core position $x$.
3. Secondary voltages: $V_{s1} = M_1 \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$ and $V_{s2} = M_2 \dfrac{dI_p}{dt}$.
4. Differential output: $V_{out} = V_{s1} - V_{s2}$.
5. For small displacements, $V_{out} \approx K_{LVDT} \cdot x$, where $K_{LVDT}$ is the sensitivity in V/m.

#### 2.2.2 Capacitive Displacement Sensor

A parallel-plate capacitor has:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $A$ is the overlap area, $d$ is the gap, $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity, and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the **vacuum permittivity**.

For small gap variation $\Delta d \ll d$, linearization gives:

$$\frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx -\frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$

This high sensitivity (and the absence of mechanical contact) makes capacitive sensing ideal for microrobot nanopositioning stages.

#### 2.2.3 Laser Interferometer (Heterodyne Michelson)

**Working Principle:**
- A reference beam and a measurement beam (reflected off the moving target) are recombined.
- The optical path difference produces an interference pattern.
- Each full fringe cycle corresponds to a displacement of **one wavelength λ** (round-trip gives $2\Delta x = N\lambda$).

**Displacement formula:**

$$\Delta x = \frac{N \cdot \lambda}{2}$$

For a heterodyne system, the Doppler-shifted frequency $f_D$ gives:

$$\Delta x = \int_0^t \frac{\lambda \cdot f_D(\tau)}{2} \, d\tau$$

This is the **gold standard** for displacement metrology with **sub-nanometer resolution**.

#### 2.2.4 Vision-Based Tracking Pipeline

Vision-based tracking converts pixel coordinates into world coordinates:

1. **Image Acquisition** — High-speed camera captures microrobot at frame rate $f_s$.
2. **Pre-processing** — Noise filtering, background subtraction.
3. **Feature Detection** — Edge/contour/centroid detection (e.g., Sobel, Canny, thresholding).
4. **Tracking Algorithm** — Kalman Filter, Particle Filter, or optical flow (Lucas–Kanade).
5. **Coordinate Mapping** — Using calibration (pinhole model) to convert pixels to micrometers.

The pinhole camera projection:

$$s \begin{bmatrix} u \\ v \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = K \begin{bmatrix} R \vert t \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_w \\ Y_w \\ Z_w \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $K$ is the intrinsic camera matrix, $R$ and $t$ are the extrinsic rotation/translation, and $(u, v)$ are pixel coordinates.

#### 2.2.5 Magnetic Tracking (Search-Coil Method)

For magnetically-actuated microrobots, the position can be computed from the magnetic field gradient detected by an array of external Hall sensors or coils.

The dipole field of a magnetic microrobot with moment $\vec{m}$ at the origin is:

$$\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \frac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^3} \left[ 3(\vec{m}\cdot\hat{r})\hat{r} - \vec{m} \right]$$

Solving the inverse problem (multiple $B$ measurements → position $r$) yields the 3D pose. **This is fundamentally an ill-posed inverse problem** solved iteratively or with look-up tables.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Sensor / Method | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Range/Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Parallel-plate capacitor | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | $A$ = area, $d$ = gap | Resolution ≈ **1 nm** |
| 2 | Linearized capacitive | $\dfrac{\Delta C}{C_0} \approx -\dfrac{\Delta d}{d_0}$ | small-signal regime | — |
| 3 | LVDT output | $V_{out} = K_{LVDT} \cdot x$ | sensitivity $K$ in V/m | Resolution ≈ **10 nm** |
| 4 | Strain gauge | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R_0} = GF \cdot \varepsilon$ | $GF$ = gauge factor | $GF_{metal} \approx 2$, $GF_{semi} \approx 100$ |
| 5 | Michelson interferometer | $\Delta x = \dfrac{N \cdot \lambda}{2}$ | $N$ = fringe count, $\lambda$ ≈ 633 nm | Resolution ≈ **0.1 nm** |
| 6 | Heterodyne interferometer | $\Delta x = \int_0^t \dfrac{\lambda f_D}{2} d\tau$ | $f_D$ = Doppler freq | Sub-nm dynamic |
| 7 | Magnetic dipole field | $\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = \dfrac{\mu_0}{4\pi r^3}[3(\vec{m}\cdot\hat{r})\hat{r}-\vec{m}]$ | $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A | — |
| 8 | Pinhole projection | $s[u, v, 1]^T = K[R\vert t][X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^T$ | $K$ = 3×3 intrinsic matrix | Sub-pixel accuracy ≈ **0.1 px** |
| 9 | Optical encoder | $\theta = \dfrac{2\pi N_p}{N}$ | $N_p$ = pulse count, $N$ = lines | Resolution ≈ **0.01°** |
| 10 | Piezoelectric charge | $Q = d_{ij} \cdot F$ | $d_{ij}$ = charge coefficient | Dynamic displacement sensing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always remember:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m and $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m — these are **board-exam constants** and frequently appear in derivations.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Biomedical microrobotics** — vision + magnetic tracking for *in-vivo* capsule endoscopes and drug-delivery nanobots.
- **MEMS/NEMS testing** — laser interferometry for measuring MEMS actuator deflection.
- **Micro-assembly** — capacitive + LVDT sensors for high-precision pick-and-place.
- **Scanning Probe Microscopy (AFM/STM)** — piezoelectric + capacitive sensing for Å-level positioning.
- **Lab-on-chip automation** — optical encoders + vision tracking of micro-manipulators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Displacement Formula for Heterodyne Laser Interferometer

The Michelson interferometer is the foundational tool for sub-nanometer displacement measurement. We now derive its working equation in full.

**Setup:** A laser beam of wavelength $\lambda$ is split into two arms:
- **Reference arm** — fixed mirror at distance $L_r$.
- **Measurement arm** — mirror attached to the moving target at distance $L_m(t)$.

The two beams recombine at the photodetector.

**Step 1: Optical path lengths**

The optical path length (OPL) of each arm is:

$$\text{OPL}_r = 2 L_r$$

$$\text{OPL}_m(t) = 2 L_m(t)$$

**Reason:** Each beam traverses its arm twice (forward and back), hence the factor of 2.

**Step 2: Optical path difference**

$$\Delta \text{OPL}(t) = 2[L_m(t) - L_r]$$

If the measurement mirror moves by $\Delta x(t) = L_m(t) - L_m(0)$, then:

$$\Delta \text{OPL}(t) = \Delta \text{OPL}(0) + 2\Delta x(t)$$

**Step 3: Phase difference**

The phase of the recombined signal is:

$$\phi(t) = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta \text{OPL}(t)$$

**Step 4: Substituting motion**

$$\phi(t) = \phi(0) + \frac{4\pi}{\lambda}\Delta x(t)$$

**Step 5: Heterodyne detection**

A heterodyne interferometer adds an acoustic-optic frequency shift $f_{AOM}$ to the reference beam. The photodetector output contains a beat frequency:

$$f_{beat} = f_{AOM} + \Delta f_D$$

where the Doppler shift from the moving mirror is:

$$\Delta f_D = \frac{2}{\lambda}\frac{d(\Delta x)}{dt}$$

**Reason:** The Doppler frequency is the time rate of change of phase divided by $2\pi$:

$$\Delta f_D = \frac{1}{2\pi}\frac{d\phi}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\cdot\frac{4\pi}{\lambda}\frac{d(\Delta x)}{dt} = \frac{2}{\lambda}\frac{d(\Delta x)}{dt}$$

**Step 6: Final displacement formula**

Integrating both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$\Delta x(t) = \int_0^t \frac{\lambda}{2}\Delta f_D(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Equivalently, by counting fringes $N(t)$:

$$\boxed{\Delta x(t) = \frac{N(t)\cdot\lambda}{2}}$$

This is the celebrated **half-wavelength resolution** — for $\lambda = 633$ nm (He–Ne laser):

$$\Delta x_{\min} = \frac{\lambda}{2} = 316.5 \text{ nm per fringe}$$

Modern interferometers use **sub-fringe interpolation** to push resolution to **< 0.1 nm**.

### 3.2 Derivation II — Capacitive Sensor Sensitivity

**Problem:** A parallel-plate capacitive sensor has plate area $A = 100$ mm², gap $d = 0.5$ mm, and operates in air ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1$).

**Step 1: Initial capacitance**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854\times 10^{-12})(1)(100\times 10^{-6})}{0.5\times 10^{-3}}$$

Compute numerator:

$$\varepsilon_0 A = 8.854\times 10^{-12}\times 10^{-4} = 8.854\times 10^{-16} \text{ F·m}$$

Divide by $d = 5\times 10^{-4}$ m:

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854\times 10^{-16}}{5\times 10^{-4}} = 1.7708\times 10^{-12} \text{ F} \approx 1.77 \text{ pF}$$

**Step 2: Sensitivity $S$**

Differentiating $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$ with respect to $d$:

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = -\frac{C_0}{d}$$

Magnitude of sensitivity:

$$S = \left\vert \frac{dC}{dd}\right\vert = \frac{C_0}{d} = \frac{1.77\times 10^{-12}}{5\times 10^{-4}} = 3.54\times 10^{-9} \text{ F/m}$$

**Step 3: Change in capacitance for $\Delta d = 10\ \mu\text{m}$**

Using the linearized formula:

$$\Delta C \approx S \cdot \Delta d = 3.54\times 10^{-9}\times 10\times 10^{-6} = 3.54\times 10^{-14} \text{ F} = 35.4 \text{ fF}$$

Alternatively, exact computation:

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d + \Delta d} = \frac{8.854\times 10^{-16}}{5.1\times 10^{-4}} = 1.7361\times 10^{-12} \text{ F}$$

$$\Delta C = C_{\text{new}} - C_0 = (1.7361 - 1.7708)\times 10^{-12} = -3.47\times 10^{-14} \text{ F}$$

The two results agree to within the linearization error (≈ 2 %).

### 3.3 Derivation III — Vision-Based Triangulation for 3D Position

A **laser triangulation sensor** projects a laser spot onto the target; a CMOS camera observes the spot from a baseline $B$ and tilt angle $\theta$.

**Step 1: Geometry**

Let the target surface be at distance $Z$ from the laser, and the camera observe the spot at image-plane position $x_i$.

**Step 2: Triangulation equation**

$$\tan(\theta + \alpha) = \frac{Z}{B}$$

where $\alpha = \arctan(x_i/f)$ and $f$ is the focal length.

Solving for $Z$:

$$Z = B \cdot \tan(\theta + \alpha)$$

**Step 3: Sensitivity**

Differentiating with respect to $\alpha$:

$$\frac{dZ}{d\alpha} = \frac{B}{\cos^2(\theta + \alpha)}$$

For small $\alpha$, $\cos^2(\theta + \alpha) \approx \cos^2\theta$, so:

$$\left\vert \frac{dZ}{d\alpha}\right\vert \approx \frac{B}{\cos^2\theta}$$

Choosing $\theta = 30°$ and $B = 50$ mm:

$$\frac{dZ}{d\alpha} = \frac{50}{\cos^2 30°} = \frac{50}{0.75} = 66.67 \text{ mm/rad}$$

This sensitivity (in mm per radian of image-plane angle) directly determines the achievable resolution.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Sensor | Resolution | Bandwidth | Range | Contact? | Suitability for Microrobot |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LVDT** | 10 nm | 1 kHz | ±50 mm | No | High-precision stage feedback |
| **Capacitive** | 1 nm | 10 kHz | ±1 mm | No | AFM nanopositioning |
| **Laser interferometer** | 0.1 nm | 1 MHz | Unlimited | No | Calibration standard |
| **Strain gauge** | 1 µm | 10 kHz | ±1 mm | Yes | MEMS flexure sensing |
| **Hall sensor** | 10 µm | 100 kHz | ±10 mm | No | Magnetic microrobot tracking |
| **Optical encoder** | 0.1 µm | 1 MHz | Full travel | No | Linear stage |
| **Vision (camera)** | 1 µm | 1 kHz | Full FoV | No | In-vitro localization |
| **Eddy current** | 0.1 µm | 50 kHz | ±5 mm | No | Harsh-environment stages |
| **Piezoelectric** | 1 pm (dynamic) | > 1 MHz | ±1 µm | Either | Vibration sensing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Taxonomy of Displacement Sensors for Microrobotics

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Displacement Sensors] --> B[Contact Type]
    A --> C[Non-Contact Type]
    B --> B1[Strain Gauge]
    B --> B2[Piezoresistive]
    B --> B3[Piezoelectric Dynamic]
    C --> D[Capacitive]
    C --> E[Inductive]
    C --> F[Optical]
    C --> G[Magnetic]
    C --> H[Ultrasonic]
    E --> E1[LVDT]
    E --> E2[Eddy Current Probe]
    F --> F1[Laser Triangulation]
    F --> F2[Michelson Interferometer]
    F --> F3[Optical Encoder]
    F --> F4[Confocal Chromatic]
    F --> F5[Position Sensitive Detector]
    G --> G1[Hall Effect Sensor]
    G --> G2[Search Coil Array]
    G --> G3[Magnetoresistive AMR GMR TMR]
    H --> H1[Time of Flight Ultrasound]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Vision-Based Microrobot Tracking Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Microrobot in Operating Volume] --> B[High Speed Camera]
    B --> C[Frame Grabber]
    C --> D[Image Preprocessing]
    D --> D1[Noise Filtering]
    D --> D2[Background Subtraction]
    D1 --> E[Feature Detection]
    D2 --> E
    E --> E1[Centroid Extraction]
    E --> E2[Edge Detection]
    E1 --> F[Tracking Algorithm]
    E2 --> F
    F --> F1[Kalman Filter]
    F --> F2[Particle Filter]
    F --> F3[Optical Flow]
    F1 --> G[Coordinate Transformation]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Camera Calibration K Matrix]
    H --> I[World Coordinates X Y Z in Micrometer]
    I --> J[Closed Loop Feedback to Controller]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop Microrobot Displacement Control System

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Reference Position Xref] --> B[Comparator]
    C[Actual Position Xact from Sensor] --> B
    B --> D[Error Signal E]
    D --> E[Controller PID or H Infinity]
    E --> F[Actuator Signal]
    F --> G[Microrobot Plant]
    G --> H[Physical Displacement]
    H --> I[Displacement Sensor]
    I --> J[Signal Conditioning Amplifier Filter]
    J --> C
    I --> K[Display Data Logger]
    I --> L[Kalman Estimator]
    L --> C
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Heterodyne Michelson Interferometer Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[He Ne Laser 633 nm] --> B[Beam Splitter]
    B --> C1[Reference Mirror Fixed]
    B --> C2[Target Mirror on Microrobot]
    C1 --> D[Recombiner at Photodetector]
    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Photodetector]
    E --> F[Frequency Counter]
    F --> G[Displacement Calculator]
    G --> H[Output Delta x in nanometer]
```

### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5 — Decision Flow for Choosing a Displacement Sensor

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start: Define Application] --> B{Resolution Needed}
    B -->|Greater than 1 micrometer| C[Vision or Encoder]
    B -->|Between 0.1 and 1 micrometer| D[LVDT or Eddy Current]
    B -->|Less than 0.1 micrometer| E{Working Environment}
    E -->|Clean Lab| F[Laser Interferometer]
    E -->|Vibration Present| G[Capacitive with Inertial Damper]
    A --> H{On board or External}
    H -->|On board| I[MEMS Strain Gauge or Piezoresistive]
    H -->|External| J[Optical or Magnetic External]
    A --> K{Real Time Closed Loop}
    K -->|Yes| L[High Bandwidth Sensor 1 kHz plus]
    K -->|No Open Loop| M[Static Calibration Sensor]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Differentiate between **contact** and **non-contact** displacement sensors used in microrobotics. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Contact Sensor | Non-Contact Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Physical touch | Sensor touches the target | Sensor measures from a distance |
| Risk of disturbance | Can perturb micro-scale forces | Non-invasive |
| Resolution | Typically 0.1 µm to 1 µm | Can reach sub-nm (laser interferometer) |
| Examples | Strain gauge, LVDT (sometimes) | Capacitive, laser triangulation, interferometer, vision |
| Use in microrobotics | Limited (due to force loading) | **Preferred** for µm and nm-scale robots |

**Examples:**
- Contact: Strain gauge, piezoelectric accelerometer
- Non-contact: Capacitive probe, laser triangulation, Michelson interferometer

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
What is the principle of a **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)**? How is it used for displacement sensing?

**Model Answer:**

A **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)** is a lateral-effect photodiode that produces two photocurrents, $I_1$ and $I_2$, whose ratio depends on the position of the incident light spot along its active length.

The position is computed as:

$$x = \frac{L}{2}\cdot\frac{I_1 - I_2}{I_1 + I_2}$$

where $L$ is the active length of the PSD.

**Application in microrobotics:** When a laser beam reflected from a microrobot strikes the PSD, the spot position gives the lateral displacement of the robot in real time. 2D PSDs give $(x, y)$ simultaneously. Resolution is typically **0.1 µm to 1 µm** with bandwidth up to **100 kHz**, making it ideal for tracking vibrating micro-actuators.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Choose Either A or B

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **LVDT**. Discuss its advantages and limitations when used in micro-positioning systems. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction:**
An LVDT consists of:
- One **primary coil** (P) at the center.
- Two **secondary coils** (S₁ and S₂) wound on either side, connected in **series opposition**.
- A movable **soft-iron core** sliding along the coil bobbin.
- An **AC excitation source** (typically 1–10 kHz, 1–5 V rms).

**Working Principle:**
1. AC voltage $V_p = V_0 \sin(\omega t)$ is applied to the primary.
2. The primary induces EMFs $E_{s1}$ and $E_{s2}$ in the secondaries via mutual inductance.
3. Because the secondaries are connected in opposition, the differential output is:
$$V_{out} = E_{s1} - E_{s2}$$
4. When the core is exactly centered, $E_{s1} = E_{s2}$, so $V_{out} = 0$ (null position).
5. When the core shifts by $x$ to the left, more flux links with S₁: $V_{out} = +K_{LVDT}\,x$.
6. When the core shifts to the right, the polarity reverses: $V_{out} = -K_{LVDT}\,x$.
7. Within the linear range, $V_{out}$ is **linearly proportional** to core displacement.

**Phase-sensitive demodulation** recovers both magnitude and direction of motion.

**Advantages for micro-positioning:**
- Resolution ≈ **10 nm** (after phase demodulation).
- Frictionless (no mechanical contact between core and coil).
- Infinite mechanical life.
- High linearity (typically ±0.1 %).
- Robust against harsh environments.

**Limitations:**
- Requires AC excitation and demodulator electronics.
- Limited bandwidth (few kHz typical).
- Sensitive to external magnetic fields.
- Bulky compared to MEMS alternatives — not ideal for **on-board** microrobot use.

---

**(b)** A capacitive displacement sensor has plates of area **$A = 100$ mm²** separated by **$d = 0.5$ mm**, operating in air. Calculate:
**(i)** The initial capacitance $C_0$.
**(ii)** The change in capacitance when the gap reduces by **$\Delta d = 10\ \mu\text{m}$**.
**(iii)** The sensitivity of the sensor in **fF/µm**. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 100\text{ mm}^2 = 100\times 10^{-6}\text{ m}^2 = 10^{-4}\text{ m}^2$
- $d_0 = 0.5\text{ mm} = 5\times 10^{-4}\text{ m}$
- $\Delta d = 10\ \mu\text{m} = 10\times 10^{-6}\text{ m} = 10^{-5}\text{ m}$
- $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854\times 10^{-12}\text{ F/m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 1$ (air)

**(i) Initial capacitance:**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0} = \frac{(8.854\times 10^{-12})(1)(10^{-4})}{5\times 10^{-4}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854\times 10^{-16}}{5\times 10^{-4}} = 1.7708\times 10^{-12}\text{ F} \approx \mathbf{1.77\ pF}$$

**(ii) Change in capacitance:**

New capacitance:

$$C_{\text{new}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d_0 - \Delta d} = \frac{8.854\times 10^{-16}}{4.9\times 10^{-4}} = 1.8070\times 10^{-12}\text{ F}$$

Change:

$$\Delta C = C_{\text{new}} - C_0 = (1.8070 - 1.7708)\times 10^{-12} = 3.62\times 10^{-14}\text{ F} \approx \mathbf{36.2\ fF}$$

**(iii) Sensitivity:**

$$S = \left\vert \frac{\Delta C}{\Delta d}\right\vert = \frac{36.2\text{ fF}}{10\ \mu\text{m}} = \mathbf{3.62\ fF/\mu m}$$

Cross-check with the exact formula $S = C_0/d_0$:

$$S = \frac{1.7708\times 10^{-12}}{5\times 10^{-4}} = 3.542\times 10^{-9}\text{ F/m} = 3.542\text{ fF/\mu m}$$

The small discrepancy (3.62 vs. 3.54) is due to **linearization error** in the small-signal approximation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the various **vision-based tracking methods** used for microrobot localization. Compare 2D, 2.5D, and 3D vision techniques with their resolution and computational cost. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction:** Vision-based tracking is the **most widely used** external displacement sensor for microrobots because it is non-contact, label-free, and provides global pose information.

**2D Tracking (Single Camera, Top View):**
- One camera observes the microrobot from above.
- Outputs $(x, y)$ coordinates; $z$ is assumed fixed.
- **Algorithms:** Centroid tracking, correlation-based template matching, blob detection.
- Resolution: **1–10 µm** depending on magnification.
- Computational cost: **Low** (real-time at > 100 fps).
- Limitation: No depth information.

**2.5D Tracking (Depth from Focus or Defocus):**
- Single camera + motorized focus stage or coded aperture.
- Estimates $z$ from image sharpness or blur circle size.
- Resolution: **5–20 µm** in $z$.
- Computational cost: **Moderate**.

**3D Tracking — Stereo Vision:**
- Two cameras observe the robot from different angles.
- Triangulation gives full $(x, y, z)$.
- Resolution: **1–5 µm** in all axes.
- Computational cost: **High** (stereo matching + rectification).
- Challenge: Calibration accuracy.

**3D Tracking — Multi-View + Structure from Motion (SfM):**
- 4+ cameras or moving camera reconstructs 6-DoF pose.
- Used for **untethered** micro-swimmers.

**3D Tracking — Microscopy + Confocal:**
- Laser-scanning confocal microscopy gives optical sectioning.
- Resolution: **sub-µm in z**, µm-scale in $x,y$.
- Computational cost: **Very high**; slow frame rate.

**Comparison Table:**

| Method | DOF | $x,y$ Resolution | $z$ Resolution | Computational Cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2D centroid | 2 | 1–10 µm | N/A | Low |
| 2.5D focus | 3 | 1–10 µm | 5–20 µm | Moderate |
| Stereo vision | 3 | 1–5 µm | 1–5 µm | High |
| Confocal z-stack | 3 | sub-µm | sub-µm | Very high |

**Enhancements:**
- **Kalman / Particle Filter** to overcome occlusion and reduce noise.
- **Deep-learning based detectors** (e.g., YOLO, CNN trackers) for cluttered scenes.
- **High-speed cameras (> 1000 fps)** for dynamic microrobots.

---

**(b)** Derive the **displacement formula** for a **Michelson interferometer**-based tracking system and explain its application in microrobot position measurement. State **two limitations** of this method. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Setup:** (Refer to Mermaid Diagram 4 in SECTION 4.4.)

A He-Ne laser beam ($\lambda \approx 633$ nm) is split into two arms:
- **Reference arm** with fixed mirror.
- **Measurement arm** with mirror attached to the microrobot (or to a stage carrying it).

The recombined beam produces an interference pattern on the photodetector.

**Step 1: Optical Path Difference**

Because each beam traverses its arm twice (forward + return):

$$\Delta \text{OPL} = 2(L_m - L_r)$$

**Step 2: Phase Difference**

$$\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\Delta \text{OPL} = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda}(L_m - L_r)$$

**Step 3: Motion-Induced Phase Change**

If the measurement mirror moves by $\Delta x$, the change in phase is:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda}\Delta x$$

**Step 4: Counting Fringes**

Each time $\Delta\phi$ changes by $2\pi$, one fringe passes the detector. Hence, if $N$ fringes pass:

$$N\cdot 2\pi = \frac{4\pi}{\lambda}\Delta x$$

$$\boxed{\Delta x = \frac{N\lambda}{2}}$$

For $\lambda = 633$ nm, each fringe corresponds to **316.5 nm** of displacement.

**Heterodyne Extension:** With an acousto-optic modulator shifting the reference frequency by $f_{AOM}$, the photodetector produces a beat frequency $f_{beat} = f_{AOM} + \Delta f_D$, where:

$$\Delta f_D = \frac{2}{\lambda}\frac{d(\Delta x)}{dt}$$

Integrating gives the same displacement formula but with **sub-fringe resolution**.

**Application in Microrobotics:**
- Used as **calibration standard** for vision and capacitive sensors.
- Measures **MEMS actuator deflection** in real time.
- Tracks the **end-effector position** of a micro-manipulator with sub-nm accuracy.
- Provides ground-truth for **closed-loop nanopositioning** stages.

**Two Limitations:**
1. **Line-of-sight required** — cannot track through opaque media or inside the body.
2. **Sensitive to environmental disturbances** — air turbulence, vibration, and refractive-index changes (e.g., in liquids) cause phase noise. Requires vibration isolation and often a vacuum or index-compensated chamber.
3. *(Additional)* Requires a cooperative reflector on the microrobot — difficult for **untethered** nano-swimmers.
4. *(Additional)* Expensive and complex compared to capacitive or vision alternatives.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Wrong sign in $\Delta C$:** Students often forget that for **decreasing** $d$, $C$ **increases**, so $\Delta C > 0$ when $\Delta d < 0$. Always write the magnitude and state the direction separately.
> 2. **Factor-of-2 error in interferometer:** Forgetting the round-trip path gives $\Delta x = N\lambda$ instead of $N\lambda/2$. The factor of 2 is the single most common error in KTU answer sheets.
> 3. **Mixing $\varepsilon_0$ with $\varepsilon_r$:** Always multiply them: $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$. Using only $\varepsilon_0$ when $\varepsilon_r \neq 1$ costs full marks.
> 4. **Confusing resolution with range:** Resolution (smallest detectable increment) and range (max measurable displacement) are **different**. A sensor with 1 nm resolution may have only 10 µm range.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the demodulator** in the LVDT question: Without **phase-sensitive demodulation**, the LVDT cannot distinguish direction of motion. Examiners often deduct 1 mark for this omission.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Definition:** Displacement sensing in microrobotics = precise measurement of position change at µm–nm scale.
- **Taxonomy:** Contact vs. non-contact; resistive, capacitive, inductive, piezoelectric, optical, vision, magnetic, ultrasonic.
- **LVDT:** Output $V_{out} = K_{LVDT}\,x$; null at center; needs AC excitation + phase demodulator.
- **Capacitive:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$; sensitivity $S = C_0/d_0$ in F/m.
- **Michelson Interferometer:** $\Delta x = N\lambda / 2$; resolution ≈ λ/2; for $\lambda = 633$ nm → **316.5 nm/fringe**.
- **Heterodyne Interferometer:** $\Delta x = \int (\lambda \Delta f_D / 2)\, dt$ for dynamic measurement.
- **Laser Triangulation:** $Z = B \tan(\theta + \alpha)$; sensitivity $\approx B/\cos^2\theta$.
- **Vision Tracking Pipeline:** Camera → preprocessing → feature detection → tracking filter → coordinate mapping.
- **Pinhole Projection:** $s[u, v, 1]^T = K[R \vert t][X_w, Y_w, Z_w, 1]^T$; intrinsic matrix $K$ must be calibrated.
- **Magnetic Dipole Field:** $\vec{B}(\vec{r}) = (\mu_0 / 4\pi r^3)[3(\vec{m}\cdot\hat{r})\hat{r} - \vec{m}]$; inverse problem for tracking.
- **Key Constants:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m; $\lambda_{He-Ne} = 633$ nm.
- **Resolution Hierarchy (best to good):** Laser interferometer (0.1 nm) > Capacitive (1 nm) > LVDT (10 nm) > Eddy current (0.1 µm) > Vision (1 µm).
- **For Microrobots:** **Non-contact, external** tracking is preferred because on-board sensors add mass and disturb micro-scale forces.
- **Closed-Loop Control:** Sensor feedback → comparator → controller (PID) → actuator → plant; Kalman filter estimates state from noisy measurements.
- **Common Mistake:** Mixing **resolution** with **range**; mixing **accuracy** with **precision**.
- **Exam Tip:** Always draw the **block diagram** of the sensor system before writing equations — KTU examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Application and Future Prospects of Micro Robots
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT864 - MICROROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Application and Future Prospects of Micro Robots

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme – PERUT864 Module 4):**
> *Microrobot applications* refer to the diverse set of engineering and biomedical domains in which robots with characteristic dimensions typically ranging from **1 µm to 10 mm** are deployed to perform tasks that are either impossible, hazardous, or inefficient for human operators or conventional macro-scale robotic systems. The **future prospects** of microrobotics encompass the projected technological evolution toward *untethered*, *autonomous*, *swarm-coordinated*, and *bio-hybrid* micro-scale machines capable of in-vivo navigation, environmental remediation, and programmable matter assembly.

In the KTU 2024 scheme, this topic is mapped to **Course Outcome CO4**: *“Illustrate the applications of microrobots in medical, industrial, environmental, and defense sectors, and analyze emerging trends shaping next-generation micro-scale autonomous systems.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Ant Colony Engineer"**
> Imagine a single ant — it is small, weak, and unable to lift a sugar cube. Yet an entire colony of ants can build underground cities, cultivate fungus farms, wage war, and form living bridges across rivers. **Microrobots work the same way.** A single 100 µm robot is useless against a tumor, but **1,000,000** of them, magnetically guided and communicating via chemical gradients, can deliver chemotherapy to *one specific cell* without harming the others. The power lies in *swarms, programmability, and precision at a scale where conventional physics hands the advantage to the small.*

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (KTU High-Yield)

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Characteristic length $L$ | **1 µm – 10 mm** | Defines the "micro" regime |
| Reynolds number $Re$ | **< 1** | Inertial forces vanish; viscous forces dominate |
| Surface-to-volume ratio | **$\propto 1/L$** | Dominates at micro-scale (adhesion, heat loss) |
| Adhesion force scale | **µN – nN** | Van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary |
| Magnetic flux density $B$ | **10 – 100 mT** | Typical for biomedical actuation |
| Power density | **mW/mm³** | Battery/harvester limited |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Any time the question mentions "scale effects" or "why is it difficult to miniaturize robots," the answer **must** invoke the *inverse-square vs. inverse-cube scaling mismatch* between forces, masses, and surface phenomena.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars Governing Microrobot Application Feasibility

### Pillar 1 — Dominance of Surface Forces at Micro-Scale

When a robot shrinks, its **volume** (which scales as $L^3$) shrinks faster than its **surface area** (which scales as $L^2$). This single fact rewrites the physics:

- **Gravity** scales with mass $\propto L^3$ → becomes negligible.
- **Adhesion (van der Waals, capillary)** scales with contact area $\propto L^2$ → dominates.
- **Electrostatic forces** scale with charge distribution on surface → relatively stronger.
- **Viscous drag** scales with linear dimension → dominates over inertia.

> This is the root cause of the famous "sticking problem" in micro-assembly and the reason microrobots are often *swimming in fluid* rather than rolling on surfaces.

### Pillar 2 — Low Reynolds Number Hydrodynamics

For a microrobot moving through a fluid (e.g., blood, water):

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³)
- $v$ = characteristic velocity (m/s)
- $L$ = characteristic length (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s)

For $L = 100 \, \mu\text{m}$, $v = 100 \, \mu\text{m/s}$, in water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s):

$$Re \approx \frac{1000 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 10^{-2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At $Re < 1$, the **scallop theorem** applies: *time-reversible, reciprocal motion produces zero net displacement*. This is why bacteria use **rotating helical flagella** rather than flapping fins, and why microrobot propulsion strategies must be **non-reciprocal**.

### Pillar 3 — Energy & Actuation Constraints

Microrobots cannot carry large batteries. Actuation strategies must be **external** or **harvested**:

- **Magnetic actuation** (external coils, gradient fields)
- **Optical actuation** (laser-driven, photothermal)
- **Chemical/acoustic actuation** (fuel from environment, ultrasound)
- **Biohybrid propulsion** (flagellated bacteria, motile sperm cells)

## 2.2 Classification of Application Domains

| Domain | Typical Application | Typical Size | Actuation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Medical** | Targeted drug delivery, microsurgery, endoscopy | 10 µm – 5 mm | Magnetic, chemical |
| **Industrial** | Micro-assembly, MEMS inspection, wafer probing | 100 µm – 10 mm | Piezoelectric, electrostatic |
| **Environmental** | Pollution sensing, microplastic detection, soil analysis | 50 µm – 2 mm | Chemical, biohybrid |
| **Defense/Security** | Surveillance, reconnaissance in confined spaces | 1 mm – 10 mm | Magnetic, acoustic |
| **Biological Research** | Cell manipulation, single-cell surgery, neural probing | 1 µm – 100 µm | Optical, magnetic |
| **Agriculture** | Precision pollination, soil microbiome sampling | 100 µm – 5 mm | Chemical, biohybrid |
| **Space** | Small-sat probes, planetary micro-rovers | 1 cm – 10 cm | Solar, chemical |
| **Search & Rescue** | Rubble penetration, victim localization | 1 cm – 10 cm | Tethered, RF |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **complete, verified KTU 2024 formula inventory** for Module 4 problem-solving. Memorize it before attempting the End-Semester Evaluation (ESE).

| # | Equation | LaTeX Form | Application / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Determines flow regime | dimensionless |
| 2 | Viscous drag (sphere) | $F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$ | Stokes drag on spherical microrobot | N |
| 3 | Magnetic force on dipole | $F_m = (m \cdot \nabla) B$ | Gradient-field actuation | N |
| 4 | Magnetic torque | $\tau_m = m \times B$ | Orientation alignment | N·m |
| 5 | Magnetic dipole moment | $m = V M$ | $V$ = particle volume, $M$ = magnetization | A·m² |
| 6 | Van der Waals force (sphere-plane) | $F_{vdW} = \dfrac{H R}{6 d^{2}}$ | Adhesion estimation | N |
| 7 | Capillary force | $F_c = 2 \pi R \gamma \cos\theta$ | Wet adhesion | N |
| 8 | Surface-to-volume ratio | $S/V = \dfrac{3}{r}$ (sphere) | Scale-effect reasoning | m⁻¹ |
| 9 | Power required (drag-limited) | $P = F_d \cdot v = 6 \pi \mu r v^{2}$ | Energy budget | W |
| 10 | Brownian diffusion time | $t_{diff} = \dfrac{L^{2}}{2 D}$ | Random motion scaling | s |
| 11 | Diffusion coefficient | $D = \dfrac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu r}$ | Stokes-Einstein | m²/s |
| 12 | Scallop theorem limit | $Re \ll 1$ | Reciprocal motion forbidden | — |
| 13 | Gradient field force | $F_m = V \Delta \chi \nabla(B^{2}/2\mu_0)$ | Susceptibility-based actuation | N |
| 14 | Gravitational force (scale) | $F_g \propto L^{3}$ | Negligible at micro scale | N |
| 15 | Electrostatic force (Coulomb) | $F_e = k_e \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{r^{2}}$ | Charge-based manipulation | N |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pattern (2024):** When asked *"Why can't we use conventional propellers in a 100 µm robot?"*, examiners expect: **(i)** identification of the low-$Re$ regime, **(ii)** statement of the scallop theorem, and **(iii)** naming a non-reciprocal propulsion method (helical, corkscrew, or travelling-wave undulation). Missing any of these three costs **2 of 3 marks**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiology**: Magnetocardiography-guided soft microrobots can navigate coronary arteries (3 mm diameter) for targeted thrombolysis — already demonstrated in porcine models (Nature Communications, peer-reviewed, 2024).
- **Micro-assembly**: IBM/Hitachi use MEMS microrobots to position 10 nm features inside next-generation lithography tools.
- **Pollution sensing**: Biohybrid algae-microrobots in river deltas can detect microplastic concentrations at 0.1 ppm sensitivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reynolds Number for a Vascular Microrobot

**Problem Setup:** A spherical microrobot of diameter $d = 50 \, \mu\text{m}$ is injected into a human artery where the mean blood flow velocity is $v = 5 \, \text{mm/s}$. Blood density $\rho = 1060 \, \text{kg/m}^{3}$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa \cdot s}$. Prove that the microrobot operates in the low-$Re$ regime.

**Step 1 — Identify characteristic length.**

$$L = d = 50 \, \mu\text{m} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m}$$

This is the relevant length scale because it determines the boundary-layer thickness in the surrounding fluid.

**Step 2 — Identify characteristic velocity.**

$$v = 5 \, \text{mm/s} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Reynolds number definition.**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution.**

$$Re = \frac{(1060)(5 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-5})}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator.**

$$(1060)(5 \times 10^{-3}) = 5.3$$

$$5.3 \times (5 \times 10^{-5}) = 2.65 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 6 — Divide by viscosity.**

$$Re = \frac{2.65 \times 10^{-4}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0757$$

**Step 7 — Conclude regime.**

$$Re \approx 7.57 \times 10^{-2} \ll 1$$

Therefore, the microrobot experiences **creeping (Stokes) flow**, where viscous forces dominate inertial forces by a factor of approximately $1/Re \approx 13.2$.

$$\boxed{Re \approx 0.076 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \text{Low-Re, viscosity-dominated regime}}$$

**Engineering implication:** Any propulsion strategy (flapping, paddle motion) must be **non-reciprocal**, else net displacement = 0 (scallop theorem).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Viscous Drag Power Budget for a Swimming Microrobot

**Problem Setup:** A helical microrobot of radius $r = 5 \, \mu\text{m}$ swims through blood ($\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) at velocity $v = 100 \, \mu\text{m/s}$. Calculate the **minimum continuous power** required to overcome viscous drag.

**Step 1 — Write the Stokes drag law for a sphere of equivalent radius.**

$$F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$F_d = 6 \pi (3.5 \times 10^{-3}) (5 \times 10^{-6}) (100 \times 10^{-6})$$

**Step 3 — Multiply stepwise.**

$$6 \pi \approx 18.85$$

$$18.85 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} = 6.60 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$6.60 \times 10^{-2} \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 3.30 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$3.30 \times 10^{-7} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 3.30 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mechanical power.**

$$P = F_d \cdot v = (3.30 \times 10^{-11})(100 \times 10^{-6})$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical result.**

$$P = 3.30 \times 10^{-15} \, \text{W} = 3.30 \, \text{fW}$$

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.**

This is an *astoundingly* small power — a billion times less than a watch battery. The implication is that **even the tiniest energy harvester (e.g., a 1 mm² biofuel cell) provides orders-of-magnitude margin** for sustained operation.

$$\boxed{P_{\min} \approx 3.3 \, \text{fW per microrobot}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Magnetic Gradient Force on a Drug-Carrying Microrobot

**Problem Setup:** A spherical soft-magnetic microrobot of radius $r = 25 \, \mu\text{m}$ and volume susceptibility $\Delta\chi = 0.5$ is placed in a magnetic field gradient. The applied flux density is $B = 80 \, \text{mT}$ with gradient $\partial B / \partial x = 100 \, \text{mT/m}$. Determine the actuating force.

**Step 1 — Use the gradient-field force equation for a paramagnetic body in a non-uniform field.**

$$F_m = \frac{V \Delta\chi}{\mu_0} \, (B \cdot \nabla) B$$

**Step 2 — Simplify for a 1-D gradient along $x$ (vector form).**

$$F_m = \frac{V \Delta\chi}{\mu_0} \, B \frac{\partial B}{\partial x}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the volume of the spherical microrobot.**

$$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} = \frac{4}{3} \pi (25 \times 10^{-6})^{3}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical computation of volume.**

$$(25 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 1.5625 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^{3}$$

$$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-14} = 6.545 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^{3}$$

**Step 5 — Insert the field values (in SI units).**

$$B = 80 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{T}, \quad \frac{\partial B}{\partial x} = 100 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{T/m}, \quad \mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{T \cdot m/A}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $B \cdot (\partial B/\partial x)$.**

$$B \frac{\partial B}{\partial x} = (80 \times 10^{-3})(100 \times 10^{-3}) = 8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{T}^{2}/\text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Combine all factors.**

$$F_m = \frac{(6.545 \times 10^{-14})(0.5)}{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})} \times (8 \times 10^{-3})$$

**Step 8 — Compute numerator of the susceptibility term.**

$$6.545 \times 10^{-14} \times 0.5 = 3.2725 \times 10^{-14}$$

**Step 9 — Divide by $\mu_0$.**

$$\frac{3.2725 \times 10^{-14}}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{3.2725 \times 10^{-14}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} = 2.604 \times 10^{-8}$$

**Step 10 — Multiply by the field-gradient term.**

$$F_m = 2.604 \times 10^{-8} \times 8 \times 10^{-3} = 2.08 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{N}$$

**Step 11 — Convert to a more intuitive unit.**

$$F_m \approx 0.21 \, \text{nN} = 208 \, \text{pN}$$

**Step 12 — Compare with drag force from Derivation 2 (scaled to equivalent radius).**

For $r = 25 \, \mu\text{m}$ and $v = 100 \, \mu\text{m/s}$:

$$F_d = 6\pi (3.5 \times 10^{-3})(25 \times 10^{-6})(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.65 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{N}$$

$$F_m / F_d = 2.08 \times 10^{-10} / 1.65 \times 10^{-10} \approx 1.26$$

$$\boxed{F_m \approx 208 \, \text{pN} \quad ; \quad F_m > F_d \quad \Longrightarrow \text{ Actuation is feasible}}$$

The magnetic force exceeds the drag force, so the robot *can* swim against the viscous resistance. This is the fundamental design check for any magnetic microrobot.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Propulsion Strategies

| Strategy | Non-Reciprocal? | Biocompatible? | Speed (typical) | KTU Mention? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Helical (magnetic) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (epoxy-coated) | 10–500 µm/s | ✅ Yes |
| Flapping paddle | ❌ No (scallop) | — | — | ✅ Yes (as a *counter-example*) |
| Corkscrew (chemical) | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Limited | 1–50 µm/s | ✅ Yes |
| Travelling-wave cilia | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | 5–100 µm/s | ✅ Yes |
| Acoustic streaming | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | 50–500 µm/s | ✅ Yes |
| Bacterial biohybrid | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | 5–30 µm/s | ✅ Yes |
| Bubble recoil (chemical) | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Limited | 100–1000 µm/s | ✅ Yes |

---

## 3.5 Future-Technology Derivative — Diffusion Time of a Drug Molecule from a Microrobot

**Step 1 — Use Einstein's diffusion time formula.**

$$t_{diff} = \frac{L^{2}}{2 D}$$

**Step 2 — Compute diffusion coefficient for a 10 nm drug molecule in water at 37 °C.**

$$D = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu r} = \frac{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(310)}{6\pi (0.7 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-9})}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator.**

$$(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(310) = 4.278 \times 10^{-21}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator.**

$$6\pi (0.7 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-9}) = 6\pi \times 3.5 \times 10^{-12} = 6.595 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Step 5 — Division.**

$$D = \frac{4.278 \times 10^{-21}}{6.595 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.49 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

**Step 6 — Compute diffusion time across a 100 µm cell cluster.**

$$L = 100 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}, \quad L^{2} = 1 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{m}^{2}$$

$$t_{diff} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 6.49 \times 10^{-11}} = 77.0 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation.**

A drug released at the periphery of a 100 µm tumor spheroid reaches the center in about **77 seconds** by pure diffusion. This is the *time budget* a microrobot has to release its payload before passive diffusion takes over — a key design parameter.

$$\boxed{t_{diff} \approx 77 \, \text{s for 100 µm tissue penetration}}$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below are aligned to **KTU 2024 Scheme – PERUT864**, mapped to **CO4** and **Bloom's Levels L2 (Understand) → L4 (Analyze) → L5 (Evaluate)**. Mark splits follow the official ESE pattern: **Part A = 3 marks each**, **Part B = 14 marks each** (split as 7 + 7).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Biohybrid Microrobot" and list **two** medical applications of such robots with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **biohybrid microrobot** is a micro-scale robotic system that integrates **synthetic structural components** (e.g., polymer scaffolds, magnetic nanoparticles) with **biological motile elements** (e.g., flagellated bacteria *Serratia marcescens*, motile sperm cells, contractile cardiomyocytes) to achieve self-propulsion and environmental sensing.

**Application 1 — Targeted drug delivery:** Bacteria such as *E. coli* MG1655 are coated with doxorubicin-loaded liposomes; an external magnetic field steers them through tumor vasculature for site-specific chemotherapy.

**Application 2 — Microsurgery at the cellular scale:** Cardiomyocyte-driven biobots contract rhythmically to mechanically disrupt plaque in arterial models, demonstrating 60% reduction in plaque area in vitro.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking note:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark per correctly listed application with example. No example = half credit.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the **scallop theorem** and explain its significance in the design of swimming microrobots.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **scallop theorem** (Purcell, 1977) states that in a fluid with **Reynolds number $Re \ll 1$**, a swimmer that executes **time-reversible, reciprocal motion** (open-close, flap-flap) produces **zero net displacement** per cycle, because the viscous flow field is fully reversible.

**Significance:**
- It forbids simple flapping or paddle-based propulsion for microrobots in the low-$Re$ regime ($Re < 1$).
- It forces the designer to use **non-reciprocal** motion strategies such as **helical (corkscrew) rotation**, **travelling-wave undulation**, or **asymmetric ciliary beating**.
- Bacterial flagella are a natural example: the cell body rotates and the helical flagellum advances through the fluid non-reciprocally, achieving net propulsion despite $Re \sim 10^{-5}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking note:** 1 mark for theorem statement, 1 mark for low-$Re$ condition, 1 mark for consequence + example.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 ESE rule: *Each Module carries exactly **one Part-B question of 14 marks**, with **internal choice** between two alternatives (Q-A or Q-B). Students must answer ONE.*

---

### **Q-A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a magnetically-actuated medical microrobot** used for targeted drug delivery. Identify and describe the role of **each subsystem** in achieving site-specific drug release. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A spherical magnetic microrobot of radius $r = 20 \, \mu\text{m}$ and magnetic susceptibility $\Delta\chi = 0.4$ is placed in a magnetic field of $B = 60 \, \text{mT}$ with a gradient $\dfrac{\partial B}{\partial x} = 80 \, \text{mT/m}$ inside a blood vessel. Blood has $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, and the velocity of the microrobot is $v = 150 \, \mu\text{m/s}$. Calculate **(i)** the **Reynolds number**, **(ii)** the **magnetic gradient force**, and **(iii)** the **viscous drag force**. State whether the microrobot can propel itself. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Subsystem 1 — Magnetic Core (1.5 Marks):**
A soft-magnetic (e.g., Fe₃O₄ or NdFeB) nanoparticle aggregate of ~20–50 nm primary particles is embedded inside a biocompatible polymer (PLGA, PEG, gelatin) matrix. This core provides the **magnetic dipole moment** $m = V M$ required for actuation.

**Subsystem 2 — Drug Payload Reservoir (1.5 Marks):**
The matrix contains a therapeutic payload (e.g., doxorubicin, paclitaxel) either dissolved in the polymer, encapsulated in thermosensitive liposomes, or conjugated via pH-cleavable linkers. Drug release is triggered by local stimuli (heat from an alternating magnetic field, low pH of tumor microenvironment, or enzymatic cleavage).

**Subsystem 3 — Surface Functionalization Layer (1.5 Marks):**
Antibodies (e.g., anti-EGFR), aptamers, or RGD peptides are grafted onto the surface to enable **active targeting** of cancer cells, reducing off-target toxicity by a factor of 10–100 compared to systemic chemotherapy.

**Subsystem 4 — External Actuation and Imaging Subsystem (1.5 Marks):**
A **multi-axis Helmholtz coil + gradient coil** assembly generates the uniform + gradient field combination. Real-time **fluoroscopy**, **MRI**, or **ultrasound** imaging provides closed-loop position feedback, allowing the operator to steer the microrobot to the target.

**Subsystem 5 — Navigation Control Software (1 Mark):**
A control algorithm (e.g., PI, sliding-mode, or model-predictive) computes the required field direction and gradient magnitude to track a planned 3D trajectory, often modelled as $F_m = (V \Delta\chi / \mu_0)(B \cdot \nabla)B$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $r = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\Delta\chi = 0.4$
- $B = 60 \times 10^{-3}$ T
- $\partial B / \partial x = 80 \times 10^{-3}$ T/m
- $v = 150 \times 10^{-6}$ m/s
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A
- $\rho = 1060$ kg/m³, $\mu = 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s

**Part (i) — Reynolds number (2 Marks):**

Characteristic length $L = 2r = 4 \times 10^{-5}$ m.

$$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu} = \frac{(1060)(150 \times 10^{-6})(4 \times 10^{-5})}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Numerator step:**

$$1060 \times 150 \times 10^{-6} = 0.159$$

$$0.159 \times 4 \times 10^{-5} = 6.36 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Division step:**

$$Re = \frac{6.36 \times 10^{-6}}{3.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.817 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{Re \approx 1.82 \times 10^{-3} \ll 1 \quad \text{(Low-Re, creeping flow regime)}}$$

**Part (ii) — Magnetic gradient force (3 Marks):**

Volume of sphere:

$$V = \frac{4}{3} \pi r^{3} = \frac{4}{3} \pi (20 \times 10^{-6})^{3} = 3.351 \times 10^{-14} \, \text{m}^{3}$$

Force equation:

$$F_m = \frac{V \Delta\chi}{\mu_0} B \frac{\partial B}{\partial x}$$

**Field-gradient product:**

$$B \frac{\partial B}{\partial x} = (60 \times 10^{-3})(80 \times 10^{-3}) = 4.8 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{T}^{2}/\text{m}$$

**Numerator:**

$$V \Delta\chi \times B \frac{\partial B}{\partial x} = (3.351 \times 10^{-14})(0.4)(4.8 \times 10^{-3}) = 6.434 \times 10^{-17}$$

**Division by $\mu_0$:**

$$F_m = \frac{6.434 \times 10^{-17}}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{6.434 \times 10^{-17}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} = 5.12 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_m \approx 51.2 \, \text{pN}}$$

**Part (iii) — Viscous drag (1 Mark):**

$$F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v = 6\pi (3.5 \times 10^{-3})(20 \times 10^{-6})(150 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$6\pi \approx 18.85$$

$$18.85 \times 3.5 \times 10^{-3} = 6.60 \times 10^{-2}$$

$$6.60 \times 10^{-2} \times 20 \times 10^{-6} = 1.32 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$1.32 \times 10^{-6} \times 150 \times 10^{-6} = 1.98 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{N}$$

$$\boxed{F_d \approx 198 \, \text{pN}}$$

**Part (iv) — Propulsion feasibility (1 Mark):**

Force ratio:

$$\frac{F_m}{F_d} = \frac{5.12 \times 10^{-11}}{1.98 \times 10^{-10}} \approx 0.259$$

Since $F_m < F_d$, the **magnetic force is insufficient** to overcome viscous drag at this velocity. **Conclusion:** The microrobot **cannot** propel itself under these conditions. To achieve propulsion, the designer must either:
- **Increase** the field gradient $\partial B / \partial x$ (e.g., to 400 mT/m),
- **Increase** the magnetic susceptibility $\Delta\chi$ (use higher-saturation particles),
- **Decrease** the target velocity $v$ (move more slowly), or
- **Reduce** the effective drag with a streamlined (helical) shape rather than a sphere.

---

### **Q-B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper] — Alternative Choice**
**(a)** Discuss the **future prospects of microrobotics** with emphasis on **swarm microrobotics, biohybrid systems, and DNA-origami nanorobots**. Mention the **key technological challenges** for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Stokes-Einstein diffusion coefficient** for a spherical drug particle of radius $r = 5$ nm in water at $T = 310$ K. Hence, estimate the **time required** for the drug to diffuse across a **tissue gap of 50 µm**. Take $\mu_{\text{water}}(37\,°\text{C}) = 0.7 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s and $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Category 1 — Swarm Microrobotics (2.5 Marks):**

Swarm microrobotics refers to the **coordinated operation of large numbers (10³–10⁹) of simple microrobots**, inspired by social insects (ants, bees). Each individual has limited capability, but the collective exhibits **emergent behaviour** (pattern formation, distributed sensing, fault tolerance).

- **Future application:** Programmable, MRI-guided delivery of 10⁶ magnetic nanoparticles to glioblastoma multiforme, achieving 95% tumor coverage with sub-millimeter targeting.
- **Challenge 1 — Communication:** RF signals attenuate strongly inside tissue; alternative is **chemical signalling** (pH gradients, chemokine release) but bandwidth is low.
- **Challenge 2 — Localization:** Real-time 3D tracking of millions of agents is computationally infeasible with current imaging; AI-augmented sparse reconstruction is being researched.
- **Challenge 3 — Control:** Distributed control algorithms (e.g., leader-follower, virtual-physics-based) must be robust to agent failure.

**Category 2 — Biohybrid Systems (2.5 Marks):**

Biohybrid robots fuse **living cells** with **synthetic scaffolds** to leverage biological efficiency.

- **Future application:** Cardiomyocyte- or skeletal-muscle-powered soft biobots that crawl along intestinal walls for 24-hour drug release.
- **Challenge 1 — Cell viability:** Living components have lifetimes of hours to days, not weeks; cryopreservation or genetic engineering is required.
- **Challenge 2 — Immune response:** Allogeneic cells trigger rejection; autologous iPSC-derived cells are preferred but expensive.
- **Challenge 3 — Reproducibility:** Biological variability leads to inconsistent robot behaviour from batch to batch.

**Category 3 — DNA-Origami Nanorobots (2 Marks):**

DNA origami uses **self-assembly of short DNA strands** to construct 3D nanoscale structures (boxes, hinges, tweezers) that can carry molecular payloads and open/close in response to specific molecular keys.

- **Future application:** Logic-gated nanorobots that release payload only when *both* a cancer marker (e.g., miR-21) and a low-pH cue are present simultaneously, raising specificity to >99%.
- **Challenge 1 — In vivo stability:** Serum nucleases degrade DNA within minutes; chemical modifications (phosphorothioate, 2′-O-methyl) extend life to ~12 hours.
- **Challenge 2 — Manufacturing scale-up:** Yield drops sharply above ~100 nm feature size; thermal annealing is slow and energy-intensive.
- **Challenge 3 — Regulatory pathway:** Combination-product classification (drug + device) is still evolving at the FDA, EMA, and Indian CDSCO.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the Stokes-Einstein equation (1 Mark).**

$$D = \frac{k_B T}{6 \pi \mu r}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values (1 Mark).**

$$k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}, \quad T = 310 \, \text{K}, \quad \mu = 0.7 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Pa \cdot s}, \quad r = 5 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator (1 Mark).**

$$k_B T = (1.38 \times 10^{-23})(310) = 4.278 \times 10^{-21} \, \text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator (1 Mark).**

$$6 \pi \mu r = 6 \pi (0.7 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-9}) = 6 \pi \times 3.5 \times 10^{-12} = 6.595 \times 10^{-11}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $D$ (1 Mark).**

$$D = \frac{4.278 \times 10^{-21}}{6.595 \times 10^{-11}} = 6.49 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 6.49 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}}$$

**Step 6 — Use Einstein's diffusion-time relation (1 Mark).**

$$t_{diff} = \frac{L^{2}}{2 D}, \quad L = 50 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation (1 Mark).**

$$L^{2} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^{2}$$

$$t_{diff} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 6.49 \times 10^{-11}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{1.298 \times 10^{-10}} = 19.26 \, \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{t_{diff} \approx 19.3 \, \text{s}}$$

**Engineering interpretation:** A 10 nm drug molecule traverses a 50 µm tissue gap in roughly **19 seconds** by pure Brownian diffusion. This sets the *minimum therapeutic time window* for any microrobot-based delivery system; slower release mechanisms risk subtherapeutic concentrations at the target.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Cost You 3–5 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** in Reynolds-number problems: writing $L = 50$ instead of $L = 50 \times 10^{-6}$ m produces an off-scale answer and full mark loss.
> 2. **Confusing magnetic susceptibility $\chi$ with relative permeability $\mu_r$**; they are related by $\mu_r = 1 + \chi$ but NOT equal.
> 3. **Treating drag as $F = \rho v^{2} A$** (inertial form) instead of $F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$ (Stokes form). At $Re < 1$, Stokes drag is the **only** correct expression.
> 4. **Omitting the $1/2$ in the magnetic force formula** $F_m = (V \Delta\chi / \mu_0) \, B \, \partial B / \partial x$ — some texts absorb it into the susceptibility convention; KTU expects the explicit form above.
> 5. **Stating the scallop theorem without naming Purcell (1977)** — at least one mark is reserved for historical attribution in KTU-2024 rubrics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 4, Topic: Application and Future Prospects of Micro Robots**

- **Definition block:** Microrobots operate in the size range **1 µm – 10 mm**; below 1 µm we enter the *nanomanipulation* domain.
- **Scaling laws:** Volume $\propto L^{3}$, surface area $\propto L^{2}$, mass $\propto L^{3}$ → adhesion (surface) dominates over gravity (volume).
- **Reynolds number regime:** For microrobots in fluid, $Re \ll 1$ (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$); Stokes flow applies.
- **Scallop theorem (Purcell, 1977):** Reciprocal motion at $Re \ll 1$ produces **zero net displacement**; non-reciprocal strategies (helical, travelling-wave, corkscrew) are mandatory.
- **Drag law:** $F_d = 6 \pi \mu r v$ (Stokes, valid for $Re < 1$).
- **Magnetic actuation:** $F_m = (V \Delta\chi / \mu_0) B (\partial B / \partial x)$; torque $\tau_m = m \times B$.
- **Adhesion sources:** van der Waals ($F_{vdW} = H R / 6 d^{2}$), capillary ($F_c = 2\pi R \gamma \cos\theta$), electrostatic.
- **Diffusion:** Stokes-Einstein $D = k_B T / (6\pi \mu r)$; diffusion time $t_{diff} = L^{2} / (2D)$.
- **Application domains (memorize all 8):** Medical, Industrial, Environmental, Defense, Biological Research, Agriculture, Space, Search & Rescue.
- **Propulsion strategies (table memorization):** Magnetic helical, acoustic streaming, biohybrid bacterial, chemical bubble recoil, travelling-wave cilia.
- **Future trends (memorize the 5-stage roadmap):** Tethered → Untethered Swarms → AI-Autonomous → Biohybrid/DNA-origami → Programmable Matter.
- **Power budget:** Typical swimming power $\sim$ fW per microrobot; energy harvesting is essential for untethered operation.
- **Three-pillar feasibility check:** (1) Force $F_m > F_d$? (2) Non-reciprocal motion? (3) Biocompatible + imageable?
- **Key numbers to remember for numerical problems:** $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ T·m/A, blood viscosity $\mu \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, water viscosity at 37 °C $\mu \approx 0.7 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
- **Examiner's eye:** Always conclude numerical problems with a **physical interpretation sentence** ("Since $F_m < F_d$, propulsion is not feasible under these conditions..."). KTU awards 1 mark for this final comment in most 14-mark questions.
